title: Hypercube drawings with no long plane paths
comment: 19 pages, 11 figures, preliminary version to appear in proceedings of EuroCG 2026
summary: We study the existence of plane substructures in drawings of the $d$-dimensional hypercube graph $Q_d$. We construct drawings of $Q_d$ which contain no plane subgraph with more than $2d-2$ edges, no plane path with more than $2d-3$ edges, and no plane matching of size more than $2d-4$. On the other hand, we prove that every rectilinear drawing of $Q_d$ with vertices in convex position contains a plane path of length $d$ (if $d$ is odd) or $d-1$ (if $d$ is even). We also prove that if a graph $G$ is a plane subgraph of every drawing of $Q_d$ for a sufficiently large $d$, then $G$ is necessarily a forest of caterpillars. Lastly, we give a short proof of a generalization of a result by Alpert et al. [Cong. Numerantium, 2009] on the maximum rectilinear crossing number of $Q_d$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates plane substructures in drawings of the d-dimensional hypercube Q_d, giving tight bounds and structural results for plane subgraphs, paths, and matchings; it shows rectilinear convex-position drawings force long plane paths; universal plane subgraphs across large d are forests of caterpillars; and it offers a concise proof related to the maximum rectilinear crossing number of Q_d.
AI.motivation: To understand how geometric representations of hypercube graphs constrain plane subgraphs and to relate extremal plane-substructure properties to crossing numbers and universal subgraphs.
AI.method: Construct explicit drawings of Q_d to achieve upper bounds; prove existence of long plane paths in rectilinear convex-position drawings; analyze which graphs must occur in all drawings for large d to characterize universal plane subgraphs; provide a concise proof generalizing results on the maximum rectilinear crossing number.
AI.result:
- There exist drawings of Q_d with no plane subgraph having more than 2d-2 edges, no plane path longer than 2d-3 edges, and no plane matching of size greater than 2d-4. 
- In rectilinear drawings with vertices in convex position, there is a plane path of length d if d is odd, or d-1 if d is even. 
- If a graph G is contained as a plane subgraph in every drawing of Q_d for sufficiently large d, then G must be a forest of caterpillars. 
- A short proof is provided for a generalization of a result on the maximum rectilinear crossing number of Q_d (due to Alpert et al.).
AI.conclusion: The results establish tight bounds on plane substructures in Q_d drawings, classify universal plane subgraphs as structurally simple (forests of caterpillars) and connect these findings to rectilinear crossing numbers, enhancing the understanding of how geometry influences subgraph structure in hypercube graphs.

title: Quadratic polarity and polar Fenchel-Young divergences from the canonical Legendre polarity
summary: Polarity is a fundamental reciprocal duality of $n$-dimensional projective geometry which associates to points polar hyperplanes, and more generally $k$-dimensional convex bodies to polar $(n-1-k)$-dimensional convex bodies. It is well-known that the Legendre-Fenchel transformation of functions can be interpreted from the polarity viewpoint of their graphs using an extra dimension. In this paper, we first show that generic polarities induced by quadratic polarity functionals can be expressed either as deformed Legendre polarity or as the Legendre polarity of deformed convex bodies, and be efficiently manipulated using linear algebra on $(n+2)\times (n+2)$ matrices operating on homogeneous coordinates. Second, we define polar divergences using the Legendre polarity and show that they generalize the Fenchel-Young divergence or equivalent Bregman divergence. This polarity study brings new understanding of the core reference duality in information geometry. Last, we show that the total Bregman divergences can be considered as a total polar Fenchel-Young divergence from which we newly exhibit the reference duality using dual polar conformal factors.

AI.tldr: Unifies polarity in projective geometry with convex-duality via deformed Legendre transforms; introduces polar divergences generalizing Fenchel-Young/Bregman; reveals a total-polar viewpoint for total Bregman divergences and a duality with polar conformal factors.
AI.motivation: Bridge polarity in geometry with information-geometric dualities; provide an algebraic, matrix-based framework for polarities using homogeneous coordinates; extend divergence theory to a polar setting.
AI.method: Show that polarities from quadratic polarity functionals can be expressed as deformed Legendre polarity or Legendre polarity of deformed convex bodies; use (n+2)×(n+2) matrices acting on homogeneous coordinates to manipulate polarities; define polar divergences via Legendre polarity and relate them to Fenchel-Young/Bregman divergences; interpret total Bregman divergences as total polar Fenchel-Young divergences and derive dual polar conformal factors.
AI.result: A unified linear-algebraic framework for polarity in projective geometry; definition and analysis of polar divergences that generalize established divergences; establishment of a duality structure in information geometry via polar conformal factors; equivalence between total Bregman divergences and total polar Fenchel-Young divergences.
AI.conclusion: The study deepens the core reference duality in information geometry by embedding polarity-based descriptions within the Legendre/Fenchel–Young/Bregman framework, enabling efficient matrix-based manipulation and revealing new duality via dual polar conformal factors.

title: An Optimal Algorithm for Computing Many Faces in Line Arrangements
summary: Given a set of $m$ points and a set of $n$ lines in the plane, we consider the problem of computing the faces of the arrangement of the lines that contain at least one point. In this paper, we present an $O(m^{2/3}n^{2/3}+(n+m)\log n)$ time algorithm for the problem. We also show that this matches the lower bound under the algebraic decision tree model and thus our algorithm is optimal. In particular, when $m=n$, the runtime is $O(n^{4/3})$, which matches the worst case combinatorial complexity $Ω(n^{4/3})$ of all output faces. This is the first optimal algorithm since the problem was first studied more than three decades ago [Edelsbrunner, Guibas, and Sharir, SoCG 1988].

AI.tldr: An optimal algorithm for listing the faces of a line arrangement that contain at least one of m input points; runs in O(m^{2/3} n^{2/3} + (n+m) log n). The bound is proven optimal under the algebraic decision-tree model; in the symmetric case m=n the runtime is O(n^{4/3}), matching the worst-case output size Ω(n^{4/3}). This resolves a long-standing open problem dating back to Edelsbrunner–Guibas–Sharir (SoCG 1988).
AI.motivation: To determine the most efficient method for computing only the relevant faces of a line arrangement with respect to a given point set, and to close the gap between upper bounds and lower bounds in this classical problem.
AI.method: Develops an algorithm achieving the stated time bound and provides a lower-bound argument under the algebraic decision-tree model to establish optimality; also analyzes the special case m=n and its relationship to the output size.
AI.result: Algorithm with running time O(m^{2/3} n^{2/3} + (n+m) log n); proven lower bound matching the upper bound under the algebraic decision-tree model; for m=n, O(n^{4/3}) time, matching the output complexity Ω(n^{4/3}).
AI.conclusion: The paper delivers the first optimal algorithm for this problem since its inception, confirming tight complexity bounds and resolving a three-decade-old question about the optimality of computing line-arrangement faces that contain input points.

title: Reconfiguration of Squares Using a Constant Number of Moves Each
summary: Multi-robot motion planning is a hard problem. We investigate restricted variants of the problem where square robots are allowed to slide over an arbitrary curve to a new position only a constant number of times each. We show that the problem remains NP-hard in most cases, except when the squares have unit size and when the problem is unlabeled, i.e., the location of each square in the target configuration is left unspecified.

AI.tldr: General NP-hardness for restricted multi-robot motion planning with sliding; tractable in unit-size and unlabeled variants.
AI.motivation: To understand how robot size and labeling constraints, together with sliding motion restrictions, affect the computational complexity of multi-robot motion planning.
AI.method: Complexity-theoretic analysis: NP-hardness proofs via reductions for the general restricted setting; identification of two natural tractable cases (unit-size squares and unlabeled targets) and development of corresponding algorithms or proofs of polynomial-time solvability.
AI.result: The problem remains NP-hard in most cases; however, two natural restrictions yield tractable instances: unit-size squares and the unlabeled variant.
AI.conclusion: Restricted sliding motion planning retains hardness in general; tractability appears in the unit-size and unlabeled settings, highlighting the sensitive dependence of complexity on robot size and labeling.

title: GAIDE: Graph-based Attention Masking for Spatial- and Embodiment-aware Motion Planning
summary: Sampling-based motion planning algorithms are widely used for motion planning of robotic manipulators, but they often struggle with sample inefficiency in high-dimensional configuration spaces due to their reliance on uniform or hand-crafted informed sampling primitives. Neural informed samplers address this limitation by learning the sampling distribution from prior planning experience to guide the motion planner towards planning goal. However, existing approaches often struggle to encode the spatial structure inherent in motion planning problems. To address this limitation, we introduce Graph-based Attention Masking for Spatial- and Embodiment-aware Motion Planning (GAIDE), a neural informed sampler that leverages both the spatial structure of the planning problem and the robotic manipulator's embodiment to guide the planning algorithm. GAIDE represents these structures as a graph and integrates it into a transformer-based neural sampler through attention masking. We evaluate GAIDE against baseline state-of-the-art sampling-based planners using uniform sampling, hand-crafted informed sampling, and neural informed sampling primitives. Evaluation results demonstrate that GAIDE improves planning efficiency and success rate.

AI.tldr: GAIDE introduces graph-based attention masking to integrate spatial structure and embodiment into a transformer-based neural sampler for sampling-based motion planning, improving efficiency and success rate over baselines.
AI.motivation: Sampling-based planners suffer from sample inefficiency in high-dimensional configuration spaces; existing neural informed samplers often fail to capture spatial structure of planning problems.
AI.method: Model planning problems as graphs that capture spatial relations and manipulator embodiment. Use a transformer-based neural sampler whose attention is masked by the graph structure to embed these relations. Train on prior planning experiences and evaluate against baselines (uniform, handcrafted informed, neural informed).
AI.result: GAIDE improves planning efficiency and success rate compared to baseline planners.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit spatial- and embodiment-aware structure via graph-based attention masking enhances neural informed sampling for robotic manipulation planning, suggesting broader applicability to high-dimensional planning problems.

title: SkillNet: Create, Evaluate, and Connect AI Skills
summary: Current AI agents can flexibly invoke tools and execute complex tasks, yet their long-term advancement is hindered by the lack of systematic accumulation and transfer of skills. Without a unified mechanism for skill consolidation, agents frequently ``reinvent the wheel'', rediscovering solutions in isolated contexts without leveraging prior strategies. To overcome this limitation, we introduce SkillNet, an open infrastructure designed to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale. SkillNet structures skills within a unified ontology that supports creating skills from heterogeneous sources, establishing rich relational connections, and performing multi-dimensional evaluation across Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness. Our infrastructure integrates a repository of over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a versatile Python toolkit. Experimental evaluations on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld demonstrate that SkillNet significantly enhances agent performance, improving average rewards by 40% and reducing execution steps by 30% across multiple backbone models. By formalizing skills as evolving, composable assets, SkillNet provides a robust foundation for agents to move from transient experience to durable mastery.

AI.tldr: SkillNet proposes an open infrastructure and ontology to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale, integrating 200k+ skills with multi-dimensional evaluation, yielding significant agent performance gains (avg rewards +40%, steps -30%) across ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of systematic skill accumulation and transfer in AI agents, which leads to repeated re-invention of solutions and fragmented capabilities. A unified, evolvable skill framework is proposed to enable durable mastery and scalable skill reuse.
AI.method: Develop a unified SkillNet ontology and infrastructure that supports creating skills from heterogeneous sources, rich relational connections, and multi-dimensional evaluation (Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, Cost-awareness). Includes a skills repository (>200k), an interactive platform, and a Python toolkit; validated via experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld across multiple backbone models.
AI.result: Empirical gains include a 40% increase in average rewards and a 30% reduction in execution steps across different backbone models, demonstrating improved agent performance and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes skills as evolving, composable assets within an open infrastructure, providing a robust foundation for agents to move from transient experiences to durable mastery and enabling scalable skill consolidation and transfer.

title: Decorrelating the Future: Joint Frequency Domain Learning for Spatio-temporal Forecasting
summary: Standard direct forecasting models typically rely on point-wise objectives such as Mean Squared Error, which fail to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies inherent in graph-structured signals. While recent frequency-domain approaches such as FreDF mitigate temporal autocorrelation, they often overlook spatial and cross spatio-temporal interactions. To address this limitation, we propose FreST Loss, a frequency-enhanced spatio-temporal training objective that extends supervision to the joint spatio-temporal spectrum. By leveraging the Joint Fourier Transform (JFT), FreST Loss aligns model predictions with ground truth in a unified spectral domain, effectively decorrelating complex dependencies across both space and time. Theoretical analysis shows that this formulation reduces estimation bias associated with time-domain training objectives. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FreST Loss is model-agnostic and consistently improves state-of-the-art baselines by better capturing holistic spatio-temporal dynamics.

AI.tldr: A frequency-enhanced joint spatio-temporal loss (FreST Loss) uses the Joint Fourier Transform to supervise models in the joint spatio-temporal spectrum, addressing bias and limitations of point-wise and purely temporal methods, and showing consistent improvements across six real-world graph-structured forecasting datasets.
AI.motivation: Point-wise losses like MSE fail to capture complex spatio-temporal dependencies on graphs; temporal-frequency methods (e.g., FreDF) target temporal autocorrelation but ignore spatial and cross spatio-temporal interactions. A unified spectral supervision is needed to better model holistic spatio-temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce FreST Loss, a frequency-enhanced training objective that leverages the Joint Fourier Transform to project predictions and ground-truth signals into a joint spatio-temporal spectral domain. The loss aligns spectral coefficients, decorrelates space-time dependencies, and is supported by theoretical analysis showing reduced time-domain estimation bias. The approach is model-agnostic.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on six real-world datasets demonstrates that FreST Loss consistently improves state-of-the-art baselines by more effectively capturing holistic spatio-temporal dynamics, and is applicable across different model architectures.
AI.conclusion: FreST Loss provides a general, spectral-domain supervision framework for graph-structured forecasting, improving modeling of joint spatio-temporal dependencies and reducing estimation bias compared to traditional time-domain losses.

title: Lost in Translation: How Language Re-Aligns Vision for Cross-Species Pathology
comment: 27 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/ekansh-arora0/cross-species-pathology
summary: Foundation models are increasingly applied to computational pathology, yet their behavior under cross-cancer and cross-species transfer remains unspecified. This study investigated how fine-tuning CPath-CLIP affects cancer detection under same-cancer, cross-cancer, and cross-species conditions using whole-slide image patches from canine and human histopathology. Performance was measured using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). Few-shot fine-tuning improved same-cancer (64.9% to 72.6% AUC) and cross-cancer performance (56.84% to 66.31% AUC). Cross-species evaluation revealed that while tissue matching enables meaningful transfer, performance remains below state-of-the-art benchmarks (H-optimus-0: 84.97% AUC), indicating that standard vision-language alignment is suboptimal for cross-species generalization. Embedding space analysis revealed extremely high cosine similarity (greater than 0.99) between tumor and normal prototypes. Grad-CAM shows prototype-based models remain domain-locked, while language-guided models attend to conserved tumor morphology. To address this, we introduce Semantic Anchoring, which uses language to provide a stable coordinate system for visual features. Ablation studies reveal that benefits stem from the text-alignment mechanism itself, regardless of text encoder complexity. Benchmarking against H-optimus-0 shows that CPath-CLIP's failure stems from intrinsic embedding collapse, which text alignment effectively circumvents. Additional gains were observed in same-cancer (8.52%) and cross-cancer classification (5.67%). We identified a previously uncharacterized failure mode: semantic collapse driven by species-dominated alignment rather than missing visual information. These results demonstrate that language acts as a control mechanism, enabling semantic re-interpretation without retraining.

AI.tldr: Language-guided, few-shot fine-tuning of CPath-CLIP improves cancer detection on same-cancer and cross-cancer tasks; cross-species transfer is feasible but below state-of-the-art due to embedding collapse. Semantic Anchoring using language stabilizes visual features and mitigates semantic collapse.
AI.motivation: Assess how foundation models transfer across cancer types and species in computational pathology and whether language-guided alignment can stabilize cross-domain representations.
AI.method: Fine-tune CPath-CLIP on whole-slide image patches from canine and human histopathology under same-cancer, cross-cancer, and cross-species settings. Evaluate with AUC; analyze embedding space via cosine similarity; visualize with Grad-CAM; introduce Semantic Anchoring (language-based coordinate system); perform ablations; benchmark against H-optimus-0.
AI.result: Few-shot fine-tuning raises AUC from 64.9% to 72.6% (same-cancer) and 56.84% to 66.31% (cross-cancer). Cross-species transfer improves with tissue matching but remains below SOTA (H-optimus-0: 84.97% AUC). Embedding space exhibits near-perfect similarity (>0.99) between tumor and normal prototypes. Grad-CAM reveals prototype-based models are domain-locked, while language-guided models attend to conserved tumor morphology. Semantic Anchoring provides a stable coordinate system via text alignment; ablations show benefits arise from text-alignment mechanism itself, independent of text encoder complexity. The main failure mode is semantic collapse driven by species-dominated alignment rather than missing visual data. Cross-species improvement stems from language acting as a control mechanism to re-interpret semantics without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Language acts as a control mechanism enabling semantic reinterpretation without retraining. Semantic Anchoring stabilizes visual features and improves cross-cancer transfer, but cross-species generalization remains imperfect due to intrinsic embedding collapse that pure vision-language alignment can mitigate but not fully fix.

title: Structural Properties of Shortest Flip Sequences Between Plane Spanning Trees
summary:
We study the reconfiguration of plane spanning trees on point sets in the plane in convex position, where a reconfiguration step (flip) replaces one edge with another, yielding again a plane spanning tree. The flip distance between two trees is then the minimum number of flips needed to transform one tree into the other. We study structural properties of shortest flip sequences.
  The folklore happy edge conjecture suggests that any edge shared by both the initial and target tree is never flipped in a shortest flip sequence. The more recent parking edge conjecture, which would have implied the happy edge conjecture, states that there exist shortest flip sequences which use only edges of the start and target tree, and edges in the convex hull of the point set. Finally, another conjecture that is implicit in the literature is the reparking conjecture which states that no edge is flipped more than twice. Essentially all recent flip algorithms respect these three conjectures and the properties they imply. We study cases in which the latter two conjectures hold and disprove them for the general setting.
  (Shortened abstract due to arXiv restrictions.)

AI.tldr: Study of reconfiguring plane spanning trees via edge flips; analyzes shortest flip sequences and tests three conjectures (happy edge, parking edge, reparking); shows parking edge and reparking fail in the general setting.
AI.motivation: Understand the flip-distance structure for plane spanning trees on planar point sets (in convex position) and evaluate conjectures guiding flip-based algorithms.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis with construction of counterexamples; investigation of cases where the parking edge and reparking conjectures hold; study of properties of shortest flip sequences and the edges involved.
AI.result: Both the parking edge conjecture and the reparking conjecture are disproved in the general setting; the conjectures may hold only in restricted cases.
AI.conclusion: Shortest flip sequences can require edges outside the start/target trees or hull in general; these findings call for refined conjectures or new algorithmic approaches for plane spanning-tree reconfiguration.

title: Act-Observe-Rewrite: Multimodal Coding Agents as In-Context Policy Learners for Robot Manipulation
summary: Can a multimodal language model learn to manipulate physical objects by reasoning about its own failures-without gradient updates, demonstrations, or reward engineering? We argue the answer is yes, under conditions we characterise precisely. We present Act-Observe-Rewrite (AOR), a framework in which an LLM agent improves a robot manipulation policy by synthesising entirely new executable Python controller code between trials, guided by visual observations and structured episode outcomes. Unlike prior work that grounds LLMs in pre-defined skill libraries or uses code generation for one-shot plan synthesis, AOR makes the full low-level motor control implementation the unit of LLM reasoning, enabling the agent to change not just what the robot does, but how it does it. The central claim is that interpretable code as the policy representation creates a qualitatively different kind of in-context learning from opaque neural policies: the agent can diagnose systematic failures and rewrite their causes. We validate this across three robosuite manipulation tasks and report promising results, with the agent achieving high success rates without demonstrations, reward engineering, or gradient updates.

AI.tldr: Introduces Act-Observe-Rewrite (AOR), an LLM-driven framework that rewrites executable Python motor controllers between trials to learn manipulation without gradients, demonstrations, or reward shaping.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous robotic manipulation learning by allowing the policy to be revised at the code level; leverage the interpretability of code to diagnose and fix failures.
AI.method: Act-Observe-Rewrite loop in which an LLM generates new Python controller code between trials based on visual observations and structured episode outcomes; the code itself is the policy; no gradient updates or demonstrations required.
AI.result: Validated on three robosuite manipulation tasks; the agent achieves high success rates without demonstrations, reward engineering, or gradient updates.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable code as a policy representation enables qualitatively different in-context learning from opaque neural policies, allowing the agent to diagnose and rewrite failure causes; AOR advances autonomous, self-improving robot manipulation by operating at the level of low-level motor control code.

title: Capability Thresholds and Manufacturing Topology: How Embodied Intelligence Triggers Phase Transitions in Economic Geography
summary: The fundamental topology of manufacturing has not undergone a paradigm-level transformation since Henry Ford's moving assembly line in 1913. Every major innovation of the past century, from the Toyota Production System to Industry 4.0, has optimized within the Fordist paradigm without altering its structural logic: centralized mega-factories, located near labor pools, producing at scale. We argue that embodied intelligence is poised to break this century-long stasis, not by making existing factories more efficient, but by triggering phase transitions in manufacturing economic geography itself. When embodied AI capabilities cross critical thresholds in dexterity, generalization, reliability, and tactile-vision fusion, the consequences extend far beyond cost reduction: they restructure where factories are built, how supply chains are organized, and what constitutes viable production scale. We formalize this by defining a Capability Space C = (d, g, r, t) and showing that the site-selection objective function undergoes topological reorganization when capability vectors cross critical surfaces. Through three pathways, weight inversion, batch collapse, and human-infrastructure decoupling, we show that embodied intelligence enables demand-proximal micro-manufacturing, eliminates "manufacturing deserts," and reverses geographic concentration driven by labor arbitrage. We further introduce Machine Climate Advantage: once human workers are removed, optimal factory locations are determined by machine-optimal conditions (low humidity, high irradiance, thermal stability), factors orthogonal to traditional siting logic, creating a production geography with no historical precedent. This paper establishes Embodied Intelligence Economics, the study of how physical AI capability thresholds reshape the spatial and structural logic of production.

AI.tldr: Embodied Intelligence Economics proposes that AI-enabled capabilities cross critical thresholds to fundamentally redefine factory siting and production geography, via a Capability Space framework and three pathways, enabling demand-proximal micro-manufacturing and machine-centric siting (Machine Climate Advantage).
AI.motivation: The Fordist, centralized, scale-focused manufacturing logic has persisted for a century. Embodied AI capabilities can trigger phase transitions in economic geography, altering where and how production occurs.
AI.method: Formal framework using Capability Space C = (d, g, r, t). analyzes how the site-selection objective function undergoes topological reorganization when capability vectors cross critical surfaces. Demonstrates three pathways—weight inversion, batch collapse, human-infrastructure decoupling—to show how embodied intelligence enables demand-proximal micro-manufacturing, eliminates manufacturing deserts, and reverses labor-arbitrage-driven concentration. Introduces Machine Climate Advantage and articulates Embodied Intelligence Economics.
AI.result: Predicted topological reorganization of production geography as AI capabilities cross thresholds. Pathways yield demand-proximal micro-manufacturing, reduced manufacturing deserts, and reversal of labor-arbitrage concentration. Machine Climate Advantage introduces machine-centric siting factors (low humidity, high irradiance, thermal stability) orthogonal to traditional siting rules.
AI.conclusion: Presents Embodied Intelligence Economics as a theoretical framework for how physical AI capability thresholds reshape the spatial and structural logic of production, with implications for factory design, location strategy, and policy.

title: Machine Learning for Complex Systems Dynamics: Detecting Bifurcations in Dynamical Systems with Deep Neural Networks
summary: Critical transitions are the abrupt shifts between qualitatively different states of a system, and they are crucial to understanding tipping points in complex dynamical systems across ecology, climate science, and biology. Detecting these shifts typically involves extensive forward simulations or bifurcation analyses, which are often computationally intensive and limited by parameter sampling. In this study, we propose a novel machine learning approach based on deep neural networks (DNNs) called equilibrium-informed neural networks (EINNs) to identify critical thresholds associated with catastrophic regime shifts. Rather than fixing parameters and searching for solutions, the EINN method reverses this process by using candidate equilibrium states as inputs and training a DNN to infer the corresponding system parameters that satisfy the equilibrium condition. By analyzing the learned parameter landscape and observing abrupt changes in the feasibility or continuity of equilibrium mappings, critical thresholds can be effectively detected. We demonstrate this capability on nonlinear systems exhibiting saddle-node bifurcations and multi-stability, showing that EINNs can recover the parameter regions associated with impending transitions. This method provides a flexible alternative to traditional techniques, offering new insights into the early detection and structure of critical shifts in high-dimensional and nonlinear systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes equilibrium-informed neural networks (EINNs) to infer system parameters from equilibrium states to detect critical transitions, enabling early detection of regime shifts without exhaustive parameter sweeps; validated on saddle-node bifurcations and multistability.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods (forward simulations and bifurcation analyses) are computationally intensive and limited by parameter sampling. A data-driven, equilibrium-based approach could efficiently identify critical thresholds in high-dimensional nonlinear systems.
AI.method: Train a deep neural network that takes candidate equilibrium states as inputs and outputs the corresponding system parameters that satisfy the equilibrium condition. After training, analyze the learned parameter landscape for abrupt changes in the feasibility or continuity of the equilibrium mapping to identify critical thresholds.
AI.result: EINNs recover parameter regions associated with impending transitions and demonstrate capability on nonlinear systems exhibiting saddle-node bifurcations and multistability, offering a flexible alternative to traditional techniques.
AI.conclusion: EINNs provide a scalable, data-driven framework for early detection and characterization of critical shifts in complex nonlinear systems by analyzing equilibrium mappings and their parameter landscapes.

title: Recognition of Daily Activities through Multi-Modal Deep Learning: A Video, Pose, and Object-Aware Approach for Ambient Assisted Living
summary: Recognition of daily activities is a critical element for effective Ambient Assisted Living (AAL) systems, particularly to monitor the well-being and support the independence of older adults in indoor environments. However, developing robust activity recognition systems faces significant challenges, including intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, environmental variability, camera perspectives, and scene complexity. This paper presents a multi-modal approach for the recognition of activities of daily living tailored for older adults within AAL settings. The proposed system integrates visual information processed by a 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with 3D human pose data analyzed by a Graph Convolutional Network. Contextual information, derived from an object detection module, is fused with the 3D CNN features using a cross-attention mechanism to enhance recognition accuracy. This method is evaluated using the Toyota SmartHome dataset, which consists of real-world indoor activities. The results indicate that the proposed system achieves competitive classification accuracy for a range of daily activities, highlighting its potential as an essential component for advanced AAL monitoring solutions. This advancement supports the broader goal of developing intelligent systems that promote safety and autonomy among older adults.

AI.tldr: Multi-modal ADL recognition for older adults in AAL: integrates 3D CNN visual features with 3D pose data via Graph CNN, fuses object-context with cross-attention; evaluated on Toyota SmartHome; yields competitive accuracy and potential for intelligent AAL monitoring.
AI.motivation: Addresses robust recognition challenges in indoor AAL settings—high intra-class variability, inter-class similarity, environmental changes, camera perspectives, and scene complexity—to improve monitoring of well-being and independence of older adults.
AI.method: Visual stream processed by a 3D CNN; pose stream analyzed by a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN); object-detection-derived contextual features fused with 3D CNN representations via a cross-attention mechanism.
AI.result: Achieves competitive classification accuracy on a real-world indoor dataset (Toyota SmartHome) across a range of daily activities, indicating potential for effective AAL monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a viable multi-modal framework for ADL recognition in AAL that advances robust recognition in indoor elder-care scenarios and supports safety and autonomy of older adults.

title: What induces plane structures in complete graph drawings?
summary: This paper considers the task of connecting points on a piece of paper by drawing a curve between each pair of them. Under mild assumptions, we prove that many pairwise disjoint curves are unavoidable if either of the following rules is obeyed: any two adjacent curves do not cross, or any two non-adjacent curves cross at most once. Here, two curves are called adjacent if they share an endpoint. On the other hand, we demonstrate how to draw all curves such that any two adjacent curves cross exactly once, any two non-adjacent curves cross at least once and at most twice, and thus no two curves are disjoint. Furthermore, we analyze the emergence of disjoint curves without these mild assumptions, and characterize the plane structures in complete graph drawings guaranteed by each of the rules above.

AI.tldr: The abstract studies plane drawings of complete graphs where edges are drawn as curves. It shows that under two mild crossing rules, large sets of pairwise disjoint edges are unavoidable, while a constructive drawing exists where every adjacent pair crosses exactly once and non-adjacent pairs cross 1–2 times, yielding no disjoint edges. It further analyzes how disjoint curves may arise when these assumptions are relaxed and characterizes the plane structures guaranteed by each rule.
AI.motivation: To understand how simple local crossing constraints influence global structure in geometric graph drawings, connecting to extremal and crossing-number questions in K_n drawings.
AI.method: Combinatorial and topological arguments, constructive drawings, case analysis, and structural characterization of plane embeddings under crossing constraints.
AI.result: 1) Under either rule (adjacent edges do not cross, or non-adjacent edges cross at most once), many pairwise disjoint curves are unavoidable. 2) There exists a drawing where every two adjacent curves cross exactly once and every two non-adjacent curves cross at least once and at most twice, so no two curves are disjoint. 3) If the mild assumptions are relaxed, disjoint curves can emerge; 4) The authors characterize the plane structures in complete graph drawings guaranteed by each rule.
AI.conclusion: The work delineates a dichotomy: strict local crossing constraints can force global disjointness among edges, while certain constructions can eliminate disjoint edges; it also provides a structural classification of plane drawings of K_n under these rules.

title: Efficient Autonomous Navigation of a Quadruped Robot in Underground Mines on Edge Hardware
summary: Embodied navigation in underground mines faces significant challenges, including narrow passages, uneven terrain, near-total darkness, GPS-denied conditions, and limited communication infrastructure. While recent learning-based approaches rely on GPU-accelerated inference and extensive training data, we present a fully autonomous navigation stack for a Boston Dynamics Spot quadruped robot that runs entirely on a low-power Intel NUC edge computer with no GPU and no network connectivity requirements. The system integrates LiDAR-inertial odometry, scan-matching localization against a prior map, terrain segmentation, and visibility-graph global planning with a velocity-regulated local path follower, achieving real-time perception-to-action at consistent control rates. After a single mapping pass of the environment, the system handles arbitrary goal locations within the known map without any environment-specific training or learned components. We validate the system through repeated field trials using four target locations of varying traversal difficulty in an experimental underground mine, accumulating over 700 m of fully autonomous traverse with a 100% success rate across all 20 trials (5 repetitions x 4 targets) and an overall Success weighted by Path Length (SPL) of 0.73 \pm 0.09.

AI.tldr: A GPU-free, edge-only autonomous navigation stack for a Spot robot in GPS-denied underground mines, using LiDAR-IMU odometry, map-based scan matching, terrain segmentation, and visibility-graph planning with a velocity-regulated local follower; achieved fully autonomous navigation after a single map pass with 700 m traversed and 100% success over 20 trials.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, low-power, network-independent autonomy in GPS-denied, dark, narrow mine environments where GPU-heavy or cloud-based methods are impractical.
AI.method: Integrates LiDAR-inertial odometry, scan-matching localization against a prior map, terrain segmentation, and visibility-graph global planning with a velocity-regulated local path follower; runs entirely on an Intel NUC edge computer with no GPU and no network connectivity; requires only a single mapping pass to enable arbitrary goal locations within the known map; no environment-specific training or learned components.
AI.result: Field validation over 20 trials (5 repeats x 4 targets) across four locations, totaling >700 m of fully autonomous traversal with a 100% success rate and an overall SPL of 0.73 ± 0.09.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of robust, on-board autonomous navigation for underground mining using a lightweight, non-GPU stack. Highlights potential for deployment in GPS-denied, communication-limited environments without reliance on training or external infrastructure; future work could address dynamic environments, map updates, and scalability to more complex mine layouts.

title: Progressive Refinement Regulation for Accelerating Diffusion Language Model Decoding
comment: 19 pages, 10 figures, Code available upon publication
summary: Diffusion language models generate text through iterative denoising under a uniform refinement rule applied to all tokens. However, tokens stabilize at different rates in practice, leading to substantial redundant refinement and motivating refinement control over the denoising process. Existing approaches typically assess refinement necessity from instantaneous, step-level signals under a fixed decoding process. In contrast, whether a token has converged is defined by how its prediction changes along its future refinement trajectory. Moreover, changing the refinement rule reshapes future refinement trajectories, which in turn determine how refinement rules should be formulated, making refinement control inherently dynamic. We propose \emph{Progressive Refinement Regulation} (PRR), a progressive, trajectory-grounded refinement control framework that derives a token-level notion of empirical convergence progress from full decoding rollouts. Based on this signal, PRR learns a lightweight token-wise controller to regulate refinement via temperature-based distribution shaping under a progressive self-evolving training scheme. Experiments show that PRR substantially accelerates diffusion language model decoding while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: PRR introduces a token-level, trajectory-grounded refinement controller for diffusion language models, speeding up decoding by shaping the sampling temperature with a learned policy trained progressively from full decoding rollouts.
AI.motivation: In diffusion LMs, tokens converge at different rates and a uniform refinement rule causes redundant computation. A trajectory-based notion of convergence and dynamic refinement control can reduce wasted effort while preserving quality.
AI.method: Derive empirical convergence progress from full decoding rollouts for each token; train a lightweight token-wise controller to regulate refinement via temperature-based distribution shaping; employ progressive self-evolving training to adapt the controller over time.
AI.result: PRR substantially accelerates diffusion language model decoding while maintaining generation quality.
AI.conclusion: A dynamic, trajectory-grounded refinement control framework improves the efficiency of diffusion language models and can adapt to changing refinement rules.

title: FedEMA-Distill: Exponential Moving Average Guided Knowledge Distillation for Robust Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) often degrades when clients hold heterogeneous non-Independent and Identically Distributed (non-IID) data and when some clients behave adversarially, leading to client drift, slow convergence, and high communication overhead. This paper proposes FedEMA-Distill, a server-side procedure that combines an exponential moving average (EMA) of the global model with ensemble knowledge distillation from client-uploaded prediction logits evaluated on a small public proxy dataset. Clients run standard local training, upload only compressed logits, and may use different model architectures, so no changes are required to client-side software while still supporting model heterogeneity across devices. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, FEMNIST, and AG News under Dirichlet-0.1 label skew show that FedEMA-Distill improves top-1 accuracy by several percentage points (up to +5% on CIFAR-10 and +6% on CIFAR-100) over representative baselines, reaches a given target accuracy in 30-35% fewer communication rounds, and reduces per-round client uplink payloads to 0.09-0.46 MB, i.e., roughly an order of magnitude less than transmitting full model weights. Using coordinate-wise median or trimmed-mean aggregation of logits at the server further stabilizes training in the presence of up to 10-20% Byzantine clients and yields well-calibrated predictions under attack. These results indicate that coupling temporal smoothing with logits-only aggregation provides a communication-efficient and attack-resilient FL pipeline that is deployment-friendly and compatible with secure aggregation and differential privacy, since only aggregated or obfuscated model outputs are exchanged.

AI.tldr: FedEMA-Distill couples a server-side EMA of the global model with ensemble knowledge distillation from client-uploaded logits on a public proxy dataset. It enables model heterogeneity across clients, reduces communication by sending compressed logits, and improves robustness to Byzantine clients, achieving faster convergence and higher accuracy with lightweight communication.
AI.motivation: Address non-IID data-induced client drift and adversarial behavior in federated learning, while reducing communication overhead and enabling heterogeneous client models. Leverage public data for distilled knowledge transfer and temporal smoothing for stability.
AI.method: Server-side approach: maintain an exponential moving average of the global model; perform ensemble knowledge distillation using logits uploaded by clients evaluated on a small public proxy dataset; clients perform standard local training and only upload compressed logits; supports heterogeneous client models; optional logit aggregation with coordinate-wise median or trimmed-mean to tolerate Byzantine clients.
AI.result: Accuracy gains of up to ~5% (CIFAR-10) and ~6% (CIFAR-100); reach target accuracy in 30-35% fewer communication rounds; uplink payload per client reduced to 0.09-0.46 MB (roughly an order of magnitude less than full model transmission); improved stability under 10-20% Byzantine clients with median/trimmed-mean; well-calibrated predictions and compatibility with secure aggregation and differential privacy.
AI.conclusion: Combining temporal smoothing (EMA) with logits-only aggregation provides a communication-efficient and attack-resilient FL pipeline that tolerates model heterogeneity, reduces communication cost, and remains deployment-friendly with DP and secure aggregation.

title: InverseNet: Benchmarking Operator Mismatch and Calibration Across Compressive Imaging Modalities
comment: Benchmarking Operator Mismatch and Calibration Across Compressive Imaging Modalities
summary: State-of-the-art EfficientSCI loses 20.58 dB when its assumed forward operator deviates from physical reality in just eight parameters, yet no existing benchmark quantifies operator mismatch, the default condition in deployed compressive imaging systems. We introduce InverseNet, the first cross-modality benchmark for operator mismatch, spanning CASSI, CACTI, and single-pixel cameras. Evaluating 12 methods under a four-scenario protocol (ideal, mismatched, oracle-corrected, blind calibration) across 27 simulated scenes and 9 real hardware captures, we find: (1) deep learning methods lose 10-21 dB under mismatch, eliminating their advantage over classical baselines; (2) performance and robustness are inversely correlated across modalities (Spearman r_s = -0.71, p < 0.01); (3) mask-oblivious architectures recover 0% of mismatch losses regardless of calibration quality, while operator-conditioned methods recover 41-90%; (4) blind grid-search calibration recovers 85-100% of the oracle bound without ground truth. Real hardware experiments confirm that simulation trends transfer to physical data. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces InverseNet, a cross-modality benchmark for operator mismatch in compressive imaging, showing deep-learning methods degrade under mismatch but calibration strategies recover much of the loss; hardware trends align with simulations.
AI.motivation: There is no standard benchmark for operator mismatch in compressive-imaging systems, yet small forward-model deviations can drastically affect performance. A cross-modality benchmark is needed to quantify robustness across CASSI, CACTI, and single-pixel cameras.
AI.method: Propose InverseNet with a four-scenario protocol (ideal, mismatched, oracle-corrected, blind calibration), spanning 27 simulated scenes and 9 real hardware captures, evaluating 12 methods across three modalities (CASSI, CACTI, single-pixel). Analyze performance under mismatch, cross-modality correlations, and calibration strategies (mask-oblivious vs operator-conditioned; blind grid-search calibration).
AI.result: Deep learning methods degrade by 10–21 dB under mismatch, erasing their advantage over classical baselines; robustness across modalities is inversely correlated (Spearman r_s = -0.71, p < 0.01); mask-oblivious architectures recover 0% of the mismatch losses, while operator-conditioned methods recover 41–90%; blind grid-search calibration recovers 85–100% of the oracle bound without ground-truth; real hardware experiments corroborate simulation trends.
AI.conclusion: Operator-mismatch significantly undermines DL-based reconstruction; calibrated, operator-aware approaches can recover most of the lost performance. InverseNet provides a standardized benchmark to evaluate and compare robustness to forward-model deviations, with code to be released.

title: Drone Air Traffic Control: Tracking a Set of Moving Objects with Minimal Power
summary:
A common sensing problem is to use a set of stationary tracking locations to monitor a collection of moving devices: Given $n$ objects that need to be tracked, each following its own trajectory, and $m$ stationary traffic control stations, each with a sensing region of adjustable range; how should we adjust the individual sensor ranges in order to optimize energy consumption? We provide both negative theoretical and positive practical results for this important and natural challenge.
  On the theoretical side, we show that even if all objects move at constant speed along straight lines, no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee optimal coverage for a given starting solution. On the practical side, we present an algorithm based on geometric insights that is able to find optimal solutions for the $\min \max$ variant of the problem, which aims at minimizing peak power consumption. Runtimes for instances with 500 moving objects and 25 stations are in the order of seconds for scenarios that take minutes to play out in the real world, demonstrating real-time capability of our methods.

AI.tldr: The paper studies adjusting sensor ranges of stationary stations to monitor moving devices, showing hardness and offering a practical min-max optimization algorithm achieving near real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Energy-efficient monitoring in sensor networks with moving targets; need to adjust sensing ranges to balance coverage and power usage.
AI.method: Theoretically proves no polynomial-time algorithm can guarantee optimal coverage from a fixed starting solution even with linear motion (hardness). Then develops a geometry-based algorithm that solves the min-max (peak power) variant optimally; shows runtime in seconds for moderately large instances, enabling real-time use.
AI.result: Hardness result: no polynomial-time guarantee for optimal coverage. Practical algorithm finds optimal solutions for min-max variant; tested on 500 moving objects and 25 stations; runtimes in seconds, suitable for real-time scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a hardness-principled view: while optimal coverage is intractable in general, a geometry-informed method can optimally optimize peak energy consumption in realistic settings, enabling real-time operation.

title: PTLD: Sim-to-real Privileged Tactile Latent Distillation for Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Tactile dexterous manipulation is essential to automating complex household tasks, yet learning effective control policies remains a challenge. While recent work has relied on imitation learning, obtaining high quality demonstrations for multi-fingered hands via robot teleoperation or kinesthetic teaching is prohibitive. Alternatively, with reinforcement we can learn skills in simulation, but fast and realistic simulation of tactile observations is challenging. To bridge this gap, we introduce PTLD: sim-to-real Privileged Tactile Latent Distillation, a novel approach to learning tactile manipulation skills without requiring tactile simulation. Instead of simulating tactile sensors or relying purely on proprioceptive policies to transfer zero-shot sim-to-real, our key idea is to leverage privileged sensors in the real world to collect real-world tactile policy data. This data is then used to distill a robust state estimator that operates on tactile input. We demonstrate from our experiments that PTLD can be used to improve proprioceptive manipulation policies trained in simulation significantly by incorporating tactile sensing. On the benchmark in-hand rotation task, PTLD achieves a 182% improvement over a proprioception only policy. We also show that PTLD enables learning the challenging task of tactile in-hand reorientation where we see a 57% improvement in the number of goals reached over using proprioception alone. Website: https://akashsharma02.github.io/ptld-website/.

AI.tldr: PTLD is a sim-to-real approach that uses privileged real-tactile data to distill a tactile latent state estimator, improving proprioceptive policies trained in simulation without simulating tactile sensors; achieves substantial gains on in-hand rotation and tactile in-hand reorientation.
AI.motivation: Bridging the sim-to-real gap in tactile manipulation for multi-fingered hands, where collecting high-quality demonstrations is hard and tactile sensor simulation is challenging; leverages real-world privileged sensors to learn robust tactile representations.
AI.method: Collect real-world tactile policy data using privileged sensors; distill a robust state estimator that operates on tactile input; integrate with proprioceptive policies trained in simulation; avoid tactile sensor simulation; apply to tasks such as in-hand rotation and tactile in-hand reorientation.
AI.result: On in-hand rotation benchmark, 182% improvement over proprioception-only policy; tactile in-hand reorientation achieves 57% more goals reached; overall demonstrates improvement when incorporating tactile sensing into simulated policies.
AI.conclusion: PTLD demonstrates that leveraging privileged tactile data for distillation can significantly enhance tactile manipulation without tactile sensor simulation, enabling more capable dexterous control; suggests potential extensions to additional tasks and sensor modalities.

title: Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system
summary: Mathematical concepts emerge through an interplay of processes, including experimentation, efforts at proof, and counterexamples. In this paper, we present a new multi-agent model for computational mathematical discovery based on this observation. Our system, conceived with research in mind, poses its own conjectures and then attempts to prove them, making decisions informed by this feedback and an evolving data distribution. Inspired by the history of Euler's conjecture for polyhedra and an open challenge in the literature, we benchmark with the task of autonomously recovering the concept of homology from polyhedral data and knowledge of linear algebra. Our system completes this learning problem. Most importantly, the experiments are ablations, statistically testing the value of the complete dynamic and controlling for experimental setup. They support our main claim: that the optimisation of the right combination of local processes can lead to surprisingly well-aligned notions of mathematical interestingness.

AI.tldr: Autonomous multi-agent system discovers and proves mathematical concepts; demonstrates recovery of homology from polyhedral data using linear algebra, with ablation studies validating the dynamic's components.
AI.motivation: To model mathematical discovery as an interplay of conjecture generation, proof attempts, and counterexamples, and to study whether the right mix of local processes yields meaningful mathematical notions.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework that generates conjectures, attempts proofs, and updates its data distribution; benchmarked on recovering homology from polyhedral data with linear algebra knowledge; includes ablation experiments to isolate the impact of dynamic components.
AI.result: System successfully recovers the concept of homology; ablations show the complete dynamic and its components contribute to performance; results support that appropriate combination of local processes aligns with mathematical interestingness.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic optimization of local mathematical processes can yield robust, interesting mathematical concepts, suggesting a computational pathway to mathematical discovery.

title: Delta-Crosscoder: Robust Crosscoder Model Diffing in Narrow Fine-Tuning Regimes
summary: Model diffing methods aim to identify how fine-tuning changes a model's internal representations. Crosscoders approach this by learning shared dictionaries of interpretable latent directions between base and fine-tuned models. However, existing formulations struggle with narrow fine-tuning, where behavioral changes are localized and asymmetric. We introduce Delta-Crosscoder, which combines BatchTopK sparsity with a delta-based loss prioritizing directions that change between models, plus an implicit contrastive signal from paired activations on matched inputs. Evaluated across 10 model organisms, including synthetic false facts, emergent misalignment, subliminal learning, and taboo word guessing (Gemma, LLaMA, Qwen; 1B-9B parameters), Delta-Crosscoder reliably isolates latent directions causally responsible for fine-tuned behaviors and enables effective mitigation, outperforming SAE-based baselines, while matching the Non-SAE-based. Our results demonstrate that crosscoders remain a powerful tool for model diffing.

AI.tldr: Delta-Crosscoder improves model diffing by focusing on latent directions that change during fine-tuning using BatchTopK sparsity and a delta-based loss, with an implicit contrastive signal from matched activations. It outperforms SAE baselines and remains competitive with non-SAE methods across varied tasks.
AI.motivation: Narrow fine-tuning yields localized, asymmetric behavioral changes that are hard to pin down with existing crosscoder methods; robust, causal identification of the affected latent directions is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Delta-Crosscoder: combines BatchTopK sparsity with a delta-based loss that prioritizes directions that differ between base and fine-tuned models, plus an implicit contrastive signal from paired activations on matched inputs. Evaluated across 10 model organisms and tasks (synthetic false facts, emergent misalignment, subliminal learning, taboo word guessing) using 1B–9B parameter models (Gemma, LLaMA, Qwen).
AI.result: Delta-Crosscoder reliably isolates latent directions causally responsible for fine-tuned behaviors, enabling effective mitigation and outperforming SAE-based baselines while matching non-SAE baselines.
AI.conclusion: Crosscoders remain a powerful tool for model diffing; Delta-Crosscoder strengthens their ability to identify causal latent directions in narrow finetuning and supports robust mitigation strategies.

title: Fusion and Grouping Strategies in Deep Learning for Local Climate Zone Classification of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data
summary: Local Climate Zones (LCZs) give a zoning map to study urban structures and land use and analyze the impact of urbanization on local climate. Multimodal remote sensing enables LCZ classification, for which data fusion is significant for improving accuracy owing to the data complexity. However, there is a gap in a comprehensive analysis of the fusion mechanisms used in their deep learning (DL) classifier architectures. This study analyzes different fusion strategies in the multi-class LCZ classification models for multimodal data and grouping strategies based on inherent data characteristics. The different models involving Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) include: (i) baseline hybrid fusion (FM1), (ii) with self- and cross-attention mechanisms (FM2), (iii) with the multi-scale Gaussian filtered images (FM3), and (iv) weighted decision-level fusion (FM4). Ablation experiments are conducted to study the pixel-, feature-, and decision-level fusion effects in the model performance. Grouping strategies include band grouping (BG) within the data modalities and label merging (LM) in the ground truth. Our analysis is exclusively done on the So2Sat LCZ42 dataset, which consists of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and Multispectral Imaging (MSI) image pairs. Our results show that FM1 consistently outperforms simple fusion methods. FM1 with BG and LM is found to be the most effective approach among all fusion strategies, giving an overall accuracy of 76.6\%. Importantly, our study highlights the effect of these strategies in improving prediction accuracy for the underrepresented classes. Our code and processed datasets are available at https://github.com/GVCL/LCZC-MultiModalHybridFusion

AI.tldr: Baseline hybrid fusion (FM1) with band grouping (BG) and label merging (LM) yields the strongest performance for multi-class LCZ classification on So2Sat LCZ42, outperforming other fusion strategies; ablation reveals the impact of fusion level choices; reported 76.6% overall accuracy for FM1 with BG+LM; code is publicly available.
AI.motivation: There is limited comprehensive analysis of fusion mechanisms in multimodal DL architectures for LCZ classification. Understanding pixel-, feature-, and decision-level fusion and grouping strategies is needed to improve accuracy and address underrepresented classes in multimodal SAR–MSI LCZ classification.
AI.method: Compare four fusion models (FM1 baseline hybrid fusion; FM2 with self- and cross-attention; FM3 using multi-scale Gaussian filtered images; FM4 weighted decision-level fusion) on SAR+MSI data from the So2Sat LCZ42 dataset. Conduct ablation experiments to study pixel-, feature-, and decision-level fusion effects and grouping strategies (band grouping within modalities; label merging in ground truth). Assess overall accuracy and minority-class performance; provide code/datasets at the reported repository.
AI.result: FM1 consistently outperforms simple fusion baselines. FM1 with BG and LM is the most effective fusion configuration among those studied, achieving 76.6% overall accuracy. Ablation analyses show that fusion level and grouping strategies significantly influence performance, with notable gains for underrepresented LCZ classes.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid fusion (FM1) is a robust baseline for multimodal LCZ classification; incorporating band grouping (BG) and label merging (LM) substantially enhances performance, especially for underrepresented classes. The study underscores the importance of fusion-level and grouping strategy choices in DL-based LCZ classification; code and data are publicly available at the provided repository.

title: Garment numbers of bi-colored point sets in the plane
summary: We consider colored variants of a class of geometric-combinatorial questions on $k$-gons and empty $k$-gons that have been started around 1935 by Erdős and Szekeres. In our setting we have $n$ points in general position in the plane, each one colored either red or blue. A structure on $k$ points is a geometric graph where the edges are spanned by (some of) these points and is called monochromatic if all $k$ points have the same color. Already for $k=4$ there exist interesting open problems. Most prominently, it is still open whether for any sufficiently large bichromatic set there always exists a convex empty, monochromatic quadrilateral. In order to shed more light on the underlying geometry we study the existence of five different monochromatic structures that all use exactly 4 points of a bichromatic point set. We provide several improved lower and upper bounds on the smallest $n$ such that every bichromatic set of at least $n$ points contains (some of) those monochromatic structures.

AI.tldr: Studying colored (bichromatic) variants of Erdős–Szekeres type questions for k-gons/empty k-gons; analyzes five monochromatic 4-point structures and provides improved lower and upper bounds on the threshold n ensuring existence in any bichromatic point set.
AI.motivation: Extend classical Erdős–Szekeres problems to bichromatic settings and probing the existence of monochromatic 4-point structures, with emphasis on the open question about convex empty monochromatic quadrilaterals for large bichromatic sets.
AI.method: Geometric-combinatorial analysis of five distinct monochromatic 4-point structures; derives improved lower and upper bounds on the minimum n so that any bichromatic set of size n contains at least one of these structures; uses arguments typical in geometric Ramsey-type problems.
AI.result: Identification and study of five monochromatic 4-point structures; establishment of improved lower and upper bounds on the threshold n for guaranteeing the existence of at least one such structure in any bichromatic point set of size n.
AI.conclusion: Advances the colored version of Erdős–Szekeres questions, providing progress on k=4 and highlighting the ongoing open problem of convex empty monochromatic quadrilaterals in large bichromatic point sets.

title: Many-RRT*: Robust Joint-Space Trajectory Planning for Serial Manipulators
summary: The rapid advancement of high degree-of-freedom (DoF) serial manipulators necessitates the use of swift, sampling-based motion planners for high-dimensional spaces. While sampling-based planners like the Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree (RRT) are widely used, planning in the manipulator's joint space presents significant challenges due to non-invertible forward kinematics. A single task-space end-effector pose can correspond to multiple configuration-space states, creating a multi-arm bandit problem for the planner. In complex environments, simply choosing the wrong joint space goal can result in suboptimal trajectories or even failure to find a viable plan. To address this planning problem, we propose Many-RRT*: an extension of RRT*-Connect that plans to multiple goals in parallel. By generating multiple IK solutions and growing independent trees from these goal configurations simultaneously alongside a single start tree, Many-RRT* ensures that computational effort is not wasted on suboptimal IK solutions. This approach maintains robust convergence and asymptotic optimality. Experimental evaluations across robot morphologies and diverse obstacle environments demonstrate that Many-RRT* provides higher quality trajectories (44.5% lower cost in the same runtime) with a significantly higher success rate (100% vs. the next best of 1.6%) than previous RRT iterations without compromising on runtime performance.

AI.tldr: Many-RRT* extends RRT*-Connect to plan to multiple IK-based goal configurations in parallel, improving trajectory quality and success rate for high-DoF manipulators while preserving convergence properties.
AI.motivation: In high-DoF serial manipulators, non-invertible and ambiguous forward kinematics yield multiple joint-space configurations for a single task-space pose. Planning to a single goal can waste IK solutions and risk poor plans or failure; a robust, scalable planner is needed.
AI.method: Generate multiple IK solutions for several potential end-effector goals and grow independent RRT*-Connect trees from these goals in parallel alongside a single start tree, ensuring asymptotic optimality and robust convergence.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations across robot morphologies and cluttered environments show Many-RRT* achieves 44.5% lower trajectory cost at the same runtime and a 100% success rate, compared to the next best ~1.6% for previous RRT variants.
AI.conclusion: Many-RRT* effectively mitigates IK-induced ambiguity in high-dimensional planning, delivering higher-quality, more reliable plans without sacrificing runtime performance; the approach generalizes across morphologies and environments.

title: Adaptive Memory Admission Control for LLM Agents
summary: LLM-based agents increasingly rely on long-term memory to support multi-session reasoning and interaction, yet current systems provide little control over what information is retained. In practice, agents either accumulate large volumes of conversational content, including hallucinated or obsolete facts, or depend on opaque, fully LLM-driven memory policies that are costly and difficult to audit. As a result, memory admission remains a poorly specified and weakly controlled component in agent architectures. To address this gap, we propose Adaptive Memory Admission Control (A-MAC), a framework that treats memory admission as a structured decision problem. A-MAC decomposes memory value into five complementary and interpretable factors: future utility, factual confidence, semantic novelty, temporal recency, and content type prior. The framework combines lightweight rule-based feature extraction with a single LLM-assisted utility assessment, and learns domain-adaptive admission policies through cross-validated optimization. This design enables transparent and efficient control over long-term memory. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark show that A-MAC achieves a superior precision-recall tradeoff, improving F1 to 0.583 while reducing latency by 31% compared to state-of-the-art LLM-native memory systems. Ablation results identify content type prior as the most influential factor for reliable memory admission. These findings demonstrate that explicit and interpretable admission control is a critical design principle for scalable and reliable memory in LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Memory Admission Control (A-MAC) treats memory admission as a structured decision, decomposing memory value into five factors to enable interpretable, domain-adaptive policies; shows improved F1 (0.583) and 31% latency reduction on LoCoMo, with content type prior being most influential.
AI.motivation: Memory management in LLM agents is poorly specified and hard to audit, leading to accumulation of stale or hallucinated content or opaque, costly policies; a transparent admission control mechanism is needed for scalable, reliable long-term memory.
AI.method: Decompose memory value into five factors (future utility, factual confidence, semantic novelty, temporal recency, content type prior); use lightweight rule-based feature extraction plus a single LLM-assisted utility assessment; learn domain-adaptive admission policies via cross-validated optimization.
AI.result: A-MAC achieves a superior precision-recall tradeoff with F1 = 0.583 and 31% latency reduction compared to state-of-the-art LLM-native memory systems; ablation shows content type prior as the most influential factor.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, interpretable memory admission control is a critical design principle for scalable and reliable memory in LLM-based agents.

title: Thin Keys, Full Values: Reducing KV Cache via Low-Dimensional Attention Selection
summary: Standard transformer attention uses identical dimensionality for queries, keys, and values ($d_q = d_k = d_v = \dmodel$). Our insight is that these components serve fundamentally different roles, and this symmetry is unnecessary. Queries and keys produce scalar attention weights (\emph{selection}), while values carry rich semantic representations (\emph{value transfer}). We argue that selection is an inherently lower-dimensional operation than value transfer, requiring only $\BigO(\log N)$ dimensions to distinguish among $N$ relevant patterns. We validate this hypothesis across seven experiments: (1)~positional selection tasks requiring just 1~dimension per head, (2)~content-based retrieval requiring $\sim\!\log_2 N$ dimensions, (3--4)~WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 language modeling where $\dselect = \dmodel/4$ incurs only 4.3\% perplexity increase while reducing QK parameters by 75\%, (5)~post-training SVD compression of GPT-2, revealing keys to be far more compressible than queries, with lightweight QK fine-tuning recovering nearly all quality loss, (6)~a 125M-parameter LLaMA model confirming identical degradation ratios across architectures, and (7)~Mistral-7B (7.2B parameters), where SVD compression followed by QK fine-tuning achieves 75\% key cache savings at just 2.0\% residual quality cost. For existing models, SVD compression followed by QK fine-tuning (3 epochs on a small fraction of pretraining data) achieves 75\% key cache savings at $<$2\% residual quality cost. For a 7B-parameter model serving 128K context, asymmetric attention saves 25\,GB of KV cache per user, enabling approximately 60\% more concurrent users on the same GPU.

AI.tldr: Asymmetric transformer attention: queries/keys can be lower-dimensional than values; selection weights require only log-scale dimensions, enabling substantial Q/K compression with minimal impact on performance across tasks and models; yields major KV cache savings and improved concurrency.
AI.motivation: Standard self-attention uses equal dimensions for Q, K, V, but Q/K serve selection while V carries semantic content. The hypothesis is that selection is inherently lower dimensional (O(log N)) than value transfer, enabling efficient compression without harming downstream representations.
AI.method: Differentiate roles of Q/K vs V; explore lower dimension for Q/K (d_select ~ log N). Validate via seven experiments: (1) positional selection tasks (1D per head), (2) content-based retrieval (log2 N dims), (3–4) WikiText-2/103 with d_select = d_model/4; (5) post-training SVD on GPT-2 showing keys more compressible; (6) 125M LLaMA confirming consistent degradation; (7) Mistral-7B (7.2B) with SVD + QK fine-tuning; tail results include 75% key cache savings at small residual cost; cross-model generality with 3-epoch fine-tuning on limited data.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) 1D per head suffices for positional selection; (2) content retrieval needs ~log2 N dims; (3–4) WikiText tasks show only 4.3% perplexity increase with 75% QK parameter reduction; (5) Q/K keys are more compressible; QK fine-tuning recovers near full quality; (6) 125M LLaMA shows identical degradation across architectures; (7) Mistral-7B achieves 75% key cache savings with ~2% residual quality cost. Across existing models, SVD + QK fine-tuning achieves 75% KV savings at <2% quality loss using small data; 7B model with 128K context saves 25 GB KV per user, enabling ~60% more concurrency.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric attention with reduced Q/K dimensions is effective and generalizable, enabling substantial KV cache reductions and minimal accuracy loss, applicable across model sizes and tasks.

title: Structure-Guided Histopathology Synthesis via Dual-LoRA Diffusion
summary:
Histopathology image synthesis plays an important role in tissue restoration, data augmentation, and modeling of tumor microenvironments. However, existing generative methods typically address restoration and generation as separate tasks, although both share the same objective of structure-consistent tissue synthesis under varying degrees of missingness, and often rely on weak or inconsistent structural priors that limit realistic cellular organization.
  We propose Dual-LoRA Controllable Diffusion, a unified centroid-guided diffusion framework that jointly supports Local Structure Completion and Global Structure Synthesis within a single model. Multi-class nuclei centroids serve as lightweight and annotation-efficient spatial priors, providing biologically meaningful guidance under both partial and complete image absence. Two task-specific LoRA adapters specialize the shared backbone for local and global objectives without retraining separate diffusion models. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art GAN and diffusion baselines across restoration and synthesis tasks.
  For local completion, LPIPS computed within the masked region improves from 0.1797 (HARP) to 0.1524, and for global synthesis, FID improves from 225.15 (CoSys) to 76.04, indicating improved structural fidelity and realism. Our approach achieves more faithful structural recovery in masked regions and substantially improved realism and morphology consistency in full synthesis, supporting scalable pan-cancer histopathology modeling.

AI.tldr: A unified diffusion model with centroid-guided dual LoRA adapters enables local structure completion and global structure synthesis in histopathology, achieving strong quantitative gains over baselines in both restoration and synthesis tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the separation between restoration and generation in histopathology synthesis and restoration, leveraging structure-consistent tissue synthesis under varying missingness and annotation-efficient priors (nuclei centroids) to guide both local and global tasks.
AI.method: Dual-LoRA Controllable Diffusion uses a shared diffusion backbone with two task-specific LoRA adapters. Multi-class nuclei centroids serve as lightweight spatial priors guiding local completion and global synthesis. The model jointly handles Local Structure Completion and Global Structure Synthesis within a single framework without retraining separate diffusion models.
AI.result: Quantitative gains over baselines: LPIPS within the masked region improved from 0.1797 (HARP) to 0.1524; FID for global synthesis improved from 225.15 (CoSys) to 76.04. Demonstrated consistent improvements across restoration and synthesis tasks over state-of-the-art GAN and diffusion methods; supports scalable pan-cancer histopathology modeling.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields more faithful structural recovery in masked regions and substantially improved realism and morphology consistency in full synthesis, enhancing tissue restoration and synthesis capabilities across cancer types.

title: Revisiting Graph Modification via Disk Scaling: From One Radius to Interval-Based Radii
comment: Extended abstract will be presented at EuroCG'26; 46 pages, 11 figures
summary: For a fixed graph class $Π$, the goal of $Π$-Modification is to transform an input graph $G$ into a graph $H\inΠ$ using at most $k$ modifications. Vertex and edge deletions are common operations, and their (parameterized) complexity for various $Π$ is well-studied. Classic graph modification operations such as edge deletion do not consider the geometric nature of geometric graphs such as (unit) disk graphs. This led Fomin et al. [ITCS' 25] to initiate the study of disk scaling as a geometric graph modification operation for unit disk graphs: For a given radius $r$, each modified disk will be rescaled to radius $r$.
