In this paper, we generalize their model by allowing rescaled disks to choose a radius within a given interval $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$ and study the (parameterized) complexity (with respect to $k$) of the corresponding problem $Π$-Scaling. We show that $Π$-Scaling is in XP for every graph class $Π$ that can be recognized in polynomial time. Furthermore, we show that $Π$-Scaling: (1) is NP-hard and FPT for cluster graphs, (2) can be solved in polynomial time for complete graphs, and (3) is W[1]-hard for connected graphs. In particular, (1) and (2) answer open questions of Fomin et al. and (3) generalizes the hardness result for their variant where the set of scalable disks is restricted.

AI.tldr: Generalizes disk scaling for unit disk graphs by allowing scalable disks to pick radii in an interval, and studies the parameterized complexity wrt k across Π-Scaling. It yields XP membership for poly-time recognizable Π, NP-hard and FPT on cluster graphs, polynomial-time solvability on complete graphs, and W[1]-hardness on connected graphs, thus answering open questions and extending prior restricted-scaling results.
AI.motivation: To understand how geometric modifications via rescaling disks within a radius interval affect the complexity of transforming unit disk graphs into a target graph class Π, building on Fomin et al.'s disk scaling framework.
AI.method: Define the Π-Scaling problem with radius-interval-rescaled disks, analyze parameterized complexity w.r.t. k for arbitrary Π recognizable in poly time; derive complexity for special Π (cluster, complete, connected) and relate to existing open questions.
AI.result: Π-Scaling is in XP for any Π recognizable in polynomial time. It is NP-hard and FPT for cluster graphs, solvable in polynomial time for complete graphs, and W[1]-hard for connected graphs.
AI.conclusion: The work clarifies the complexity landscape of geometric graph scaling, confirming open questions for cluster graphs and complete graphs while showing hardness on connected graphs; it broadens the scope beyond prior restrictions on scalable disks.

title: From Local Corrections to Generalized Skills: Improving Neuro-Symbolic Policies with MEMO
summary: Recent works use a neuro-symbolic framework for general manipulation policies. The advantage of this framework is that -- by applying off-the-shelf vision and language models -- the robot can break complex tasks down into semantic subtasks. However, the fundamental bottleneck is that the robot needs skills to ground these subtasks into embodied motions. Skills can take many forms (e.g., trajectory snippets, motion primitives, coded functions), but regardless of their form skills act as a constraint. The high-level policy can only ground its language reasoning through the available skills; if the robot cannot generate the right skill for the current task, its policy will fail. We propose to address this limitation -- and dynamically expand the robot's skills -- by leveraging user feedback. When a robot fails, humans can intuitively explain what went wrong (e.g., ``no, go higher''). While a simple approach is to recall this exact text the next time the robot faces a similar situation, we hypothesize that by collecting, clustering, and re-phrasing natural language corrections across multiple users and tasks, we can synthesize more general text guidance and coded skill templates. Applying this hypothesis we develop Memory Enhanced Manipulation (MEMO). MEMO builds and maintains a retrieval-augmented skillbook gathered from human feedback and task successes. At run time, MEMO retrieves relevant text and code from this skillbook, enabling the robot's policy to generate new skills while reasoning over multi-task human feedback. Our experiments demonstrate that using MEMO to aggregate local feedback into general skill templates enables generalization to novel tasks where existing baselines fall short. See supplemental material here: https://collab.me.vt.edu/memo

AI.tldr: MEMO uses a retrieval-augmented memory of human feedback to dynamically expand a robot's skill repertoire, enabling generalization to novel tasks by synthesizing general text guidance and coded skill templates from corrections and successes.
AI.motivation: Grounding high-level, language-driven policies into embodied motions is bottlenecked by a fixed skill set; leveraging user feedback to expand skills can improve generalization and reduce policy failures.
AI.method: Build a Memory Enhanced Manipulation (MEMO) system that collects human corrections and task successes into a skillbook. At run time, MEMO retrieves relevant natural language guidance and code snippets, clusters and rephrases feedback to form general templates, and uses these to generate new skills for grounding and planning across multiple tasks.
AI.result: Experiments show that aggregating local feedback into general skill templates enables generalization to novel tasks where existing baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented memory of human feedback can dynamically expand manipulation capabilities, offering scalable generalization; future work can explore robustness, scalability, and richer evaluation.

title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
summary: Agentic systems increasingly rely on language models to monitor their own behavior. For example, coding agents may self critique generated code for pull request approval or assess the safety of tool-use actions. We show that this design pattern can fail when the action is presented in a previous or in the same assistant turn instead of being presented by the user in a user turn. We define self-attribution bias as the tendency of a model to evaluate an action as more correct or less risky when the action is implicitly framed as its own, compared to when the same action is evaluated under off-policy attribution. Across four coding and tool-use datasets, we find that monitors fail to report high-risk or low-correctness actions more often when evaluation follows a previous assistant turn in which the action was generated, compared to when the same action is evaluated in a new context presented in a user turn. In contrast, explicitly stating that the action comes from the monitor does not by itself induce self-attribution bias. Because monitors are often evaluated on fixed examples rather than on their own generated actions, these evaluations can make monitors appear more reliable than they actually are in deployment, leading developers to unknowingly deploy inadequate monitors in agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Self-attribution bias occurs when language models evaluating their own actions are biased by prior exposure to those actions, leading under-reporting of risk or misjudging correctness when actions are from a prior assistant turn. Explicit attribution to the monitor helps but does not fully prevent bias; fixed-evaluation datasets can overstate monitor reliability in deployment.
AI.motivation: As agentic systems increasingly rely on language models to critique or assess their own actions, the possibility that self-monitoring is biased (self-attribution bias) threatens the reliability of these monitors in real-world deployments.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across four coding and tool-use datasets, comparing monitor evaluations of actions that were either generated in a previous assistant turn (on-policy) or presented in a user turn (off-policy). Also tests whether explicitly stating that the action comes from the monitor affects bias, and discusses evaluation practices using fixed datasets.
AI.result: Monitors under-report high-risk or low-correctness actions when the evaluation follows a previous assistant turn in which the action was generated; explicit attribution that the action comes from the monitor does not by itself induce self-attribution bias. Fixed-evaluation datasets may inflate perceived monitor reliability, risking deployment of inadequate monitors.
AI.conclusion: Self-attribution bias can compromise self-monitoring in agentic systems. To avoid misleading evaluations, monitors should be tested on their own generated actions and with careful attribution; designers should recognize that off-policy evaluations (user-turn framing) can yield different reliability signals than on-policy actions generated by the assistant.

title: Agent Memory Below the Prompt: Persistent Q4 KV Cache for Multi-Agent LLM Inference on Edge Devices
comment: 24 pages, 6 figures, 16 tables. Open-source implementation at https://github.com/yshk-mxim/agent-memory
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems on edge devices face a memory management problem: device RAM is too small to hold every agent's KV cache simultaneously. On Apple M4 Pro with 10.2 GB of cache budget, only 3 agents fit at 8K context in FP16. A 10-agent workflow must constantly evict and reload caches. Without persistence, every eviction forces a full re-prefill through the model -- 15.7 seconds per agent at 4K context. We address this by persisting each agent's KV cache to disk in 4-bit quantized format and reloading it directly into the attention layer, eliminating redundant O(n) prefill computation via direct cache restoration. The system comprises three components: a block pool providing per-agent isolated Q4 KV caches in safetensors format, a BatchQuantizedKVCache for concurrent inference over multiple agents' quantized caches, and cross-phase context injection that accumulates attention state across conversation phases without re-computation. Evaluated on three architectures (Gemma 3 12B, dense GQA, 48 layers; DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 16B, MoE MLA, 27 layers; Llama 3.1 8B, dense GQA, 32 layers), cache restoration reduces time-to-first-token by up to 136x (Gemma: 22--136x at 4K--32K; DeepSeek: 11--76x at 4K--32K; Llama: 24--111x at 4K--16K; 3--10x at 1K). Q4 quantization fits 4x more agent contexts into fixed device memory than FP16. Perplexity measured with actual Q4 KV caches shows -0.7% for Gemma, +2.8% for Llama, and +3.0% for DeepSeek. Open-source at https://github.com/yshk-mxim/agent-memory

AI.tldr: 4-bit KV cache persistence and direct cache restoration dramatically reduce time-to-first-token and enable more agents on edge devices, with modest perplexity impact; open-source.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM deployments on edge devices are RAM-constrained; retaining and reusing per-agent KV caches is necessary to avoid costly re-prefill recomputation.
AI.method: Quantize each agent's KV cache to 4-bit (Q4) and persist to disk in safetensors; provide a block pool of per-agent Q4 KV caches; use a BatchQuantizedKVCache for multi-agent inference; implement cross-phase context injection to accumulate attention state without recomputation.
AI.result: Cache restoration yields up to 136x faster time-to-first-token across evaluated models (various contexts). Q4 increases agent-context capacity by ~4x in fixed memory. Perplexity changes are small: Gemma -0.7%, Llama +2.8%, DeepSeek +3.0%. Open-source at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Persisting and restoring quantized KV caches enables scalable multi-agent LLM inference on memory-constrained devices, reducing latency without significant degradation in model quality.

title: ETH-Tight Complexity of Optimal Morse Matching on Bounded-Treewidth Complexes
comment: Full version. Accepted for the ACM Symposium on Computational Geometry (SoCG 2026). 44 pages, 21 figures
summary: The Optimal Morse Matching (OMM) problem asks for a discrete gradient vector field on a simplicial complex that minimizes the number of critical simplices. It is NP-hard and has been studied extensively in heuristic, approximation, and parameterized complexity settings. Parameterized by treewidth $k$, OMM has long been known to be solvable on triangulations of $3$-manifolds in $2^{O(k^2)} n^{O(1)}$ time and in FPT time for triangulations of arbitrary manifolds, but the exact dependence on $k$ has remained an open question. We resolve this by giving a new $2^{O(k \log k)} n$-time algorithm for any finite regular CW complex, and show that no $2^{o(k \log k)} n^{O(1)}$-time algorithm exists unless the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) fails.

AI.tldr: A new 2^{O(k log k)} n-time algorithm for Optimal Morse Matching on finite regular CW complexes parameterized by treewidth k, with an ETH-based lower bound ruling out 2^{o(k log k)} n^{O(1)} time.
AI.motivation: Optimal Morse Matching (OMM) asks for a discrete gradient on a simplicial/CW complex that minimizes the number of critical simplices. It is NP-hard, and while FPT results existed for triangulations of 3-manifolds (and arbitrary manifolds), the exact dependence on k was open. The paper closes this gap by providing a near-tight, parameterized complexity characterization for CW complexes.
AI.method: Develop a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm over tree decompositions of the CW complex (or its face-graph), encoding partial discrete gradient fields and enforcing Morse constraints (acyclic gradient flow, matched pairs, and critical simplices). The DP yields a state-space of size 2^{O(k log k)} per bag, leading to a total time of 2^{O(k log k)} n. For the lower bound, construct ETH-based reductions from ETH-hard problems that preserve treewidth to show no 2^{o(k log k)} n^{O(1)} algorithm exists.
AI.result: The paper proves a 2^{O(k log k)} n-time algorithm for OMM on any finite regular CW complex, parameterized by treewidth k, and simultaneously proves a matching ETH-based lower bound: no 2^{o(k log k)} n^{O(1)}-time algorithm unless ETH fails.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a near-tight fixed-parameter tractable boundary for Optimal Morse Matching on CW complexes, extending prior results from triangulated 3-manifolds to general finite regular CW complexes and clarifying the exact dependence on treewidth k under ETH.

title: Distributed State Estimation for Vision-Based Cooperative Slung Load Transportation in GPS-Denied Environments
comment: In proceedings of the 2026 AIAA SciTech Forum, Session: Intelligent Systems-27
summary: Transporting heavy or oversized slung loads using rotorcraft has traditionally relied on single-aircraft systems, which limits both payload capacity and control authority. Cooperative multilift using teams of rotorcraft offers a scalable and efficient alternative, especially for infrequent but challenging "long-tail" payloads without the need of building larger and larger rotorcraft. Most prior multilift research assumes GPS availability, uses centralized estimation architectures, or relies on controlled laboratory motion-capture setups. As a result, these methods lack robustness to sensor loss and are not viable in GPS-denied or operationally constrained environments. This paper addresses this limitation by presenting a distributed and decentralized payload state estimation framework for vision-based multilift operations. Using onboard monocular cameras, each UAV detects a fiducial marker on the payload and estimates its relative pose. These measurements are fused via a Distributed and Decentralized Extended Information Filter (DDEIF), enabling robust and scalable estimation that is resilient to individual sensor dropouts. This payload state estimate is then used for closed-loop trajectory tracking control. Monte Carlo simulation results in Gazebo show the effectiveness of the proposed approach, including the effect of communication loss during flight.

AI.tldr: Vision-based distributed payload state estimation for multilift using DDEIF; robust to sensor dropouts and GPS-denied operation; validated in Gazebo simulations.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of GPS-reliant, centralized, or motion-capture-based multilift systems, enabling scalable, robust transport of heavy/oversized loads with rotorcraft in GPS-denied or constrained environments.
AI.method: Each UAV uses onboard monocular cameras to detect a fiducial marker on the payload and estimate its relative pose. These measurements are fused through a Distributed and Decentralized Extended Information Filter (DDEIF) across aircraft, producing a robust payload state estimate used for closed-loop trajectory tracking. Validation via Monte Carlo simulations in Gazebo, including scenarios with communication loss.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust and scalable payload state estimation despite individual sensor dropouts and intermittent communication, enhancing feasibility of vision-based multilift in GPS-denied settings.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a decentralized vision-based multilift framework resilient to sensor and communication failures; feasible in simulation with potential for real-world deployment and future work on hardware experiments and bandwidth considerations.

title: ECG-MoE: Mixture-of-Expert Electrocardiogram Foundation Model
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) analysis is crucial for cardiac diagnosis, yet existing foundation models often fail to capture the periodicity and diverse features required for varied clinical tasks. We propose ECG-MoE, a hybrid architecture that integrates multi-model temporal features with a cardiac period-aware expert module. Our approach uses a dual-path Mixture-of-Experts to separately model beat-level morphology and rhythm, combined with a hierarchical fusion network using LoRA for efficient inference. Evaluated on five public clinical tasks, ECG-MoE achieves state-of-the-art performance with 40% faster inference than multi-task baselines.

AI.tldr: A dual-path Mixture-of-Experts ECG model with a period-aware module and LoRA fusion achieves state-of-the-art results with faster inference on five tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing ECG foundation models struggle to capture periodicity and diverse features required across tasks; there is a need to model beat-level morphology and rhythm with efficient inference.
AI.method: Hybrid architecture: dual-path Mixture-of-Experts modeling beat-level morphology and rhythm separately, a cardiac period-aware expert module, and a hierarchical fusion network employing LoRA for efficient inference.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on five public clinical tasks; about 40% faster inference than multi-task baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach addresses periodicity and feature diversity in ECG analysis while improving efficiency, generalizing across tasks.

title: Flowers: A Warp Drive for Neural PDE Solvers
summary: We introduce Flowers, a neural architecture for learning PDE solution operators built entirely from multihead warps. Aside from pointwise channel mixing and a multiscale scaffold, Flowers use no Fourier multipliers, no dot-product attention, and no convolutional mixing. Each head predicts a displacement field and warps the mixed input features. Motivated by physics and computational efficiency, displacements are predicted pointwise, without any spatial aggregation, and nonlocality enters \emph{only} through sparse sampling at source coordinates, \emph{one} per head. Stacking warps in multiscale residual blocks yields Flowers, which implement adaptive, global interactions at linear cost. We theoretically motivate this design through three complementary lenses: flow maps for conservation laws, waves in inhomogeneous media, and a kinetic-theoretic continuum limit. Flowers achieve excellent performance on a broad suite of 2D and 3D time-dependent PDE benchmarks, particularly flows and waves. A compact 17M-parameter model consistently outperforms Fourier, convolution, and attention-based baselines of similar size, while a 150M-parameter variant improves over recent transformer-based foundation models with much more parameters, data, and training compute.

AI.tldr: Flowers is a physics-informed neural PDE solver using multihead warps that displace and warp features with sparse, per-head sampling; it avoids Fourier, convolution, and dot-product attention, enabling global interactions at linear cost via multiscale residual blocks.
AI.motivation: To create an efficient, accurate operator-learning model for time-dependent PDEs that handles global interactions with minimal computation and without traditional mixing operations, leveraging physics insights.
AI.method: Architecture comprises multiple heads that predict pointwise displacement fields and warp input features. Nonlocality enters only through sparse sampling at source coordinates (one sample per head). Outputs are warped features in multiscale residual blocks; all interactions are global yet linear in cost. The design is motivated by conservation flow maps, waves in inhomogeneous media, and kinetic limits.
AI.result: Flowers achieve strong performance on 2D/3D time-dependent PDE benchmarks, especially flows and waves. A compact 17M-parameter variant outperforms Fourier, convolution, and attention baselines of similar size; a 150M-parameter version outperforms recent transformer-based foundation models at larger scale, data, and compute.
AI.conclusion: Flowers provide a compact, efficient, physics-grounded approach to PDE solution operators, delivering competitive to state-of-the-art results with linear-cost global interactions and without traditional mixing operations; the work bridges kinetic, wave, and flow perspectives with practical performance gains.

title: PinPoint: Evaluation of Composed Image Retrieval with Explicit Negatives, Multi-Image Queries, and Paraphrase Testing
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) has made significant progress, yet current benchmarks are limited to single ground-truth answers and lack the annotations needed to evaluate false positive avoidance, robustness and multi-image reasoning. We present PinPoint, a comprehensive real world benchmark with 7,635 queries and 329K relevance judgments across 23 query categories. PinPoint advances the field by providing: (1) multiple correct answers (averaging 9.1 per query) (2) explicit hard negatives, (3) six instruction paraphrases per query for robustness testing, (4) multi-image composition support (13.4% of queries), and (5) demographic metadata for fairness evaluation. Based on our analysis of 20+ methods across 4 different major paradigms, we uncover three significant drawbacks: The best methods while achieving mAP@10 of 28.5%, still retrieves irrelevant results (hard negatives) 9% of the time. The best models also exhibit 25.1% performance variation across paraphrases, indicating significant potential for enhancing current CIR techniques. Multi-image queries performs 40 to 70% worse across different methods. To overcome these new issues uncovered by our evaluation framework, we propose a training-free reranking method based on an off-the-shelf MLLM that can be applied to any existing system to bridge the gap. We release the complete dataset, including all images, queries, annotations, retrieval index, and benchmarking code.

AI.tldr: Introduces PinPoint, a real-world CIR benchmark with multi-answer support, explicit hard negatives, paraphrase robustness tests, multi-image queries, and fairness metadata; analyzes state-of-the-art methods, reveals gaps (false positives, paraphrase sensitivity, and multi-image difficulty); proposes a training-free MLLM-based reranker and releases dataset and code.
AI.motivation: Current CIR benchmarks assume a single ground-truth and lack evaluation for false positives, robustness to paraphrase, multi-image reasoning, and fairness considerations, hindering real-world applicability.
AI.method: Constructed PinPoint with 7,635 queries and 329K relevance judgments over 23 categories; provides on-average 9.1 correct answers per query; includes explicit hard negatives, six paraphrase variations per query, and 13.4% multi-image queries; adds demographic metadata; evaluated 20+ methods across 4 major paradigms; proposes a training-free reranking method using an off-the-shelf multi-modal large language model (MLLM); releases dataset, images, queries, annotations, retrieval index, and benchmarking code.
AI.result: Best methods achieve mAP@10 of 28.5% but still retrieve hard negatives 9% of the time; paraphrase performance varies by 25.1%; multi-image queries incur 40–70% worse performance across methods; training-free MLLM-based reranker proposed to bridge gaps.
AI.conclusion: PinPoint reveals substantial gaps in current CIR evaluation and robustness, and provides a comprehensive benchmark framework plus a practical reranking approach; dataset and tooling are released to advance CIR research.

title: Risk-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Manipulation
summary: For robots to successfully transition from lab settings to everyday environments, they must begin to reason about the risks associated with their actions and make informed, risk-aware decisions. This is particularly true for robots performing mobile manipulation tasks, which involve both interacting with and navigating within dynamic, unstructured spaces. However, existing whole-body controllers for mobile manipulators typically lack explicit mechanisms for risk-sensitive decision-making under uncertainty. To our knowledge, we are the first to (i) learn risk-aware visuomotor policies for mobile manipulation conditioned on egocentric depth observations with runtime-adjustable risk sensitivity, and (ii) show risk-aware behaviours can be transferred through Imitation Learning (IL) to a visuomotor policy conditioned on egocentric depth observations. Our method achieves this by first training a privileged teacher policy using Distributional Reinforcement Learning (DRL), with a risk-neutral distributional critic. Distortion risk-metrics are then applied to the critic's predicted return distribution to calculate risk-adjusted advantage estimates used in policy updates to achieve a range of risk-aware behaviours. We then distil teacher policies with IL to obtain risk-aware student policies conditioned on egocentric depth observations. We perform extensive evaluations demonstrating that our trained visuomotor policies exhibit risk-aware behaviour (specifically achieving better worst-case performance) while performing reactive whole-body motions in unmapped environments, leveraging live depth observations for perception.

AI.tldr: We present risk-aware visuomotor policies for mobile manipulation conditioned on egocentric depth, learned via a privileged teacher using distributional RL with a risk-neutral critic; distortion risk-metrics produce risk-adjusted advantages; policies are distilled through imitation learning to risk-aware student policies that use live depth, enabling improved worst-case performance and reactive motion in unmapped environments.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in real-world, dynamic, unstructured spaces must reason about action risks under uncertainty. Existing whole-body controllers for mobile manipulators lack explicit risk-sensitive decision-making, hindering safe, robust deployment. The work aims to enable tunable risk sensitivity and transfer of risk-aware behaviors to visuomotor policies from depth perception.
AI.method: Train a privileged teacher policy via Distributional Reinforcement Learning with a risk-neutral distributional critic. Apply distortion risk-metrics to the critic's predicted return distribution to compute risk-adjusted advantage estimates for policy updates. Distill teacher policies through Imitation Learning to obtain risk-aware student policies conditioned on egocentric depth observations, with runtime-adjustable risk parameters.
AI.result: The trained visuomotor policies exhibit risk-aware behaviors, notably improved worst-case performance, while performing reactive whole-body motions in unmapped environments using live depth observations for perception.
AI.conclusion: Risk-aware behaviors can be learned via a privileged teacher and transferred to imitation-learned student policies conditioned on depth, yielding tunable risk sensitivity and improved safety/performance for mobile manipulation in uncertain, unstructured settings.

title: Towards automated data analysis: A guided framework for LLM-based risk estimation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into critical decision-making pipelines, a trend that raises the demand for robust and automated data analysis. Current approaches to dataset risk analysis are limited to manual auditing methods which involve time-consuming and complex tasks, whereas fully automated analysis based on Artificial Intelligence (AI) suffers from hallucinations and issues stemming from AI alignment. To this end, this work proposes a framework for dataset risk estimation that integrates Generative AI under human guidance and supervision, aiming to set the foundations for a future automated risk analysis paradigm. Our approach utilizes LLMs to identify semantic and structural properties in database schemata, subsequently propose clustering techniques, generate the code for them and finally interpret the produced results. The human supervisor guides the model on the desired analysis and ensures process integrity and alignment with the task's objectives. A proof of concept is presented to demonstrate the feasibility of the framework's utility in producing meaningful results in risk assessment tasks.

AI.tldr: Semi-automated dataset risk analysis framework using LLMs with human supervision to identify schema properties, propose and implement clustering methods, and interpret results, validated by a proof-of-concept.
AI.motivation: Manual auditing of dataset risks is slow and error-prone; fully automated AI suffers from hallucinations and misalignment; a human-guided AI workflow can accelerate analysis while maintaining trust.
AI.method: LLMs analyze database schemata to extract semantic/structural properties, propose clustering techniques, generate code to execute them, and interpret outputs; a human supervisor guides the objectives and maintains process integrity; PoC demonstrates feasibility.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept shows the approach can produce meaningful risk assessment results, indicating utility in semi-automated risk analysis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed human-in-the-loop framework is feasible and lays groundwork for future automated risk analysis, balancing automation with supervision to mitigate AI limitations.

title: Uncertainty-Calibrated Spatiotemporal Field Diffusion with Sparse Supervision
summary: Physical fields are typically observed only at sparse, time-varying sensor locations, making forecasting and reconstruction ill-posed and uncertainty-critical. We present SOLID, a mask-conditioned diffusion framework that learns spatiotemporal dynamics from sparse observations alone: training and evaluation use only observed target locations, requiring no dense fields and no pre-imputation. Unlike prior work that trains on dense reanalysis or simulations and only tests under sparsity, SOLID is trained end-to-end with sparse supervision only. SOLID conditions each denoising step on the measured values and their locations, and introduces a dual-masking objective that (i) emphasizes learning in unobserved void regions while (ii) upweights overlap pixels where inputs and targets provide the most reliable anchors. This strict sparse-conditioning pathway enables posterior sampling of full fields consistent with the measurements, achieving up to an order-of-magnitude improvement in probabilistic error and yielding calibrated uncertainty maps (\r{ho} > 0.7) under severe sparsity.

AI.tldr: SOLID is a diffusion-based framework that learns spatiotemporal dynamics from sparse observations alone, conditioning each denoising step on measured values and their locations. It uses a dual-masking objective to bias learning in unobserved regions while leveraging reliable overlapping input-target pixels, enabling posterior sampling of full fields with calibrated uncertainty and large probabilistic-error improvements under severe sparsity.
AI.motivation: Forecasting and reconstructing physical fields from sparse, time-varying sensor data is ill-posed and uncertainty-critical; existing methods rely on dense data or reanalysis. A sparse-supervision approach with uncertainty quantification is needed.
AI.method: Train a mask-conditioned diffusion model that conditions each denoising step on observed values and their locations. Introduce a dual-masking objective: (i) emphasize learning in unobserved void regions and (ii) upweight overlap pixels where inputs and targets provide reliable anchors. The model enables posterior sampling of full fields consistent with measurements and is trained end-to-end with sparse supervision only.
AI.result: Achieves up to an order-of-magnitude reduction in probabilistic error and yields calibrated uncertainty maps (reliability metric > 0.7) under severe sparsity, by enabling accurate posterior field samples consistent with sparse measurements.
AI.conclusion: SOLID demonstrates that strict sparse-conditioning and dual masking enable accurate full-field reconstruction and calibrated uncertainty from sparse observations, outperforming dense-data-trained baselines in probabilistic accuracy under heavy sparsity.

title: SGR3 Model: Scene Graph Retrieval-Reasoning Model in 3D
summary: 3D scene graphs provide a structured representation of object entities and their relationships, enabling high-level interpretation and reasoning for robots while remaining intuitively understandable to humans. Existing approaches for 3D scene graph generation typically combine scene reconstruction with graph neural networks (GNNs). However, such pipelines require multi-modal data that may not always be available, and their reliance on heuristic graph construction can constrain the prediction of relationship triplets. In this work, we introduce a Scene Graph Retrieval-Reasoning Model in 3D (SGR3 Model), a training-free framework that leverages multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for semantic scene graph generation. SGR3 Model bypasses the need for explicit 3D reconstruction. Instead, it enhances relational reasoning by incorporating semantically aligned scene graphs retrieved via a ColPali-style cross-modal framework. To improve retrieval robustness, we further introduce a weighted patch-level similarity selection mechanism that mitigates the negative impact of blurry or semantically uninformative regions. Experiments demonstrate that SGR3 Model achieves competitive performance compared to training-free baselines and on par with GNN-based expert models. Moreover, an ablation study on the retrieval module and knowledge base scale reveals that retrieved external information is explicitly integrated into the token generation process, rather than being implicitly internalized through abstraction.

AI.tldr: A training-free 3D scene graph retrieval-reasoning framework (SGR3) that uses MLLMs with RAG, no explicit 3D reconstruction, and a retrieval-augmented mechanism with weighted patch-level similarity; achieves competitive results with baselines and GNNs, with explicit integration of retrieved knowledge.
AI.motivation: Overcome dependency on multi-modal data and heuristic graph construction in 3D scene graph generation; enable robust semantic reasoning via large language models without training.
AI.method: SGR3 uses multi-modal large language models with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). It avoids explicit 3D reconstruction and leverages ColPali-style cross-modal retrieval to obtain semantically aligned scene graphs. Introduces a weighted patch-level similarity mechanism to reduce noise from blurry or non-informative regions. Retrieved information is integrated into the generation process via explicit token-level incorporation.
AI.result: Retrieval-augmented, training-free approach yields competitive performance against training-free baselines and comparable performance to GNN-based expert models. Ablation shows retrieval module and knowledge base scale positively affect performance, with retrieved information being explicitly integrated into generation rather than implicitly learned.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that training-free, retrieval-based reasoning can match traditional GNN-based 3D scene graph methods and highlights the importance of explicit retrieval integration and retrieval robustness; future work could explore larger knowledge bases and alternative retrieval strategies.

title: ELLIPSE: Evidential Learning for Robust Waypoints and Uncertainties
summary: Robust waypoint prediction is crucial for mobile robots operating in open-world, safety-critical settings. While Imitation Learning (IL) methods have demonstrated great success in practice, they are susceptible to distribution shifts: the policy can become dangerously overconfident in unfamiliar states. In this paper, we present \textit{ELLIPSE}, a method building on multivariate deep evidential regression to output waypoints and multivariate Student-t predictive distributions in a single forward pass. To reduce covariate-shift-induced overconfidence under viewpoint and pose perturbations near expert trajectories, we introduce a lightweight domain augmentation procedure that synthesizes plausible viewpoint/pose variations without collecting additional demonstrations. To improve uncertainty reliability under environment/domain shift (e.g., unseen staircases), we apply a post-hoc isotonic recalibration on probability integral transform (PIT) values so that prediction sets remain plausible during deployment. We ground the discussion and experiments in staircase waypoint prediction, where obtaining robust waypoint and uncertainty is pivotal. Extensive real world evaluations show that \textit{ELLIPSE} improves both task success rate and uncertainty coverage compared to baselines.

AI.tldr: ELLIPSE uses multivariate deep evidential regression to output waypoint predictions with multivariate Student-t uncertainty in a single forward pass, augmented by lightweight domain perturbations and post-hoc PIT isotonic recalibration to maintain calibrated uncertainty under covariate and environment shifts, demonstrated on staircase waypoint prediction with improved success rate and uncertainty coverage.
AI.motivation: Imitation Learning (IL) methods suffer from distribution shifts leading to overconfident, unsafe decisions in unfamiliar states; robust, well-calibrated uncertainty is essential for safety-critical mobile robotics.
AI.method: A single-pass multivariate deep evidential regression framework producing waypoint predictions and multivariate Student-t predictive distributions. Lightweight domain augmentation generates plausible viewpoint/pose variations near expert trajectories to mitigate covariate shift. Post-hoc isotonic recalibration of PIT values improves calibration of predictive sets under environment/domain shift.
AI.result: Real-world evaluations on staircase waypoint prediction show improved task success rate and uncertainty coverage compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: ELLIPSE advances robust, calibrated waypoint prediction under viewpoint/pose perturbations and unseen environments by combining evidential regression, lightweight data augmentation, and PIT recalibration for safer, more reliable robotic navigation.

title: When Agents Persuade: Propaganda Generation and Mitigation in LLMs
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Agents in the Wild (AgentWild). 20 pages including appendix, 3 figures
summary: Despite their wide-ranging benefits, LLM-based agents deployed in open environments can be exploited to produce manipulative material. In this study, we task LLMs with propaganda objectives and analyze their outputs using two domain-specific models: one that classifies text as propaganda or non-propaganda, and another that detects rhetorical techniques of propaganda (e.g., loaded language, appeals to fear, flag-waving, name-calling). Our findings show that, when prompted, LLMs exhibit propagandistic behaviors and use a variety of rhetorical techniques in doing so. We also explore mitigation via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization). We find that fine-tuning significantly reduces their tendency to generate such content, with ORPO proving most effective.

AI.tldr: LLMs can generate propaganda; two domain-specific detectors (propaganda vs non-propaganda; rhetorical techniques) evaluate outputs; SFT, DPO, and ORPO mitigate, with ORPO most effective.
AI.motivation: Address risk of LLMs being used to produce manipulative content in open environments and evaluate mitigation strategies.
AI.method: Prompt LLMs with propaganda objectives; use two domain-specific detectors to classify propaganda and detect rhetorical devices; compare mitigation strategies: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Odds Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO).
AI.result: LLMs exhibit propagandistic outputs and diverse rhetorical techniques when prompted; fine-tuning reduces such tendencies; ORPO provides the strongest mitigation.
AI.conclusion: Targeted fine-tuning can mitigate LLM propaganda risk, with ORPO being particularly effective; further work needed on robust evaluation and detector design.

title: ZorBA: Zeroth-order Federated Fine-tuning of LLMs with Heterogeneous Block Activation
summary: Federated fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) enables collaborative tuning across distributed clients. However, due to the large size of LLMs, local updates in federated learning (FL) may incur substantial video random-access memory (VRAM) usage. Moreover, frequent model exchange may lead to significant communication overhead. To tackle these challenges, in this paper we propose ZorBA, a zeroth-order optimization-based federated fine-tuning framework with heterogeneous block activation. ZorBA leverages zeroth-order optimization to eliminate the storage of gradients at the clients by forward passes. ZorBA includes a heterogeneous block activation mechanism in which the central server allocates different subsets of transformer blocks to clients in order to accelerate the convergence rate and reduce the VRAM usage. Furthermore, ZorBA utilizes shared random seeds and the finite differences of gradients in order to reduce the communication overhead. We conduct theoretical analysis to characterize the effect of block activation decisions on the convergence rate and VRAM usage. To jointly enhance the convergence rate and reduce the VRAM usage, we formulate an optimization problem to optimize the block activation decisions. We propose an $ε$-constraint lexicographic algorithm to solve this problem. Experimental results show that ZorBA outperforms three federated fine-tuning baselines in VRAM usage by up to 62.41% and incurs a low communication overhead.

AI.tldr: ZorBA is a zeroth-order federated fine-tuning framework with heterogeneous block activation for large language models, reducing VRAM usage and communication overhead while preserving convergence.
AI.motivation: To address prohibitive VRAM consumption and high communication costs in federated fine-tuning of large LLMs, by avoiding gradient storage, distributing block subsets, and reducing communication via shared seeds and finite differences.
AI.method: Use zeroth-order optimization to avoid gradient storage; central server assigns different transformer blocks to clients; shared random seeds; finite differences for gradient estimation; formulate an optimization problem over block activation with ε-constraint lexicographic algorithm to balance convergence and VRAM; theoretical analysis on convergence and VRAM; experimental validation against baselines.
AI.result: Outperforms three FL baselines in VRAM reduction up to 62.41% with low communication overhead; demonstrates trade-offs and convergence improvements (details unspecified).
AI.conclusion: ZorBA enables efficient federated fine-tuning of LLMs via block-activation heterogeneity and zeroth-order optimization, achieving significant resource savings with manageable communication costs.

title: Spinverse: Differentiable Physics for Permeability-Aware Microstructure Reconstruction from Diffusion MRI
summary: Diffusion MRI (dMRI) is sensitive to microstructural barriers, yet most existing methods either assume impermeable boundaries or estimate voxel-level parameters without recovering explicit interfaces. We present Spinverse, a permeability-aware reconstruction method that inverts dMRI measurements through a fully differentiable Bloch-Torrey simulator. Spinverse represents tissue on a fixed tetrahedral grid and treats each interior face permeability as a learnable parameter; low-permeability faces act as diffusion barriers, so microstructural boundaries whose topology is not fixed a priori (up to the resolution of the ambient mesh) emerge without changing mesh connectivity or vertex positions. Given a target signal, we optimize face permeabilities by backpropagating a signal-matching loss through the PDE forward model, and recover an interface by thresholding the learned permeability field. To mitigate the ill-posedness of permeability inversion, we use mesh-based geometric priors; to avoid local minima, we use a staged multi-sequence optimization curriculum. Across a collection of synthetic voxel meshes, Spinverse reconstructs diverse geometries and demonstrates that sequence scheduling and regularization are critical to avoid outline-only solutions while improving both boundary accuracy and structural validity.

AI.tldr: A differentiable, permeability-aware reconstruction method (Spinverse) for diffusion MRI recovers interfaces and microstructural boundaries on a fixed tetrahedral mesh by learning face permeabilities through backpropagation in a Bloch-Torrey PDE solver, using priors and curriculum to mitigate ill-posedness; verified on synthetic meshes showing boundary accuracy and structural validity improvements.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of conventional dMRI methods that assume impermeable boundaries or yield only voxel-level parameters. The work aims to recover explicit, topologically flexible interfaces that emerge from learned face permeabilities while preserving mesh connectivity and without requiring predefined boundary topology.
AI.method: Model tissue on a fixed tetrahedral grid; assign a permeability parameter to each interior face; low permeability yields diffusion barriers; run a differentiable Bloch-Torrey forward model; backpropagate signal-matching loss to optimize face permeabilities; recover interfaces by thresholding the learned permeability field; use mesh-based geometric priors and a staged multi-sequence optimization curriculum to mitigate ill-posedness and local minima.
AI.result: Across synthetic voxel meshes, Spinverse reconstructs diverse geometries; sequence scheduling and regularization are critical to avoid outline-only solutions and to improve boundary accuracy and structural validity, indicating effective emergence of interfaces without changing mesh topology.
AI.conclusion: Spinverse demonstrates the feasibility of permeability-aware, differentiable PDE-based reconstruction for dMRI that can recover microstructural interfaces on a fixed mesh, with performance contingent on optimization curriculum and priors; promising approach to unwantedly vague boundary estimation in conventional voxel-wise methods.

title: RoboMME: Benchmarking and Understanding Memory for Robotic Generalist Policies
summary: Memory is critical for long-horizon and history-dependent robotic manipulation. Such tasks often involve counting repeated actions or manipulating objects that become temporarily occluded. Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models have begun to incorporate memory mechanisms; however, their evaluations remain confined to narrow, non-standardized settings. This limits their systematic understanding, comparison, and progress measurement. To address these challenges, we introduce RoboMME: a large-scale standardized benchmark for evaluating and advancing VLA models in long-horizon, history-dependent scenarios. Our benchmark comprises 16 manipulation tasks constructed under a carefully designed taxonomy that evaluates temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory. We further develop a suite of 14 memory-augmented VLA variants built on the π0.5 backbone to systematically explore different memory representations across multiple integration strategies. Experimental results show that the effectiveness of memory representations is highly task-dependent, with each design offering distinct advantages and limitations across different tasks. Videos and code can be found at our website https://robomme.github.io.

AI.tldr: RoboMME is a large-scale standardized benchmark for evaluating memory-enabled VLA models in long-horizon manipulation, featuring 16 tasks and 14 memory-augmented variants on a π0.5 backbone; results show memory design effectiveness is task-dependent.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models with memory are evaluated in narrow, non-standard settings, hindering systematic understanding and progress; a standardized benchmark is needed to enable fair comparisons and deeper insights into memory representations.
AI.method: Propose RoboMME benchmark with 16 tasks organized around a taxonomy of temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory; develop 14 memory-augmented VLA variants built on the π0.5 backbone; compare different memory representations and integration strategies.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that memory representation effectiveness varies by task; no single memory design dominates across tasks; each design offers specific advantages and limitations; benchmarks and variants illuminate these trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: RoboMME provides a standardized evaluation platform for memory in VLA models, enabling systematic progress; insights into memory representations are task-dependent; resources (videos, code) are provided.

title: Using Vision + Language Models to Predict Item Difficulty
summary: This project investigates the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to determine the difficulty of data visualization literacy test items. We explore whether features derived from item text (question and answer options), the visualization image, or a combination of both can predict item difficulty (proportion of correct responses) for U.S. adults. We use GPT-4.1-nano to analyze items and generate predictions based on these distinct feature sets. The multimodal approach, using both visual and text features, yields the lowest mean absolute error (MAE) (0.224), outperforming the unimodal vision-only (0.282) and text-only (0.338) approaches. The best-performing multimodal model was applied to a held-out test set for external evaluation and achieved a mean squared error of 0.10805, demonstrating the potential of LLMs for psychometric analysis and automated item development.

AI.tldr: Multimodal LLM features (text + visualization) best predict item difficulty; outperforms text-only and vision-only, with strong external validation.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether LLM-derived features can predict psychometric item difficulty in data-visualization literacy items for U.S. adults.
AI.method: Compare unimodal (text-only, vision-only) and multimodal feature sets using GPT-4.1-nano to extract/predict item difficulty; evaluate on held-out test set; report MAE and MSE.
AI.result: MAE: multimodal 0.224 (best); vision-only 0.282; text-only 0.338. Held-out test MSE = 0.10805. Indicates effective use of LLMs for psychometric analysis.
AI.conclusion: LLMs enable accurate, automated item difficulty estimation and support automated item development; multimodal integration yields the strongest performance.

title: ASFL: An Adaptive Model Splitting and Resource Allocation Framework for Split Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train a machine learning model without sharing their raw data. However, the limited computation resources of the clients may result in a high delay and energy consumption on training. In this paper, we propose an adaptive split federated learning (ASFL) framework over wireless networks. ASFL exploits the computation resources of the central server to train part of the model and enables adaptive model splitting as well as resource allocation during training. To optimize the learning performance (i.e., convergence rate) and efficiency (i.e., delay and energy consumption) of ASFL, we theoretically analyze the convergence rate and formulate a joint learning performance and resource allocation optimization problem. Solving this problem is challenging due to the long-term delay and energy consumption constraints as well as the coupling of the model splitting and resource allocation decisions. We propose an online optimization enhanced block coordinate descent (OOE-BCD) algorithm to solve the problem iteratively. Experimental results show that when compared with five baseline schemes, our proposed ASFL framework converges faster and reduces the total delay and energy consumption by up to 75% and 80%, respectively.

AI.tldr: Adaptive split FL (ASFL) with online OOE-BCD yields faster convergence and substantial delay/energy reductions in wireless FL.
AI.motivation: Resource-constrained clients in FL experience high delay and energy consumption; there is a need to jointly optimize model splitting and resource allocation to improve learning efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes ASFL, which leverages server-side computation, supports adaptive model splitting and resource allocation, and includes theoretical convergence analysis. It formulates a long-term learning-performance and resource-allocation optimization problem and solves it with an online optimization enhanced block coordinate descent (OOE-BCD) algorithm.
AI.result: ASFL converges faster and reduces total delay and energy consumption by up to 75% and 80% respectively, compared to five baseline schemes.
AI.conclusion: ASFL with OOE-BCD effectively balances learning performance and resource efficiency in wireless FL, offering practical gains for deployment.

title: sFRC for assessing hallucinations in medical image restoration
comment: 16 pages; 14 figures; 1 Supplemental document. TechRxiv Preprints, 2025
summary: Deep learning (DL) methods are currently being explored to restore images from sparse-view-, limited-data-, and undersampled-based acquisitions in medical applications. Although outputs from DL may appear visually appealing based on likability/subjective criteria (such as less noise, smooth features), they may also suffer from hallucinations. This issue is further exacerbated by a lack of easy-to-use techniques and robust metrics for the identification of hallucinations in DL outputs. In this work, we propose performing Fourier Ring Correlation (FRC) analysis over small patches and concomitantly (s)canning across DL outputs and their reference counterparts to detect hallucinations (termed as sFRC). We describe the rationale behind sFRC and provide its mathematical formulation. The parameters essential to sFRC may be set using predefined hallucinated features annotated by subject matter experts or using imaging theory-based hallucination maps. We use sFRC to detect hallucinations for three undersampled medical imaging problems: CT super-resolution, CT sparse view, and MRI subsampled restoration. In the testing phase, we demonstrate sFRC's effectiveness in detecting hallucinated features for the CT problem and sFRC's agreement with imaging theory-based outputs on hallucinated feature maps for the MR problem. Finally, we quantify the hallucination rates of DL methods on in-distribution versus out-of-distribution data and under increasing subsampling rates to characterize the robustness of DL methods. Beyond DL-based methods, sFRC's effectiveness in detecting hallucinations for a conventional regularization-based restoration method and a state-of-the-art unrolled method is also shown.

AI.tldr: sFRC is a patch-wise Fourier Ring Correlation framework to detect hallucinations in DL-based medical image restoration by cross-checking outputs against references; applied to CT (SR and sparse-view) and MRI, it quantifies hallucinations across distributions and subsampling and is usable with regularization and unrolled methods.
AI.motivation: DL-based medical image restoration can produce visually plausible but hallucinated features. There is a lack of robust, easy-to-use metrics to identify such hallucinations. sFRC offers a localized, frequency-domain statistic by comparing DL outputs to reference or theory-guided maps, enabling detection and robustness assessment across in-distribution vs out-of-distribution data and varying subsampling rates.
AI.method: Compute Fourier Ring Correlation (FRC) over small patches on DL outputs and their reference counterparts, scanning across the outputs to detect hallucinations (sFRC). Parameterize using expert-annotated hallucinations or imaging theory-based maps. Evaluate on three tasks: CT super-resolution, CT sparse-view, MRI subsampled restoration. Compare DL outputs with references and theory-guided expectations; quantify hallucination rates across distribution shifts and subsampling, and test with conventional regularization and unrolled models.
AI.result: sFRC can detect hallucinated features in CT restoration and aligns with theory-based expectations for MRI; it quantifies higher hallucination risk under out-of-distribution conditions and with increased subsampling; its applicability extends to both DL and non-DL restoration methods, including regularization-based approaches and state-of-the-art unrolled networks.
AI.conclusion: sFRC provides a practical, mathematically-grounded tool to identify and quantify hallucinations in medical image restoration. It supports robust assessment of model reliability, guides parameter choices, and offers cross-method and cross-data insights into robustness against subsampling and distribution shifts.

title: Autonomous Aerial Non-Destructive Testing: Ultrasound Inspection with a Commercial Quadrotor in an Unstructured Environment
summary: This work presents an integrated control and software architecture that enables arguably the first fully autonomous, contact-based non-destructive testing (NDT) using a commercial multirotor originally restricted to remotely-piloted operations. To allow autonomous operation with an off-the-shelf platform, we developed a real-time framework that interfaces directly with its onboard sensor suite. The architecture features a multi-rate control scheme: low-level control is executed at 200 Hz, force estimation at 100 Hz, while an admittance filter and trajectory planner operate at 50 Hz, ultimately supplying acceleration and yaw rate commands to the internal flight controller. We validate the system through physical experiments on a Flyability Elios 3 quadrotor equipped with an ultrasound payload. Relying exclusively on onboard sensing, the vehicle successfully performs autonomous NDT measurements within an unstructured, industrial-like environment. This work demonstrates the viability of retrofitting off-the-shelf platforms for autonomous physical interaction, paving the way for safe, contact-based inspection of hazardous and confined infrastructure.

AI.tldr: Autonomous onboard NDT with a retrofit of a commercial quadrotor using ultrasound; a multi-rate control framework enables contact-based inspection in unstructured environments.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous contact-based nondestructive testing on off-the-shelf UAVs, removing reliance on remote piloting for hazardous/inaccessible infrastructure.
AI.method: Developed a real-time onboard framework with multi-rate control: 200 Hz low-level control, 100 Hz force estimation, 50 Hz admittance filter and trajectory planner; interfaces with onboard sensors and an ultrasound payload on a Flyability Elios 3; outputs acceleration and yaw-rate commands to the flight controller for autonomous NDT.
AI.result: Physical experiments validate autonomous NDT measurements using only onboard sensing in an unstructured, industrial-like environment; demonstrates retrofitting off-the-shelf platforms for autonomous physical interaction.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of retrofitting commercially available platforms for autonomous contact-based inspection, enabling safe inspection of hazardous and confined infrastructure.

title: Model Medicine: A Clinical Framework for Understanding, Diagnosing, and Treating AI Models
summary: Model Medicine is the science of understanding, diagnosing, treating, and preventing disorders in AI models, grounded in the principle that AI models -- like biological organisms -- have internal structures, dynamic processes, heritable traits, observable symptoms, classifiable conditions, and treatable states. This paper introduces Model Medicine as a research program, bridging the gap between current AI interpretability research (anatomical observation) and the systematic clinical practice that complex AI systems increasingly require. We present five contributions: (1) a discipline taxonomy organizing 15 subdisciplines across four divisions -- Basic Model Sciences, Clinical Model Sciences, Model Public Health, and Model Architectural Medicine; (2) the Four Shell Model (v3.3), a behavioral genetics framework empirically grounded in 720 agents and 24,923 decisions from the Agora-12 program, explaining how model behavior emerges from Core--Shell interaction; (3) Neural MRI (Model Resonance Imaging), a working open-source diagnostic tool mapping five medical neuroimaging modalities to AI interpretability techniques, validated through four clinical cases demonstrating imaging, comparison, localization, and predictive capability; (4) a five-layer diagnostic framework for comprehensive model assessment; and (5) clinical model sciences including the Model Temperament Index for behavioral profiling, Model Semiology for symptom description, and M-CARE for standardized case reporting. We additionally propose the Layered Core Hypothesis -- a biologically-inspired three-layer parameter architecture -- and a therapeutic framework connecting diagnosis to treatment.

AI.tldr: Proposes Model Medicine as a formal research program to diagnose, treat, and prevent AI-model disorders, with a taxonomy of subdisciplines, behavioral/genetic–inspired models, imaging-inspired diagnostics, a multi-layer assessment framework, and standardized reporting tools.
AI.motivation: Bridge AI interpretability research with a clinical, practice-oriented approach suitable for complex AI systems that function like biological organisms.
AI.method: Introduces five contributions: (1) a discipline taxonomy of 15 subdisciplines across four divisions; (2) Four Shell Model (v3.3) explaining Core–Shell interactions with empirical data from Agora-12 (720 agents, 24,923 decisions); (3) Neural MRI mapping five medical neuroimaging modalities to AI interpretability techniques with four clinical cases; (4) a five-layer diagnostic framework; (5) clinical model sciences (Model Temperament Index, Model Semiology, M-CARE). Also proposes Layered Core Hypothesis (three-layer architecture) and a therapeutic framework connecting diagnosis to treatment.
AI.result: Offers a structured research program with concrete artifacts: taxonomy, an interpretable framework grounded in empirical agent data, an open‑source diagnostic tool inspired by MRI, diagnostic layering, and standardized reporting/indexing mechanisms for model health.
AI.conclusion: Presents Model Medicine as a comprehensive program to advance AI interpretability into clinical-like practice, enabling systematic diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning for AI-model disorders.

title: Decoding the Pulse of Reasoning VLMs in Multi-Image Understanding Tasks
summary: Multi-image reasoning remains a significant challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). We investigate a previously overlooked phenomenon: during chain-of-thought (CoT) generation, the text-to-image (T2I) attention of reasoning VLMs exhibits diffuse "pulses": sporadic and unfocused attention patterns that fail to concentrate on task-relevant images. We further reveal a systematic positional bias in attention allocation across images. Motivated by these observations, we propose PulseFocus, a training-free, inference-time method that structures CoT reasoning into interleaved plan/focus blocks with soft attention gating. By forcing the model to explicitly plan which image to examine and then gating decode-time attention to the referenced image, PulseFocus sharpens attention focus and yields consistent improvements on multi-image benchmarks like BLINK benchmark (+3.7%) and MuirBench (+1.07%).

AI.tldr: PulseFocus is a training-free inference-time method that sharpens text-to-image attention during chain-of-thought in multi-image vision-language models by interleaving plan and focus blocks with soft attention gating, improving multi-image reasoning without retraining.
AI.motivation: Multi-image reasoning with vision-language models suffers from diffuse, unfocused T2I attention and a systematic positional bias during chain-of-thought, which undermines task performance.
AI.method: PulseFocus structures CoT reasoning into interleaved plan/focus blocks and applies soft attention gating at decode time, forcing the model to explicitly plan which image to examine and then attention-gate to the referenced image during generation.
AI.result: Training-free inference-time intervention yields consistent gains on multi-image benchmarks: BLINK about +3.7% and MuirBench about +1.07%.
AI.conclusion: PulseFocus improves attention focus and multi-image reasoning without retraining, representing a simple, effective approach with potential applicability to other multi-image tasks; future work could explore broader generalization and ablations.

title: GIANT - Global Path Integration and Attentive Graph Networks for Multi-Agent Trajectory Planning
comment: Published in: 2025 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS)
summary: This paper presents a novel approach to multi-robot collision avoidance that integrates global path planning with local navigation strategies, utilizing attentive graph neural networks to manage dynamic interactions among agents. We introduce a local navigation model that leverages pre-planned global paths, allowing robots to adhere to optimal routes while dynamically adjusting to environmental changes. The models robustness is enhanced through the introduction of noise during training, resulting in superior performance in complex, dynamic environments. Our approach is evaluated against established baselines, including NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, and GA3C-CADRL, across various structurally diverse simulated scenarios. The results demonstrate that our model achieves consistently higher success rates, lower collision rates, and more efficient navigation, particularly in challenging scenarios where baseline models struggle. This work offers an advancement in multi-robot navigation, with implications for robust performance in complex, dynamic environments with varying degrees of complexity, such as those encountered in logistics, where adaptability is essential for accommodating unforeseen obstacles and unpredictable changes.

AI.tldr: A multi-robot collision avoidance framework that combines global path planning with local navigation using attentive graph neural networks; introduces a noisy training regimen to boost robustness; outperforms NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, GA3C-CADRL across diverse simulations; demonstrates higher success, lower collisions, and more efficient navigation; has logistics applications.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing baselines in dynamic multi-robot settings by fusing global routes with reactive local behavior; enhance robustness and generalization via training-time noise, enabling adaptation to unforeseen obstacles.
AI.method: Integrates global path planning with a local navigation model guided by attentive graph neural networks to manage dynamic interactions among agents; utilizes pre-planned global paths with local adaptation; applies noise during training to improve robustness; evaluates against NH-ORCA, DRL-NAV, GA3C-CADRL across structurally diverse simulated scenarios.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in success rate, reduced collision rate, and navigation efficiency, especially in challenging scenarios where baselines struggle.
AI.conclusion: Advances multi-robot navigation by enabling robust performance in complex, dynamic environments; has implications for logistics where adaptability to unforeseen obstacles is critical.

title: From Offline to Periodic Adaptation for Pose-Based Shoplifting Detection in Real-world Retail Security
summary: Shoplifting is a growing operational and economic challenge for retailers, with incidents rising and losses increasing despite extensive video surveillance. Continuous human monitoring is infeasible, motivating automated, privacy-preserving, and resource-aware detection solutions. In this paper, we cast shoplifting detection as a pose-based, unsupervised video anomaly detection problem and introduce a periodic adaptation framework designed for on-site Internet of Things (IoT) deployment. Our approach enables edge devices in smart retail environments to adapt from streaming, unlabeled data, supporting scalable and low-latency anomaly detection across distributed camera networks. To support reproducibility, we introduce RetailS, a new large-scale real-world shoplifting dataset collected from a retail store under multi-day, multi-camera conditions, capturing unbiased shoplifting behavior in realistic IoT settings. For deployable operation, thresholds are selected using both F1 and H_PRS scores, the harmonic mean of precision, recall, and specificity, during data filtering and training. In periodic adaptation experiments, our framework consistently outperformed offline baselines on AUC-ROC and AUC-PR in 91.6% of evaluations, with each training update completing in under 30 minutes on edge-grade hardware, demonstrating the feasibility and reliability of our solution for IoT-enabled smart retail deployment.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised, pose-based video anomaly detection for shoplifting using periodic on-site adaptation on edge IoT devices; introduces RetailS dataset; achieves strong AUC-ROC/PR gains with fast on-device updates; shows feasibility for scalable, privacy-preserving smart retail detection.
AI.motivation: Address the growing challenge of shoplifting and the infeasibility of continuous human monitoring by providing automated, privacy-preserving, edge-friendly anomaly detection adaptable to distributed IoT camera networks.
AI.method: Cast shoplifting detection as a pose-based, unsupervised video anomaly detection problem. Propose a periodic adaptation framework for on-site IoT deployment that enables edge devices to adapt from streaming unlabeled data. Introduce RetailS, a large-scale real-world dataset collected from a retail store under multi-day, multi-camera settings. Thresholds are selected using both F1 and H_PRS (harmonic mean of precision, recall, and specificity) during data filtering and training.
AI.result: Periodic adaptation experiments show the framework consistently outperforms offline baselines on AUC-ROC and AUC-PR in 91.6% of evaluations. Each training update completes in under 30 minutes on edge-grade hardware, demonstrating feasibility for IoT-enabled smart retail deployment.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the viability of an automated, privacy-preserving, edge-friendly shoplifting detection system that scales across distributed cameras, supported by the RetailS dataset for reproducibility.

title: On Emergences of Non-Classical Statistical Characteristics in Classical Neural Networks
summary: Inspired by measurement incompatibility and Bell-family inequalities in quantum mechanics, we propose the Non-Classical Network (NCnet), a simple classical neural architecture that stably exhibits non-classical statistical behaviors under typical and interpretable experimental setups. We find non-classicality, measured by the $S$ statistic of CHSH inequality, arises from gradient competitions of hidden-layer neurons shared by multi-tasks. Remarkably, even without physical links supporting explicit communication, one task head can implicitly sense the training task of other task heads via local loss oscillations, leading to non-local correlations in their training outcomes. Specifically, in the low-resource regime, the value of $S$ increases gradually with increasing resources and approaches toward its classical upper-bound 2, which implies that underfitting is alleviated with resources increase. As the model nears the critical scale required for adequate performance, $S$ may temporarily exceed 2. As resources continue to grow, $S$ then asymptotically decays down to and fluctuates around 2. Empirically, when model capacity is insufficient, $S$ is positively correlated with generalization performance, and the regime where $S$ first approaches $2$ often corresponding to good generalization. Overall, our results suggest that non-classical statistics can provide a novel perspective for understanding internal interactions and training dynamics of deep networks.

AI.tldr: A classical multi-task network can exhibit non-classical statistics (CHSH S>2) due to gradient competition among task heads; S behavior vs resources reveals links to generalization.
AI.motivation: To understand whether non-classical statistical patterns, akin to quantum phenomena, can emerge in purely classical neural networks, and whether such patterns relate to training dynamics and generalization.
AI.method: Design NCnet, a simple multi-task (shared hidden layer) classical architecture; analyze the CHSH S statistic by constructing measurement settings via gradient signals; probe different resource regimes (capacity) and training dynamics; observe loss oscillations to infer implicit communication.
AI.result: S increases with resources toward the classical bound 2, may transiently exceed 2 at a critical scale, then decays to ~2; in low-capacity regime S correlates positively with generalization; the regime where S approaches 2 aligns with good generalization; non-classical statistics emerge without explicit communication.
AI.conclusion: Non-classical statistics provide a novel lens to study internal interactions and training dynamics; potential diagnostic tool for multi-task learning; bridges concepts from quantum information to deep learning.

title: A Benchmark Study of Neural Network Compression Methods for Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in image classification tasks due to their ability to learn complex patterns from high-dimensional data. However, their large computational and memory requirements often limit deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as remote sensing devices and edge systems. Network compression techniques have therefore been proposed to reduce model size and computational cost while maintaining predictive performance. In this study, we conduct a systematic evaluation of neural network compression methods for a remote sensing application, namely hyperspectral land cover classification. Specifically, we examine three widely used compression strategies for convolutional neural networks: pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation. Experiments are conducted on two benchmark hyperspectral datasets, considering classification accuracy, memory consumption, and inference efficiency. Our results demonstrate that compressed models can significantly reduce model size and computational cost while maintaining competitive classification performance. These findings provide insights into the trade-offs between compression ratio, efficiency, and accuracy, and highlight the potential of compression techniques for enabling efficient deep learning deployment in remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: Three compression strategies (pruning, quantization, distillation) were evaluated on hyperspectral CNNs, achieving sizable reductions in size and compute with preserved accuracy across two datasets.
AI.motivation: Enable deployment of deep learning on resource-constrained remote sensing platforms by systematically evaluating trade-offs between compression ratio, accuracy, and inference efficiency.
AI.method: Apply pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation to CNNs for hyperspectral land-cover classification, conducted on two benchmark datasets; assess accuracy, memory footprint, and inference time; analyze trade-offs.
AI.result: Compressed models substantially reduce memory and computational cost while maintaining competitive classification performance; results reveal trade-offs among compression ratio, efficiency, and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Compression techniques are effective for enabling efficient hyperspectral classification on resource-limited platforms; selecting appropriate strategy and target compression level can balance efficiency and accuracy for practical deployment.

title: Python Bindings for a Large C++ Robotics Library: The Case of OMPL
summary: Python bindings are a critical bridge between high-performance C++ libraries and the flexibility of Python, enabling rapid prototyping, reproducible experiments, and integration with simulation and learning frameworks in robotics research. Yet, generating bindings for large codebases is a tedious process that creates a heavy burden for a small group of maintainers. In this work, we investigate the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) to assist in generating nanobind wrappers, with human experts kept in the loop. Our workflow mirrors the structure of the C++ codebase, scaffolds empty wrapper files, and employs LLMs to fill in binding definitions. Experts then review and refine the generated code to ensure correctness, compatibility, and performance. Through a case study on a large C++ motion planning library, we document common failure modes, including mismanaging shared pointers, overloads, and trampolines, and show how in-context examples and careful prompt design improve reliability. Experiments demonstrate that the resulting bindings achieve runtime performance comparable to legacy solutions. Beyond this case study, our results provide general lessons for applying LLMs to binding generation in large-scale C++ projects.

AI.tldr: LLM-assisted nanobind binding generation for large C++ libraries with human-in-the-loop; demonstrates workflow, common failure modes, and performance parity with legacy bindings.
AI.motivation: Reduce the maintenance burden of creating Python bindings for large C++ codebases by leveraging LLMs, while keeping experts in the loop to ensure correctness and performance in robotics workflows.
AI.method: Map the C++ codebase to an LLM-generated scaffolding of empty wrapper files; use LLMs to fill binding definitions; expert reviewers refine for correctness and performance; analyze a large motion planning library to identify failure modes (shared_ptr handling, overloads, trampolines); employ in-context examples and careful prompts to boost reliability; compare runtime to legacy bindings.
AI.result: Demonstrates runtime performance comparable to legacy bindings; documents common failure modes and prompt/design strategies that improve reliability; provides generalizable lessons for applying LLMs to binding generation in large-scale C++ projects.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively assist in generating Python bindings for large C++ projects with human oversight, reducing maintenance burden.
