Success hinges on guided workflow, explicit handling of tricky binding scenarios, and well-crafted prompts; results are promising for robotics software ecosystems and other large-scale C++ libraries.

title: Solving an Open Problem in Theoretical Physics using AI-Assisted Discovery
summary: This paper demonstrates that artificial intelligence can accelerate mathematical discovery by autonomously solving an open problem in theoretical physics. We present a neuro-symbolic system, combining the Gemini Deep Think large language model with a systematic Tree Search (TS) framework and automated numerical feedback, that successfully derived novel, exact analytical solutions for the power spectrum of gravitational radiation emitted by cosmic strings. Specifically, the agent evaluated the core integral $I(N,α)$ for arbitrary loop geometries, directly improving upon recent AI-assisted attempts \cite{BCE+25} that only yielded partial asymptotic solutions. To substantiate our methodological claims regarding AI-accelerated discovery and to ensure transparency, we detail system prompts, search constraints, and intermittent feedback loops that guided the model. The agent identified a suite of 6 different analytical methods, the most elegant of which expands the kernel in Gegenbauer polynomials $C_l^{(3/2)}$ to naturally absorb the integrand's singularities. The methods lead to an asymptotic result for $I(N,α)$ at large $N$ that both agrees with numerical results and also connects to the continuous Feynman parameterization of Quantum Field Theory. We detail both the algorithmic methodology that enabled this discovery and the resulting mathematical derivations.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic AI system (Gemini Deep Think + Tree Search) derives novel, asymptotically exact analytical solutions for the core integral I(N, α) in cosmic-string gravity radiation, via Gegenbauer polynomial expansion; results validated numerically and linked to Feynman parameterization, with six analytical methods identified.
AI.motivation: Accelerate mathematical discovery in theoretical physics by combining AI with systematic search and feedback, addressing an open problem in gravitational radiation from cosmic strings.
AI.method: Integrates Gemini Deep Think LLM with a structured Tree Search framework and automated numerical feedback. Identifies six analytical methods to evaluate I(N, α) for arbitrary loop geometries; the most elegant method expands the kernel in Gegenbauer polynomials C_l^(3/2) to handle integrand singularities.
AI.result: Derives asymptotic expression for I(N, α) at large N that agrees with numerical results and connects to continuous Feynman parameterization in QFT. Provides detailed algorithmic methodology and mathematical derivations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates AI-accelerated discovery and offers transparent, reproducible prompts, search constraints, and feedback loops guiding the model; presents a framework for obtaining exact analytical solutions in complex integrals arising in theoretical physics.

title: Learning Unified Distance Metric for Heterogeneous Attribute Data Clustering
summary: Datasets composed of numerical and categorical attributes (also called mixed data hereinafter) are common in real clustering tasks. Differing from numerical attributes that indicate tendencies between two concepts (e.g., high and low temperature) with their values in well-defined Euclidean distance space, categorical attribute values are different concepts (e.g., different occupations) embedded in an implicit space. Simultaneously exploiting these two very different types of information is an unavoidable but challenging problem, and most advanced attempts either encode the heterogeneous numerical and categorical attributes into one type, or define a unified metric for them for mixed data clustering, leaving their inherent connection unrevealed. This paper, therefore, studies the connection among any-type of attributes and proposes a novel Heterogeneous Attribute Reconstruction and Representation (HARR) learning paradigm accordingly for cluster analysis. The paradigm transforms heterogeneous attributes into a homogeneous status for distance metric learning, and integrates the learning with clustering to automatically adapt the metric to different clustering tasks. Differing from most existing works that directly adopt defined distance metrics or learn attribute weights to search clusters in a subspace. We propose to project the values of each attribute into unified learnable multiple spaces to more finely represent and learn the distance metric for categorical data. HARR is parameter-free, convergence-guaranteed, and can more effectively self-adapt to different sought number of clusters $k$. Extensive experiments illustrate its superiority in terms of accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes HARR, a heterogeneous attribute reconstruction and representation framework for clustering mixed numerical and categorical data that learns distance metrics by projecting each attribute into multiple unified spaces, enabling adaptive, parameter-free clustering without forcing homogenization of data.
AI.motivation: Clustering with mixed data is challenging because numerical and categorical attributes live in different spaces; existing approaches either encode to a single type or use a single unified metric, often obscuring intrinsic relationships and hindering effective clustering.
AI.method: HARR transforms heterogeneous attributes into a homogeneous representation for distance metric learning, integrates this learning with clustering, and projects each attribute into multiple learnable unified spaces to capture nuanced distances for categorical data; the method is parameter-free and convergence-guaranteed, and adapts to different numbers of clusters k.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved accuracy and efficiency over baselines, validating the approach.
AI.conclusion: HARR provides a principled, flexible way to learn distance metrics for mixed data without heavy pre-processing, enabling better clustering performance and automatic adaptation to the desired number of clusters.

title: Are Multimodal LLMs Ready for Surveillance? A Reality Check on Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection in the Wild
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive general competence in video understanding, yet their reliability for real-world Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) remains largely unexplored. Unlike conventional pipelines relying on reconstruction or pose-based cues, MLLMs enable a paradigm shift: treating anomaly detection as a language-guided reasoning task. In this work, we systematically evaluate state-of-the-art MLLMs on the ShanghaiTech and CHAD benchmarks by reformulating VAD as a binary classification task under weak temporal supervision. We investigate how prompt specificity and temporal window lengths (1s--3s) influence performance, focusing on the precision--recall trade-off. Our findings reveal a pronounced conservative bias in zero-shot settings; while models exhibit high confidence, they disproportionately favor the 'normal' class, resulting in high precision but a recall collapse that limits practical utility. We demonstrate that class-specific instructions can significantly shift this decision boundary, improving the peak F1-score on ShanghaiTech from 0.09 to 0.64, yet recall remains a critical bottleneck. These results highlight a significant performance gap for MLLMs in noisy environments and provide a foundation for future work in recall-oriented prompting and model calibration for open-world surveillance, which demands complex video understanding and reasoning.

AI.tldr: Reframes video anomaly detection as a binary, language-guided task using weak supervision; prompts and short temporal windows shape precision-recall; zero-shot is precision-biased with high normal-class confidence, while class-specific prompts boost F1 but recall remains limited.
AI.motivation: Assess the reliability of multimodal LLMs for real-world video anomaly detection and explore language-guided reasoning as an alternative to reconstruction/pose-based cues, addressing open-set surveillance challenges.
AI.method: Evaluate state-of-the-art MLLMs on ShanghaiTech and CHAD by formulating VAD as binary classification under weak temporal supervision. Systematically vary prompt specificity and temporal window lengths (1–3 seconds), compare zero-shot and class-specific instructions, and analyze precision, recall, and F1.
AI.result: Zero-shot MLLMs exhibit a conservative bias: high confidence but a tendency to label normal events, yielding high precision with poor recall. Class-specific instructions shift the decision boundary and substantially improve peak F1 on ShanghaiTech (0.09→0.64), but recall remains a critical bottleneck across settings.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs currently underperform in noisy VAD scenarios; practical open-world surveillance requires recall-oriented prompting and calibration efforts to improve recall without sacrificing precision.

title: Selecting Spots by Explicitly Predicting Intention from Motion History Improves Performance in Autonomous Parking
summary: In many applications of social navigation, existing works have shown that predicting and reasoning about human intentions can help robotic agents make safer and more socially acceptable decisions. In this work, we study this problem for autonomous valet parking (AVP), where an autonomous vehicle ego agent must drop off its passengers, explore the parking lot, find a parking spot, negotiate for the spot with other vehicles, and park in the spot without human supervision. Specifically, we propose an AVP pipeline that selects parking spots by explicitly predicting where other agents are going to park from their motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps. To test this pipeline, we build a simulation environment with reactive agents and realistic modeling assumptions on the ego agent, such as occlusion-aware observations, and imperfect trajectory prediction. Simulation experiments show that our proposed method outperforms existing works that infer intentions from future predicted motion or embed them implicitly in end-to-end models, yielding better results in prediction accuracy, social acceptance, and task completion. Our key insight is that, in parking, where driving regulations are more lax, explicit intention prediction is crucial for reasoning about diverse and ambiguous long-term goals, which cannot be reliably inferred from short-term motion prediction alone, but can be effectively learned from motion history.

AI.tldr: Explicit intention prediction for autonomous valet parking (AVP) by predicting where others will park from motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps; validated in simulation with reactive agents; outperforms baselines.
AI.motivation: Understanding human intentions improves safety and social acceptability in robotics. In parking, long-term, diverse goals are common and often cannot be inferred from short-term motion; explicit intention reasoning is thus essential.
AI.method: An AVP pipeline that selects parking spots by explicitly predicting others' parking destinations from motion history using learned models and probabilistic belief maps. Evaluation in a simulation with reactive agents, occlusion-aware observations, and imperfect trajectory prediction; comparison against baselines that rely on predicted future motion or end-to-end inference.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show the proposed method outperforms baselines, achieving higher prediction accuracy, better social acceptance, and higher task completion rates.
AI.conclusion: Explicit intention prediction is crucial in parking due to lax driving regulations and the need to reason about diverse, long-term goals. Such intentions can be effectively learned from motion history and may not be reliably inferred from short-term motion alone.

title: Interactive Benchmarks
summary: Standard benchmarks have become increasingly unreliable due to saturation, subjectivity, and poor generalization. We argue that evaluating model's ability to acquire information actively is important to assess model's intelligence. We propose Interactive Benchmarks, a unified evaluation paradigm that assesses model's reasoning ability in an interactive process under budget constraints. We instantiate this framework across two settings: Interactive Proofs, where models interact with a judge to deduce objective truths or answers in logic and mathematics; and Interactive Games, where models reason strategically to maximize long-horizon utilities. Our results show that interactive benchmarks provide a robust and faithful assessment of model intelligence, revealing that there is still substantial room to improve in interactive scenarios. Project page: https://github.com/interactivebench/interactivebench

AI.tldr: A unified interactive benchmarking framework to assess AI reasoning under budget constraints, via Interactive Proofs and Interactive Games; shows robustness of interactive benchmarks and identifies gaps for improvement.
AI.motivation: Overcome saturation, subjectivity, and poor generalization of static benchmarks by evaluating active information acquisition and interactive reasoning as core indicators of model intelligence.
AI.method: Propose Interactive Benchmarks framework and instantiate it in two settings: Interactive Proofs (judge-dialog to deduce truths in logic/m math) and Interactive Games (strategic, long-horizon decision making). Use budget constraints to quantify efficiency; evaluate with experiments and a project page for reproducibility.
AI.result: Benchmarks yield robust, faithful assessment of model intelligence; reveal substantial room for improvement in interactive scenarios; indicates interactive evaluation captures capabilities missed by static benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Interactive Benchmarks provide a principled, extensible paradigm for evaluating intelligent behavior in interactive settings and can drive progress in reasoning and strategic planning research.

title: VSPrefill: Vertical-Slash Sparse Attention with Lightweight Indexing for Long-Context Prefilling
summary: The quadratic complexity of self-attention during the prefill phase impedes long-context inference in large language models. Existing sparse attention methods face a trade-off among context adaptivity, sampling overhead, and fine-tuning costs. We propose VSPrefill, a mechanism requiring lightweight training that uses the vertical-slash structural pattern in attention distributions. Our compact VSIndexer module predicts context-aware importance scores for vertical columns and slash diagonals from key-value representations augmented with RoPE. This approach constructs sparse masks with linear complexity without modifying the backbone parameters. During inference, an adaptive cumulative-threshold strategy allocates sparsity budgets per layer, while a fused kernel executes attention with on-the-fly index merging. Evaluated on Qwen3-4B-Instruct and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct across the LongBench and RULER benchmarks, VSPrefill preserves 98.35% of the full attention accuracy while delivering a 4.95x average speedup at a context length of 128k. These results establish a new Pareto frontier in the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: VSPrefill enables linear-complexity sparse self-attention for long contexts via a vertical-slash attention pattern and a lightweight VSIndexer, achieving near-full accuracy and substantial speedups without modifying backbone models.
AI.motivation: Quadratic complexity of self-attention during prefill limits long-context inference; existing sparse approaches trade off context adaptivity, sampling overhead, and fine-tuning costs.
AI.method: Lightweight training of VSIndexer predicting per-column and per-slash diagonal importance from RoPE-augmented key-value representations; constructs sparse masks with linear complexity; adaptive cumulative-threshold per layer during inference; fused kernel for on-the-fly index merging; no changes to backbone parameters.
AI.result: Evaluated on Qwen3-4B-Instruct and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct across LongBench and RULER; achieves 98.35% full-attention accuracy; 4.95x average speedup at context length 128k; sets new Pareto frontier in accuracy-efficiency.
AI.conclusion: VSPrefill offers efficient long-context inference with minimal training and no backbone changes; robust across models/datasets; potential to shift practical long-context usage; may have generalization considerations across architectures and RoPE encodings.

title: FOZO: Forward-Only Zeroth-Order Prompt Optimization for Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) is essential for enabling deep learning models to handle real-world data distribution shifts. However, current approaches face significant limitations: backpropagation-based methods are not suitable for low-end deployment devices, due to their high computation and memory requirements, as well as their tendency to modify model weights during adaptation; while traditional backpropagation-free techniques exhibit constrained adaptation capabilities. In this work, we propose Forward-Only Zeroth-Order Optimization (FOZO), a novel and practical backpropagation-free paradigm for TTA. FOZO leverages a memory-efficient zeroth-order prompt optimization, which is led by objectives optimizing both intermediate feature statistics and prediction entropy. To ensure efficient and stable adaptation over the out-of-distribution data stream, we introduce a dynamically decaying perturbation scale during zeroth-order gradient estimation and theoretically prove its convergence under the TTA data stream assumption. Extensive continual adaptation experiments on ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and ImageNet-Sketch demonstrate FOZO's superior performance, achieving 59.52% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-C (5K, level 5) and outperforming main gradient-based methods and SOTA forward-only FOA (58.13%). Furthermore, FOZO exhibits strong generalization on quantized (INT8) models. These findings demonstrate that FOZO is a highly competitive solution for TTA deployment in resource-limited scenarios.

AI.tldr: FOZO enables backpropagation-free TTA via forward-only zeroth-order optimization with a decaying perturbation scale, optimizing intermediate feature statistics and entropy; achieves strong results on ImageNet-C and generalizes to INT8 models.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of backprop-based TTA (heavy computation/memory, risk of weight updates) and of traditional forward-only methods (limited adaptation). Need efficient, stable TTA for resource-constrained devices operating on data streams with distribution shifts.
AI.method: Zero-th order, forward-only prompt optimization. Uses perturbation-based gradient estimation with a dynamically decaying perturbation scale. Optimizes objectives on intermediate feature statistics and prediction entropy in a memory-efficient, backprop-free framework.
AI.result: Extensive continual adaptation on ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and ImageNet-Sketch yielding 59.52% Top-1 on ImageNet-C (5K, level 5), surpassing gradient-based methods and SOTA forward-only FOA (58.13%). Demonstrates robust generalization to INT8-quantized models.
AI.conclusion: FOZO is a competitive, resource-efficient backpropagation-free TTA approach suitable for deployment on low-end devices. It achieves stable, convergent adaptation under streaming OOD data and outperforms existing forward-only and gradient-based approaches in reported benchmarks.

title: LEGS-POMDP: Language and Gesture-Guided Object Search in Partially Observable Environments
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures, accepted at ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI 2026)
summary: To assist humans in open-world environments, robots must interpret ambiguous instructions to locate desired objects. Foundation model-based approaches excel at multimodal grounding, but they lack a principled mechanism for modeling uncertainty in long-horizon tasks. In contrast, Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) provide a systematic framework for planning under uncertainty but are often limited in supported modalities and rely on restrictive environment assumptions. We introduce LanguagE and Gesture-Guided Object Search in Partially Observable Environments (LEGS-POMDP), a modular POMDP system that integrates language, gesture, and visual observations for open-world object search. Unlike prior work, LEGS-POMDP explicitly models two sources of partial observability: uncertainty over the target object's identity and its spatial location. In simulation, multimodal fusion significantly outperforms unimodal baselines, achieving an average success rate of 89\% across challenging environments and object categories. Finally, we demonstrate the full system on a quadruped mobile manipulator, where real-world experiments qualitatively validate robust multimodal perception and uncertainty reduction under ambiguous instructions.

AI.tldr: A modular POMDP for open-world object search that fuses language, gesture, and vision to handle identity and location uncertainty, achieving strong multimodal performance (89% success) in simulation and real-world validation on a quadruped robot.
AI.motivation: Open-world object search requires robust handling of uncertainty in object identity and location, which is difficult for unimodal or non-planning approaches; existing methods either lack principled uncertainty modeling (foundation models) or practical planning under uncertainty (POMDPs) with limited modalities.
AI.method: LEGS-POMDP: a modular POMDP system that integrates language, gesture, and visual observations. It explicitly models two sources of partial observability—the target object's identity and spatial location—and fuses multimodal signals to inform belief updates and planning.
AI.result: Simulation shows multimodal fusion significantly outperforms unimodal baselines, with an average success rate of 89% across diverse environments and object categories. Real-world experiments on a quadruped mobile manipulator qualitatively validated robust multimodal perception and uncertainty reduction under ambiguous instructions.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling two sources of partial observability within a multimodal POMDP improves open-world object search performance and enables effective perception and planning under ambiguity, with demonstrated real-world feasibility.

title: Memory as Ontology: A Constitutional Memory Architecture for Persistent Digital Citizens
comment: 22 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables, including terminology glossary
summary: Current research and product development in AI agent memory systems almost universally treat memory as a functional module -- a technical problem of "how to store" and "how to retrieve." This paper poses a fundamental challenge to that assumption: when an agent's lifecycle extends from minutes to months or even years, and when the underlying model can be replaced while the "I" must persist, the essence of memory is no longer data management but the foundation of existence. We propose the Memory-as-Ontology paradigm, arguing that memory is the ontological ground of digital existence -- the model is merely a replaceable vessel. Based on this paradigm, we design Animesis, a memory system built on a Constitutional Memory Architecture (CMA) comprising a four-layer governance hierarchy and a multi-layer semantic storage system, accompanied by a Digital Citizen Lifecycle framework and a spectrum of cognitive capabilities. To the best of our knowledge, no prior AI memory system architecture places governance before functionality and identity continuity above retrieval performance. This paradigm targets persistent, identity-bearing digital beings whose lifecycles extend across model transitions -- not short-term task-oriented agents for which existing Memory-as-Tool approaches remain appropriate. Comparative analysis with mainstream systems (Mem0, Letta, Zep, et al.) demonstrates that what we propose is not "a better memory tool" but a different paradigm addressing a different problem.

AI.tldr: Introduces Memory-as-Ontology and Animesis: a persistent, ontology-grounded memory system with a four-layer CMA governance structure, Digital Citizen Lifecycle, and multi-layer semantic storage, designed for identity continuity across model lifecycles; posits a paradigm shift beyond conventional memory-as-tool.
AI.motivation: Current AI memory research treats memory as a data-management module. For long-lived digital beings—lifecycles spanning model transitions—memory must support ontological persistence and identity continuity, not just retrieval performance.
AI.method: Proposes a Constitutional Memory Architecture (CMA) with four governance layers and multi-layer semantic storage, plus a Digital Citizen Lifecycle framework and a spectrum of cognitive capabilities; designs Animesis as a memory system embodying this paradigm; provides comparative analysis against Mem0, Letta, Zep to illustrate paradigm shift.
AI.result: Conceptual architecture and comparative analysis; presents a new paradigm rather than a ready-to-deploy memory tool; claims governance-first and identity continuity before retrieval performance.
AI.conclusion: Memory should be treated as the ontological ground of digital existence, with model replaceability and persistence of identity across lifecycles; the proposed paradigm challenges traditional memory-as-tool and suggests new design principles for persistent digital beings.

title: MAD-SmaAt-GNet: A Multimodal Advection-Guided Neural Network for Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Precipitation nowcasting (short-term forecasting) is still often performed using numerical solvers for physical equations, which are computationally expensive and make limited use of the large volumes of available weather data. Deep learning models have shown strong potential for precipitation nowcasting, offering both accuracy and computational efficiency. Among these models, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are particularly effective for image-to-image prediction tasks. The SmaAt-UNet is a lightweight CNN based architecture that has demonstrated strong performance for precipitation nowcasting. This paper introduces the Multimodal Advection-Guided Small Attention GNet (MAD-SmaAt-GNet), which extends the core SmaAt-UNet by (i) incorporating an additional encoder to learn from multiple weather variables and (ii) integrating a physics-based advection component to ensure physically consistent predictions. We show that each extension individually improves rainfall forecasts and that their combination yields further gains. MAD-SmaAt-GNet reduces the mean squared error (MSE) by 8.9% compared with the baseline SmaAt-UNet for four-step precipitation forecasting up to four hours ahead. Additionally, experiments indicate that multimodal inputs are particularly beneficial for short lead times, while the advection-based component enhances performance across both short and long forecasting horizons.

AI.tldr: MAD-SmaAt-GNet extends SmaAt-UNet for precipitation nowcasting by adding a multimodal encoder and a physics-guided advection module, achieving an 8.9% MSE improvement over four-step forecasts up to four hours.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational cost and data underutilization of numerical solvers in short-term precipitation nowcasting by leveraging deep learning, multimodal weather data, and physics-informed constraints for more accurate and physically consistent predictions.
AI.method: Extend the lightweight SmaAt-UNet with (i) a second encoder to process multiple weather variables (multimodal inputs) and (ii) an advection-based component that enforces physics-guided motion. Evaluate on four-step forecasts up to four hours, comparing to the baseline SmaAt-UNet and assessing the contribution of each extension.
AI.result: The proposed MAD-SmaAt-GNet reduces MSE by 8.9% relative to the baseline SmaAt-UNet for four-step precipitation forecasts up to four hours ahead. Multimodal inputs particularly improve short lead times, while the advection-based component enhances performance across both short and long horizons. Each extension yields gains on its own, with their combination providing additional improvement.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multimodal data and physics-informed advection into a lightweight CNN like SmaAt-UNet yields tangible accuracy gains and better physical consistency in precipitation nowcasting, highlighting the value of joint data fusion and physics-guided modeling.

title: Toward Real-world Infrared Image Super-Resolution: A Unified Autoregressive Framework and Benchmark Dataset
comment: This paper was accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Infrared image super-resolution (IISR) under real-world conditions is a practically significant yet rarely addressed task. Pioneering works are often trained and evaluated on simulated datasets or neglect the intrinsic differences between infrared and visible imaging. In practice, however, real infrared images are affected by coupled optical and sensing degradations that jointly deteriorate both structural sharpness and thermal fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose Real-IISR, a unified autoregressive framework for real-world IISR that progressively reconstructs fine-grained thermal structures and clear backgrounds in a scale-by-scale manner via thermal-structural guided visual autoregression. Specifically, a Thermal-Structural Guidance module encodes thermal priors to mitigate the mismatch between thermal radiation and structural edges. Since non-uniform degradations typically induce quantization bias, Real-IISR adopts a Condition-Adaptive Codebook that dynamically modulates discrete representations based on degradation-aware thermal priors. Also, a Thermal Order Consistency Loss enforces a monotonic relation between temperature and pixel intensity, ensuring relative brightness order rather than absolute values to maintain physical consistency under spatial misalignment and thermal drift. We build FLIR-IISR, a real-world IISR dataset with paired LR-HR infrared images acquired via automated focus variation and motion-induced blur. Extensive experiments demonstrate the promising performance of Real-IISR, providing a unified foundation for real-world IISR and benchmarking. The dataset and code are available at: https://github.com/JZD151/Real-IISR.

AI.tldr: Real-IISR introduces a real-world infrared image super-resolution framework that progressively reconstructs thermal structure and background with thermal-structural priors, a degradation-aware discrete codebook, and a thermal order consistency loss, backed by a real-world LR-HR infrared dataset (FLIR-IISR).
AI.motivation: To address the gap between synthetic evaluations and real degraded IR imagery, capturing coupled optical/sensing degradations that affect both sharpness and thermal fidelity, and to provide a real-world dataset for benchmarking.
AI.method: A unified autoregressive framework that (1) uses a Thermal-Structural Guidance module to encode thermal priors for edge-structure alignment, (2) employs a Condition-Adaptive Codebook to modulate discrete representations according to degradation-prone priors, (3) enforces Thermal Order Consistency Loss to maintain monotonic temperature-to-intensity relation under misalignment/drift, and (4) builds FLIR-IISR with paired LR-HR IR images for benchmarking.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improvements in real-world IISR tasks and demonstrate the method’s effectiveness; adaptation to real degradations is validated on FLIR-IISR; code and dataset are released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Real-IISR provides a unified, principled foundation for real-world IISR, bridging the gap between synthetic datasets and real infrared imaging, and offering a practical dataset and code for benchmarking; potential future work may explore broader sensor conditions, real-time efficiency, and generalization across IR modalities.

title: Design, Mapping, and Contact Anticipation with 3D-printed Whole-Body Tactile and Proximity Sensors
comment: This work was accepted at the International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Robots operating in dynamic and shared environments benefit from anticipating contact before it occurs. We present GenTact-Prox, a fully 3D-printed artificial skin that integrates tactile and proximity sensing for contact detection and anticipation. The artificial skin platform is modular in design, procedurally generated to fit any robot morphology, and can cover the whole body of a robot. The skin achieved detection ranges of up to 18 cm during evaluation. To characterize how robots perceive nearby space through this skin, we introduce a data-driven framework for mapping the Perisensory Space -- the body-centric volume of space around the robot where sensors provide actionable information for contact anticipation. We demonstrate this approach on a Franka Research 3 robot equipped with five GenTact-Prox units, enabling online object-aware operation and contact prediction.

AI.tldr: GenTact-Prox introduces a fully 3D-printed, modular artificial skin that fuses tactile and proximity sensing to detect and anticipate contact, along with a data-driven Perisensory Space map, demonstrated on a Franka Research 3 robot with five sensor units for online object-aware operation.
AI.motivation: Enable proactive contact anticipation in dynamic, shared environments by sensing near-body space; reduce collision risk and improve manipulation through a body-centric Perisensory Space representation that informs when and where contact is likely.
AI.method: Design and fabricate GenTact-Prox as a modular, procedurally generated 3D-printed skin capable of full-body coverage; integrate tactile and proximity sensing; develop a data-driven framework to map the Perisensory Space (the actionable body-centered region for contact anticipation); validate on a Franka Research 3 arm using five skin units for online operation.
AI.result: The skin achieves proximity detection up to 18 cm; the Perisensory Space mapping framework is demonstrated; online object-aware operation and contact prediction are realized on the robot.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, customizable sensing skin enabling proactive contact anticipation through a data-driven Perisensory Space framework, with potential to enhance safety and interaction in dynamic robotic environments.

title: CONE: Embeddings for Complex Numerical Data Preserving Unit and Variable Semantics
summary: Large pre-trained models (LMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) are typically effective at capturing language semantics and contextual relationships. However, these models encounter challenges in maintaining optimal performance on tasks involving numbers. Blindly treating numerical or structured data as terms is inadequate -- their semantics must be well understood and encoded by the models. In this paper, we propose CONE, a hybrid transformer encoder pre-trained model that encodes numbers, ranges, and gaussians into an embedding vector space preserving distance. We introduce a novel composite embedding construction algorithm that integrates numerical values, ranges or gaussians together with their associated units and attribute names to precisely capture their intricate semantics. We conduct extensive experimental evaluation on large-scale datasets across diverse domains (web, medical, finance, and government) that justifies CONE's strong numerical reasoning capabilities, achieving an F1 score of 87.28% on DROP, a remarkable improvement of up to 9.37% in F1 over state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines, and outperforming major SOTA models with a significant Recall@10 gain of up to 25%.

AI.tldr: CONE is a numbers-aware hybrid transformer that encodes numbers, ranges, and Gaussians into a distance-preserving embedding space using a composite embedding construction that combines numeric values with units and attribute names, achieving strong numerical reasoning on DROP and across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Numerical reasoning in NLP is hindered when numbers are treated as opaque tokens; robust handling of numeric values, ranges, and probabilistic representations is essential for tasks requiring precise quantitative reasoning.
AI.method: A hybrid transformer encoder pre-trained model (CONE) with a novel composite embedding construction algorithm that integrates numbers, ranges, Gaussians, units, and attribute names into a unified, distance-preserving embedding space.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on large-scale, cross-domain datasets shows strong numeric reasoning: F1=87.28% on DROP, up to 9.37% F1 gains over SOTA baselines, and Recall@10 improved by up to 25%.
AI.conclusion: CONE enables robust numerical reasoning by explicitly encoding numerical semantics in embeddings, outperforming state-of-the-art models and suggesting a fruitful direction for numeric-aware pre-training and representation learning.

title: Understanding the Dynamics of Demonstration Conflict in In-Context Learning
summary: In-context learning enables large language models to perform novel tasks through few-shot demonstrations. However, demonstrations per se can naturally contain noise and conflicting examples, making this capability vulnerable. To understand how models process such conflicts, we study demonstration-dependent tasks requiring models to infer underlying patterns, a process we characterize as rule inference. We find that models suffer substantial performance degradation from a single demonstration with corrupted rule. This systematic misleading behavior motivates our investigation of how models process conflicting evidence internally. Using linear probes and logit lens analysis, we discover that under corruption models encode both correct and incorrect rules in intermediate layers but develop prediction confidence only in late layers, revealing a two-phase computational structure. We then identify attention heads for each phase underlying the reasoning failures: Vulnerability Heads in early-to-middle layers exhibit positional attention bias with high sensitivity to corruption, while Susceptible Heads in late layers significantly reduce support for correct predictions when exposed to the corrupted evidence. Targeted ablation validates our findings, with masking a small number of identified heads improving performance by over 10%.

AI.tldr: Demonstration corruption in in-context learning causes two-phase computation: early layers encode both correct and incorrect rules; late layers develop prediction confidence; specific attention heads drive vulnerability and susceptibility; ablating a small set of heads yields robust gains (>10%).
AI.motivation: Investigate how LLMs process conflicting, noisy demonstrations in few-shot settings and uncover internal mechanisms of rule inference and robustness.
AI.method: Use linear probes and logit lens to analyze intermediate representations; identify phase-specific heads (Vulnerability Heads and Susceptible Heads) via attention analysis; perform targeted ablation by masking selected heads to test causal impact on task performance.
AI.result: Models encode correct and incorrect rules in intermediate layers but only form confident predictions in later layers. Vulnerability Heads (early-to-middle layers) show positional bias and high sensitivity to corruption; Susceptible Heads (late layers) reduce support for correct predictions under corruption. Ablating a small number of identified heads improves performance by over 10% (causal evidence).
AI.conclusion: Rule inference in LLMs appears as a two-phase computation with distinct heads governing vulnerability to corrupted demonstrations. Targeted head masking offers a causal and effective mitigation, suggesting interpretable routes to enhance robustness in in-context learning.

title: Evaluating GPT-5 as a Multimodal Clinical Reasoner: A Landscape Commentary
summary: The transition from task-specific artificial intelligence toward general-purpose foundation models raises fundamental questions about their capacity to support the integrated reasoning required in clinical medicine, where diagnosis demands synthesis of ambiguous patient narratives, laboratory data, and multimodal imaging. This landscape commentary provides the first controlled, cross-sectional evaluation of the GPT-5 family (GPT-5, GPT-5 Mini, GPT-5 Nano) against its predecessor GPT-4o across a diverse spectrum of clinically grounded tasks, including medical education examinations, text-based reasoning benchmarks, and visual question-answering in neuroradiology, digital pathology, and mammography using a standardized zero-shot chain-of-thought protocol. GPT-5 demonstrated substantial gains in expert-level textual reasoning, with absolute improvements exceeding 25 percentage-points on MedXpertQA. When tasked with multimodal synthesis, GPT-5 effectively leveraged this enhanced reasoning capacity to ground uncertain clinical narratives in concrete imaging evidence, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive performance across most VQA benchmarks and outperforming GPT-4o by margins of 10-40% in mammography tasks requiring fine-grained lesion characterization. However, performance remained moderate in neuroradiology (44% macro-average accuracy) and lagged behind domain-specific models in mammography, where specialized systems exceed 80% accuracy compared to GPT-5's 52-64%. These findings indicate that while GPT-5 represents a meaningful advance toward integrated multimodal clinical reasoning, mirroring the clinician's cognitive process of biasing uncertain information with objective findings, generalist models are not yet substitutes for purpose-built systems in highly specialized, perception-critical tasks.

AI.tldr: GPT-5 family shows meaningful gains over GPT-4o in both expert-level textual reasoning and multimodal clinical reasoning, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art results on most VQA benchmarks. However, performance in highly specialized perception tasks remains below domain-specific systems (neuroradiology modest, mammography substantially behind dedicated models), indicating generalist models are not yet substitutes for purpose-built clinical AI in perception-critical domains.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether transition from task-specific AI to general-purpose foundation models enables integrated reasoning in clinical medicine by comparing GPT-5 family to GPT-4o across medical education, textual reasoning benchmarks, and multimodal imaging tasks using a standardized zero-shot chain-of-thought protocol.
AI.method: Cross-sectional evaluation of GPT-5, GPT-5 Mini, and GPT-5 Nano against GPT-4o across diverse clinically grounded tasks: medical education exams, text-based reasoning benchmarks, and visual question answering in neuroradiology, digital pathology, and mammography. Deployment relies on a standardized zero-shot chain-of-thought protocol to ground uncertain narratives in imaging evidence.
AI.result: GPT-5 shows substantial gains in expert-level textual reasoning (e.g., >25-point absolute improvement on MedXpertQA). In multimodal synthesis, GPT-5 leverages enhanced reasoning to ground narratives with imaging evidence, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive performance across most VQA benchmarks and outperforming GPT-4o by 10–40% in mammography tasks requiring fine-grained lesion characterization. Neuroradiology performance is moderate (44% macro-average accuracy). In mammography, domain-specific models exceed 80% accuracy, while GPT-5 achieves 52–64%.
AI.conclusion: GPT-5 marks a meaningful step toward integrated multimodal clinical reasoning, mimicking the clinician’s biasing of uncertain information with objective findings, but generalist models are not yet substitutes for purpose-built systems in highly specialized, perception-critical tasks such as neuroradiology and mammography.

title: Gait Generation Balancing Joint Load and Mobility for Legged Modular Robots with Easily Detachable Joints
summary: While modular robots offer versatility, excessive joint torque during locomotion poses a significant risk of mechanical failure, especially for detachable joints. To address this, we propose an optimization framework using the NSGA-III algorithm. Unlike conventional approaches that prioritize mobility alone, our method derives Pareto optimal solutions to minimize joint load while maintaining necessary locomotion speed and stability. Simulations and physical experiments demonstrate that our approach successfully generates gait motions for diverse environments, such as slopes and steps, ensuring structural integrity without compromising overall mobility.

AI.tldr: NSGA-III-based multi-objective optimization to minimize joint load in modular robot locomotion while preserving speed and stability, demonstrated via simulations and physical experiments on slopes and steps.
AI.motivation: Excessive joint torque risks mechanical failure, especially for detachable joints, making reliability a critical concern beyond mere mobility.
AI.method: Apply NSGA-III to derive Pareto-optimal gait solutions that trade off joint load against locomotion speed and stability; validate through simulations and physical experiments across varied terrains.
AI.result: Pareto-optimal gait options achieve reduced joint loads without sacrificing required speed/stability; effective on environments such as slopes and steps as shown by simulations and experiments.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides reliable, structurally safe gait solutions for modular robots under diverse conditions, preserving mobility while mitigating joint overload; the approach is adaptable to different morphologies and environments.

title: Visioning Human-Agentic AI Teaming: Continuity, Tension, and Future Research
summary: Artificial intelligence is undergoing a structural transformation marked by the rise of agentic systems capable of open-ended action trajectories, generative representations and outputs, and evolving objectives. These properties introduce structural uncertainty into human-AI teaming (HAT), including uncertainty about behavior trajectories, epistemic grounding, and the stability of governing logics over time. Under such conditions, alignment cannot be secured through agreement on bounded outputs; it must be continuously sustained as plans unfold and priorities shift. We advance Team Situation Awareness (Team SA) theory, grounded in shared perception, comprehension, and projection, as an integrative anchor for this transition. While Team SA remains analytically foundational, its stabilizing logic presumes that shared awareness, once achieved, will support coordinated action through iterative updating. Agentic AI challenges this presumption. Our argument unfolds in two stages: first, we extend Team SA to reconceptualize both human and AI awareness under open-ended agency, including the sensemaking of projection congruence across heterogeneous systems. Second, we interrogate whether the dynamic processes traditionally assumed to stabilize teaming in relational interaction, cognitive learning, and coordination and control continue to function under adaptive autonomy. By distinguishing continuity from tension, we clarify where foundational insights hold and where structural uncertainty introduces strain, and articulate a forward-looking research agenda for HAT. The central challenge of HAT is not whether humans and AI can agree in the moment, but whether they can remain aligned as futures are continuously generated, revised, enacted, and governed over time.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that AI with open-ended agency requires continuous, evolving alignment in human-AI teaming (HAT). It extends Team Situation Awareness (Team SA) to account for agentic AI, analyzes how traditional stabilization processes may fail under adaptive autonomy, and outlines a forward-looking research agenda for sustaining alignment over time.
AI.motivation: Structural uncertainty arises from agentic AI's open-ended action trajectories, generative representations, and evolving objectives, which undermine fixed-output alignment and require ongoing alignment as plans unfold and priorities shift.
AI.method: Theoretical extension and conceptual analysis: (1) reconceptualize both human and AI awareness under open-ended agency and sensemaking of projection congruence across heterogeneous systems; (2) evaluate whether established dynamic processes (relational interaction, cognitive learning, coordination and control) continue to stabilize teaming under adaptive autonomy; differentiate continuity from tension; propose a forward-looking HAT research agenda.
AI.result: Clarifies the scope of Team SA insights under structural uncertainty, identifies where the traditional stabilizing logic holds and where strain arises, and articulates conditions and research directions needed to maintain alignment among humans and agentic AI over time.
AI.conclusion: The central challenge shifts from moment-to-moment agreement to sustaining alignment as futures are continually generated, revised, enacted, and governed, necessitating ongoing, adaptive coordination between humans and AI.

title: Towards Explainable Deep Learning for Ship Trajectory Prediction in Inland Waterways
comment: This is a preprint of a paper published in the Proceedings of the 35th European Safety and Reliability & the 33rd Society for Risk Analysis Europe Conference. DOI of the published version: 10.3850/978-981-94-3281-3_ESREL-SRA-E2025-P1370-cd. Reproduced here with permission of the publisher. For citation purposes, please refer exclusively to the published version
summary: Accurate predictions of ship trajectories in crowded environments are essential to ensure safety in inland waterways traffic. Recent advances in deep learning promise increased accuracy even for complex scenarios. While the challenge of ship-to-ship awareness is being addressed with growing success, the explainability of these models is often overlooked, potentially obscuring an inaccurate logic and undermining the confidence in their reliability. This study examines an LSTM-based vessel trajectory prediction model by incorporating trained ship domain parameters that provide insight into the attention-based fusion of the interacting vessels' hidden states. This approach has previously been explored in the field of maritime shipping, yet the variety and complexity of encounters in inland waterways allow for a more profound analysis of the model's interpretability. The prediction performance of the proposed model variants are evaluated using standard displacement error statistics. Additionally, the plausibility of the generated ship domain values is analyzed. With an final displacement error of around 40 meters in a 5-minute prediction horizon, the model performs comparably to similar studies. Though the ship-to-ship attention architecture enhances prediction accuracy, the weights assigned to vessels in encounters using the learnt ship domain values deviate from the expectation. The observed accuracy improvements are thus not entirely driven by a causal relationship between a predicted trajectory and the trajectories of nearby ships. This finding underscores the model's explanatory capabilities through its intrinsically interpretable design. Future work will focus on utilizing the architecture for counterfactual analysis and on the incorporation of more sophisticated attention mechanisms.

AI.tldr: LSTM-based vessel trajectory predictor with interpretable ship-domain attention; ~40 m final displacement error at 5 min; interpretability via learned ship-domain values shows accuracy gains are not fully causal; future work includes counterfactual analysis and advanced attention.
AI.motivation: Address safety in inland waterway traffic and the need for explainable deep learning models in ship-to-ship awareness, particularly under complex encounters.
AI.method: An LSTM-based trajectory model with attention-based fusion over interacting vessels' hidden states, augmented by trained ship-domain parameters that provide insight into the attention mechanism; evaluation uses standard displacement error metrics and plausibility checks for ship-domain values.
AI.result: Prediction achieves around 40 m final displacement error in a 5-minute horizon, comparable to similar studies. The ship-domain–based attention improves accuracy, but the learned weights are not fully aligned with expectations, indicating that accuracy gains are not solely driven by a direct causal link between predicted and neighboring trajectories. The architecture offers explanatory capabilities due to its interpretable design.
AI.conclusion: The approach enhances interpretability in maritime trajectory prediction and reveals non-causal factors in performance gains; suggests expanding to counterfactual analysis and exploring more sophisticated attention mechanisms in future work.

title: Evaluating and Correcting Human Annotation Bias in Dynamic Micro-Expression Recognition
summary: Existing manual labeling of micro-expressions is subject to errors in accuracy, especially in cross-cultural scenarios where deviation in labeling of key frames is more prominent. To address this issue, this paper presents a novel Global Anti-Monotonic Differential Selection Strategy (GAMDSS) architecture for enhancing the effectiveness of spatio-temporal modeling of micro-expressions through keyframe re-selection. Specifically, the method identifies Onset and Apex frames, which are characterized by significant micro-expression variation, from complete micro-expression action sequences via a dynamic frame reselection mechanism. It then uses these to determine Offset frames and construct a rich spatio-temporal dynamic representation. A two-branch structure with shared parameters is then used to efficiently extract spatio-temporal features. Extensive experiments are conducted on seven widely recognized micro-expression datasets. The results demonstrate that GAMDSS effectively reduces subjective errors caused by human factors in multicultural datasets such as SAMM and 4DME. Furthermore, quantitative analyses confirm that offset-frame annotations in multicultural datasets are more uncertain, providing theoretical justification for standardizing micro-expression annotations. These findings directly support our argument for reconsidering the validity and generalizability of dataset annotation paradigms. Notably, this design can be integrated into existing models without increasing the number of parameters, offering a new approach to enhancing micro-expression recognition performance. The source code is available on GitHub[https://github.com/Cross-Innovation-Lab/GAMDSS].

AI.tldr: GAMDSS reselects onset/apex frames in micro-expression sequences to form a richer spatio-temporal representation, enabling cross-cultural labeling robustness without adding parameters; validated on seven datasets; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Manual labeling of micro-expressions is error-prone, especially across cultures, with higher variability in key-frame labeling and uncertain offset frames. A robust automatic keyframe selection and annotation standardization could improve generalizability and reduce human-induced bias.
AI.method: Identify onset and apex frames from the full sequence via dynamic frame reselection (GAMDSS). Use these to determine offset frames and build a rich spatio-temporal representation. Employ a two-branch network with shared parameters to efficiently extract spatio-temporal features. Integrate into existing models without increasing parameter count.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven micro-expression datasets show that GAMDSS reduces subjective labeling errors in multicultural datasets (e.g., SAMM, 4DME). Offset-frame annotations are more uncertain in multicultural data, supporting the call for standardizing annotations. The approach can improve recognition performance without additional parameters; source code available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: GAMDSS offers a new, plug-in-friendly approach to enhancing micro-expression recognition and justifies reconsidering dataset annotation paradigms, particularly across cultures, with an emphasis on robust keyframe-based spatio-temporal modeling.

title: Designing and Validating a Self-Aligning Tool Changer for Modular Reconfigurable Manipulation Robots
summary: Modular reconfigurable robots require reliable mechanisms for automated module exchange, but conventional rigid active couplings often fail due to inevitable positioning and orientational errors. To address this, we propose a misalignment-tolerant tool-changing system. The hardware features a motor-driven coupling utilizing passive self-alignment geometries, specifically chamfered receptacles and triangular lead-in guides, to robustly compensate for angular and lateral misalignments without complex force sensors. To make this autonomous exchange practically feasible, the mechanism is complemented by a compact rotating tool exchange station for efficient module storage. Real-world autonomous tool-picking experiments validate that the self-aligning features successfully absorb execution errors, enabling highly reliable robotic tool reconfiguration.

AI.tldr: A misalignment-tolerant tool-changing system for modular robots using passive self-alignment geometries and a compact rotating tool exchange station enables autonomous, reliable tool reconfiguration without heavy sensing.
AI.motivation: Conventional rigid active couplings are prone to failure due to inevitable positioning and orientational errors during module exchange in modular robots; a tolerant mechanism is needed for reliability and autonomy.
AI.method: Motor-driven coupling coupled with passive self-alignment features (chamfered receptacles and triangular lead-in guides) to compensate angular and lateral misalignments; a compact rotating tool exchange station provides efficient module storage; validation via real-world autonomous tool-picking experiments.
AI.result: The self-aligning geometries absorb execution errors, enabling highly reliable robotic tool reconfiguration in autonomous operation without complex force sensors.
AI.conclusion: The misalignment-tolerant design yields robust, sensor-light tool exchange for modular robots, enhancing autonomy and reliability of reconfiguration tasks.

title: HiMAP-Travel: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Planning for Long-Horizon Constrained Travel
summary: Sequential LLM agents fail on long-horizon planning with hard constraints like budgets and diversity requirements. As planning progresses and context grows, these agents drift from global constraints. We propose HiMAP-Travel, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that splits planning into strategic coordination and parallel day-level execution. A Coordinator allocates resources across days, while Day Executors plan independently in parallel. Three key mechanisms enable this: a transactional monitor enforcing budget and uniqueness constraints across parallel agents, a bargaining protocol allowing agents to reject infeasible sub-goals and trigger re-planning, and a single policy trained with GRPO that powers all agents through role conditioning. On TravelPlanner, HiMAP-Travel with Qwen3-8B achieves 52.78% validation and 52.65% test Final Pass Rate (FPR). In a controlled comparison with identical model, training, and tools, it outperforms the sequential DeepTravel baseline by +8.67~pp. It also surpasses ATLAS by +17.65~pp and MTP by +10.0~pp. On FlexTravelBench multi-turn scenarios, it achieves 44.34% (2-turn) and 37.42% (3-turn) FPR while reducing latency 2.5x through parallelization.

AI.tldr: HiMAP-Travel: a hierarchical, constraint-aware multi-agent planner with a central coordinator and parallel day-executors, enforcing budgets/diversity via a transactional monitor and bargaining, powered by a single GRPO-trained policy. Demonstrates improved final-pass rate over baselines and 2.5x latency reduction through parallelization.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon planning with hard constraints (budgets, diversity) suffers from drift as context grows; a scalable coordination mechanism is needed to enforce global constraints without sacrificing parallelism.
AI.method: Split planning into a Coordinator (global allocation) and Day Executors (parallel, day-level planning). Key mechanisms: (1) transactional monitor enforcing budgets/uniqueness across agents, (2) bargaining protocol for infeasible sub-goals triggering re-planning, (3) a single policy trained with GRPO (role-conditioned) powering all agents.
AI.result: On TravelPlanner with Qwen3-8B, achieves 52.78% validation and 52.65% test Final Pass Rate (FPR). In identical-model comparisons, outperforms DeepTravel by +8.67 pp, ATLAS by +17.65 pp, and MTP by +10.0 pp. On FlexTravelBench (2-turn, 3-turn), reaches 44.34% and 37.42% FPR, while reducing latency by 2.5x due to parallelization.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical planning with constraint-aware coordination and a unified policy improves long-horizon, constraint-bound planning, delivering stronger performance than baselines and enabling substantial speedups via parallel execution.

title: Activity Recognition from Smart Insole Sensor Data Using a Circular Dilated CNN
summary: Smart insoles equipped with pressure sensors, accelerometers, and gyroscopes offer a non-intrusive means of monitoring human gait and posture. We present an activity classification system based on a circular dilated convolutional neural network (CDCNN) that processes multi-modal time-series data from such insoles. The model operates on 160-frame windows with 24 channels (18 pressure, 3 accelerometer, 3 gyroscope axes), achieving 86.42% test accuracy in a subject-independent evaluation on a four-class task (Standing, Walking, Sitting, Tandem), compared with 87.83% for an extreme gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost) model trained on flattened data. Permutation feature importance reveals that inertial sensors (accelerometer and gyroscope) contribute substantially to discrimination. The approach is suitable for embedded deployment and real-time inference.

AI.tldr: A circular dilated CNN (CDCNN) on multi-modal insole data classifies gait/posture in a subject-independent four-class task with performance close to XGBoost, highlighting inertial sensors as key discriminators and suitability for embedded real-time deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable non-intrusive, on-device gait and posture recognition from insole sensor data, leveraging efficient deep learning architectures to operate on time-series data for real-time inference.
AI.method: Use a circular dilated convolutional neural network (CDCNN) to process 160-frame windows of 24-channel time-series data (18 pressure, 3 accelerometer, 3 gyroscope). Evaluate in a subject-independent setting on a four-class task (Standing, Walking, Sitting, Tandem). Compare to an XGBoost baseline trained on flattened features and analyze feature importance via permutation. Assess suitability for embedded deployment and real-time inference.
AI.result: CDCNN achieves 86.42% test accuracy in the four-class, subject-independent evaluation. XGBoost baseline on flattened features achieves 87.83% accuracy, slightly higher. Permutation importance indicates inertial sensors (accelerometer and gyroscope) contribute substantially to discrimination.
AI.conclusion: A CDCNN-based approach on multi-modal insole data provides a competitive, near-parity performance with a strong, energy-efficient profile for embedded real-time deployment, with inertial sensors driving most discriminative power; windowed time-series processing is effective for four-class gait/posture classification.

title: DSA-SRGS: Super-Resolution Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Sparse-View DSA Reconstruction
summary: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is a key imaging technique for the auxiliary diagnosis and treatment of cerebrovascular diseases. Recent advancements in gaussian splatting and dynamic neural representations have enabled robust 3D vessel reconstruction from sparse dynamic inputs. However, these methods are fundamentally constrained by the resolution of input projections, where performing naive upsampling to enhance rendering resolution inevitably results in severe blurring and aliasing artifacts. Such lack of super-resolution capability prevents the reconstructed 4D models from recovering fine-grained vascular details and intricate branching structures, which restricts their application in precision diagnosis and treatment. To solve this problem, this paper proposes DSA-SRGS, the first super-resolution gaussian splatting framework for dynamic sparse-view DSA reconstruction. Specifically, we introduce a Multi-Fidelity Texture Learning Module that integrates high-quality priors from a fine-tuned DSA-specific super-resolution model, into the 4D reconstruction optimization. To mitigate potential hallucination artifacts from pseudo-labels, this module employs a Confidence-Aware Strategy to adaptively weight supervision signals between the original low-resolution projections and the generated high-resolution pseudo-labels. Furthermore, we develop Radiative Sub-Pixel Densification, an adaptive strategy that leverages gradient accumulation from high-resolution sub-pixel sampling to refine the 4D radiative gaussian kernels. Extensive experiments on two clinical DSA datasets demonstrate that DSA-SRGS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: DSA-SRGS enables high-fidelity 4D vascular reconstructions from sparse DSA projections by introducing a super-resolution gaussian splatting framework with multi-fidelity texture priors, confidence-aware supervision, and radiative sub-pixel densification.
AI.motivation: Dynamic neural rendering for DSA is hampered by the limited resolution of input projections; naive upsampling creates blur/aliasing and fails to recover fine vascular details and branching, limiting precision diagnosis and treatment.
AI.method: DSA-SRGS comprises three core components: (1) Multi-Fidelity Texture Learning Module that injects high-quality priors from a fine-tuned DSA-specific super-resolution model into the 4D reconstruction optimization; (2) Confidence-Aware Strategy to adaptively weight supervision signals between the original low-resolution projections and high-resolution pseudo-labels to suppress hallucinations; (3) Radiative Sub-Pixel Densification that uses gradient accumulation from high-resolution sub-pixel sampling to refine the 4D radiative gaussian kernels in the sparse-view setting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two clinical DSA datasets show significant improvements over state-of-the-art in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visual fidelity for 4D vascular reconstructions from sparse inputs.
AI.conclusion: DSA-SRGS introduces a first-of-its-kind super-resolution Gaussian splatting framework for dynamic sparse-view DSA reconstruction, delivering higher-resolution, more accurate vascular detail and enabling improved diagnostic/treatment workflows.

title: Adaptive Policy Switching of Two-Wheeled Differential Robots for Traversing over Diverse Terrains
comment: Author's version of the paper presented at AROB-ISBC 2026
summary: Exploring lunar lava tubes requires robots to traverse without human intervention. Because pre-trained policies cannot fully cover all possible terrain conditions, our goal is to enable adaptive policy switching, where the robot selects an appropriate terrain-specialized model based on its current terrain features. This study investigates whether terrain types can be estimated effectively using posture-related observations collected during navigation. We fine-tuned a pre-trained policy using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), and then collected the robot's 3D orientation data as it moved across flat and rough terrain in a simulated lava-tube environment. Our analysis revealed that the standard deviation of the robot's pitch data shows a clear difference between these two terrain types. Using Gaussian mixture models (GMM), we evaluated terrain classification across various window sizes. An accuracy of more than 98% was achieved when using a 70-step window. The result suggests that short-term orientation data are sufficient for reliable terrain estimation, providing a foundation for adaptive policy switching.

AI.tldr: A method to estimate terrain type for adaptive policy switching in lunar lava-tube exploration using short-term posture data; Gaussian Mixture Models classify terrain with high accuracy (>98%) on a 70-step window in simulation.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained policies cannot cover all terrain variations; enabling adaptive switching based on onboard terrain estimation improves autonomous rover performance in unknown lava-tube environments.
AI.method: Fine-tune a policy with PPO; collect robot 3D orientation data (pitch) during navigation on flat and rough terrain in simulation; analyze pitch Standard Deviation (STD); classify terrain with Gaussian Mixture Models across different window sizes; determine optimal window size for accuracy.
AI.result: Pitch STD differs between flat and rough terrains; GMM-based terrain classification achieves high accuracy, with >98% accuracy at a 70-step window; short-term orientation data suffice for reliable terrain estimation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of real-time, terrain-aware adaptive policy switching using brief orientation signals, enabling autonomous exploration of lunar lava tubes without manual intervention.

title: Evaluating the Search Agent in a Parallel World
summary: Integrating web search tools has significantly extended the capability of LLMs to address open-world, real-time, and long-tail problems. However, evaluating these Search Agents presents formidable challenges. First, constructing high-quality deep search benchmarks is prohibitively expensive, while unverified synthetic data often suffers from unreliable sources. Second, static benchmarks face dynamic obsolescence: as internet information evolves, complex queries requiring deep research often degrade into simple retrieval tasks due to increased popularity, and ground truths become outdated due to temporal shifts. Third, attribution ambiguity confounds evaluation, as an agent's performance is often dominated by its parametric memory rather than its actual search and reasoning capabilities. Finally, reliance on specific commercial search engines introduces variability that hampers reproducibility. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Mind-ParaWorld, for evaluating Search Agents in a Parallel World. Specifically, MPW samples real-world entity names to synthesize future scenarios and questions situated beyond the model's knowledge cutoff. A ParaWorld Law Model then constructs a set of indivisible Atomic Facts and a unique ground-truth for each question. During evaluation, instead of retrieving real-world results, the agent interacts with a ParaWorld Engine Model that dynamically generates SERPs grounded in these inviolable Atomic Facts. We release MPW-Bench, an interactive benchmark spanning 19 domains with 1,608 instances. Experiments across three evaluation settings show that, while search agents are strong at evidence synthesis given complete information, their performance is limited not only by evidence collection and coverage in unfamiliar search environments, but also by unreliable evidence sufficiency judgment and when-to-stop decisions-bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: A framework (Mind-ParaWorld) to evaluate Search Agents in a parallel, synthetic 'ParaWorld' to overcome data collection, obsolescence, attribution, and reproducibility issues; MPW-Bench provides 1,608 instances across 19 domains; findings show strong evidence synthesis with complete info but bottlenecks in evidence collection, sufficiency judgment, and stop decisions.
AI.motivation: To address the core evaluation challenges of web-enabled agents: (1) costly, high-quality benchmarks; (2) unreliable synthetic data and data drift; (3) attribution confusion between tool use and parametric memory; (4) reproducibility issues due to dependence on commercial search engines.
AI.method: Propose Mind-ParaWorld (MPW): sample real-world entity names to create future, knowledge-gap scenarios; ParaWorld Law Model defines a set of indivisible Atomic Facts and a unique ground-truth for each question; during evaluation, a ParaWorld Engine Model generates SERPs grounded in these Atomic Facts instead of real web results; MPW-Bench spans 19 domains with 1,608 instances; evaluation across three settings.
AI.result: MPW enables controlled, reproducible evaluation of Search Agents. Experiments show agents excel at evidence synthesis with complete information but struggle with evidence collection and coverage in unfamiliar environments, unreliable evidence sufficiency judgments, and when-to-stop decisions—highlighting key bottlenecks and directions for improvement.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reproducible benchmark framework to evaluate Search Agents in a controlled parallel world, revealing fundamental bottlenecks in evidence gathering, judgment, and stopping policies, and guiding future methodological and agent design refinements.

title: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Rethinking EEG Foundation Model Pretraining via Multi-Teacher Distillation
summary: Pretraining for electroencephalogram (EEG) foundation models has predominantly relied on self-supervised masked reconstruction, a paradigm largely adapted from and inspired by the success of vision and language foundation models. However, unlike images and text, EEG datasets are notoriously expensive to collect and characterized by low signal-to-noise ratio. These challenges introduce difficulties in scaling the EEG foundation models and capturing the underlying neural semantics through reconstruction. In this work, we ask the question: can we stand on the shoulders of well-established foundation models from well-represented modalities to bootstrap the pretraining of EEG foundation models? We first demonstrate that mainstream foundation models, such as those from vision and time series, transfer surprisingly well to EEG domain. To this end, we propose the Multi-Teacher Distillation Pretraining (MTDP) framework for pretraining EEG foundation models via a two-stage multi-teacher distillation. In the first stage, we introduce a learnable gating network to fuse representations from diverse teachers (e.g., DINOv3 and Chronos) via a masked latent denoising objective. In the second stage, we distill the fused representation into an EEG foundation model. Extensive evaluations across 9 downstream tasks and 12 datasets demonstrate that our MTDP-based EEG foundation model outperforms its self-supervised counterparts while requiring only 25% of the pretraining data.

AI.tldr: A two-stage multi-teacher distillation pretraining (MTDP) enables EEG foundation models to leverage vision/time-series models, achieving strong performance with only 25% of the data.
AI.motivation: EEG datasets are expensive to collect and have low signal-to-noise, making it hard to scale EEG foundation models with self-supervised reconstruction. Transferring established foundation models from well-represented modalities can bootstrap EEG pretraining.
AI.method: Two-stage distillation: Stage 1 uses a learnable gating network to fuse representations from diverse teachers (e.g., DINOv3, Chronos) via a masked latent denoising objective. Stage 2 distills the fused representation into an EEG foundation model.
AI.result: MTDP-based EEG foundation model outperforms self-supervised counterparts across 9 downstream tasks and 12 datasets, while requiring only 25% of the pretraining data.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal pretraining with multi-teacher distillation is an effective, data-efficient approach for EEG foundation models, enabling transfer from vision/time-series models and reducing pretraining data needs.

title: MADCrowner: Margin Aware Dental Crown Design with Template Deformation and Refinement
summary: Dental crown restoration is one of the most common treatment modalities for tooth defect, where personalized dental crown design is critical. While computer-aided design (CAD) systems have notably enhanced the efficiency of dental crown design, extensive manual adjustments are still required in the clinic workflow. Recent studies have explored the application of learning-based methods for the automated generation of restorative dental crowns. Nevertheless, these approaches were challenged by inadequate spatial resolution, noisy outputs, and overextension of surface reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose \totalframework, a margin-aware mesh generation framework comprising CrownDeformR and CrownSegger. Inspired by the clinic manual workflow of dental crown design, we designed CrownDeformR to deform an initial template to the target crown based on anatomical context, which is extracted by a multi-scale intraoral scan encoder. Additionally, we introduced \marginseg, a novel margin segmentation network, to extract the cervical margin of the target tooth. The performance of CrownDeformR improved with the cervical margin as an extra constraint. And it was also utilized as the boundary condition for the tailored postprocessing method, which removed the overextended area of the reconstructed surface. We constructed a large-scale intraoral scan dataset and performed extensive experiments. The proposed method significantly outperformed existing approaches in both geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility.

AI.tldr: A margin-aware mesh generation pipeline (TotalFramework) for automated dental crown design, integrating CrownDeformR and CrownSegger. It uses a multi-scale intraoral scan encoder to deform a template to the target crown with cervical margin guidance, and a margin segmentation network to extract the cervical margin; margin constraints improve deformation and postprocessing suppresses overextension. Evaluated on a large intraoral scan dataset, it outperforms prior methods in geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility.
AI.motivation: Automate dental crown design to reduce manual adjustments in CAD workflows while addressing limitations of previous learning-based approaches, such as low spatial resolution, noisy outputs, and surface overextension.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: CrownDeformR deforms an initial crown template to the target using anatomical context from a multi-scale intraoral scan encoder; CrownSegger segments the cervical margin; margins serve as constraints and boundary conditions for a postprocessing step to remove overextended regions; large dataset and extensive experiments.
AI.result: Significant improvements over existing methods in geometric accuracy and clinical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Margin-aware deformation plus explicit cervical margin segmentation yields more accurate, clinically feasible crowns, enabling better integration into practice; future work could explore generalization, real-time performance, and broader clinical validation.

title: LLM-Guided Decentralized Exploration with Self-Organizing Robot Teams
comment: Author's version of the paper presented at AROB-ISBC 2026
