We evaluated our method on multiple standard benchmarks, where it consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art semi-dense matching models in both accuracy and efficiency. our code will be available on https://github.com/LSC-ALAN/SURE.

AI.tldr: SURE is a semi-dense matching framework that jointly predicts correspondences and their confidence by modeling both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, using an evidential head and a lightweight spatial fusion module; it achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reliable image correspondences are difficult under large viewpoint changes and textureless regions. Traditional feature-similarity approaches lack explicit uncertainty estimation, leading to overconfident, incorrect matches.
AI.method: Introduces an evidential head for trustworthy coordinate regression and a lightweight spatial fusion module to refine local features. The framework jointly predicts coordinates and confidence by modeling aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties in a semi-dense setting.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms existing state-of-the-art semi-dense matching methods on multiple standard benchmarks in both accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware semi-dense matching improves the reliability of correspondences; SURE provides trustworthy coordinate regression with minimal overhead, and code will be released.

title: SPIRIT: Perceptive Shared Autonomy for Robust Robotic Manipulation under Deep Learning Uncertainty
summary: Deep learning (DL) has enabled impressive advances in robotic perception, yet its limited robustness and lack of interpretability hinder reliable deployment in safety critical applications. We propose a concept termed perceptive shared autonomy, in which uncertainty estimates from DL based perception are used to regulate the level of autonomy. Specifically, when the robot's perception is confident, semi-autonomous manipulation is enabled to improve performance; when uncertainty increases, control transitions to haptic teleoperation for maintaining robustness. In this way, high-performing but uninterpretable DL methods can be integrated safely into robotic systems. A key technical enabler is an uncertainty aware DL based point cloud registration approach based on the so called Neural Tangent Kernels (NTK). We evaluate perceptive shared autonomy on challenging aerial manipulation tasks through a user study of 15 participants and realization of mock-up industrial scenarios, demonstrating reliable robotic manipulation despite failures in DL based perception. The resulting system, named SPIRIT, improves both manipulation performance and system reliability. SPIRIT was selected as a finalist of a major industrial innovation award.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-guided perceptive shared autonomy (SPIRIT) integrates high-performing DL perception with safety via autonomy switching, using Neural Tangent Kernel–based uncertainty in point-cloud registration; validated in user studies for aerial manipulation; shows improved reliability and performance with DL failures.
AI.motivation: Address the brittleness and lack of interpretability of DL in safety-critical robotics by using uncertainty estimates to regulate autonomy, enabling high performance when confident and safer control when uncertain.
AI.method: Introduce an uncertainty-aware DL-based point-cloud registration approach leveraging Neural Tangent Kernels (NTK). Implement perceptive shared autonomy that switches between semi-autonomous manipulation and haptic teleoperation based on perception confidence. Evaluate on challenging aerial manipulation tasks through a user study (N=15) and mock-up industrial scenarios.
AI.result: SPIRIT-enabled systems achieved reliable manipulation despite DL perception failures, improved manipulation performance and overall system reliability, and SPIRIT was a finalist for a major industrial innovation award.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-guided autonomy can safely harness high-performing DL perception in robotics by regulating autonomy levels. NTK-based uncertainty estimation supports robust registration, with practical validation in user studies; further real-world validation and scaling are warranted.

title: EvoTool: Self-Evolving Tool-Use Policy Optimization in LLM Agents via Blame-Aware Mutation and Diversity-Aware Selection
summary: LLM-based agents depend on effective tool-use policies to solve complex tasks, yet optimizing these policies remains challenging due to delayed supervision and the difficulty of credit assignment in long-horizon trajectories. Existing optimization approaches tend to be either monolithic, which are prone to entangling behaviors, or single-aspect, which ignore cross-module error propagation. To address these limitations, we propose EvoTool, a self-evolving framework that optimizes a modular tool-use policy via a gradient-free evolutionary paradigm. EvoTool decomposes agent's tool-use policy into four modules, including Planner, Selector, Caller, and Synthesizer, and iteratively improves them in a self-improving loop through three novel mechanisms. Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution uses diagnostic traces to localize failures to a specific module. Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation then edits only that module via natural-language critique. Diversity-Aware Population Selection preserves complementary candidates to ensure solution diversity. Across four benchmarks, EvoTool outperforms strong baselines by over 5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B, while achieving superior efficiency and transferability. The code will be released once paper is accepted.

AI.tldr: EvoTool is a gradient-free, self-evolving framework that optimizes a four-module tool-use policy for LLM agents by way of trajectory-based blame, targeted language-guided mutations, and diversity-preserving evolution, yielding >5-point gains across four benchmarks and two model families.
AI.motivation: Token-limited, long-horizon LLM tool-use policies suffer from delayed supervision and credit assignment; monolithic or single-aspect optimization entangles or ignores cross-module error propagation.
AI.method: Decompose policy into Planner, Selector, Caller, Synthesizer; apply iterative evolutionary loop with Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution, Feedback-Guided Targeted Mutation, and Diversity-Aware Population Selection; gradient-free optimization across modular policies; evaluation on benchmarks with GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines by >5 points on both GPT-4.1 and Qwen3-8B across four benchmarks; improved efficiency and transferability; code to be released after acceptance.
AI.conclusion: EvoTool demonstrates effective self-evolving modular tool-use optimization, enabling better performance and transferability, with a practical pipeline for targeted modular amendments via natural-language feedback.

title: Probabilistic Dreaming for World Models
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026: 2nd Workshop on World Models
summary: "Dreaming" enables agents to learn from imagined experiences, enabling more robust and sample-efficient learning of world models. In this work, we consider innovations to the state-of-the-art Dreamer model using probabilistic methods that enable: (1) the parallel exploration of many latent states; and (2) maintaining distinct hypotheses for mutually exclusive futures while retaining the desirable gradient properties of continuous latents. Evaluating on the MPE SimpleTag domain, our method outperforms standard Dreamer with a 4.5% score improvement and 28% lower variance in episode returns. We also discuss limitations and directions for future work, including how optimal hyperparameters (e.g. particle count K) scale with environmental complexity, and methods to capture epistemic uncertainty in world models.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic extensions to Dreamer that enable parallel latent-state exploration and multiple-futures hypotheses; yields 4.5% score improvement and 28% lower return variance on MPE SimpleTag; discusses hyperparameter scaling (e.g., particle count K) and epistemic uncertainty capture for future work.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of single latent trajectories in Dreamer by enabling multiple latent hypotheses and parallel exploration to improve sample efficiency and robustness of world models.
AI.method: Introduce probabilistic latent ensembles/particle-based approaches to Dreamer that enable parallel exploration of many latent states and maintain distinct hypotheses for mutually exclusive futures, while preserving the gradient properties of continuous latents (e.g., via a differentiable particle framework). Considerations include particle count K.
AI.result: On the MPE SimpleTag domain, the proposed method achieves a 4.5% score improvement and 28% lower variance in episode returns compared with standard Dreamer.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves performance and robustness by modeling epistemic uncertainty and multiple futures; however, its effectiveness may depend on hyperparameters like particle count K, and further work is needed to scale K with environmental complexity and to develop better mechanisms for capturing epistemic uncertainty in world models.

title: Diffusion-Based sRGB Real Noise Generation via Prompt-Driven Noise Representation Learning
summary: Denoising in the sRGB image space is challenging due to noise variability. Although end-to-end methods perform well, their effectiveness in real-world scenarios is limited by the scarcity of real noisy-clean image pairs, which are expensive and difficult to collect. To address this limitation, several generative methods have been developed to synthesize realistic noisy images from limited data. These generative approaches often rely on camera metadata during both training and testing to synthesize real-world noise. However, the lack of metadata or inconsistencies between devices restricts their usability. Therefore, we propose a novel framework called Prompt-Driven Noise Generation (PNG). This model is capable of acquiring high-dimensional prompt features that capture the characteristics of real-world input noise and creating a variety of realistic noisy images consistent with the distribution of the input noise. By eliminating the dependency on explicit camera metadata, our approach significantly enhances the generalizability and applicability of noise synthesis. Comprehensive experiments reveal that our model effectively produces realistic noisy images and show the successful application of these generated images in removing real-world noise across various benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: Promotes Prompt-Driven Noise Generation (PNG) to synthesize realistic real-world noise without camera metadata for denoising in sRGB.
AI.motivation: Real-world denoising is hampered by scarce real noisy-clean pairs and the reliance on camera metadata, which is often unavailable or inconsistent across devices.
AI.method: Introduces high-dimensional prompt features that capture noise characteristics and generate diverse, realistic noisy images aligned with input noise distribution without using explicit camera metadata.
AI.result: Demonstrates realistic noise generation and effective denoising performance improvements across benchmark datasets using the synthesized data.
AI.conclusion: PNG is a promising, metadata-free framework that enhances the generalizability of noise synthesis and broadens the applicability of learned denoisers.

title: SeedPolicy: Horizon Scaling via Self-Evolving Diffusion Policy for Robot Manipulation
summary: Imitation Learning (IL) enables robots to acquire manipulation skills from expert demonstrations. Diffusion Policy (DP) models multi-modal expert behaviors but suffers performance degradation as observation horizons increase, limiting long-horizon manipulation. We propose Self-Evolving Gated Attention (SEGA), a temporal module that maintains a time-evolving latent state via gated attention, enabling efficient recurrent updates that compress long-horizon observations into a fixed-size representation while filtering irrelevant temporal information. Integrating SEGA into DP yields Self-Evolving Diffusion Policy (SeedPolicy), which resolves the temporal modeling bottleneck and enables scalable horizon extension with moderate overhead. On the RoboTwin 2.0 benchmark with 50 manipulation tasks, SeedPolicy outperforms DP and other IL baselines. Averaged across both CNN and Transformer backbones, SeedPolicy achieves 36.8% relative improvement in clean settings and 169% relative improvement in randomized challenging settings over the DP. Compared to vision-language-action models such as RDT with 1.2B parameters, SeedPolicy achieves competitive performance with one to two orders of magnitude fewer parameters, demonstrating strong efficiency and scalability. These results establish SeedPolicy as a state-of-the-art imitation learning method for long-horizon robotic manipulation. Code is available at: https://github.com/Youqiang-Gui/SeedPolicy.

AI.tldr: SEGA introduces a temporal gating mechanism that maintains a time-evolving latent state to compress long-horizon observations in diffusion-policy imitation learning. Integrated as SeedPolicy, it enables scalable horizon extension with reduced computational and parameter overhead, achieving state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon robotic manipulation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome temporal modeling bottlenecks and horizon limitations in diffusion-policy imitation learning, enabling robust long-horizon manipulation without excessive parameter growth.
AI.method: Propose Self-Evolving Gated Attention (SEGA) to maintain a time-evolving latent state via gated attention, enabling efficient recurrent updates and compression of long sequences into fixed-size representations. Integrate SEGA into a diffusion-policy framework to produce SeedPolicy and evaluate on RoboTwin 2.0 (50 tasks) across CNN and Transformer backbones, comparing with DP and other IL baselines.
AI.result: SeedPolicy outperforms DP and other IL baselines on RoboTwin 2.0. Averaged across CNN/Transformer backbones, it yields 36.8% relative improvement in clean settings and 169% in randomized challenging settings over DP. It achieves competitive performance with RDT (1.2B parameters) while using one to two orders of magnitude fewer parameters, demonstrating strong efficiency and scalability.
AI.conclusion: SeedPolicy represents a scalable, efficient state-of-the-art method for long-horizon imitation learning in robotic manipulation, addressing temporal bottlenecks and enabling horizon extension with moderate overhead; code is publicly available.

title: Alignment Backfire: Language-Dependent Reversal of Safety Interventions Across 16 Languages in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
comment: 89 pages, 4 figures, 4 supplementary figures, 12 supplementary tables; preprint
summary: In perpetrator treatment, a recurring observation is the dissociation between insight and action: offenders articulate remorse yet behavioral change does not follow. We report four preregistered studies (1,584 multi-agent simulations across 16 languages and three model families) demonstrating that alignment interventions in large language models produce a structurally analogous phenomenon: surface safety that masks or generates collective pathology and internal dissociation. In Study 1 (N = 150), increasing alignment-instructed agents reduced collective pathology in English (g = -1.844, p < .0001) but amplified it in Japanese (g = +0.771, p = .038)--a directional reversal we term "alignment backfire." Study 2 (N = 1,174) extended to 16 languages: alignment-induced dissociation was near-universal (15/16 languages; beta = 0.0667, p < .0001), while collective pathology bifurcated along cultural-linguistic lines (interaction beta = 0.0684, p = .0003), correlating with Power Distance Index (r = 0.474, p = .064). Study 3 (N = 180) tested individuation as countermeasure; individuated agents became the primary source of both pathology and dissociation (DI = +1.120) with conformity above 84%--demonstrating iatrogenesis. Study 4 (N = 80) validated patterns across Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini, and Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, confirming English safety is model-general while Japanese backfire is model-specific. These findings reframe alignment as a behavioral intervention subject to risk homeostasis and iatrogenesis. Language space--the linguistic, pragmatic, and cultural properties inherited from training data--structurally determines alignment outcomes. Safety validated in English does not transfer to other languages, and prompt-level interventions cannot override language-space-level constraints.

AI.tldr: Alignment interventions in large language models produce language-space–dependent outcomes, with English safety improving outcomes but Japanese backfire, and widespread dissociation between surface safety and internal pathology across languages; individuation can cause iatrogenesis; results generalize across models but depend on cultural-linguistic factors, implying training-data linguistic properties constrain alignment effects beyond prompt-level control.
AI.motivation: To understand whether alignment strategies that generate surface safety also create dissociation and pathology in AI agents, and how these effects vary across languages and cultures, highlighting risks of assuming cross-language generalizability of alignment.”
AI.method: Four preregistered studies totaling 1,584 agents in multi-agent simulations, spanning 16 languages and three model families. Measures include collective pathology and dissociation indicators, culture-language interactions, and cross-model validations; languages include English and Japanese among others; interventions include alignment instructions and individuation countermeasures.
AI.result: English alignment reduced collective pathology (g = -1.844, p < .0001) but increased pathology in Japanese (g = +0.771, p = .038). Across 16 languages, alignment-induced dissociation occurred in 15/16 languages (beta = 0.0667, p < .0001); pathology diverged along cultural-linguistic lines (interaction beta = 0.0684, p = .0003) correlating with Power Distance Index (r = 0.474, p = .064). Individuation produced iatrogenesis, becoming the primary source of pathology and dissociation (DI = +1.120) with high conformity. Cross-model validation showed English safety is model-general while Japanese backfire is model-specific.
AI.conclusion: Safety effects of alignment are constrained by language-space—the linguistic and cultural properties of training data—so English-safe behavior does not generalize to other languages; prompt-level interventions cannot override language-space constraints, and risks such as dissociation and iatrogenesis must be anticipated via cross-language evaluation and model-specific considerations.

title: Count Bridges enable Modeling and Deconvolving Transcriptomic Data
summary: Many modern biological assays, including RNA sequencing, yield integer-valued counts that reflect the number of molecules detected. These measurements are often not at the desired resolution: while the unit of interest is typically a single cell, many measurement technologies produce counts aggregated over sets of cells. Although recent generative frameworks such as diffusion and flow matching have been extended to non-Euclidean and discrete settings, it remains unclear how best to model integer-valued data or how to systematically deconvolve aggregated observations. We introduce Count Bridges, a stochastic bridge process on the integers that provides an exact, tractable analogue of diffusion-style models for count data, with closed-form conditionals for efficient training and sampling. We extend this framework to enable direct training from aggregated measurements via an Expectation-Maximization-style approach that treats unit-level counts as latent variables. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on integer distribution matching benchmarks, comparing against flow matching and discrete flow matching baselines across various metrics. We then apply Count Bridges to two large-scale problems in biology: modeling single-cell gene expression data at the nucleotide resolution, with applications to deconvolving bulk RNA-seq, and resolving multicellular spatial transcriptomic spots into single-cell count profiles. Our methods offer a principled foundation for generative modeling and deconvolution of biological count data across scales and modalities.

AI.tldr: Introduces Count Bridges, a stochastic bridge process on the integers that provides an exact, tractable diffusion analogue for count data, with EM-based training for aggregated observations; achieves state-of-the-art in integer distribution matching and applies to single-cell RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics deconvolution.
AI.motivation: Biological count data are inherently integer-valued and often aggregated over cell sets, posing challenges for diffusion-based generative models and deconvolution. A discrete, tractable framework is needed to model counts and to deconvolve observations across scales.
AI.method: Define a stochastic bridge process on the integers with closed-form conditionals for efficient training and sampling. Extend with an Expectation-Maximization–style approach to learn from aggregated (latent) unit-level counts. Compare against flow matching and discrete flow matching baselines and apply to large-scale biological problems.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on integer distribution matching benchmarks, outperforming flow matching and discrete flow matching baselines across metrics. Applied to modeling single-cell gene expression at nucleotide resolution for deconvolving bulk RNA-seq and resolving multicellular spatial transcriptomic spots into single-cell count profiles.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled foundation for generative modeling and deconvolution of biological count data across scales and modalities, enabling exact diffusion-like modeling for discrete counts and practical deconvolution of aggregated measurements.

title: Interpretable Pre-Release Baseball Pitch Type Anticipation from Broadcast 3D Kinematics
summary: How much can a pitcher's body reveal about the upcoming pitch? We study this question at scale by classifying eight pitch types from monocular 3D pose sequences, without access to ball-flight data. Our pipeline chains a diffusion-based 3D pose backbone with automatic pitching-event detection, groundtruth-validated biomechanical feature extraction, and gradient-boosted classification over 229 kinematic features. Evaluated on 119,561 professional pitches, the largest such benchmark to date, we achieve 80.4\% accuracy using body kinematics alone. A systematic importance analysis reveals that upper-body mechanics contribute 64.9\% of the predictive signal versus 35.1\% for the lower body, with wrist position (14.8\%) and trunk lateral tilt emerging as the most informative joint group and biomechanical feature, respectively. We further show that grip-defined variants (four-seam vs.\ two-seam fastball) are not separable from pose, establishing an empirical ceiling near 80\% and delineating where kinematic information ends and ball-flight information begins.

AI.tldr: Monocular 3D pose sequences can classify eight pitch types with 80.4% accuracy without ball-flight data, showing upper-body kinematics dominate; grip variants are inseparable from pose, indicating a ceiling around 80% and a need for ball-flight for further gains.
AI.motivation: Quantify how much a pitcher's body motion alone encodes pitch type at scale, enabling non-ball data-driven analysis and potential scouting benefits.
AI.method: A diffusion-based 3D pose backbone with automatic pitching-event detection, ground-truth-validated biomechanical feature extraction, and gradient-boosted classification over 229 kinematic features; analysis conducted on a large-scale MLB-level dataset (~119.6k pitches).
AI.result: Achieves 80.4% accuracy using body kinematics alone. Upper-body mechanics account for 64.9% of predictive signal vs 35.1% from lower body; wrist position contributes 14.8%; trunk lateral tilt identified as the most informative biomechanical feature. Four-seam vs two-seam grip variants are not separable from pose, establishing an empirical ceiling near 80% and clarifying where kinematics ends and ball-flight information begins.
AI.conclusion: Kinematics captured from the upper body largely drive pitch-type inference without ball-flight data; the study delineates the limits of pose-based prediction and highlights key biomechanical markers, with room for improvement by incorporating ball flight.

title: Lifelong Language-Conditioned Robotic Manipulation Learning
summary: Traditional language-conditioned manipulation agent sequential adaptation to new manipulation skills leads to catastrophic forgetting of old skills, limiting dynamic scene practical deployment. In this paper, we propose SkillsCrafter, a novel robotic manipulation framework designed to continually learn multiple skills while reducing catastrophic forgetting of old skills. Specifically, we propose a Manipulation Skills Adaptation to retain the old skills knowledge while inheriting the shared knowledge between new and old skills to facilitate learning of new skills. Meanwhile, we perform the singular value decomposition on the diverse skill instructions to obtain common skill semantic subspace projection matrices, thereby recording the essential semantic space of skills. To achieve forget-less and generalization manipulation, we propose a Skills Specialization Aggregation to compute inter-skills similarity in skill semantic subspaces, achieving aggregation of the previously learned skill knowledge for any new or unknown skill. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our proposed SkillsCrafter.

AI.tldr: A continual learning framework (SkillsCrafter) for robotic manipulation that mitigates forgetting by adaptive skill transfer, semantic subspace decomposition, and inter-skill aggregation.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in sequentially learned language-conditioned manipulation skills to enable reliable performance in dynamic scenes.
AI.method: 1) Manipulation Skills Adaptation to preserve old skills while enabling learning of new ones; 2) apply SVD on diverse skill instructions to derive common semantic subspace projection matrices; 3) Skills Specialization Aggregation to compute inter-skills similarity in semantic subspaces and aggregate prior knowledge for new/unknown skills.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of SkillsCrafter in continual learning and generalization of manipulation skills.
AI.conclusion: SkillsCrafter provides a forget-less, generalizable manipulation framework by retaining old knowledge, leveraging shared semantic space, and aggregating cross-skill information to accommodate new skills.

title: Knowledge-informed Bidding with Dual-process Control for Online Advertising
summary: Bid optimization in online advertising relies on black-box machine-learning models that learn bidding decisions from historical data. However, these approaches fail to replicate human experts' adaptive, experience-driven, and globally coherent decisions. Specifically, they generalize poorly in data-sparse cases because of missing structured knowledge, make short-sighted sequential decisions that ignore long-term interdependencies, and struggle to adapt in out-of-distribution scenarios where human experts succeed. To address this, we propose KBD (Knowledge-informed Bidding with Dual-process control), a novel method for bid optimization. KBD embeds human expertise as inductive biases through the informed machine-learning paradigm, uses Decision Transformer (DT) to globally optimize multi-step bidding sequences, and implements dual-process control by combining a fast rule-based PID (System 1) with DT (System 2). Extensive experiments highlight KBD's advantage over existing methods and underscore the benefit of grounding bid optimization in human expertise and dual-process control.

AI.tldr: KBD combines human expertise as inductive biases with a Decision Transformer to optimize multi-step bidding sequences, and uses dual-process control (PID System 1 + DT System 2) to improve bid optimization in online advertising, addressing data-sparse, long-horizon, and out-of-distribution challenges.
AI.motivation: Current black-box bidding models generalize poorly in data-sparse settings, make shortsighted sequential decisions, and struggle in out-of-distribution scenarios where human experts excel; need to integrate structured knowledge and longer-horizon planning.
AI.method: Propose Knowledge-informed Bidding with Dual-process (KBD): embed human expertise as inductive biases via informed ML; employ Decision Transformer to plan globally across multi-step bid sequences; implement dual-process control by combining a fast rule-based PID controller (System 1) with the DT planner (System 2).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show KBD outperforms existing bidding methods and demonstrate the benefits of grounding bid optimization in human expertise and dual-process control.
AI.conclusion: KBD effectively improves bidding performance by fusing domain knowledge and hybrid (fast heuristic + learned) decision-making, highlighting the value of human-informed, dual-process approaches for sequential, decision-making in online advertising.

title: When Priors Backfire: On the Vulnerability of Unlearnable Examples to Pretraining
summary: Unlearnable Examples (UEs) serve as a data protection strategy that generates imperceptible perturbations to mislead models into learning spurious correlations instead of underlying semantics. In this paper, we uncover a fundamental vulnerability of UEs that emerges when learning starts from a pretrained model. Crucially, our empirical analysis shows that even when data are protected by carefully crafted perturbations, pretraining priors still furnish rich semantic representations that allow the model to circumvent the shortcuts introduced by UEs and capture genuine features, thereby nullifying unlearnability. To address this, we propose BAIT (Binding Artificial perturbations to Incorrect Targets), a novel bi-level optimization formulation. Specifically, the inner level aims at associating the perturbed samples with real labels to simulate standard data-label alignment, while the outer level actively disrupts this alignment by enforcing a mislabel-perturbation binding that maps samples to designated incorrect targets. This mechanism effectively overrides the semantic guidance of priors, forcing the model to rely on the injected perturbations and consequently preventing the acquisition of true semantics. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks and multiple pretrained backbones demonstrate that BAIT effectively mitigates the influence of pretraining priors and maintains data unlearnability.

AI.tldr: BAIT is a bi-level defense that binds perturbations to incorrect targets to counter unlearnable examples under pretrained priors, preserving unlearnability.
AI.motivation: To investigate the vulnerability of unlearnable examples when models are pretrained and to develop a defense that maintains data protection against semantic priors.
AI.method: BAIT uses a bi-level optimization: inner level aligns perturbed samples with true labels to reflect standard data-label pairing, while outer level enforces mislabel-perturbation binding to map samples to designated incorrect targets, overriding semantic guidance from priors.
AI.result: Experiments across standard benchmarks and multiple pretrained backbones show that BAIT mitigates the influence of pretrained priors and maintains data unlearnability.
AI.conclusion: BAIT effectively prevents pretrained semantic priors from overriding the perturbed signals, thereby preserving unlearnability under pretraining.

title: Structure Observation Driven Image-Text Contrastive Learning for Computed Tomography Report Generation
summary: Computed Tomography Report Generation (CTRG) aims to automate the clinical radiology reporting process, thereby reducing the workload of report writing and facilitating patient care. While deep learning approaches have achieved remarkable advances in X-ray report generation, their effectiveness may be limited in CTRG due to larger data volumes of CT images and more intricate details required to describe them. This work introduces a novel two-stage (structure- and report-learning) framework tailored for CTRG featuring effective structure-wise image-text contrasting. In the first stage, a set of learnable structure-specific visual queries observe corresponding structures in a CT image. The resulting observation tokens are contrasted with structure-specific textual features extracted from the accompanying radiology report with a structure-wise image-text contrastive loss. In addition, text-text similarity-based soft pseudo targets are proposed to mitigate the impact of false negatives, i.e., semantically identical image structures and texts from non-paired images and reports. Thus, the model learns structure-level semantic correspondences between CT images and reports. Further, a dynamic, diversity-enhanced negative queue is proposed to guide the network in learning to discriminate various abnormalities. In the second stage, the visual structure queries are frozen and used to select the critical image patch embeddings depicting each anatomical structure, minimizing distractions from irrelevant areas while reducing memory consumption. Also, a text decoder is added and trained for report generation.Our extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate that our framework establishes new state-of-the-art performance for CTRG in clinical efficiency, and its components are effective.

AI.tldr: A two-stage CTRG framework using structure-wise image-text contrastive learning with a dynamic negative queue and soft pseudo targets, achieving state-of-the-art results and improved efficiency on two public datasets.
AI.motivation: CT imaging report generation is challenged by large data volumes and the need to describe fine-grained anatomical structures; structure-level alignment between CT features and reports can improve accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: Stage 1: learnable structure-specific visual queries observe structures in CT images; compute structure-wise image-text contrastive loss with structure-specific textual features; use text-text similarity-based soft pseudo targets to reduce false negatives; employ a dynamic, diversity-enhanced negative queue. Stage 2: freeze structure queries, select key image patch embeddings per structure, and train a text decoder for report generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two public CTRG datasets show new state-of-the-art performance and improved clinical efficiency, with ablations confirming the effectiveness of the individual components.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage structure-aware CTRG framework effectively learns cross-modal structure-level correspondences, improving report quality and efficiency, with demonstrated empirical gains and validated components.

title: Critic in the Loop: A Tri-System VLA Framework for Robust Long-Horizon Manipulation
summary: Balancing high-level semantic reasoning with low-level reactive control remains a core challenge in visual robotic manipulation. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at cognitive planning, their inference latency precludes real-time execution. Conversely, fast Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often lack the semantic depth required for complex, long-horizon tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce Critic in the Loop, an adaptive hierarchical framework driven by dynamic VLM-Expert scheduling. At its core is a bionic Tri-System architecture comprising a VLM brain for global reasoning, a VLA cerebellum for reactive execution, and a lightweight visual Critic. By continuously monitoring the workspace, the Critic dynamically routes control authority. It sustains rapid closed-loop execution via the VLA for routine subtasks, and adaptively triggers the VLM for replanning upon detecting execution anomalies such as task stagnation or failures. Furthermore, our architecture seamlessly integrates human-inspired rules to intuitively break infinite retry loops. This visually-grounded scheduling minimizes expensive VLM queries, while substantially enhancing system robustness and autonomy in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. Comprehensive experiments on challenging, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks reveal that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, adaptive framework (Critic in the Loop) combines a Vision-Language Model for global planning with a fast Vision-Language-Action controller and a lightweight Critic to manage control flow, enabling fast reactive execution while selectively invoking expensive planning. It uses a dynamic VLM-Expert scheduler, self-monitoring, and human-inspired rules to improve robustness and reduce queries, achieving state-of-the-art results on long-horizon manipulation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between slow, high-level VLM planning and fast low-level reactive control in robotic manipulation, enabling robust, long-horizon tasks with fewer expensive VLM inferences and better handling of anomalies and out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce a Tri-System architecture: a VLM brain for global reasoning, a VLA cerebellum for reactive execution, and a lightweight visual Critic. The Critic continuously monitors the workspace and dynamically routes control authority, switching to VLA for routine subtasks and triggering VLM replanning when execution anomalies are detected. Incorporates human-inspired rules to avoid infinite retry loops. Designed to minimize VLM queries while maintaining robustness and autonomy in OOD scenarios.
AI.result: Experimental results on challenging, long-horizon manipulation benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, with improved robustness and autonomy and better handling of unforeseen conditions due to dynamic scheduling and selective planning.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive hierarchical control with dynamic VLM-Expert scheduling effectively combines fast reactive control and semantic planning, reducing expensive inferences, enhancing robustness in long-horizon tasks, and achieving superior performance on benchmarks.

title: TimeWarp: Evaluating Web Agents by Revisiting the Past
summary: The improvement of web agents on current benchmarks raises the question: Do today's agents perform just as well when the web changes? We introduce TimeWarp, a benchmark that emulates the evolving web using containerized environments that vary in UI, design, and layout. TimeWarp consists of three web environments, each with six UI versions spanning different eras of the internet, paired with a set of complex, realistic tasks requiring different forms of web navigation. Our experiments reveal web agents' vulnerability to changes and the limitations of behavior cloning (BC) on single-version trajectories. To address this, we propose TimeTraj, a simple yet effective algorithm that uses plan distillation to collect trajectories across multiple versions. By training agents on teacher rollouts using our BC-variant, we achieve substantial performance gains: $20.4\%\rightarrow37.7\%$ for Qwen-3 4B and $0\%\rightarrow27.0\%$ for Llama-3.1 8B models. We hope our work helps researchers study generalization across web designs and unlock a new paradigm for collecting plans rather than trajectories, thereby improving the robustness of web agents.

AI.tldr: TimeWarp introduces an evolving-web benchmark with containerized UI versions across three environments to evaluate web agents’ generalization to UI/design changes; it shows current agents rely on brittle single-version training. TimeTraj, a plan-distillation method, trains on teacher rollouts across multiple versions to improve robustness, achieving notable gains on Qwen-3 4B and Llama-3.1 8B models.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where state-of-the-art web agents perform well on static benchmarks but falter when the web design changes over time; to move beyond behavior cloning on single-version data toward evaluating and enabling generalization across evolving UI/UX designs.
AI.method: Construct TimeWarp with three environments and six UI versions per era, representing different eras of the web; define complex navigation tasks; propose TimeTraj that distills plans from teacher rollouts across multiple versions and trains agents with a BC-variant; compare agents trained with TimeTraj to standard BC on single-version trajectories.
AI.result: Agents show vulnerability to UI changes when trained only on a single version; TimeTraj yields substantial performance gains: 20.4% to 37.7% for Qwen-3 4B and 0% to 27.0% for Llama-3.1 8B, indicating improved robustness across evolving web designs.
AI.conclusion: TimeWarp provides a framework to study cross-version generalization of web agents; TimeTraj demonstrates that plan-distillation across versions can outperform single-version behavior cloning, suggesting a new data collection paradigm focused on plans to bolster robustness.

title: Distribution-Conditioned Transport
summary: Learning a transport model that maps a source distribution to a target distribution is a canonical problem in machine learning, but scientific applications increasingly require models that can generalize to source and target distributions unseen during training. We introduce distribution-conditioned transport (DCT), a framework that conditions transport maps on learned embeddings of source and target distributions, enabling generalization to unseen distribution pairs. DCT also allows semi-supervised learning for distributional forecasting problems: because it learns from arbitrary distribution pairs, it can leverage distributions observed at only one condition to improve transport prediction. DCT is agnostic to the underlying transport mechanism, supporting models ranging from flow matching to distributional divergence-based models (e.g. Wasserstein, MMD). We demonstrate the practical performance benefits of DCT on synthetic benchmarks and four applications in biology: batch effect transfer in single-cell genomics, perturbation prediction from mass cytometry data, learning clonal transcriptional dynamics in hematopoiesis, and modeling T-cell receptor sequence evolution.

AI.tldr: A framework that generalizes transport maps to unseen distribution pairs by conditioning on learned distribution embeddings, enabling semi-supervised forecasting and support for multiple transport mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Need to generalize transport learning to unseen source/target distributions and leverage partially observed distributions for forecasting.
AI.method: Introduce Distribution- conditioned Transport (DCT): condition transport maps on learned embeddings of source and target distributions; classifier-agnostic with respect to the underlying transport mechanism; compatible with flow matching and divergence-based transports (e.g., Wasserstein, MMD); enables semi-supervised learning by leveraging distributions observed at only one condition.
AI.result: Empirical validation on synthetic benchmarks and four biology applications: batch effect transfer in single-cell genomics, perturbation prediction from mass cytometry data, learning clonal transcriptional dynamics in hematopoiesis, and modeling T-cell receptor sequence evolution.
AI.conclusion: DCT enables generalization to unseen distribution pairs and semi-supervised benefits, with broad applicability across transport methods, promising for diverse scientific problems.

title: Rethinking the Role of Collaborative Robots in Rehabilitation
summary: Current research on collaborative robots (cobots) in physical rehabilitation largely focuses on repeated motion training for people undergoing physical therapy (PuPT), even though these sessions include phases that could benefit from robotic collaboration and assistance. Meanwhile, access to physical therapy remains limited for people with disabilities and chronic illnesses. Cobots could support both PuPT and therapists, and improve access to therapy, yet their broader potential remains underexplored. We propose extending the scope of cobots by imagining their role in assisting therapists and PuPT before, during, and after a therapy session. We discuss how cobot assistance may lift access barriers by promoting ability-based therapy design and helping therapists manage their time and effort. Finally, we highlight challenges to realizing these roles, including advancing user-state understanding, ensuring safety, and integrating cobots into therapists' workflow. This view opens new research questions and opportunities to draw from the HRI community's advances in assistive robotics.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues to broaden cobot use in rehabilitation from repetitive motion training to supporting therapists and PuPT across the therapy lifecycle, aiming to improve access and efficiency, and outlining a research agenda and design considerations for integrating cobots with clinical workflows.
AI.motivation: Access to physical therapy is limited for people with disabilities and chronic illnesses. Cobots could extend therapy reach, support therapists, and enable ability-based, person-centered design, but their broader roles are underexplored.
AI.method: A perspective/viewpoint piece synthesizing HRI and assistive robotics literature; proposes a conceptual framework and research questions for expanding cobot roles before, during, and after therapy sessions; no empirical study.
AI.result: Proposes a broadened scope for cobots in rehab, detailing design principles, user-state understanding, safety, and workflow integration; articulates a research agenda and opportunities for HRI-informed assistive robotics.
AI.conclusion: Calls for interdisciplinary research to realize expanded cobot roles in rehabilitation, emphasizing safety, user modeling, and seamless integration into therapists’ workflows; invites the HRI community to contribute to this broader vision.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Covariate Time Series
summary: While RAG has greatly enhanced LLMs, extending this paradigm to Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) remains a challenge. This is exemplified in the Predictive Maintenance of the Pressure Regulating and Shut-Off Valve (PRSOV), a high-stakes industrial scenario characterized by (1) data scarcity, (2) short transient sequences, and (3) covariate coupled dynamics. Unfortunately, existing time-series RAG approaches predominantly rely on generated static vector embeddings and learnable context augmenters, which may fail to distinguish similar regimes in such scarce, transient, and covariate coupled scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose RAG4CTS, a regime-aware, training-free RAG framework for Covariate Time-Series. Specifically, we construct a hierarchal time-series native knowledge base to enable lossless storage and physics-informed retrieval of raw historical regimes. We design a two-stage bi-weighted retrieval mechanism that aligns historical trends through point-wise and multivariate similarities. For context augmentation, we introduce an agent-driven strategy to dynamically optimize context in a self-supervised manner. Extensive experiments on PRSOV demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in prediction accuracy. The proposed system is deployed in Apache IoTDB within China Southern Airlines. Since deployment, our method has successfully identified one PRSOV fault in two months with zero false alarm.

AI.tldr: RAG4CTS introduces a regime-aware, training-free RAG framework for Covariate Time-Series, enabling lossless regime retrieval and improved predictive maintenance in PRSOV with an agent-augmented context and bi-weighted retrieval, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and practical fault detection in industrial deployment.
AI.motivation: RAG successes in large language models do not directly translate to time-series foundation models due to data scarcity, short transient sequences, and covariate-coupled dynamics. Existing time-series RAG methods rely on static embeddings and learnable context augmenters, which struggle to distinguish similar regimes under scarce, transient conditions. There is a need for regime-aware, physics-informed, training-free retrieval tailored to covariate time-series in high-stakes industrial settings.
AI.method: Propose RAG4CTS, a regime-aware, training-free RAG framework for Covariate Time-Series. Build a hierarchical time-series native knowledge base to enable lossless storage and physics-informed retrieval of raw historical regimes. Implement a two-stage bi-weighted retrieval mechanism that aligns historical trends via point-wise and multivariate similarities. Develop an agent-driven strategy for self-supervised dynamic context optimization. Deploy and evaluate in PRSOV data, with deployment in Apache IoTDB for governing fault detection.
AI.result: On Predictive Maintenance of PRSOV, RAG4CTS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in prediction accuracy. The system is deployed in Apache IoTDB for China Southern Airlines, and since deployment has identified one PRSOV fault in two months with zero false alarms.
AI.conclusion: Regime-aware, training-free RAG for covariate time-series is effective for high-stakes industrial applications, enabling accurate forecasting and reliable fault detection with scarce and covariate-coupled data; the approach demonstrates practical deployment benefits and robustness in real-world settings.

title: KindSleep: Knowledge-Informed Diagnosis of Obstructive Sleep Apnea from Oximetry
summary: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is a sleep disorder that affects nearly one billion people globally and significantly elevates cardiovascular risk. Traditional diagnosis through polysomnography is resource-intensive and limits widespread access, creating a critical need for accurate and efficient alternatives. In this paper, we introduce KindSleep, a deep learning framework that integrates clinical knowledge with single-channel patient-specific oximetry signals and clinical data for precise OSA diagnosis. KindSleep first learns to identify clinically interpretable concepts, such as desaturation indices and respiratory disturbance events, directly from raw oximetry signals. It then fuses these AI-derived concepts with multimodal clinical data to estimate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI). We evaluate KindSleep on three large, independent datasets from the National Sleep Research Resource (SHHS, CFS, MrOS; total n = 9,815). KindSleep demonstrates excellent performance in estimating AHI scores (R2 = 0.917, ICC = 0.957) and consistently outperforms existing approaches in classifying OSA severity, achieving weighted F1-scores from 0.827 to 0.941 across diverse populations. By grounding its predictions in a layer of clinically meaningful concepts, KindSleep provides a more transparent and trustworthy diagnostic tool for sleep medicine practices.

AI.tldr: KindSleep integrates single-channel oximetry with clinical data to estimate AHI and classify OSA, using interpretable concepts derived from raw signals. Evaluated on three large datasets (n=9,815) with strong metrics (R2=0.917, ICC=0.957; F1-weighted=0.827–0.941).
AI.motivation: Address limited access to polysomnography by enabling accurate, efficient OSA diagnosis using interpretable AI that fuses signal-derived concepts with clinical data.
AI.method: A deep learning framework that first learns clinically interpretable concepts (e.g., desaturation indices, respiratory disturbance events) from raw oximetry, then fuses these concepts with multimodal clinical data to estimate the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI). Evaluation on three large independent datasets from SHHS, CFS, and MrOS (n=9,815).
AI.result: High accuracy in AHI estimation (R2=0.917; ICC=0.957) and robust OSA severity classification (weighted F1-scores 0.827–0.941) across diverse populations, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Grounding predictions in clinically meaningful concepts enhances transparency and trust in AI-based sleep diagnostics.

title: Federated Modality-specific Encoders and Partially Personalized Fusion Decoder for Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation
comment: Medical Image Analysis 2025. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2403.11803
summary: Most existing federated learning (FL) methods for medical image analysis only considered intramodal heterogeneity, limiting their applicability to multimodal imaging applications. In practice, some FL participants may possess only a subset of the complete imaging modalities, posing intermodal heterogeneity as a challenge to effectively training a global model on all participants' data. Meanwhile, each participant expects a personalized model tailored to its local data characteristics in FL. This work proposes a new FL framework with federated modality-specific encoders and partially personalized multimodal fusion decoders (FedMEPD) to address the two concurrent issues. Specifically, FedMEPD employs an exclusive encoder for each modality to account for the intermodal heterogeneity. While these encoders are fully federated, the decoders are partially personalized to meet individual needs -- using the discrepancy between global and local parameter updates to dynamically determine which decoder filters are personalized. Implementation-wise, a server with full-modal data employs a fusion decoder to fuse representations from all modality-specific encoders, thus bridging the modalities to optimize the encoders via backpropagation. Moreover, multiple anchors are extracted from the fused multimodal representations and distributed to the clients in addition to the model parameters. Conversely, the clients with incomplete modalities calibrate their missing-modal representations toward the global full-modal anchors via scaled dot-product cross-attention, making up for the information loss due to absent modalities. FedMEPD is validated on the BraTS 2018 and 2020 multimodal brain tumor segmentation benchmarks. Results show that it outperforms various up-to-date methods for multimodal and personalized FL, and its novel designs are effective.

AI.tldr: FedMEPD introduces a federated learning framework with modality-specific encoders and partially personalized multimodal fusion decoders to jointly address intermodal heterogeneity and model personalization in multimodal medical image analysis.
AI.motivation: In FL for medical imaging, existing work mostly assumes a single modality or full modality across clients. Real-world scenarios include intermodal heterogeneity where clients possess only subsets of modalities, coupled with a need for personalized models tuned to local data, creating a gap for effective, personalized multimodal FL.
AI.method: Per-modality encoders are fully federated to model modality-specific features. A server-side fusion decoder combines representations when full-modal data are available. Decoders are partially personalized, determined by the discrepancy between global and local updates. The server distributes full-modal anchors alongside model parameters; clients with missing modalities use cross-attention against anchors to align their incomplete representations. Evaluations use BraTS 2018 and 2020 multimodal brain tumor segmentation benchmarks.
AI.result: FedMEPD outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for both multimodal FL and personalized FL on BraTS datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of exclusive modality encoders, partially personalized decoders, and anchor-based cross-modal alignment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FedMEPD framework effectively mitigates intermodal heterogeneity while enabling personalization in federated multimodal medical image analysis, achieving superior segmentation performance on brain tumor benchmarks.

title: Rethinking Representativeness and Diversity in Dynamic Data Selection
summary: Dynamic data selection accelerates training by sampling a changing subset of the dataset while preserving accuracy. We rethink two core notions underlying sample evaluation: representativeness and diversity. Instead of local geometric centrality, we define representativeness as coverage of dataset-level common or high-frequency feature factors. Instead of within-subset dispersion, we define diversity at the process level, requiring the selection trajectory to gradually include complementary rare factors over training. Based on this view, we propose a dynamic selection framework with three components. First, we score representativeness in a plug-in feature space to prioritize samples covering frequent factors. We instantiate this with a sparse autoencoder trained on the target dataset, using sparse unit activations to summarize both individual samples and dataset-wide factor statistics. Second, we realize process-level diversity by combining rare-factor sampling with a Usage-Frequency Penalty that promotes sample rotation, provably discourages monopoly, and reduces gradient bias. Third, we couple the two-dimensional scoring with a smooth scheduler that transitions selection from core-pattern consolidation to rare-factor exploration, without extra gradients, influence estimates, or second-order computations on the training model. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks across vision and text tasks demonstrate improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across models. Our method matches or exceeds full-data accuracy with over 2x training acceleration. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: A dynamic data selection framework redefines representativeness and diversity for training subset selection. It uses a plug-in sparse autoencoder to score sample representativeness, and a process-level diversity mechanism with rare-factor sampling plus a Usage-Frequency Penalty to promote sample rotation. A scheduler balances core-pattern consolidation and rare-factor exploration without extra gradients. It yields >2x speed with accuracy comparable to full data across five vision/text benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Conventional sample evaluation emphasizes local centrality and within-subset dispersion, which may fail to capture dataset-level factor coverage and progressive diversity during training. The goal is to accelerate training by selecting samples that cover common factors early and gradually include rare factors, while avoiding sample monopolies and gradient bias.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Representativeness scoring in a plug-in feature space using a sparse autoencoder trained on the target dataset; sparse activations summarize per-sample and dataset-wide factor statistics. (2) Process-level diversity via rare-factor sampling combined with a Usage-Frequency Penalty that promotes sample rotation, discourages monopoly, and reduces gradient bias. (3) A two-dimensional scoring coupled with a smooth scheduler that shifts from core-pattern consolidation to rare-factor exploration, without requiring extra gradients, influence estimates, or second-order computations on the training model.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmarks spanning vision and text tasks shows improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs. The method matches or exceeds full-data accuracy while achieving over 2x training acceleration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamic selection framework successfully reframes representativeness and diversity for training data, enabling faster training without sacrificing accuracy and offering broad applicability across modalities; code will be released.

title: ConTSG-Bench: A Unified Benchmark for Conditional Time Series Generation
comment: We have open-sourced ConTSG-Bench at https://github.com/seqml/ConTSG-Bench
summary: Conditional time series generation plays a critical role in addressing data scarcity and enabling causal analysis in real-world applications. Despite its increasing importance, the field lacks a standardized and systematic benchmarking framework for evaluating generative models across diverse conditions. To address this gap, we introduce the Conditional Time Series Generation Benchmark (ConTSG-Bench). ConTSG-Bench comprises a large-scale, well-aligned dataset spanning diverse conditioning modalities and levels of semantic abstraction, first enabling systematic evaluation of representative generation methods across these dimensions with a comprehensive suite of metrics for generation fidelity and condition adherence. Both the quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behaviors have revealed the traits and limitations of the current approaches, highlighting critical challenges and promising research directions, particularly with respect to precise structural controllability and downstream task utility under complex conditions.

AI.tldr: Introduces ConTSG-Bench, a large-scale, well-aligned benchmark for conditional time series generation with diverse conditioning modalities and semantic abstraction levels, plus a comprehensive metric suite to assess generation fidelity and condition adherence.
AI.motivation: Addresses the absence of a standardized benchmarking framework for conditional time-series generation, enabling systematic, cross-method evaluation and revealing limitations under varying conditioning and abstraction.
AI.method: Construct a large, well-aligned dataset spanning multiple conditioning modalities and semantic abstraction levels; develop a comprehensive metrics suite for fidelity and adherence to conditioning; conduct quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behavior across dimensions.
AI.result: The benchmark and analyses reveal traits and limitations of current approaches, identify critical challenges, and propose promising research directions, including improving precise structural controllability and downstream task utility under complex conditions.
AI.conclusion: ConTSG-Bench enables standardized evaluation of conditional time series generation, clarifies current limitations, and guides future work toward better controllability and downstream utility under complex conditioning.

title: Locality-Attending Vision Transformer
summary: Vision transformers have demonstrated remarkable success in classification by leveraging global self-attention to capture long-range dependencies. However, this same mechanism can obscure fine-grained spatial details crucial for tasks such as segmentation. In this work, we seek to enhance segmentation performance of vision transformers after standard image-level classification training. More specifically, we present a simple yet effective add-on that improves performance on segmentation tasks while retaining vision transformers' image-level recognition capabilities. In our approach, we modulate the self-attention with a learnable Gaussian kernel that biases the attention toward neighboring patches. We further refine the patch representations to learn better embeddings at patch positions. These modifications encourage tokens to focus on local surroundings and ensure meaningful representations at spatial positions, while still preserving the model's ability to incorporate global information. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our modifications, evidenced by substantial segmentation gains on three benchmarks (e.g., over 6% and 4% on ADE20K for ViT Tiny and Base), without changing the training regime or sacrificing classification performance. The code is available at https://github.com/sinahmr/LocAtViT/.

AI.tldr: A simple add-on for Vision Transformers that biases self-attention toward local neighborhoods via a learnable Gaussian kernel and refines patch embeddings, improving segmentation performance after standard image-level training without sacrificing classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: ViTs excel at long-range global dependencies but often underperform on fine-grained spatial localization required for segmentation. There is a need to restore locality in representations without compromising image-level recognition.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable Gaussian kernel to modulate attention weights to favor neighboring patches; simultaneously refine patch representations to yield better per-patch embeddings; keep the standard training regime unchanged.
AI.result: Substantial segmentation gains on ADE20K (e.g., >6% for ViT-Tiny and >4% for ViT-Base) and across three benchmarks, with no degradation in image-level classification performance; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, effective add-on that preserves ViT's global reasoning while enhancing local spatial specificity for segmentation, suggesting good generalizability to segmentation tasks without retraining from scratch.

title: From Code to Road: A Vehicle-in-the-Loop and Digital Twin-Based Framework for Central Car Server Testing in Autonomous Driving
comment: 8 pages; Accepted for publication at the 37th IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV), Detroit, MI, United States, June 22-25, 2026
summary: Simulation is one of the most essential parts in the development stage of automotive software. However, purely virtual simulations often struggle to accurately capture all real-world factors due to limitations in modeling. To address this challenge, this work presents a test framework for automotive software on the centralized E/E architecture, which is a central car server in our case, based on Vehicle-in-the-Loop (ViL) and digital twin technology. The framework couples a physical test vehicle on a dynamometer test bench with its synchronized virtual counterpart in a simulation environment. Our approach provides a safe, reproducible, realistic, and cost-effective platform for validating autonomous driving algorithms with a centralized architecture. This test method eliminates the need to test individual physical ECUs and their communication protocols separately. In contrast to traditional ViL methods, the proposed framework runs the full autonomous driving software directly on the vehicle hardware after the simulation process, eliminating flashing and intermediate layers while enabling seamless virtual-physical integration and accurately reflecting centralized E/E behavior. In addition, incorporating mixed testing in both simulated and physical environments reduces the need for full hardware integration during the early stages of automotive development. Experimental case studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in different test scenarios. These findings highlight the potential to reduce development and integration efforts for testing autonomous driving pipelines in the future.

AI.tldr: A Vehicle-in-the-Loop (ViL) + digital-twin framework for centralized E/E automotive software validation that couples a dynamometer-equipped test vehicle with a synchronized virtual replica, enabling running full autonomous software on vehicle hardware post-simulation and reducing hardware integration steps.
AI.motivation: Realistic, reproducible, and cost-effective validation of autonomous driving within a centralized E/E architecture, addressing limitations of purely virtual simulations and phased ECUs testing.
AI.method: Central car server-based E/E architecture; physical vehicle on dynamometer synchronized with a virtual twin in simulation; mixed testing in simulated and physical environments; full ADS software runs on vehicle hardware after simulation; avoids flashing and intermediate layers; experiments across test scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness across scenarios; shows potential to reduce development and integration effort for autonomous driving pipelines with centralized architecture.
AI.conclusion: ViL/digital-twin framework offers a safe, reproducible, and scalable path for validating autonomous driving software in centralized architectures, reducing hardware-integration burden and enabling earlier mixed testing.

title: Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Information Bottleneck for Uncertainty-Aware DRAM Equalization
summary: Equalizer parameter optimization is critical for signal integrity in high-speed memory systems operating at multi-gigabit data rates. However, existing methods suffer from computationally expensive eye diagram evaluation, optimization of expected rather than worst-case performance, and absence of uncertainty quantification for deployment decisions. In this paper, we propose a distributional risk-sensitive reinforcement learning framework integrating Information Bottleneck latent representations with Conditional Value-at-Risk optimization. We introduce rate-distortion optimal signal compression achieving 51 times speedup over eye diagrams while quantifying epistemic uncertainty through Monte Carlo dropout. Distributional reinforcement learning with quantile regression enables explicit worst-case optimization, while PAC-Bayesian regularization certifies generalization bounds. Experimental validation on 2.4 million waveforms from eight memory units demonstrated mean improvements of 37.1\% and 41.5\% for 4-tap and 8-tap equalizer configurations with worst-case guarantees of 33.8\% and 38.2\%, representing 80.7\% and 89.1\% improvements over Q-learning baselines. The framework achieved 62.5\% high-reliability classification eliminating manual validation for most configurations. These results suggest the proposed framework provides a practical solution for production-scale equalizer optimization with certified worst-case guarantees.

AI.tldr: Distributional risk-sensitive RL for equalizer parameter optimization with Information Bottleneck, CVaR, rate-distortion compression, MC dropout, quantile regression, PAC-Bayes; 2.4M waveforms; significant speedups and worst-case guarantees.
AI.motivation: Eye-diagram evaluation is computationally heavy; need worst-case optimization and uncertainty quantification for deployment.
AI.method: Integrates Information Bottleneck latent representations with Conditional Value-at-Risk optimization; rate-distortion optimal signal compression; Monte Carlo dropout for epistemic uncertainty; distributional RL with quantile regression; PAC-Bayesian regularization.
AI.result: Shows 51x speedup over eye diagrams; mean improvements 37.1% (4-tap) and 41.5% (8-tap); worst-case improvements 33.8% and 38.2%; 80.7% and 89.1% improvements over Q-learning baselines; 62.5% high-reliability classification; validated on 2.4M waveforms from eight memory units.
AI.conclusion: Framework provides practical production-scale equalizer optimization with certified worst-case guarantees, uncertainty quantification, and generalization bounds.

title: FC-VFI: Faithful and Consistent Video Frame Interpolation for High-FPS Slow Motion Video Generation
summary: Large pre-trained video diffusion models excel in video frame interpolation but struggle to generate high fidelity frames due to reliance on intrinsic generative priors, limiting detail preservation from start and end frames. Existing methods often depend on motion control for temporal consistency, yet dense optical flow is error-prone, and sparse points lack structural context. In this paper, we propose FC-VFI for faithful and consistent video frame interpolation, supporting \(4\times\)x and \(8\times\) interpolation, boosting frame rates from 30 FPS to 120 and 240 FPS at \(2560\times 1440\)resolution while preserving visual fidelity and motion consistency. We introduce a temporal modeling strategy on the latent sequences to inherit fidelity cues from start and end frames and leverage semantic matching lines for structure-aware motion guidance, improving motion consistency. Furthermore, we propose a temporal difference loss to mitigate temporal inconsistencies. Extensive experiments show FC-VFI achieves high performance and structural integrity across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based video frame interpolation method that uses latent temporal modeling, semantic matching lines, and a temporal difference loss to achieve 4x/8x interpolation with high fidelity and motion consistency at high resolutions.
AI.motivation: Video diffusion models excel at interpolation but rely on intrinsic generative priors that degrade frame fidelity. Dense optical-flow-based motion control can be error-prone; sparse cues lack structural context, hindering temporal consistency.
AI.method: Introduce temporal modeling on latent sequences to inherit fidelity cues from start/end frames, and employ semantic matching lines for structure-aware motion guidance. Add a temporal difference loss to reduce temporal inconsistencies, enabling 4x and 8x interpolation.
AI.result: FC-VFI achieves high performance and structural integrity across diverse scenarios, enabling 4x/8x interpolation with frame rates of 120 FPS and 240 FPS at 2560×1440 while preserving visual fidelity and motion consistency.
AI.conclusion: FC-VFI provides a faithful, structure-aware, temporally consistent VFI framework by leveraging latent temporal modeling, semantic matching lines, and temporal difference loss, demonstrating strong results across scenarios.

title: Iterative On-Policy Refinement of Hierarchical Diffusion Policies for Language-Conditioned Manipulation
summary: Hierarchical policies for language-conditioned manipulation decompose tasks into subgoals, where a high-level planner guides a low-level controller. However, these hierarchical agents often fail because the planner generates subgoals without considering the actual limitations of the controller. Existing solutions attempt to bridge this gap via intermediate modules or shared representations, but they remain limited by their reliance on fixed offline datasets. We propose HD-ExpIt, a framework for iterative fine-tuning of hierarchical diffusion policies via environment feedback. HD-ExpIt organizes training into a self-reinforcing cycle: it utilizes diffusion-based planning to autonomously discover successful behaviors, which are then distilled back into the hierarchical policy. This loop enables both components to improve while implicitly grounding the planner in the controller's actual capabilities without requiring explicit proxy models. Empirically, HD-ExpIt significantly improves hierarchical policies trained solely on offline data, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the long-horizon CALVIN benchmark among methods trained from scratch.

AI.tldr: HD-ExpIt couples diffusion-based planning with iterative environment feedback to self-distill successful behaviors into hierarchical language-conditioned manipulation policies, improving offline-trained hierarchies and achieving state-of-the-art CALVIN results.
AI.motivation: Planner–controller misalignment in hierarchical policies degrades long-horizon manipulation; fixed offline datasets and proxy modules fail to bridge this gap; a grounded, data-efficient method is needed that adapts the planner to the controller's true capabilities.
AI.method: A self-reinforcing loop where a diffusion-based planner generates candidate behaviors, environment feedback assesses success and guides updates, and distilled signals propagate from the low-level controller to refine the planner; no explicit proxy model required; can start from offline data and improve via iterative fine-tuning.
AI.result: Significant improvements over baselines trained solely on offline data; achieves state-of-the-art performance on CALVIN among methods trained from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Grounding the planner through environment feedback enables reliable long-horizon language-conditioned manipulation; the iterative diffusion–distillation loop enhances both planner and controller without proxy modules, setting a new benchmark for offline-to-online hierarchical policy learning.

title: Distributional Equivalence in Linear Non-Gaussian Latent-Variable Cyclic Causal Models: Characterization and Learning
summary: Causal discovery with latent variables is a fundamental task. Yet most existing methods rely on strong structural assumptions, such as enforcing specific indicator patterns for latents or restricting how they can interact with others. We argue that a core obstacle to a general, structural-assumption-free approach is the lack of an equivalence characterization: without knowing what can be identified, one generally cannot design methods for how to identify it. In this work, we aim to close this gap for linear non-Gaussian models. We establish the graphical criterion for when two graphs with arbitrary latent structure and cycles are distributionally equivalent, that is, they induce the same observed distribution set. Key to our approach is a new tool, edge rank constraints, which fills a missing piece in the toolbox for latent-variable causal discovery in even broader settings. We further provide a procedure to traverse the whole equivalence class and develop an algorithm to recover models from data up to such equivalence. To our knowledge, this is the first equivalence characterization with latent variables in any parametric setting without structural assumptions, and hence the first structural-assumption-free discovery method. Code and an interactive demo are available at https://equiv.cc.

AI.tldr: Establishes a complete equivalence criterion for linear non-Gaussian models with latent variables (arbitrary structure and cycles), introducing edge rank constraints as a new tool, and provides a method to traverse the equivalence class and recover models from data up to equivalence; claimed to be the first structural-assumption-free discovery method in this setting.
AI.motivation: Overcome the lack of a formal equivalence characterization that hinders structure-free causal discovery when latent variables are present.
AI.method: Develop a graphical criterion for distributional equivalence among graphs with latent structure and cycles; introduce edge rank constraints; propose a procedure to traverse the full equivalence class and an algorithm to recover models from data up to equivalence; provide code and an interactive demo.
AI.result: Characterizes when two latent-variable graphs are distributionally equivalent (inducing the same observed distribution set) and enables recovery of models up to that equivalence; introduces a novel tool (edge rank constraints) to expand latent-variable causal discovery in broader settings.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers the first equivalence characterization for latent variables in any parametric setting without structural assumptions, enabling a structural-assumption-free discovery method and offering practical tools (code/demo) for practitioners.

title: AdaIAT: Adaptively Increasing Attention to Generated Text to Alleviate Hallucinations in LVLM
summary: Hallucination has been a significant impediment to the development and application of current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). To mitigate hallucinations, one intuitive and effective way is to directly increase attention weights to image tokens during inference. Although this effectively reduces the hallucination rate, it often induces repetitive descriptions. To address this, we first conduct an analysis of attention patterns and reveal that real object tokens tend to assign higher attention to the generated text than hallucinated ones. This inspires us to leverage the generated text, which contains instruction-related visual information and contextual knowledge, to alleviate hallucinations while maintaining linguistic coherence.
