We further reveal good properties of 3D-RFT such as robust efficacy, and valuable insights into training strategies and data impact. We hope 3D-RFT can serve as a robust and promising paradigm for future development of 3D scene understanding.

AI.tldr: Extends reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to video-based 3D scene understanding via 3D-RFT, a framework that fine-tunes 3D-aware multi-modal LLMs with verifiable rewards using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). 3D-RFT-4B achieves state-of-the-art results on video-based 3D perception tasks, outperforming larger models on detection, grounding, and spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Token-level cross-entropy objectives in supervised fine-tuning poorly align with end-task performance in video-based 3D scene understanding; a direct, verifiable reward signal can better guide learning.
AI.method: Initial SFT to activate 3D-aware multi-modal LLMs, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using strictly verifiable rewards derived from metrics such as 3D IoU and F1-Score.
AI.result: 3D-RFT-4B achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple video-based 3D tasks; notably outperforms larger models like VG LLM-8B on 3D video detection, visual grounding, and spatial reasoning; provides insights into training strategies and data impact.
AI.conclusion: 3D-RFT presents a robust RL-based paradigm for video-based 3D scene understanding, offering improved alignment between training objectives and task outcomes and potential for broader application and future improvements in rewards and data usage.

title: Loop Closure via Maximal Cliques in 3D LiDAR-Based SLAM
comment: Accepted in the 2025 European Conference on Mobile Robots (ECMR). This is the author's version of the work
summary: Reliable loop closure detection remains a critical challenge in 3D LiDAR-based SLAM, especially under sensor noise, environmental ambiguity, and viewpoint variation conditions. RANSAC is often used in the context of loop closures for geometric model fitting in the presence of outliers. However, this approach may fail, leading to map inconsistency. We introduce a novel deterministic algorithm, CliReg, for loop closure validation that replaces RANSAC verification with a maximal clique search over a compatibility graph of feature correspondences. This formulation avoids random sampling and increases robustness in the presence of noise and outliers. We integrated our approach into a real- time pipeline employing binary 3D descriptors and a Hamming distance embedding binary search tree-based matching. We evaluated it on multiple real-world datasets featuring diverse LiDAR sensors. The results demonstrate that our proposed technique consistently achieves a lower pose error and more reliable loop closures than RANSAC, especially in sparse or ambiguous conditions. Additional experiments on 2D projection-based maps confirm its generality across spatial domains, making our approach a robust and efficient alternative for loop closure detection.

AI.tldr: Deterministic loop closure verification (CliReg) using a maximal clique in a feature-compatibility graph, replacing RANSAC; demonstrates robustness and real-time performance in LiDAR SLAM and 2D projections.
AI.motivation: RANSAC-based loop-closure verification can fail under noise, outliers, and environmental ambiguity; a deterministic, robust alternative is needed for reliable loop closures in 3D LiDAR SLAM.
AI.method: Form a compatibility graph from feature correspondences and perform a maximal clique search to enforce global consistency of loop closures. Integrate into a real-time pipeline using binary 3D descriptors and a binary-search-tree-based Hamming distance matcher; evaluate across diverse LiDAR sensors and 2D projections.
AI.result: CliReg yields lower pose error and more reliable loop closures than RANSAC, particularly in sparse or ambiguous conditions; demonstrates generality across spatial domains, including 2D projection-based maps.
AI.conclusion: CliReg provides a robust, efficient, and non-random alternative for loop-closure detection that improves robustness to noise/outliers and maintains real-time performance.

title: Bidirectional Curriculum Generation: A Multi-Agent Framework for Data-Efficient Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Enhancing mathematical reasoning in Large Language Models typically demands massive datasets, yet data efficiency remains a critical bottleneck. While Curriculum Learning attempts to structure this process, standard unidirectional approaches (simple-to-complex) suffer from inefficient sample utilization: they blindly escalate complexity even when foundational gaps persist, leading to wasted computation on unsolvable problems. To maximize the instructional value of every training sample, we introduce a novel Bidirectional Curriculum Generation framework. Unlike rigid trajectories, our multi-agent ecosystem mimics adaptive pedagogy to establish a closed feedback loop. It dynamically generates data by either complicating problems to challenge the model or, crucially, simplying them to repair specific reasoning failures. This mechanism ensures that the model consumes only the most effective data at any given stage. Grounded in the Optimal Pacing Theorem, our approach optimizes the learning trajectory, significantly outperforming baselines while achieving superior reasoning performance with substantially fewer instruction samples.

AI.tldr: A Bidirectional Curriculum Generation (BCG) framework for large language models aims to improve data efficiency in mathematical reasoning by adaptively generating training samples through a multi-agent loop that both complicates and simplifies problems, guided by the Optimal Pacing Theorem, achieving superior reasoning with far fewer instruction samples.
AI.motivation: Address data efficiency in math reasoning for LLMs. Traditional curricula escalate difficulty unidirectionally and waste compute when foundational gaps persist.
AI.method: A multi-agent feedback loop that dynamically shapes data: when the model struggles, simplify problems to repair failures; when capable, increase difficulty. Data generation is guided by the Optimal Pacing Theorem to optimize learning trajectories.
AI.result: Reportedly outperforms baselines and achieves superior reasoning performance with substantially fewer instruction samples.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional curriculum with adaptive pacing improves data efficiency and reasoning performance, reducing wasted computation and offering a more effective training paradigm.

title: EVMbench: Evaluating AI Agents on Smart Contract Security
summary: Smart contracts on public blockchains now manage large amounts of value, and vulnerabilities in these systems can lead to substantial losses. As AI agents become more capable at reading, writing, and running code, it is natural to ask how well they can already navigate this landscape, both in ways that improve security and in ways that might increase risk. We introduce EVMbench, an evaluation that measures the ability of agents to detect, patch, and exploit smart contract vulnerabilities. EVMbench draws on 117 curated vulnerabilities from 40 repositories and, in the most realistic setting, uses programmatic grading based on tests and blockchain state under a local Ethereum execution environment. We evaluate a range of frontier agents and find that they are capable of discovering and exploiting vulnerabilities end-to-end against live blockchain instances. We release code, tasks, and tooling to support continued measurement of these capabilities and future work on security.

AI.tldr: EVMbench assesses AI agents' ability to detect, patch, and exploit smart-contract vulnerabilities using 117 curated flaws across 40 repos, tested in a local Ethereum environment; frontier agents can end-to-end discover/exploit vulnerabilities on live blockchain instances; code/tools released for ongoing benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Public blockchains' value and programmable contracts create security stakes; evaluating AI's current capabilities informs defense and risk of automation in smart contract security.
AI.method: Curated set of 117 vulnerabilities from 40 repositories; programmatic grading with tests and blockchain state; realistic setting in a local Ethereum execution environment; evaluation of frontier agents across tasks: detection, patching, exploitation.
AI.result: Agents demonstrate end-to-end ability to discover and exploit vulnerabilities on live blockchain instances; not sure about metrics; authors release code and tooling.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a realistic benchmarking framework (EVMbench) for AI-driven security analysis of smart contracts and encourages further measurement and research.

title: Think, Then Verify: A Hypothesis-Verification Multi-Agent Framework for Long Video Understanding
summary: Long video understanding is challenging due to dense visual redundancy, long-range temporal dependencies, and the tendency of chain-of-thought and retrieval-based agents to accumulate semantic drift and correlation-driven errors. We argue that long-video reasoning should begin not with reactive retrieval, but with deliberate task formulation: the model must first articulate what must be true in the video for each candidate answer to hold. This thinking-before-finding principle motivates VideoHV-Agent, a framework that reformulates video question answering as a structured hypothesis-verification process. Based on video summaries, a Thinker rewrites answer candidates into testable hypotheses, a Judge derives a discriminative clue specifying what evidence must be checked, a Verifier grounds and tests the clue using localized, fine-grained video content, and an Answer agent integrates validated evidence to produce the final answer. Experiments on three long-video understanding benchmarks show that VideoHV-Agent achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while providing enhanced interpretability, improved logical soundness, and lower computational cost. We make our code publicly available at: https://github.com/Haorane/VideoHV-Agent.

AI.tldr: VideoHV-Agent reframes long-video QA as a hypothesis-verification pipeline to reduce semantic drift, improve interpretability, and achieve state-of-the-art results on long-video benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long video understanding faces dense visual redundancy, long-range dependencies, and accumulation of errors in chain-of-thought and retrieval-based approaches; a deliberate task formulation can guide reasoning before retrieval.
AI.method: A Thinker rewrites candidate answers into testable hypotheses; a Judge derives a discriminative clue of what evidence to check; a Verifier grounds and tests the clue on localized video content; an Answer agent integrates validated evidence to produce the final answer.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on three long-video understanding benchmarks; enhanced interpretability and logical soundness; lower computational cost; code released.
AI.conclusion: Structured hypothesis-verification is a promising paradigm for long-video QA, offering accuracy with interpretability and potential generalization to other multimodal tasks.

title: ROScopter: A Multirotor Autopilot based on ROSflight 2.0
summary: ROScopter is a lean multirotor autopilot built for researchers. ROScopter seeks to accelerate simulation and hardware testing of research code with an architecture that is both easy to understand and simple to modify. ROScopter is designed to interface with ROSflight 2.0 and runs entirely on an onboard flight computer, leveraging the features of ROS 2 to improve modularity. This work describes the architecture of ROScopter and how it can be used to test application code in both simulated and hardware environments. Hardware results of the default ROScopter behavior are presented, showing that ROScopter achieves similar performance to another state-of-the-art autopilot for basic waypoint-following maneuvers, but with a significantly reduced and more modular code-base.

AI.tldr: ROScopter is a lean, ROS-enabled multirotor autopilot for researchers that runs onboard, interfaces with ROSflight 2.0, and emphasizes modularity; it achieves similar basic waypoint-following performance to a state-of-the-art autopilot with a smaller, more modular code base.
AI.motivation: There is a need for an easy-to-understand, easily modifiable autopilot to accelerate testing of research code in both simulation and hardware, leveraging ROS 2 for better modularity.
AI.method: Describe the architecture, its ROS 2-based onboard implementation, and its interface with ROSflight 2.0. Assess its utility for testing application code in both simulated and hardware environments. Compare hardware results against a state-of-the-art autopilot for basic waypoint-following maneuvers.
AI.result: Hardware experiments show ROScopter achieves similar performance to a state-of-the-art autopilot for basic waypoint-following maneuvers, while offering a significantly reduced and more modular code base.
AI.conclusion: ROScopter provides a lean, modular autopilot architecture that facilitates rapid testing of research code, with comparable performance to existing systems for basic tasks and improved code maintainability.

title: MedCoRAG: Interpretable Hepatology Diagnosis via Hybrid Evidence Retrieval and Multispecialty Consensus
summary: Diagnosing hepatic diseases accurately and interpretably is critical, yet it remains challenging in real-world clinical settings. Existing AI approaches for clinical diagnosis often lack transparency, structured reasoning, and deployability. Recent efforts have leveraged large language models (LLMs), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and multi-agent collaboration. However, these approaches typically retrieve evidence from a single source and fail to support iterative, role-specialized deliberation grounded in structured clinical data. To address this, we propose MedCoRAG (i.e., Medical Collaborative RAG), an end-to-end framework that generates diagnostic hypotheses from standardized abnormal findings and constructs a patient-specific evidence package by jointly retrieving and pruning UMLS knowledge graph paths and clinical guidelines. It then performs Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning: a Router Agent dynamically dispatches Specialist Agents based on case complexity; these agents iteratively reason over the evidence and trigger targeted re-retrievals when needed, while a Generalist Agent synthesizes all deliberations into a traceable consensus diagnosis that emulates multidisciplinary consultation. Experimental results on hepatic disease cases from MIMIC-IV show that MedCoRAG outperforms existing methods and closed-source models in both diagnostic performance and reasoning interpretability.

AI.tldr: MedCoRAG is an end-to-end multi-agent RAG framework for hepatic diagnosis that jointly retrieves from UMLS and clinical guidelines to build a patient-specific evidence package, enabling traceable, specialist-driven deliberation and a Generalist consensus, achieving superior diagnostic performance and interpretability on MIMIC-IV cases.
AI.motivation: Clinical AI models often lack transparency, structured reasoning, and multi-source evidence integration. There is a need for iterative, role-specialized deliberation grounded in standardized clinical data to improve deployability.
AI.method: Build a patient-specific evidence package by jointly retrieving and pruning UMLS knowledge graph paths and clinical guidelines. Employ Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning: a Router Agent dispatches Specialist Agents based on case complexity; Specialists iteratively reason over evidence and trigger targeted re-retrievals; a Generalist Agent synthesizes deliberations into a traceable consensus diagnosis that mimics multidisciplinary consultation.
AI.result: MedCoRAG outperforms existing methods and closed-source models on hepatic disease cases from MIMIC-IV in diagnostic performance and reasoning interpretability.
AI.conclusion: MedCoRAG provides a transparent, multi-agent, data-grounded diagnostic framework that emulates multidisciplinary care and improves deployability in real-world clinical settings.

title: BandPO: Bridging Trust Regions and Ratio Clipping via Probability-Aware Bounds for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Proximal constraints are fundamental to the stability of the Large Language Model reinforcement learning. While the canonical clipping mechanism in PPO serves as an efficient surrogate for trust regions, we identify a critical bottleneck: fixed bounds strictly constrain the upward update margin of low-probability actions, disproportionately suppressing high-advantage tail strategies and inducing rapid entropy collapse. To address this, we introduce Band-constrained Policy Optimization (BandPO). BandPO replaces canonical clipping with Band, a unified theoretical operator that projects trust regions defined by f-divergences into dynamic, probability-aware clipping intervals. Theoretical analysis confirms that Band effectively resolves this exploration bottleneck. We formulate this mapping as a convex optimization problem, guaranteeing a globally optimal numerical solution while deriving closed-form solutions for specific divergences. Extensive experiments across diverse models and datasets demonstrate that BandPO consistently outperforms canonical clipping and Clip-Higher, while robustly mitigating entropy collapse.

AI.tldr: BandPO introduces Band, a convex-optimization-based operator that maps f-divergence trust regions into adaptive, probability-aware clipping intervals, yielding improved exploration and reduced entropy collapse compared to PPO clipping, with empirical gains across models and datasets.
AI.motivation: Fixed clipping bounds in PPO aggressively constrain upward updates for low-probability actions, which suppresses high-advantage tail strategies and accelerates entropy collapse. A dynamic, probability-aware trust-region constraint is needed to preserve exploration while maintaining stability.
AI.method: Introduce Band, a unified operator that projects f-divergence-defined trust regions into dynamic clipping intervals. Formulated as a convex optimization problem with globally optimal solutions; closed-form solutions derived for specific divergences; integrated into policy optimization as BandPO.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows Band resolves the exploration bottleneck. Empirical experiments across diverse models and datasets show BandPO consistently outperforms canonical clipping and Clip-Higher, with robust mitigation of entropy collapse.
AI.conclusion: BandPO provides a principled, efficient framework for trust-region updates via Band, improving exploration stability and performance, and generalizing across models and datasets.

title: A Simple Baseline for Unifying Understanding, Generation, and Editing via Vanilla Next-token Prediction
comment: Technical report. This work serves as a straightforward autoregressive baseline for unifying understanding, generation, and editing
summary: In this work, we introduce Wallaroo, a simple autoregressive baseline that leverages next-token prediction to unify multi-modal understanding, image generation, and editing at the same time. Moreover, Wallaroo supports multi-resolution image input and output, as well as bilingual support for both Chinese and English. We decouple the visual encoding into separate pathways and apply a four-stage training strategy to reshape the model's capabilities. Experiments are conducted on various benchmarks where Wallaroo produces competitive performance or exceeds other unified models, suggesting the great potential of autoregressive models in unifying multi-modality understanding and generation. Our code is available at https://github.com/JiePKU/Wallaroo.

AI.tldr: Wallaroo is a simple autoregressive model that unifies multi-modal understanding, image generation, and editing via next-token prediction, supporting multi-resolution visual input/output and bilingual (Chinese/English) capabilities; it uses decoupled visual encoders and a four-stage training strategy to reshape capabilities, achieving competitive or superior results on benchmarks; code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: To unify multi-modal understanding and generation under a single, autoregressive objective, addressing fragmentation across modalities, resolutions, and languages by leveraging next-token prediction.
AI.method: An autoregressive baseline with multi-resolution image input/output and bilingual support. Visual encoding is decoupled into separate pathways. A four-stage training strategy reallocates model capabilities to support understanding, generation, and editing across modalities.
AI.result: Wallaroo yields competitive performance or surpasses other unified models on various benchmarks, illustrating the potential of autoregressive models for unified multi-modal understanding and generation.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive models with decoupled visual encoders and structured, staged training can effectively unify multimodal understanding and generation across modalities and languages, indicating strong potential for future multi-modal AI systems.

title: PhysiFlow: Physics-Aware Humanoid Whole-Body VLA via Multi-Brain Latent Flow Matching and Robust Tracking
summary: In the domain of humanoid robot control, the fusion of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) with whole-body control is essential for semantically guided execution of real-world tasks. However, existing methods encounter challenges in terms of low VLA inference efficiency or an absence of effective semantic guidance for whole-body control, resulting in instability in dynamic limb-coordinated tasks. To bridge this gap, we present a semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain VLA framework for humanoid whole-body control. A series of experiments was conducted to evaluate the performance of the proposed framework. The experimental results demonstrated that the framework enabled reliable vision-language-guided full-body coordination for humanoid robots.

AI.tldr: A semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain VLA framework enabling reliable, semantically guided whole-body control for humanoid robots; experimentally validated.
AI.motivation: Addresses low VLA inference efficiency and the lack of effective semantic guidance for whole-body control, which lead to instability in dynamic limb-coordinated tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a semantic-motion intent guided, physics-aware multi-brain Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that fuses VLA with whole-body control to provide semantically guided execution for humanoid tasks.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate reliable vision-language-guided full-body coordination for humanoid robots when using the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges the gap between VLA inference and robust whole-body control, enabling stable, semantically guided coordination in real-world humanoid manipulation and locomotion tasks.

title: KARL: Knowledge Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present a system for training enterprise search agents via reinforcement learning that achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse suite of hard-to-verify agentic search tasks. Our work makes four core contributions. First, we introduce KARLBench, a multi-capability evaluation suite spanning six distinct search regimes, including constraint-driven entity search, cross-document report synthesis, tabular numerical reasoning, exhaustive entity retrieval, procedural reasoning over technical documentation, and fact aggregation over internal enterprise notes. Second, we show that models trained across heterogeneous search behaviors generalize substantially better than those optimized for any single benchmark. Third, we develop an agentic synthesis pipeline that employs long-horizon reasoning and tool use to generate diverse, grounded, and high-quality training data, with iterative bootstrapping from increasingly capable models. Fourth, we propose a new post-training paradigm based on iterative large-batch off-policy RL that is sample efficient, robust to train-inference engine discrepancies, and naturally extends to multi-task training with out-of-distribution generalization. Compared to Claude 4.6 and GPT 5.2, KARL is Pareto-optimal on KARLBench across cost-quality and latency-quality trade-offs, including tasks that were out-of-distribution during training. With sufficient test-time compute, it surpasses the strongest closed models. These results show that tailored synthetic data in combination with multi-task reinforcement learning enables cost-efficient and high-performing knowledge agents for grounded reasoning.

AI.tldr: KARL demonstrates a reinforcement-learning-based enterprise search agent framework with a multi-task, synthetic-data-driven approach that achieves Pareto-optimal performance on a diverse evaluation suite (KARLBench) and generalizes beyond single-task benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the need for robust, generalizable enterprise search agents capable of long-horizon reasoning, tool use, and grounding across diverse tasks, while being sample-efficient and scalable.
AI.method: Introduce KARLBench evaluation suite; train models across heterogeneous search behaviors; develop an agentic synthesis pipeline with long-horizon reasoning and tool use to generate synthetic training data with bootstrapping; propose iterative large-batch off-policy RL for post-training, enabling multi-task generalization.
AI.result: KARL is Pareto-optimal on KARLBench vs Claude 4.6 and GPT 5.2 across cost-quality and latency-quality; out-of-distribution tasks during training are included; with sufficient test-time compute, KARL surpasses strongest closed models; demonstrates gains from tailored synthetic data and multi-task RL for grounded reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Tailored synthetic data plus multi-task reinforcement learning yields cost-efficient, high-performing, grounded knowledge agents for enterprise search; KARLBench provides a rigorous, diverse evaluation of agentic capabilities.

title: TAPFormer: Robust Arbitrary Point Tracking via Transient Asynchronous Fusion of Frames and Events
summary: Tracking any point (TAP) is a fundamental yet challenging task in computer vision, requiring high precision and long-term motion reasoning. Recent attempts to combine RGB frames and event streams have shown promise, yet they typically rely on synchronous or non-adaptive fusion, leading to temporal misalignment and severe degradation when one modality fails. We introduce TAPFormer, a transformer-based framework that performs asynchronous temporal-consistent fusion of frames and events for robust and high-frequency arbitrary point tracking. Our key innovation is a Transient Asynchronous Fusion (TAF) mechanism, which explicitly models the temporal evolution between discrete frames through continuous event updates, bridging the gap between low-rate frames and high-rate events. In addition, a Cross-modal Locally Weighted Fusion (CLWF) module adaptively adjusts spatial attention according to modality reliability, yielding stable and discriminative features even under blur or low light. To evaluate our approach under realistic conditions, we construct a novel real-world frame-event TAP dataset under diverse illumination and motion conditions. Our method outperforms existing point trackers, achieving a 28.2% improvement in average pixel error within threshold. Moreover, on standard point tracking benchmarks, our tracker consistently achieves the best performance. Project website: tapformer.github.io

AI.tldr: TAPFormer uses asynchronous, transformer-based fusion of frames and events for robust, high-frequency point tracking; introduces Transient Asynchronous Fusion (TAF) and Cross-modal Locally Weighted Fusion (CLWF); achieves state-of-the-art results and introduces a real-world frame-event TAP dataset.
AI.motivation: To address temporal misalignment and modality failures when fusing RGB frames with event streams, enabling accurate point tracking under challenging conditions.
AI.method: TAPFormer integrates Transient Asynchronous Fusion (TAF) to model temporal evolution from low-rate frames with high-rate event updates; CLWF adaptively weighs modalities spatially based on reliability; a real-world TAP dataset is constructed for evaluation.
AI.result: Outperforms existing trackers, achieving a 28.2% improvement in average pixel error within threshold; state-of-the-art on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Asynchronous, modality-aware fusion with transformer-based architecture yields robust, high-frequency point tracking; dataset supports evaluation under diverse illumination/motion; future work could explore further efficiency and generalization.

title: Residual RL--MPC for Robust Microrobotic Cell Pushing Under Time-Varying Flow
summary: Contact-rich micromanipulation in microfluidic flow is challenging because small disturbances can break pushing contact and induce large lateral drift. We study planar cell pushing with a magnetic rolling microrobot that tracks a waypoint-sampled reference curve under time-varying Poiseuille flow. We propose a hybrid controller that augments a nominal MPC with a learned residual policy trained by SAC. The policy outputs a bounded 2D velocity correction that is contact-gated, so residual actions are applied only during robot--cell contact, preserving reliable approach behavior and stabilizing learning. All methods share the same actuation interface and speed envelope for fair comparisons. Experiments show improved robustness and tracking accuracy over pure MPC and PID under nonstationary flow, with generalization from a clover training curve to unseen circle and square trajectories. A residual-bound sweep identifies an intermediate correction limit as the best trade-off, which we use in all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Hybrid MPC with SAC-learned residuals enhances contact-rich micromanipulation under flow disturbances, using a contact-gated residual that preserves approach behavior, achieving better robustness and tracking; generalizes to unseen trajectories with a residual-bound trade-off.
AI.motivation: Contact-rich micromanipulation in microfluidic flow is highly sensitive to disturbances; breaking contact and lateral drift are significant challenges, necessitating robust control that maintains reliable approach.
AI.method: A hybrid controller augments a nominal MPC with a learned residual policy trained via SAC. The policy outputs a bounded 2D velocity correction that is gated to apply only during robot--cell contact. All controllers share the same actuation interface and speed envelope. Empirical evaluation uses nonstationary Poiseuille flow, training on a clover curve and testing on circle/square trajectories; a residual-bound sweep identifies the optimal correction limit.
AI.result: The residual-guided controller shows improved robustness and tracking accuracy over pure MPC and PID under nonstationary flow. It generalizes from clover to unseen trajectories. The sweep identifies an intermediate residual bound as the best trade-off, used across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A contact-gated, SAC-informed residual on top of MPC enhances stability and performance in contact-rich micromanipulation under flow, with a practical residual bound optimizing trade-offs and enabling generalization to new trajectories.

title: AI+HW 2035: Shaping the Next Decade
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) and hardware (HW) are advancing at unprecedented rates, yet their trajectories have become inseparably intertwined. The global research community lacks a cohesive, long-term vision to strategically coordinate the development of AI and HW. This fragmentation constrains progress toward holistic, sustainable, and adaptive AI systems capable of learning, reasoning, and operating efficiently across cloud, edge, and physical environments. The future of AI depends not only on scaling intelligence, but on scaling efficiency, achieving exponential gains in intelligence per joule, rather than unbounded compute consumption. Addressing this grand challenge requires rethinking the entire computing stack. This vision paper lays out a 10-year roadmap for AI+HW co-design and co-development, spanning algorithms, architectures, systems, and sustainability. We articulate key insights that redefine scaling around energy efficiency, system-level integration, and cross-layer optimization. We identify key challenges and opportunities, candidly assess potential obstacles and pitfalls, and propose integrated solutions grounded in algorithmic innovation, hardware advances, and software abstraction. Looking ahead, we define what success means in 10 years: achieving a 1000x improvement in efficiency for AI training and inference; enabling energy-aware, self-optimizing systems that seamlessly span cloud, edge, and physical AI; democratizing access to advanced AI infrastructure; and embedding human-centric principles into the design of intelligent systems. Finally, we outline concrete action items for academia, industry, government, and the broader community, calling for coordinated national initiatives, shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and sustained public-private partnerships to ensure that AI+HW co-design becomes a unifying long-term mission.

AI.tldr: A long-term vision paper outlining a 10-year AI+HW co-design roadmap focused on energy efficiency, cross-layer optimization, and sustainability to enable scalable, adaptive AI across cloud, edge, and physical environments; aims for 1000x efficiency and democratized access, with concrete actions for academia, industry, and government.
AI.motivation: AI and HW trajectories are inseparably intertwined, yet there is no cohesive long-term vision coordinating their development. This fragmentation hinders progress toward sustainable, adaptive AI that can learn and operate efficiently across cloud, edge, and physical environments. The paper reframes scaling around efficiency (energy per operation) rather than compute volume, and argues for rethinking the entire computing stack.
AI.method: A conceptual roadmap paper that identifies challenges and opportunities, proposes integrated solutions spanning algorithms, architectures, systems, and sustainability, and articulates cross-layer optimization and energy-aware co-design. It includes definitions of success metrics for a 10-year horizon and concrete action items for academia, industry, government, and the broader community.
AI.result: Defines 10-year success metrics: (1) ~1000× improvement in AI training and inference efficiency; (2) energy-aware, self-optimizing systems spanning cloud–edge–physical AI; (3) democratized access to advanced AI infrastructure; (4) embedding human-centric principles into design. Proposes concrete action items and initiatives to align stakeholders and resources.
AI.conclusion: Advocates for a coordinated national and international mission in AI+HW co-design, including shared infrastructure, workforce development, cross-agency collaboration, and sustained public–private partnerships to sustain progress toward the efficiency-centric, cross-layer AI future.

title: $\nabla$-Reasoner: LLM Reasoning via Test-Time Gradient Descent in Latent Space
summary:
Scaling inference-time compute for Large Language Models (LLMs) has unlocked unprecedented reasoning capabilities. However, existing inference-time scaling methods typically rely on inefficient and suboptimal discrete search algorithms or trial-and-error prompting to improve the online policy. In this paper, we propose $
abla$-Reasoner, an iterative generation framework that integrates differentiable optimization over token logits into the decoding loop to refine the policy on the fly. Our core component, Differentiable Textual Optimization (DTO), leverages gradient signals from both the LLM's likelihood and a reward model to refine textual representations. $
abla$-Reasoner further incorporates rejection sampling and acceleration design to robustify and speed up decoding. Theoretically, we show that performing inference-time gradient descent in the sample space to maximize reward is dual to aligning an LLM policy via KL-regularized reinforcement learning. Empirically, $
abla$-Reasoner achieves over 20% accuracy improvement on a challenging mathematical reasoning benchmark, while reducing number of model calls by approximately 10-40% compared to strong baselines. Overall, our work introduces a paradigm shift from zeroth-order search to first-order optimization at test time, offering a cost-effective path to amplify LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: First-order optimization during decoding improves LLM reasoning with DTO; ~20% accuracy gain and 10-40% fewer calls.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of discrete search and prompting; enable online policy refinement with differentiable optimization.
AI.method: Introduce ∇-Reasoner with Differentiable Textual Optimization (DTO) that uses gradient signals from LLM likelihood and a reward model, plus rejection sampling and acceleration; gradient descent in sample space dual to KL-regularized RL.
AI.result: Empirical: >20% accuracy on a mathematical reasoning benchmark; 10-40% reduction in model calls vs strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Shifts paradigm from zeroth-order search to first-order test-time optimization; provides cost-effective path to boost LLM reasoning.

title: MultiGO++: Monocular 3D Clothed Human Reconstruction via Geometry-Texture Collaboration
summary: Monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction aims to generate a complete and realistic textured 3D avatar from a single image. Existing methods are commonly trained under multi-view supervision with annotated geometric priors, and during inference, these priors are estimated by the pre-trained network from the monocular input. These methods are constrained by three key limitations: texturally by unavailability of training data, geometrically by inaccurate external priors, and systematically by biased single-modality supervision, all leading to suboptimal reconstruction. To address these issues, we propose a novel reconstruction framework, named MultiGO++, which achieves effective systematic geometry-texture collaboration. It consists of three core parts: (1) A multi-source texture synthesis strategy that constructs 15,000+ 3D textured human scans to improve the performance on texture quality estimation in challenge scenarios; (2) A region-aware shape extraction module that extracts and interacts features of each body region to obtain geometry information and a Fourier geometry encoder that mitigates the modality gap to achieve effective geometry learning; (3) A dual reconstruction U-Net that leverages geometry-texture collaborative features to refine and generate high-fidelity textured 3D human meshes. Extensive experiments on two benchmarks and many in-the-wild cases show the superiority of our method over state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: A monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction method MultiGO++ introduces a geometry-texture collaborative framework with three pillars: (1) a large-scale multi-source texture synthesis to create 15k+ 3D textured scans for texture quality estimation, (2) a region-aware shape extraction with per-region features and a Fourier geometry encoder to narrow modality gaps and learn geometry, and (3) a dual reconstruction U-Net that fuses geometry-texture features to produce high-fidelity textured 3D meshes. Empirical results on two benchmarks and in-the-wild data demonstrate state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Current monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction relies on multi-view supervision and external priors, facing three bottlenecks: (i) lack of richly textured training data, (ii) inaccurate or biased geometry priors, and (iii) modality bias from single-modality supervision, which degrades reconstruction quality.
AI.method: 1) Multi-source texture synthesis to synthesize a large set of textured scans (15k+), improving texture quality estimation. 2) Region-aware shape extraction with per-region feature interaction and a Fourier geometry encoder to improve geometry learning and bridge modality gaps. 3) A dual reconstruction U-Net that fuses geometry and texture features to generate refined, high-fidelity textured meshes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two benchmarks and numerous in-the-wild cases show that MultiGO++ outperforms state-of-the-art methods in texture quality and geometric fidelity, validating effective geometry-texture collaboration.
AI.conclusion: MultiGO++ successfully enables robust geometry-texture collaboration for monocular 3D clothed human reconstruction, alleviating data scarcity, reducing reliance on imperfect priors, and achieving superior textured mesh reconstruction in diverse scenarios.

title: Observing and Controlling Features in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) have shown remarkable progress towards embodied intelligence. While their architecture partially resembles that of Large Language Models (LLMs), VLAs exhibit higher complexity due to their multi-modal inputs/outputs and often hybrid nature of transformer and diffusion heads. This is part of the reason why insights from mechanistic interpretability in LLMs, which explain how the internal model representations relate to their output behavior, do not trivially transfer to VLA counterparts. In this work, we propose to close this gap by introducing and analyzing two main concepts: feature-observability and feature-controllability. In particular, we first study features that are linearly encoded in representation space, and show how they can be observed by means of a linear classifier. Then, we use a minimal linear intervention grounded in optimal control to accurately place internal representations and steer the VLA's output towards a desired region. Our results show that targeted, lightweight interventions can reliably steer a robot's behavior while preserving closed-loop capabilities. We demonstrate on different VLA architectures ($π_{0.5}$ and OpenVLA) through simulation experiments that VLAs possess interpretable internal structure amenable to online adaptation without fine-tuning, enabling real-time alignment with user preferences and task requirements.

AI.tldr: Two concepts: feature-observability and feature-controllability in Vision-Language-Action Models; demonstrate linear feature observability and minimal linear interventions to steer behavior without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Close the gap in mechanistic interpretability for VLAs compared to LLMs and enable real-time alignment with user preferences through lightweight interventions.
AI.method: Identify linearly encoded features via a linear classifier; apply an optimal-control-based, minimal linear intervention to reposition internal representations and steer outputs; validate on simulated VLAs (π0.5 and OpenVLA).
AI.result: Lightweight, linear interventions reliably steer robot behavior while preserving closed-loop capabilities; VLAs exhibit interpretable internal structure amenable to online adaptation without fine-tuning; enabling real-time alignment with user tasks and preferences.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical path to interpretability and controllability in VLAs, showing online adaptation without re-training and facilitating user-aligned behavior in embodied tasks.

title: Reclaiming Lost Text Layers for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (SF-CDFSL) focuses on fine-tuning with limited training data from target domains (e.g., medical or satellite images), where CLIP has recently shown promising results due to its generalizability to downstream tasks. Current works indicate CLIP's text encoder is more suitable for cross-domain tasks, however, we find that \textbf{removing certain middle layers of the text encoder can effectively improve performance in SF-CDFSL}, which we call the Lost Layers. In this paper, we delve into this phenomenon for a deeper understanding. We discover that instead of being harmful for the SF-CDFSL task, the information in these layers is actually beneficial, but visual gaps prevent this useful information from being fully utilized, making these layers seem redundant. Based on this understanding, unlike current works that simply remove these layers, we propose a method to teachs the model to \textbf{re-utilize} information in these lost layers at both the layer and encoder levels, guiding the re-learning of the visual branch under domain shifts. Our approach effectively addresses the issue of underutilized information in the text encoder. Extensive experiments across various settings, backbones (CLIP, SigLip, PE-Core), and tasks (4 CDFSL datasets and 10 Meta-dataset datasets) demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Code is available at https://github.com/zhenyuZ-HUST/CVPR26-VtT.

AI.tldr: Removing middle layers of CLIP's text encoder can hurt SF-CDFSL; the authors discover these 'lost layers' hold useful information blocked by visual gaps and propose re-utilizing this information at layer and encoder levels to improve cross-domain few-shot learning.
AI.motivation: In SF-CDFSL with limited target data, CLIP's text encoder is promising, yet some middle layers appear redundant. The paper investigates why removing these layers sometimes helps and aims to leverage the information they contain.
AI.method: Propose a training strategy to re-utilize information in the lost middle layers at both the layer level (fusing/interacting intermediate representations) and encoder level (aligning and guiding the text and visual branches) to better adapt the visual branch under domain shifts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple backbones (CLIP, SigLip, PE-Core) and datasets (4 CDFSL datasets and 10 Meta-dataset datasets) show the proposed approach improves performance, indicating the usefulness of formerly discarded layers when re-utilized.
AI.conclusion: The underutilization of mid-layer text features due to visual gaps is mitigated by re-utilization at both layer and encoder levels, establishing a new direction for exploiting CLIP in SF-CDFSL and cross-domain transfer tasks.

title: Uncertainty-aware Blood Glucose Prediction from Continuous Glucose Monitoring Data
summary: In this work, we investigate uncertainty-aware neural network models for blood glucose prediction and adverse glycemic event identification in Type 1 diabetes. We consider three families of sequence models based on LSTM, GRU, and Transformer architectures, with uncertainty quantification enabled by either Monte Carlo dropout or through evidential output layers compatible with Deep Evidential Regression. Using the HUPA-UCM diabetes dataset for validation, we find that Transformer-based models equipped with evidential output heads provide the most effective uncertainty-aware framework, achieving consistently higher predictive accuracies and better-calibrated uncertainty estimates whose magnitudes significantly correlate with prediction errors. We further evaluate the clinical risk of each model using the recently proposed Diabetes Technology Society error grid, with risk categories defined by international expert consensus. Our results demonstrate the value of integrating principled uncertainty quantification into real-time machine-learning-based blood glucose prediction systems.

AI.tldr: Transformer with evidential output heads bests peers for uncertainty-aware BG prediction and risk assessment on HUPA-UCM dataset; uncertainty magnitudes align with prediction errors, improving calibration and safety assessment via Diabetes Tech Society error grid.
AI.motivation: Develop reliable, uncertainty-aware ML models for blood glucose prediction and adverse event detection in Type 1 diabetes to support real-time clinical decisions and risk stratification.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of LSTM, GRU, and Transformer sequence models with two uncertainty mechanisms: Monte Carlo dropout and Deep Evidential Regression-based evidential heads. Evaluation on HUPA-UCM diabetes dataset with Diabetes Technology Society error grid for clinical risk assessment.
AI.result: Transformer + evidential heads achieved the highest predictive accuracy and best-calibrated uncertainty estimates; uncertainty magnitudes significantly correlate with prediction errors; favorable risk categorization on the Diabetes Technology Society error grid compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Principled uncertainty quantification, particularly evidential regression in Transformer architectures, is valuable for real-time, uncertainty-aware blood glucose prediction systems and risk assessment.

title: Physics-consistent deep learning for blind aberration recovery in mobile optics
summary: Mobile photography is often limited by complex, lens-specific optical aberrations. While recent deep learning methods approach this as an end-to-end deblurring task, these "black-box" models lack explicit optical modeling and can hallucinate details. Conversely, classical blind deconvolution remains highly unstable. To bridge this gap, we present Lens2Zernike, a deep learning framework that blindly recovers physical optical parameters from a single blurred image. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has simultaneously integrated supervision across three distinct optical domains. We introduce a novel physics-consistent strategy that explicitly minimizes errors via direct Zernike coefficient regression (z), differentiable physics constraints encompassing both wavefront and point spread function derivations (p), and auxiliary multi-task spatial map predictions (m). Through an ablation study on a ResNet-18 backbone, we demonstrate that our full multi-task framework (z+p+m) yields a 35% improvement over coefficient-only baselines. Crucially, comparative analysis reveals that our approach outperforms two established deep learning methods from previous literature, achieving significantly lower regression errors. Ultimately, we demonstrate that these recovered physical parameters enable stable non-blind deconvolution, providing substantial in-domain improvement on the patented Institute for Digital Molecular Analytics and Science (IDMxS) Mobile Camera Lens Database for restoring diffraction-limited details from severely aberrated mobile captures.

AI.tldr: Lens2Zernike is a multi-task deep learning framework that recovers physical optical Zernike coefficients from a single blurred image and uses differentiable physics constraints to enable stable, physically grounded non-blind deconvolution, outperforming prior DL methods on mobile lens data.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of end-to-end blind deblurring (hallucinations, lack of explicit optics) and the instability of classical blind deconvolution by recovering interpretable optical parameters.
AI.method: Multi-task architecture with three supervised components: (z) direct regression of Zernike coefficients, (p) differentiable physics constraints covering wavefront and PSF derivations, and (m) auxiliary spatial maps; ablation study on ResNet-18 showing full z+p+m outperforms coefficient-only baselines; evaluation against two prior DL methods and demonstration on the IDMxS Mobile Camera Lens Database for in-domain deconvolution.
AI.result: Full model (z+p+m) yields ~35% improvement over coefficient-only baselines; outperforms two established deep learning methods with lower regression errors; enables stable non-blind deconvolution and restoration of diffraction-limited details in severely aberrated mobile captures.
AI.conclusion: Integrating supervision across optical domains yields better physical parameter estimation and practical deconvolution performance, enabling more reliable mobile photography under complex aberrations.

title: cuRoboV2: Dynamics-Aware Motion Generation with Depth-Fused Distance Fields for High-DoF Robots
summary: Effective robot autonomy requires motion generation that is safe, feasible, and reactive. Current methods are fragmented: fast planners output physically unexecutable trajectories, reactive controllers struggle with high-fidelity perception, and existing solvers fail on high-DoF systems. We present cuRoboV2, a unified framework with three key innovations: (1) B-spline trajectory optimization that enforces smoothness and torque limits; (2) a GPU-native TSDF/ESDF perception pipeline that generates dense signed distance fields covering the full workspace, unlike existing methods that only provide distances within sparsely allocated blocks, up to 10x faster and in 8x less memory than the state-of-the-art at manipulation scale, with up to 99% collision recall; and (3) scalable GPU-native whole-body computation, namely topology-aware kinematics, differentiable inverse dynamics, and map-reduce self-collision, that achieves up to 61x speedup while also extending to high-DoF humanoids (where previous GPU implementations fail). On benchmarks, cuRoboV2 achieves 99.7% success under 3kg payload (where baselines achieve only 72--77%), 99.6% collision-free IK on a 48-DoF humanoid (where prior methods fail entirely), and 89.5% retargeting constraint satisfaction (vs. 61% for PyRoki); these collision-free motions yield locomotion policies with 21% lower tracking error than PyRoki and 12x lower cross-seed variance than mink. A ground-up codebase redesign for discoverability enabled LLM coding assistants to author up to 73% of new modules, including hand-optimized CUDA kernels, demonstrating that well-structured robotics code can unlock productive human--LLM collaboration. Together, these advances provide a unified, dynamics-aware motion generation stack that scales from single-arm manipulators to full humanoids.

AI.tldr: CuRoboV2 delivers a unified, GPU‑accelerated motion generation stack that fuses B‑spline trajectory optimization, dense GPU TSDF/ESDF perception, and scalable whole‑body GPU computation, achieving large speedups, high success and collision‑free metrics, and enabling LLM‑assisted development from single arms to humanoids.
AI.motivation: To overcome fragmentation in robot autonomy pipelines (planning, perception, dynamics) and to enable safe, feasible, and reactive motion for high‑DoF systems with real‑time performance, while promoting scalable, collaborative development with LLMs.
AI.method: Three core innovations: (1) B‑spline trajectory optimization with smoothness and torque constraints; (2) GPU‑native TSDF/ESDF perception producing dense signed distance fields over the full workspace with up to 10× speed and 8× memory savings versus state‑of‑the‑art, and high collision recall; (3) scalable GPU‑native whole‑body computation including topology‑aware kinematics, differentiable inverse dynamics, and map‑reduce self‑collision, enabling high‑DoF humanoids and yielding up to 61× speedup; plus a ground‑up codebase redesign enabling LLMs to author a large share of new modules (up to 73%).
AI.result: On benchmarks, cuRoboV2 achieves 99.7% success under 3 kg payload (vs 72–77% baselines), 99.6% collision‑free IK on a 48‑DoF humanoid (prior methods fail), and 89.5% retargeting constraint satisfaction (vs 61% for PyRoki). Collision‑free motions support locomotion policies with 21% lower tracking error than PyRoki and 12× lower cross‑seed variance than Mink. Memory and speed claims include up to 10× faster perception and 8× less memory, 61× compute speedups for full‑body processing, and robust generalization across manipulator to humanoid scales.
AI.conclusion: cuRoboV2 provides a unified, dynamics‑aware motion generation stack scalable from single‑arm manipulators to full humanoids, delivering superior performance, safety, and reactivity, while enabling productive human–LLM collaboration through a refactored codebase.

title: GCAgent: Enhancing Group Chat Communication through Dialogue Agents System
summary: As a key form in online social platforms, group chat is a popular space for interest exchange or problem-solving, but its effectiveness is often hindered by inactivity and management challenges. While recent large language models (LLMs) have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their seamlessly integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce GCAgent, an LLM-driven system for enhancing group chats communication with both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents. The system comprises three tightly integrated modules: Agent Builder, which customizes agents to align with users' interests; Dialogue Manager, which coordinates dialogue states and manage agent invocations; and Interface Plugins, which reduce interaction barriers by three distinct tools. Through extensive experiment, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04\% of cases compared to its base model. Additionally, in real-world deployments over 350 days, it increased message volume by 28.80\%, significantly improving group activity and engagement. Overall, this work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agent from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios.

AI.tldr: GCAgent is an LLM-driven system that extends single-user dialogue agents to multi-party group chats via three modules (Agent Builder, Dialogue Manager, Interface Plugins), improving engagement and activity with strong user preference signals and real-world deployment gains.
AI.motivation: To address inactivity, management challenges, and the lack of integrated LLM support for multi-party group chats on online social platforms.
AI.method: Three-module architecture: Agent Builder (customizes agents to users' interests), Dialogue Manager (coordinates dialogue states and agent invocations), and Interface Plugins (three tools to reduce interaction barriers). Evaluation includes user preference and scoring: average 4.68/5 across criteria; 51.04% preference over base model; real-world deployment (>350 days) with a 28.80% increase in message volume.
AI.result: Demonstrated improved group activity and engagement; higher user preference over the base model; practical blueprint for scaling LLM agents from one-to-one to multi-party group conversations.
AI.conclusion: Shows the practicality of extending LLM-based dialogue agents to multi-party group settings and provides a concrete blueprint for future research and deployment.

title: WaterSIC: information-theoretically (near) optimal linear layer quantization
summary: This paper considers the problem of converting a given dense linear layer to low precision. The tradeoff between compressed length and output discrepancy is analyzed information theoretically (IT). It is shown that a popular GPTQ algorithm may have an arbitrarily large gap to the IT limit. To alleviate this problem, a novel algorithm, termed ''WaterSIC'', is proposed and is shown to be within a rate gap of 0.255 bits to the IT limit, uniformly over all possible covariance matrices of input activations. The key innovation of WaterSIC's is to allocate different quantization rates to different columns (in-features) of the weight matrix, mimicking the classical IT solution known as ''waterfilling''. Applying WaterSIC to the Llama and Qwen family of LLMs establishes new state-of-the-art performance for all quantization rates from 1 to 4 bits.

AI.tldr: Introduces WaterSIC, a per-column rate allocation quantization scheme for dense neural layers that approaches the information-theoretic limit (within 0.255 bits) and achieves state-of-the-art results for Llama/Qwen quantization at 1–4 bits, improving over GPTQ.
AI.motivation: Quantizing dense linear layers with minimal accuracy loss is essential for efficient model deployment. Existing IT-guided bounds are not tight for practical quantizers like GPTQ, motivating a method that closely approaches the IT limit across input statistics.
AI.method: Per-column (in-feature) rate allocation inspired by waterfilling, designed to minimize output discrepancy under IT constraints. The analysis yields a uniform 0.255-bit gap to the IT limit for any input covariance. WaterSIC is evaluated on Llama and Qwen to demonstrate practical gains across 1–4 bit quantization.
AI.result: The proposed WaterSIC method attains a universal rate gap of at most 0.255 bits to the IT limit, independent of input covariance, and delivers new state-of-the-art quantization performance for Llama and Qwen models across 1–4 bit regimes.
AI.conclusion: Column-wise rate adaptation guided by information-theoretic insights can nearly close the gap to the IT limit for dense layer quantization, enabling robust, low-precision deployment of large language models with strong empirical performance.

title: How far have we gone in Generative Image Restoration? A study on its capability, limitations and evaluation practices
summary: Generative Image Restoration (GIR) has achieved impressive perceptual realism, but how far have its practical capabilities truly advanced compared with previous methods? To answer this, we present a large-scale study grounded in a new multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline that assesses models on detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall quality. Our analysis covers diverse architectures, including diffusion-based, GAN-based, PSNR-oriented, and general-purpose generation models, revealing critical performance disparities. Furthermore, our analysis uncovers a key evolution in failure modes that signifies a paradigm shift for the perception-oriented low-level vision field. The central challenge is evolving from the previous problem of detail scarcity (under-generation) to the new frontier of detail quality and semantic control (preventing over-generation). We also leverage our benchmark to train a new IQA model that better aligns with human perceptual judgments. Ultimately, this work provides a systematic study of modern generative image restoration models, offering crucial insights that redefine our understanding of their true state and chart a course for future development.

AI.tldr: Large-scale, multi-dimensional evaluation of Generative Image Restoration (GIR) across architectures reveals a shift from under-generation to over-generation as the new failure mode, and introduces an IQA model aligned with human judgments.
AI.motivation: Quantify practical capabilities of GIR and establish a comprehensive benchmark to compare diffusion-based, GAN-based, PSNR-oriented, and general-purpose models on perceptual detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall quality.
AI.method: Develop a multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline; analyze diverse architectures; quantify dimensions (detail, sharpness, semantics, overall quality); examine failure modes; train a new IQA model using benchmark data to align with human perception.
AI.result: Identify significant performance disparities across architectures; observe a paradigm shift in failure modes from under-generation to over-generation controls; benchmark enables training an IQA model that better matches human judgments; provides systematic insights for future development.
AI.conclusion: A systematic study clarifies the current state of GIR, highlights critical evaluation dimensions, and guides future research directions for improving practical performance and evaluation standards.

title: Safe-SAGE: Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement through Laplace-Modulated Poisson Safety Functions
summary: Traditional safety-critical control methods, such as control barrier functions, suffer from semantic blindness, exhibiting the same behavior around obstacles regardless of contextual significance. This limitation leads to the uniform treatment of all obstacles, despite their differing semantic meanings. We present Safe-SAGE (Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement), a unified framework that bridges the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level safety-critical control through a Poisson safety function (PSF) modulated using a Laplace guidance field. Our approach perceives the environment by fusing multi-sensor point clouds with vision-based instance segmentation and persistent object tracking to maintain up-to-date semantics beyond the camera's field of view. A multi-layer safety filter is then used to modulate system inputs to achieve safe navigation using this semantic understanding of the environment. This safety filter consists of both a model predictive control layer and a control barrier function layer. Both layers utilize the PSF and flux modulation of the guidance field to introduce varying levels of conservatism and multi-agent passing norms for different obstacles in the environment. Our framework enables legged robots to navigate semantically rich, dynamic environments with context-dependent safety margins while maintaining rigorous safety guarantees.

AI.tldr: Safe-SAGE is a unified framework that couples semantic perception with a Poisson safety function (PSF) to enable context-dependent, safety-guaranteed navigation for legged robots through MPC/CBF modulation and a Laplace-guided guidance field.
AI.motivation: Traditional safety methods (e.g., control barrier functions) lack semantic awareness and treat all obstacles uniformly, leading to overly conservative or unsafe behavior in semantically distinct contexts. A semantically aware safety layer is needed for robust navigation in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Perceive with fused multi-sensor point clouds, vision-based instance segmentation, and persistent object tracking; modulate a Poisson safety function (PSF) with a Laplace guidance field; implement a multi-layer safety filter combining model predictive control (MPC) and control barrier function (CBF) layers; use PSF flux modulation to adjust conservatism and passing norms for different obstacles; apply to legged robots in semantically rich, dynamic environments.
AI.result: Proposes a framework that yields context-dependent safety margins and hypotheses of safe navigation with safety guarantees by integrating semantic information into low-level safety controls.
AI.conclusion: Safe-SAGE integrates high-level semantic understanding with low-level safety-critical control, enabling context-aware obstacle handling and adaptive safety margins while maintaining rigorous safety guarantees.

title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.

AI.tldr: X-RAY provides an explainable reasoning analysis for LLMs using calibrated, formally verified probes to quantify reasoning as extractable structure; reveals asymmetries and enables precise model differentiation and failure-mode analysis.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations emphasize task-level accuracy and often conflate pattern matching with reasoning; there is a need for controlled, structure-based probes to isolate genuine reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Define reasoning capability as a function of extractable structure. Use formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. Generate probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations; apply calibration and formal verification; maintain a contamination-free framework; evaluate LLMs on problems spanning junior to advanced math, physics, and chemistry.
AI.result: LLMs show a systematic asymmetry: robustness to constraint refinement (adding conditions narrows the solution space) but sharp degradation under solution-space restructuring (changing the underlying structure of the solution manifold). Calibrated probes differentiate models that look similar on standard benchmarks and reveal interpretable, structure-based failure modes.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables contamination-free training/testing of reasoning models and provides a precise, structure-focused lens on reasoning capabilities beyond traditional task-level benchmarks.

title: Mixture of Universal Experts: Scaling Virtual Width via Depth-Width Transformation
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decouples model capacity from per-token computation, yet their scalability remains limited by the physical dimensions of depth and width. To overcome this, we propose Mixture of Universal Experts (MOUE),a MoE generalization introducing a novel scaling dimension: Virtual Width. In general, MoUE aims to reuse a universal layer-agnostic expert pool across layers, converting depth into virtual width under a fixed per-token activation budget. However, two challenges remain: a routing path explosion from recursive expert reuse, and a mismatch between the exposure induced by reuse and the conventional load-balancing objectives. We address these with three core components: a Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing, a Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and a Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing. Empirically, MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.

AI.tldr: MOUE generalizes MoE by introducing a universal, layer-agnostic pool of experts and a virtual width scaling, enabling depth-to-width conversion under fixed activation budgets while addressing routing and exposure issues with three components; it yields empirical gains over MoE baselines and enables checkpoint reuse.
AI.motivation: MoE decouples capacity from per-token compute but scales poorly due to fixed depth/width; there is a need to scale MoE beyond traditional depth/width constraints by reusing experts across layers to effectively increase capacity without increasing per-token cost.
AI.method: Introduce three components: (1) Staggered Rotational Topology for structured, reusable expert sharing; (2) Universal Expert Load Balance to correct depth-aware exposure; (3) Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing; reuse a universal expert pool across layers to convert depth into virtual width under a fixed activation budget.
AI.result: MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes; enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains.
AI.conclusion: MoUE reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures by enabling virtual width via universal expert sharing and targeted routing/exposure strategies, improving scalability and enabling checkpoint reuse.

title: Tell2Adapt: A Unified Framework for Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation via Vision Foundation Model
comment: Accepted by IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2026)
summary: Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SFUDA) is critical for deploying deep learning models across diverse clinical settings. However, existing methods are typically designed for low-gap, specific domain shifts and cannot generalize into a unified, multi-modalities, and multi-target framework, which presents a major barrier to real-world application. To overcome this issue, we introduce Tell2Adapt, a novel SFUDA framework that harnesses the vast, generalizable knowledge of the Vision Foundation Model (VFM). Our approach ensures high-fidelity VFM prompts through Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR), which robustly translates varied text prompts into canonical instructions. This enables the generation of high-quality pseudo-labels for efficiently adapting the lightweight student model to target domain. To guarantee clinical reliability, the framework incorporates Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR), which leverages the VFM's anatomical knowledge to re-ground the adapted model's predictions in target image's low-level visual features, effectively removing noise and false positives. We conduct one of the most extensive SFUDA evaluations to date, validating our framework across 10 domain adaptation directions and 22 anatomical targets, including brain, cardiac, polyp, and abdominal targets. Our results demonstrate that Tell2Adapt consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving SOTA for a unified SFUDA framework in medical image segmentation. Code are avaliable at https://github.com/derekshiii/Tell2Adapt.

AI.tldr: Tell2Adapt is a Vision Foundation Model-guided SFUDA framework for medical image segmentation that uses Context-Aware Prompts Regularization and Visual Plausibility Refinement to generate high-quality pseudo-labels and reliable predictions, achieving state-of-the-art performance across 10 domain directions and 22 anatomical targets; code released.
AI.motivation: Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SFUDA) is essential for deploying medical imaging models across diverse clinical settings, but existing methods struggle with large domain gaps, multi-modality differences, and multi-target generalization, hindering real-world deployment.
AI.method: Tell2Adapt leverages a Vision Foundation Model (VFM) to produce prompts and pseudo-labels. Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR) stabilizes varied prompts into canonical instructions to generate high-quality pseudo-labels for adapting a lightweight student model. Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR) grounds predictions in target image low-level features using anatomical knowledge from the VFM to remove noise/false positives.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across 10 domain directions and 22 anatomical targets (including brain, cardiac, polyp, and abdominal targets) showing consistent improvements over existing methods and achieving state-of-the-art performance for a unified SFUDA framework in medical image segmentation.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of unifying SFUDA for medical image segmentation by leveraging vision foundation models, with broad evaluation and publicly available code (GitHub).

title: RoboPocket: Improve Robot Policies Instantly with Your Phone
summary: Scaling imitation learning is fundamentally constrained by the efficiency of data collection. While handheld interfaces have emerged as a scalable solution for in-the-wild data acquisition, they predominantly operate in an open-loop manner: operators blindly collect demonstrations without knowing the underlying policy's weaknesses, leading to inefficient coverage of critical state distributions. Conversely, interactive methods like DAgger effectively address covariate shift but rely on physical robot execution, which is costly and difficult to scale. To reconcile this trade-off, we introduce RoboPocket, a portable system that enables Robot-Free Instant Policy Iteration using single consumer smartphones. Its core innovation is a Remote Inference framework that visualizes the policy's predicted trajectory via Augmented Reality (AR) Visual Foresight. This immersive feedback allows collectors to proactively identify potential failures and focus data collection on the policy's weak regions without requiring a physical robot. Furthermore, we implement an asynchronous Online Finetuning pipeline that continuously updates the policy with incoming data, effectively closing the learning loop in minutes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboPocket adheres to data scaling laws and doubles the data efficiency compared to offline scaling strategies, overcoming their long-standing efficiency bottleneck. Moreover, our instant iteration loop also boosts sample efficiency by up to 2$\times$ in distributed environments a small number of interactive corrections per person. Project page and videos: https://robo-pocket.github.io.

AI.tldr: RoboPocket enables robot-free imitation learning data collection using smartphones with AR-guided interactive feedback and online finetuning, achieving fast, scalable data-efficient policy iteration.
AI.motivation: To overcome data-collection inefficiency in imitation learning: open-loop handheld demos lack policy insight, while DAgger requires costly robot execution. A scalable, low-cost, closed-loop approach is needed.
AI.method: A portable system with Remote Inference visualizes the policy trajectory via AR Visual Foresight on consumer smartphones; collectors receive immersive feedback to target policy-weak regions without a robot. An asynchronous Online Finetuning pipeline continuously updates the policy with incoming data, closing the learning loop in minutes.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows adherence to data scaling laws and a 2x improvement in data efficiency over offline scaling strategies. The instant iteration loop yields up to 2x gains in sample efficiency in distributed settings with a small number of interactive corrections per user.
AI.conclusion: RoboPocket provides a practical, scalable framework for fast, data-efficient imitation learning without physical robots, enabling rapid policy improvement and scalable data collection.

title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed.
