Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical planning framework (STRUCTUREDAGENT) combines an online dynamic AND/OR-tree planner with a structured memory to address memory, planning, and greedy-behavior issues in long-horizon web tasks, delivering interpretable plans and improved performance on long tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based web agents struggle with long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory, weak planning abilities, and greedy termination. There is a need for scalable, interpretable planning and memory mechanisms to track history and constraints across multiple steps.
AI.method: Two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner using dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search; (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework outputs interpretable hierarchical plans to facilitate debugging and human intervention when needed.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks shows STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.
AI.conclusion: The approach addresses memory and planning bottlenecks in agentic web tasks, yielding interpretable plans and improved performance, with potential for easier debugging and human collaboration in long-horizon scenarios.

title: Functionality-Oriented LLM Merging on the Fisher--Rao Manifold
summary:
Weight-space merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining, yet most existing approaches remain fundamentally parameter-space heuristics. This creates three practical limitations. First, linear averaging, task vectors, and related rules operate on Euclidean coordinates, even though the desired goal is to merge functionality, i.e., predictive behaviors across tasks. Second, when the source checkpoints are farther apart or more heterogeneous, Euclidean blends often trigger representation collapse, manifested as activation variance shrinkage and effective-rank degradation, which sharply degrades accuracy. Third, many geometry-inspired methods are most natural for two-model interpolation and do not extend cleanly to merging N>2 experts with a principled objective.
  We address these issues by formulating model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which is locally equivalent to minimizing a KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. We derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes directly to multi-expert merging. Across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics, our method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware weight-space merging method using the weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher–Rao manifold to fuse multiple fine-tuned LLMs, mitigating representation collapse and scaling to many experts, surpassing Euclidean-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing weight-space merges rely on Euclidean operations (e.g., linear averaging, task vectors) to blend models, which conceptually merge functionality but suffer when sources are heterogeneous or when merging more than two models; they can trigger activation variance shrinkage, reduced effective rank, and poor performance as sources diverge. A principled, geometry-aware approach is needed to preserve predictive behavior across tasks in multi-expert fusion.
AI.method: Formulate merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher–Rao manifold, which locally minimizes a KL-based distance between predictive distributions. Derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes to multi-expert merging (N>2).
AI.result: Empirically, the method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase and consistently outperforms prior baselines across benchmarks and collapse diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware, Fisher–Rao–based approach provides a principled, scalable solution for multi-expert weight-space merging, overcoming representation collapse and extending cleanly to N>2 models with improved predictive fidelity.

title: Generalizable Multiscale Segmentation of Heterogeneous Map Collections
summary: Historical map collections are highly diverse in style, scale, and geographic focus, often consisting of many single-sheet documents. Yet most work in map recognition focuses on specialist models tailored to homogeneous map series. In contrast, this article aims to develop generalizable semantic segmentation models and ontology. First, we introduce Semap, a new open benchmark dataset comprising 1,439 manually annotated patches designed to reflect the variety of historical map documents. Second, we present a segmentation framework that combines procedural data synthesis with multiscale integration to improve robustness and transferability. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on both the HCMSSD and Semap datasets, showing that a diversity-driven approach to map recognition is not only viable but also beneficial. The results indicate that segmentation performance remains largely stable across map collections, scales, geographic regions, and publication contexts. By proposing benchmark datasets and methods for the generic segmentation of historical maps, this work opens the way to integrating the long tail of cartographic archives to historical geographic studies.

AI.tldr: Introduces Semap, an open benchmark for historical map semantic segmentation, and a generalizable segmentation framework that uses procedural data synthesis and multiscale integration to achieve state-of-the-art results on HCMSSD and Semap, with robustness across collections and contexts.
AI.motivation: Historical map archives are diverse in style, scale, and geographic focus, leading to models that struggle to generalize. A broad benchmark and a diversity-driven segmentation approach are needed to enable scalable, cross-collection analysis and to support historical geography studies.
AI.method: (1) Compile Semap: 1,439 manually annotated patches capturing diversity in historical maps. (2) Develop a segmentation framework combining procedural data synthesis (synthetic data generation) with multiscale feature integration to improve robustness and transferability. (3) Evaluate on HCMSSD and Semap datasets.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on both HCMSSD and Semap. Segmentation performance is reported to be largely stable across map collections, scales, geographic regions, and publication contexts.
AI.conclusion: A diversity-driven, generalizable segmentation approach coupled with benchmark datasets is viable and beneficial for integrating the long tail of cartographic archives into historical geographic studies.

title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
summary: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces WebChain dataset of 31,725 real-world website trajectories with 318k steps and a Dual Mid-Training recipe achieving SOTA on GUI benchmarks, enabling reproducible web-agent research via triple-modal supervision.
AI.motivation: Address reproducibility and realism gaps in web-agent research by providing a large, annotated, real-user-like dataset and a training paradigm that separates spatial grounding from planning to improve performance.
AI.method: Collect open-source real-world website trajectories; annotate with visual, structural, and action data; define Triple Alignment; propose Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning; evaluate on WebChainBench and public GUI benchmarks.
AI.result: Dataset of 31,725 trajectories, 318k steps; state-of-the-art performance on WebChainBench and other GUI benchmarks; demonstration of scalability and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: WebChain provides data and insights to build next-gen scalable web agents; promotes rigorous evaluation and reuse.

title: Lightweight and Scalable Transfer Learning Framework for Load Disaggregation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) aims to estimate appliance-level consumption from aggregate electrical signals recorded at a single measurement point. In recent years, the field has increasingly adopted deep learning approaches; however, cross-domain generalization remains a persistent challenge due to variations in appliance characteristics, usage patterns, and background loads across homes. Transfer learning provides a practical paradigm to adapt models with limited target data. However, existing methods often assume a fixed appliance set, lack flexibility for evolving real-world deployments, remain unsuitable for edge devices, or scale poorly for real-time operation. This paper proposes RefQuery, a scalable multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set. RefQuery keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to a target home by learning only a per-appliance embedding during a lightweight backpropagation stage. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that RefQuery delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods. These results support RefQuery as a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: RefQuery is a scalable NILM framework that uses a frozen pretrained network plus per-appliance embeddings to adapt to new homes without a fixed output set, enabling real-time, edge-friendly multi-appliance disaggregation.
AI.motivation: Address cross-domain generalization in NILM, handle evolving appliance sets, and enable real-time, resource-constrained deployments.
AI.method: Propose RefQuery: a multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework where disaggregation is conditioned on compact appliance fingerprints. A shared model with a frozen pretrained network is adapted to a target home by learning per-appliance embeddings via lightweight backpropagation; no fixed output set is required.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets show RefQuery achieves favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including Transformer-based methods.
AI.conclusion: RefQuery offers a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on edge devices, accommodating streaming deployments and evolving appliance sets with minimal retraining.

title: Exploiting Intermediate Reconstructions in Optical Coherence Tomography for Test-Time Adaption of Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Primary health care frequently relies on low-cost imaging devices, which are commonly used for screening purposes. To ensure accurate diagnosis, these systems depend on advanced reconstruction algorithms designed to approximate the performance of high-quality counterparts. Such algorithms typically employ iterative reconstruction methods that incorporate domain-specific prior knowledge. However, downstream task performance is generally assessed using only the final reconstructed image, thereby disregarding the informative intermediate representations generated throughout the reconstruction process. In this work, we propose IRTTA to exploit these intermediate representations at test-time by adapting the normalization-layer parameters of a frozen downstream network via a modulator network that conditions on the current reconstruction timescale. The modulator network is learned during test-time using an averaged entropy loss across all individual timesteps. Variation among the timestep-wise segmentations additionally provides uncertainty estimates at no extra cost. This approach enhances segmentation performance and enables semantically meaningful uncertainty estimation, all without modifying either the reconstruction process or the downstream model.

AI.tldr: A test-time adaptive normalization modulator (IRTTA) leverages intermediate iterative-reconstruction representations to improve segmentation in low-cost imaging and provide uncertainty estimates without changing the reconstruction or downstream model.
AI.motivation: Primary health-care imaging often uses cheap devices with iterative reconstruction that produce informative intermediate representations. Current task evaluation focuses on final images, ignoring these intermediates that could improve downstream performance and provide uncertainty estimation.
AI.method: Freeze the downstream segmentation network and introduce a modulator network that outputs normalization-layer parameters conditioned on the current reconstruction timescale. Train the modulator at test-time using an averaged entropy loss across all timesteps. Use variation across timestep-wise segmentations to obtain uncertainty estimates at no extra cost.
AI.result: Segmentation performance is enhanced and semantically meaningful uncertainty estimates emerge from timestep-wise variability, without altering the reconstruction process or the downstream model.
AI.conclusion: IRTTA demonstrates that leveraging intermediate representations via a test-time normalization modulation approach can improve downstream segmentation and provide uncertainty estimates without modifying the reconstruction or the base model.

title: UniSTOK: Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging
summary: Spatio-temporal kriging aims to infer signals at unobserved locations from observed sensors and is critical to applications such as transportation and environmental monitoring. In practice, however, observed sensors themselves often exhibit heterogeneous missingness, forcing inductive kriging models to rely on crudely imputed inputs. This setting brings three key challenges: (1) it is unclear whether an value is a true signal or a missingness-induced artifact; (2) missingness is highly heterogeneous across sensors and time; (3) missing observations distort the local spatio-temporal structure. To address these issues, we propose Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK), a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones under missing observation. Our framework forms a dual-branch input consisting of the original observations and a jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries. The two branches are then processed in parallel by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Their outputs are finally adaptively fused via dual-channel attention. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements.

AI.tldr: A plug-and-play UniSTOK framework enhances inductive spatio-temporal kriging under missing data by using a dual-branch input (original observations and jigsaw-augmented proxies) processed by a shared backbone with mask modulation, fused via dual-channel attention, yielding consistent improvements across real datasets.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous missingness in sensor networks obscures signal, creates artifacts, and distorts local spatio-temporal structure, making robust inductive kriging challenging; a modular method to separate signal from missingness effects is needed.
AI.method: Introduce UniSTOK with a dual-branch input: one branch uses the original observations; the other uses a jigsaw-augmented proxy that synthesizes signals only at missing entries. Both branches are processed by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Outputs are adaptively fused via dual-channel attention, and the framework is plug-and-play with existing inductive kriging backbones.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns show consistent and significant improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: UniSTOK provides a robust, modular approach to missing data in spatio-temporal kriging by leveraging dual inputs and attention-based fusion, improving inference while remaining compatible with existing backbones.

title: Competitive Multi-Operator Reinforcement Learning for Joint Pricing and Fleet Rebalancing in AMoD Systems
summary: Autonomous Mobility-on-Demand (AMoD) systems promise to revolutionize urban transportation by providing affordable on-demand services to meet growing travel demand. However, realistic AMoD markets will be competitive, with multiple operators competing for passengers through strategic pricing and fleet deployment. While reinforcement learning has shown promise in optimizing single-operator AMoD control, existing work fails to capture competitive market dynamics. We investigate the impact of competition on policy learning by introducing a multi-operator reinforcement learning framework where two operators simultaneously learn pricing and fleet rebalancing policies. By integrating discrete choice theory, we enable passenger allocation and demand competition to emerge endogenously from utility-maximizing decisions. Experiments using real-world data from multiple cities demonstrate that competition fundamentally alters learned behaviors, leading to lower prices and distinct fleet positioning patterns compared to monopolistic settings. Notably, we demonstrate that learning-based approaches are robust to the additional stochasticity of competition, with competitive agents successfully converging to effective policies while accounting for partially unobserved competitor strategies.

AI.tldr: Two operators jointly learn pricing and fleet rebalancing via MARL; endogenizes demand via discrete choice; competition lowers prices and shifts fleet position; robust to stochastic competition.
AI.motivation: Realistic AMoD markets will have multiple operators whose strategic pricing and deployment affect outcomes; existing single-operator RL lacks competitive dynamics.
AI.method: A two-operator MARL framework where both operators learn pricing and fleet rebalancing policies; discrete choice-based passenger allocation leads to endogenous demand; uses real-city data.
AI.result: Competition yields lower prices and distinctive fleet patterns compared to monopolistic scenarios; learning approaches remain robust under competition; policies converge despite partially observed competitor strategies.
AI.conclusion: Integrating discrete choice-based demand modeling with multi-operator RL captures essential competitive dynamics in AMoD; has implications for market design and regulation; future work could scale to more operators and incorporate richer demand models.

title: CoIn3D: Revisiting Configuration-Invariant Multi-Camera 3D Object Detection
summary: Multi-camera 3D object detection (MC3D) has attracted increasing attention with the growing deployment of multi-sensor physical agents, such as robots and autonomous vehicles. However, MC3D models still struggle to generalize to unseen platforms with new multi-camera configurations. Current solutions simply employ a meta-camera for unified representation but lack comprehensive consideration. In this paper, we revisit this issue and identify that the devil lies in spatial prior discrepancies across source and target configurations, including different intrinsics, extrinsics, and array layouts. To address this, we propose CoIn3D, a generalizable MC3D framework that enables strong transferability from source configurations to unseen target ones. CoIn3D explicitly incorporates all identified spatial priors into both feature embedding and image observation through spatial-aware feature modulation (SFM) and camera-aware data augmentation (CDA), respectively. SFM enriches feature space by integrating four spatial representations, such as focal length, ground depth, ground gradient, and Plücker coordinate. CDA improves observation diversity under various configurations via a training-free dynamic novel-view image synthesis scheme. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoIn3D achieves strong cross-configuration performance on landmark datasets such as NuScenes, Waymo, and Lyft, under three dominant MC3D paradigms represented by BEVDepth, BEVFormer, and PETR.

AI.tldr: CoIn3D is a generalizable MC3D framework that tackles cross-configuration transfer by modeling spatial priors (intrinsics, extrinsics, array layouts) via spatial-aware feature modulation and camera-aware data augmentation, enabling robust performance across unseen camera setups and three popular MC3D paradigms.
AI.motivation: MC3D models fail to generalize to unseen platforms/configurations due to spatial prior discrepancies (intrinsics, extrinsics, array layouts). Existing meta-camera approaches are insufficient; a systematic handling of spatial priors is needed.
AI.method: CoIn3D introduces two components: (1) spatial-aware feature modulation (SFM) that embeds four spatial representations (focal length, ground depth, ground gradient, Plücker coordinates) into features; (2) camera-aware data augmentation (CDA) with a training-free dynamic novel-view synthesis to diversify observations across configurations. The framework is evaluated across BEVDepth, BEVFormer, and PETR on NuScenes, Waymo, Lyft.
AI.result: CoIn3D achieves strong cross-configuration transfer in MC3D, demonstrating robust generalization to unseen camera setups and configurations, with consistent improvements across three major paradigms (BEVDepth, BEVFormer, PETR) on landmark datasets NuScenes, Waymo, and Lyft.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling spatial priors and providing a per-configuration augmentation strategy yields substantial generalization gains for MC3D. CoIn3D is a broadly applicable solution for transferring MC3D models across diverse multi-camera configurations.

title: Building AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
comment: Work in progress, new versions will be updated continuously
summary: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

AI.tldr: OPENDEV is an open-source CLI-based autonomous coding agent designed for terminal-first software engineering, addressing context management and safety with a compound AI architecture, dual planning/execution agents, lazy tool discovery, adaptive context compaction, and cross-session memory.
AI.motivation: Shift AI coding assistants from IDE plugins to terminal-native agents that operate where developers manage source control, builds, and deployments, enabling long-horizon development tasks with greater autonomy.
AI.method: A compound AI system: workload-specialized model routing; a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution; lazy tool discovery; adaptive context compaction to progressively reduce older observations; automated memory across sessions; event-driven system reminders to counteract instruction fade-out; explicit reasoning phases to enforce safe, structured decision-making.
AI.result: A secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance and a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering, addressing context limits and safety in long-running tasks.
AI.conclusion: OPENDEV demonstrates how to balance autonomy with safety in CLI agents, providing design patterns for maintaining context efficiency, memory across sessions, and explicit reasoning to support robust, long-horizon autonomous software development.

title: Non-Euclidean Gradient Descent Operates at the Edge of Stability
summary: The Edge of Stability (EoS) is a phenomenon where the sharpness (largest eigenvalue) of the Hessian converges to $2/η$ during training with gradient descent (GD) with a step-size $η$. Despite (apparently) violating classical smoothness assumptions, EoS has been widely observed in deep learning, but its theoretical foundations remain incomplete. We provide an interpretation of EoS through the lens of Directional Smoothness Mishkin et al. [2024]. This interpretation naturally extends to non-Euclidean norms, which we use to define generalized sharpness under an arbitrary norm. Our generalized sharpness measure includes previously studied vanilla GD and preconditioned GD as special cases, as well as methods for which EoS has not been studied, such as $\ell_{\infty}$-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum. Through experiments on neural networks, we show that non-Euclidean GD with our generalized sharpness also exhibits progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around or above the threshold $2/η$. Practically, our framework provides a single, geometry-aware spectral measure that works across optimizers.

AI.tldr: Geometry-aware generalized sharpness explains Edge of Stability across non-Euclidean norms and various optimizers; experiments show progressive sharpening with oscillations near/above 2/η; provides a universal spectral measure.
AI.motivation: EoS lacks complete theoretical foundations; need a norm-agnostic, geometry-aware framework to unify sharpness across optimizers.
AI.method: Introduce generalized sharpness under arbitrary norm via Directional Smoothness; extend to non-Euclidean GD; analyze vanilla GD, preconditioned GD, ℓ∞-descent, Block Coordinate Descent, Spectral GD, Muon without momentum; validate with neural network experiments.
AI.result: Generalized sharpness accounts for EoS across methods; progressive sharpening with oscillations observed; single spectral measure applicable across optimizers.
AI.conclusion: Brings a unified, geometry-aware spectral framework for sharpness; extends EoS understanding and informs optimizer design.

title: CLIP-driven Zero-shot Learning with Ambiguous Labels
comment: Accepted by ICASSP 2026 (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing)
summary: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize unseen classes by leveraging semantic information from seen classes, but most existing methods assume accurate class labels for training instances. However, in real-world scenarios, noise and ambiguous labels can significantly reduce the performance of ZSL. To address this, we propose a new CLIP-driven partial label zero-shot learning (CLIP-PZSL) framework to handle label ambiguity. First, we use CLIP to extract instance and label features. Then, a semantic mining block fuses these features to extract discriminative label embeddings. We also introduce a partial zero-shot loss, which assigns weights to candidate labels based on their relevance to the instance and aligns instance and label embeddings to minimize semantic mismatch. As the training goes on, the ground-truth labels are progressively identified, and the refined labels and label embeddings in turn help improve the semantic alignment of instance and label features. Comprehensive experiments on several datasets demonstrate the advantage of CLIP-PZSL.

AI.tldr: Introduces CLIP-PZSL, a CLIP-based partial label zero-shot learning framework that handles noisy/ambiguous labels via semantic mining, a partial zero-shot loss with per-label weights, and progressive label refinement, achieving improved performance on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: ZSL performance degrades with label noise and ambiguity in training data. There is a need to exploit strong multimodal representations (CLIP) and iterative refinement to identify true labels without requiring fully clean annotations.
AI.method: Use CLIP to extract features for both instances and candidate labels; a semantic mining block fuses these features to produce discriminative label embeddings; a partial zero-shot loss assigns weights to candidate labels by relevance to the instance and aligns instance and label embeddings to reduce semantic mismatch; during training, ground-truth labels are progressively identified, and refined labels/embeddings further improve alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on several datasets show the proposed method outperforms baselines, demonstrating robustness to label noise and the effectiveness of semantic mining and progressive labeling; ablation studies corroborate the contributions of each component.
AI.conclusion: CLIP-PZSL offers an effective framework for learning with partial labels in ZSL by leveraging CLIP features, semantic mining, and iterative refinement, achieving superior performance over existing approaches.

title: Ailed: A Psyche-Driven Chess Engine with Dynamic Emotional Modulation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Open source: https://github.com/chrnx-dev/ailed-chess
summary:
Chess engines passed human strength years ago, but they still don't play like humans. A grandmaster under clock pressure blunders in ways a club player on a hot streak never would. Conventional engines capture none of this.
  This paper proposes a personality x psyche decomposition to produce behavioral variability in chess play, drawing on patterns observed in human games. Personality is static -- a preset that pins down the engine's character. Psyche is dynamic -- a bounded scalar ψ_t \in [-100, +100], recomputed from five positional factors after every move. These two components feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions on the fly. The chain doesn't care what engine sits behind it: any system that outputs move probabilities will do. It needs no search and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
  I test the framework across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, feeding it two probability sources that differ by ~2,800x in training data. Both show the same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp spread from stress to overconfidence), which tells us the behavioral variation comes from the signal chain, not from the model underneath. When the psyche runs overconfident, the chain mostly gets out of the way (66% agreement with vanilla Maia2). Under stress, the competitive score falls from 50.8% to 30.1%. The patterns are reminiscent of tilt and overconfidence as described in human play, but I should be upfront: this study includes no human-subject validation.

AI.tldr: A framework introducing static personality and dynamic psyche to chess engines, modulated by an audio-inspired signal chain to reshape move probabilities, aiming to mimic human behavioral variability under stress/tilt. Tested on 12,414 games against Maia2-1100 with two probability sources; observed monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp) from stress to overconfidence; under overconfidence the chain largely defers to the base engine (66% agreement), while under stress the score drops from 50.8% to 30.1%; no human-subject validation.
AI.motivation: To capture human-like variability in chess play by decoupling behavior into a static personality and a dynamic psyche, and to influence decisions via an auxiliary signal chain that reshapes move probabilities without changing the underlying search. This seeks to bridge gaps between engine strength and human play patterns (tilt, overconfidence).
AI.method: Introduce two components: (1) personality (static preset) and (2) psyche ψ_t, a bounded scalar updated after every move from five positional factors. These feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band EQ, saturation limiter) that alters move probability distributions in real time. The chain is model-agnostic and has no state beyond ψ_t; no search modification. Empirically evaluated on 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, using two probability sources differing ~2,800x in training data; measured top-move agreement and win rate under varying psyche states.
AI.result: Found a monotonic gradient in top-move agreement of about 20–25 percentage points as psyche transitions from stressed to overconfident. When psyche is overconfident, the chain mostly defers to the underlying engine, achieving ~66% agreement with vanilla Maia2. Under stress, the competitive score drops from 50.8% to 30.1%. Patterns resemble tilt/overconfidence in humans, but the study has no human-subject validation and details on the signal chain integration are limited.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that static personality plus a dynamic psyche, implemented via an audio-style signal chain, can induce human-like behavioral variability in chess engines without altering the core search. It is promising but requires more detailed methodology, ablation studies, and human-subject validation to establish fidelity and generalizability.

title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable results in various tasks. Recent studies reveal that graph backdoor attacks can poison the GNN model to predict test nodes with triggers attached as the target class. However, apart from injecting triggers to training nodes, these graph backdoor attacks generally require altering the labels of trigger-attached training nodes into the target class, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. In this work, we focus on the clean-label graph backdoor attack, a realistic but understudied topic where training labels are not modifiable. According to our preliminary analysis, existing graph backdoor attacks generally fail under the clean-label setting. Our further analysis identifies that the core failure of existing methods lies in their inability to poison the prediction logic of GNN models, leading to the triggers being deemed unimportant for prediction. Therefore, we study a novel problem of effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning the inner prediction logic of GNN models. We propose BA-Logic to solve the problem by coordinating a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BA-Logic

AI.tldr: BA-Logic enables effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning GNN's inner prediction logic via a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator, surpassing prior methods in clean-label settings.
AI.motivation: In realistic scenarios, training labels cannot be modified. Clean-label graph backdoor attacks are currently ineffective because they fail to poison the model's prediction logic; there is a need to study attacks that influence the inner decision process of GNNs under clean-label constraints.
AI.method: Proposes BA-Logic, which coordinates a poisoned node selector with a logic-poisoning trigger generator to jointly poison the inner prediction logic of GNNs, enabling successful backdoor behavior without label modification.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show that BA-Logic significantly improves attack success rate and outperforms state-of-the-art graph backdoor attacks under clean-label settings; code is released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: BA-Logic demonstrates that poisoning the inner prediction logic is an effective strategy for clean-label graph backdoor attacks, highlighting a vulnerability in GNNs under practical constraints and motivating further defense research.

title: A 360-degree Multi-camera System for Blue Emergency Light Detection Using Color Attention RT-DETR and the ABLDataset
comment: 16 pages, 17 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
summary: This study presents an advanced system for detecting blue lights on emergency vehicles, developed using ABLDataset, a curated dataset that includes images of European emergency vehicles under various climatic and geographic conditions. The system employs a configuration of four fisheye cameras, each with a 180-degree horizontal field of view, mounted on the sides of the vehicle. A calibration process enables the azimuthal localization of the detections. Additionally, a comparative analysis of major deep neural network algorithms was conducted, including YOLO (v5, v8, and v10), RetinaNet, Faster R-CNN, and RT-DETR. RT-DETR was selected as the base model and enhanced through the incorporation of a color attention block, achieving an accuracy of 94.7 percent and a recall of 94.1 percent on the test set, with field test detections reaching up to 70 meters. Furthermore, the system estimates the approach angle of the emergency vehicle relative to the center of the car using geometric transformations. Designed for integration into a multimodal system that combines visual and acoustic data, this system has demonstrated high efficiency, offering a promising approach to enhancing Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and road safety.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end system detects blue emergency-vehicle lights using a four-camera fisheye setup and azimuthal localization, evaluating multiple detectors, and selecting RT-DETR with a color attention block to achieve ~94% accuracy/recall on a European dataset, with field detections up to 70 m and angle estimation for multimodal ADAS integration.
AI.motivation: Improve robust recognition and localization of blue lights on emergency vehicles across diverse climates/geographies to enhance driver assistance and road safety.
AI.method: Data: ABLDataset of European emergency vehicles under varied climatic/geographic conditions. Hardware: four fisheye cameras (180° FOV) mounted on vehicle sides. Calibration to obtain azimuthal localization. Detector comparison across YOLO v5/v8/v10, RetinaNet, Faster R-CNN, RT-DETR. RT-DETR selected as base model and augmented with a color attention block. Additional geometric transformations to estimate the approach angle relative to the car center. Multimodal integration with acoustic data. Field testing to assess real-world performance.
AI.result: RT-DETR with color attention achieved 94.7% accuracy and 94.1% recall on the test set; field detections up to 70 meters; angle estimation via geometric transformations; system designed for integration into multimodal ADAS.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a high-performance, scalable approach for blue-light detection on emergency vehicles with robust localization and angle estimation, offering potential to improve ADAS and road safety through multimodal fusion.

title: Decoupling Task and Behavior: A Two-Stage Reward Curriculum in Reinforcement Learning for Robotics
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning is a promising tool for robotic control, yet practical application is often hindered by the difficulty of designing effective reward functions. Real-world tasks typically require optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously, necessitating precise tuning of their weights to learn a policy with the desired characteristics. To address this, we propose a two-stage reward curriculum where we decouple task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. In our method, we first train the agent on a simplified task-only reward function to ensure effective exploration before introducing the full reward that includes auxiliary behavior-related terms such as energy efficiency. Further, we analyze various transition strategies and demonstrate that reusing samples between phases is critical for training stability. We validate our approach on the DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile robot environment, modified to include auxiliary behavioral objectives. Our method proves to be simple yet effective, substantially outperforming baselines trained directly on the full reward while exhibiting higher robustness to specific reward weightings.

AI.tldr: A two-stage reward curriculum decouples task objectives from behavioral terms, improving exploration and robustness in multi-objective robotic RL. Stage 1 uses a task-only reward; Stage 2 adds auxiliary behavior terms (e.g., energy efficiency). Reusing samples between phases enhances stability. Validated on DM Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile-robot setup; outperforms full-reward baselines and is less sensitive to reward weightings.
AI.motivation: Designing effective reward functions for multi-objective robotic control is hard; naive use of full rewards can hinder exploration and require careful weight tuning. A curriculum that separates task objectives from behavioral incentives could improve learning stability and robustness.
AI.method: Two-stage curriculum: (1) train with a simplified task-only reward to encourage exploration; (2) gradually introduce the full reward including auxiliary behavioral terms. Analyze transition strategies and show that sample reuse between phases stabilizes training. Validate on DM Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile-robot environment with added behavioral objectives.
AI.result: The approach substantially outperforms baselines trained directly on the full reward. It also exhibits higher robustness to the choice of reward weightings and demonstrates stability when reusing samples across phases.
AI.conclusion: A simple two-stage reward curriculum effectively enables multi-objective RL in robotics by separating exploration-focused task rewards from behavior-focused auxiliary terms, yielding better performance and robustness across domains.

title: PACE: A Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine for 9-1-1 Call-taker Training
summary: 9-1-1 call-taking training requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, covering diverse incident types and protocol-specific nuances. A nationwide labor shortage is already straining training capacity, but effective instruction still demands that trainers tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies. This personalization burden is one that current practice cannot scale. Partnering with Metro Nashville Department of Emergency Communications (MNDEC), we propose PACE (Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine), a co-pilot system that augments trainer decision-making by (1) maintaining probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, (2) modeling individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and (3) recommending training scenarios that balance acquisition of new competencies with retention of existing ones. PACE propagates evidence over a structured skill graph to accelerate diagnostic coverage and applies contextual bandits to select scenarios that target gaps the trainee is prepared to address. Empirical results show that PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies with practicing training officers further demonstrate a 95.45% alignment rate between PACE's and experts' pedagogical judgments on real-world cases. Under estimation, PACE cuts turnaround time to merely 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes, up to 95.08% reduction.

AI.tldr: PACE is a co-pilot for 9-1-1 call-taking training that uses probabilistic skill-state tracking, a skill graph, learning/forgetting dynamics, and contextual bandits to personalize scenarios, achieving faster competence, higher mastery, and strong alignment with experts.
AI.motivation: Address the large, interdependent skill set required for 9-1-1 call-taking, coupled with a nationwide training-capacity bottleneck; enable scalable personalization to trainee competency.
AI.method: Maintain Bayesian beliefs over trainee skill states; model learning and forgetting dynamics; propagate evidence across a structured skill graph; apply contextual bandits to select training scenarios targeting gaps while preserving existing competencies.
AI.result: 19.50% faster time-to-competence; 10.95% higher terminal mastery vs state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies show 95.45% alignment with experts on real-world cases. Underestimation tests reduce turnaround time to 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes (≈95.08% reduction).
AI.conclusion: PACE demonstrates scalable, data-driven personalization that improves efficiency and outcomes in high-stakes training, validated with real-world partner data; promising for broader adoption in adaptive training for critical public-safety roles, with considerations for generalizability and integration.

title: MCEL: Margin-Based Cross-Entropy Loss for Error-Tolerant Quantized Neural Networks
summary: Robustness to bit errors is a key requirement for the reliable use of neural networks (NNs) on emerging approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. A common approach to achieve bit error tolerance in NNs is injecting bit flips during training according to a predefined error model. While effective in certain scenarios, training-time bit flip injection introduces substantial computational overhead, often degrades inference accuracy at high error rates, and scales poorly for larger NN architectures. These limitations make error injection an increasingly impractical solution for ensuring robustness on future approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. In this work, we investigate the mechanisms that enable NNs to tolerate bit errors without relying on error-aware training. We establish a direct connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer. Building on this insight, we propose a novel loss function, the Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), which explicitly promotes logit-level margin separation while preserving the favorable optimization properties of the standard cross-entropy loss. Furthermore, MCEL introduces an interpretable margin parameter that allows robustness to be tuned in a principled manner. Extensive experimental evaluations across multiple datasets of varying complexity, diverse NN architectures, and a range of quantization schemes demonstrate that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance, up to 15 % in accuracy for an error rate of 1 %. Our proposed MCEL method is simple to implement, efficient, and can be integrated as a drop-in replacement for standard CEL. It provides a scalable and principled alternative to training-time bit flip injection, offering new insights into the origins of NN robustness and enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL) to improve NN bit-error tolerance without error-aware training by promoting logit-margin separation; achieves up to 15% accuracy gain at 1% bit error rate; serves as a drop-in replacement for CEL.
AI.motivation: Training-time bit-flip injection is computationally expensive and scales poorly with network size; a lightweight, scalable approach is needed to ensure robustness on approximate computing hardware and error-prone memory. The work links bit-error tolerance to output-margin properties of the classifier.
AI.method: Proposes Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL) that explicitly enforces margin separation at the logit level through a tunable margin parameter. MCEL preserves the optimization characteristics of standard cross-entropy while providing interpretable control over robustness. It is designed as a drop-in replacement for conventional cross-entropy loss.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evaluation across multiple datasets, architectures, and quantization schemes demonstrates that MCEL substantially improves bit-error tolerance, achieving up to 15% accuracy improvement at a 1% error rate. The method is simple to implement, efficient, and scalable.
AI.conclusion: MCEL offers a principled, scalable alternative to training-time bit-flip injection, contributing new insights into the origins of neural-network robustness and enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory systems.

title: MI-DETR: A Strong Baseline for Moving Infrared Small Target Detection with Bio-Inspired Motion Integration
summary: Infrared small target detection (ISTD) is challenging because tiny, low-contrast targets are easily obscured by complex and dynamic backgrounds. Conventional multi-frame approaches typically learn motion implicitly through deep neural networks, often requiring additional motion supervision or explicit alignment modules. We propose Motion Integration DETR (MI-DETR), a bio-inspired dual-pathway detector that processes one infrared frame per time step while explicitly modeling motion. First, a retina-inspired cellular automaton (RCA) converts raw frame sequences into a motion map defined on the same pixel grid as the appearance image, enabling parvocellular-like appearance and magnocellular-like motion pathways to be supervised by a single set of bounding boxes without extra motion labels or alignment operations. Second, a Parvocellular-Magnocellular Interconnection (PMI) Block facilitates bidirectional feature interaction between the two pathways, providing a biologically motivated intermediate interconnection mechanism. Finally, a RT-DETR decoder operates on features from the two pathways to produce detection results. Surprisingly, our proposed simple yet effective approach yields strong performance on three commonly used ISTD benchmarks. MI-DETR achieves 70.3% mAP@50 and 72.7% F1 on IRDST-H (+26.35 mAP@50 over the best multi-frame baseline), 98.0% mAP@50 on DAUB-R, and 88.3% mAP@50 on ITSDT-15K, demonstrating the effectiveness of biologically inspired motion-appearance integration. Code is available at https://github.com/nliu-25/MI-DETR.

AI.tldr: MI-DETR is a simple, bio-inspired detector for infrared small target detection that explicitly integrates motion and appearance via a retina-inspired cellular automaton (RCA) and a Parvocellular-Magnocellular Interconnection (PMI), with a RT-DETR decoder, achieving state-of-the-art results on ISTD benchmarks without extra motion labels.
AI.motivation: ISTD involves tiny, low-contrast targets in dynamic backgrounds; existing multi-frame approaches rely on implicit motion modeling or require extra motion supervision/alignment. A biologically inspired explicit motion-appearance fusion could improve detection robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: Process one frame per time step. Use RCA to transform frame sequences into a motion map aligned with appearance pixels, creating two pathways: parvocellular-like appearance and magnocellular-like motion. PMI block allows bidirectional feature interaction between pathways. RT-DETR decoder uses features from both to produce detections. No extra motion labels or alignment modules required.
AI.result: On three ISTD benchmarks: IRDST-H: 70.3% mAP@50, 72.7% F1, +26.35 mAP@50 over best multi-frame baseline; DAUB-R: 98.0% mAP@50; ITSDT-15K: 88.3% mAP@50. Code: https://github.com/nliu-25/MI-DETR.
AI.conclusion: Biologically inspired motion-appearance integration via RCA and PMI enables effective single-frame-at-a-time detection with explicit motion modeling, yielding strong ISTD performance and validating the approach.

title: Act, Think or Abstain: Complexity-Aware Adaptive Inference for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Current research on Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models predominantly focuses on enhancing generalization through established reasoning techniques. While effective, these improvements invariably increase computational complexity and inference latency. Furthermore, these mechanisms are typically applied indiscriminately, resulting in the inefficient allocation of resources for trivial tasks while simultaneously failing to provide the uncertainty estimation necessary to prevent catastrophic failure on out-of-distribution tasks. Inspired by human cognition, we propose an adaptive framework that dynamically routes VLA execution based on the complexity of the perceived state. Our approach transforms the VLA's vision-language backbone into an active detection tool by projecting latent embeddings into an ensemble of parametric and non-parametric estimators. This allows the system to execute known tasks immediately (Act), reason about ambiguous scenarios (Think), and preemptively halt execution when encountering significant physical or semantic anomalies (Abstain). In our empirical analysis, we observe a phenomenon where visual embeddings alone are superior for inferring task complexity due to the semantic invariance of language. Evaluated on the LIBERO and LIBERO-PRO benchmarks as well as on a real robot, our vision-only configuration achieves 80% F1-Score using as little as 5% of training data, establishing itself as a reliable and efficient task complexity detector.

AI.tldr: An adaptive VLA framework routes execution by perceived task complexity, using a hybrid ensemble to Act/Think/Abstain. Vision embeddings drive complexity detection, achieving 80% F1 with just 5% of data on LIBERO/LIBERO-PRO and a real robot, showing improved efficiency and uncertainty handling.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational burden and latency in Vision-Language-Action systems while providing uncertainty estimates, by mimicking human-cognitive routing of tasks based on perceived state complexity.
AI.method: Transform the VLA vision-language backbone into an active detection tool that projects latent embeddings into an ensemble of parametric and non-parametric estimators, enabling three modes: Act (execute known tasks), Think (reason about ambiguity), and Abstain (halt on anomalies). Vision-only embeddings are used to detect task complexity, and evaluated on LIBERO, LIBERO-PRO, and a real robot.
AI.result: The vision-only configuration achieves ~80% F1-Score with as little as 5% of training data, demonstrating efficient and reliable task complexity detection and enabling adaptive execution routing.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive routing based on complexity can significantly improve efficiency and safety in VLA systems, with vision embeddings sufficing for complexity detection due to linguistic invariance, suggesting a promising direction for low-data, low-latency AI systems.

title: Legal interpretation and AI: from expert systems to argumentation and LLMs
summary: AI and Law research has encountered legal interpretation in different ways, in the context of its evolving approaches and methodologies. Research on expert system has focused on legal knowledge engineering, with the goal of ensuring that human-generated interpretations can be precisely transferred into knowledge-bases, to be consistently applied. Research on argumentation has aimed at representing the structure of interpretive arguments, as well as their dialectical interactions, to assess of the acceptability of interpretive claims within argumentation frameworks. Research on machine learning has focused on the automated generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments, through general and specialised language models, now being increasingly deployed in legal practice.

AI.tldr: Overview of AI-and-Law research focusing on three strands: expert systems for knowledge transfer, argumentation for structuring interpretive claims, and ML for generating interpretive suggestions in legal practice.
AI.motivation: Understand how distinct AI paradigms address legal interpretation and map their methodological differences and potential integration.
AI.method: Literature synthesis of expert systems, argumentation frameworks, and machine learning applications in legal interpretation; delineation of goals and typical workflows for each strand.
AI.result: Highlights how each approach contributes to interpretable legal interpretations: precise knowledge transfer (expert systems), structural/dialectical analysis (argumentation), and scalable proposal generation (ML).
AI.conclusion: The abstract posits a complementary landscape where diverse AI methods address legal interpretation, suggesting potential cross-fertilization and practical deployment in legal practice.

title: Asymptotic Behavior of Multi--Task Learning: Implicit Regularization and Double Descent Effects
summary: Multi--task learning seeks to improve the generalization error by leveraging the common information shared by multiple related tasks. One challenge in multi--task learning is identifying formulations capable of uncovering the common information shared between different but related tasks. This paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi--task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models. The main contribution of this paper is to precisely determine the reasons behind the benefits gained from combining multiple related tasks. Specifically, we show that combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to a traditional formulation with additional regularization terms that help improve the generalization performance. Another contribution is to empirically study the impact of combining tasks on the generalization error. In particular, we empirically show that the combination of multiple tasks postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.

AI.tldr: Asymptotic analysis of a misspecified perceptron multi-task formulation shows that combining tasks is equivalent to a regularized single-task objective; empirically, task combination delays and mitigates double descent.
AI.motivation: Explain why multi-task learning improves generalization and provide theoretical justification for the benefits, especially under model misspecification, by characterizing cross-task information as regularization in the asymptotic regime.
AI.method: Theoretical asymptotic analysis of a popular multi-task formulation for misspecified perceptron models; derivation of equivalence to a traditional objective with additional regularization terms; empirical evaluation of generalization error and double descent.
AI.result: Clarifies the mechanism behind multi-task gains: asymptotically equivalent to a regularized objective; empirical results show that combining tasks postpones and can mitigate the double descent phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Combining tasks yields better generalization via effective regularization that captures shared information; provides theoretical justification for multi-task learning and evidence for reduced double descent in practice.

title: UniM: A Unified Any-to-Any Interleaved Multimodal Benchmark
summary: In real-world multimodal applications, systems usually need to comprehend arbitrarily combined and interleaved multimodal inputs from users, while also generating outputs in any interleaved multimedia form. This capability defines the goal of any-to-any interleaved multimodal learning under a unified paradigm of understanding and generation, posing new challenges and opportunities for advancing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). To foster and benchmark this capability, this paper introduces the UniM benchmark, the first Unified Any-to-Any Interleaved Multimodal dataset. UniM contains 31K high-quality instances across 30 domains and 7 representative modalities: text, image, audio, video, document, code, and 3D, each requiring multiple intertwined reasoning and generation capabilities. We further introduce the UniM Evaluation Suite, which assesses models along three dimensions: Semantic Correctness & Generation Quality, Response Structure Integrity, and Interleaved Coherence. In addition, we propose UniMA, an agentic baseline model equipped with traceable reasoning for structured interleaved generation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the difficulty of UniM and highlight key challenges and directions for advancing unified any-to-any multimodal intelligence. The project page is https://any2any-mllm.github.io/unim.

AI.tldr: UniM introduces the first Unified Any-to-Any Interleaved Multimodal benchmark and agentic baseline for end-to-end interleaved multimodal reasoning and generation, with a 31K-instance dataset across 30 domains and 7 modalities, plus an evaluation suite and traceable UniMA model.
AI.motivation: There is a growing need for models that can understand and generate across arbitrarily interleaved multimodal inputs/outputs (any-to-any), requiring a unified framework and robust benchmarks to drive unified multimodal intelligence.
AI.method: Create the UniM dataset (31K instances, 30 domains, 7 modalities), develop the UniM Evaluation Suite (semantic correctness, response structure, interleaved coherence), and propose UniMA, an agentic baseline with traceable reasoning for structured interleaved generation; conduct comprehensive experiments to assess difficulty and directions.
AI.result: Experiments show UniM is challenging, revealing key gaps in semantic correctness, structure integrity, and interleaved coherence; the benchmark and baseline expose significant room for improvement and guide future research directions.
AI.conclusion: UniM provides a first unified benchmark and baseline for any-to-any interleaved multimodal intelligence, enabling systematic evaluation and progress toward truly unified multimodal LLMs.

title: Digital Twin Driven Textile Classification and Foreign Object Recognition in Automated Sorting Systems
comment: 10 pages,single column, 5 figures, preprint for Photomet Edumet 2026 (Klagenfurt, Austria)
summary: The increasing demand for sustainable textile recycling requires robust automation solutions capable of handling deformable garments and detecting foreign objects in cluttered environments. This work presents a digital twin driven robotic sorting system that integrates grasp prediction, multi modal perception, and semantic reasoning for real world textile classification. A dual arm robotic cell equipped with RGBD sensing, capacitive tactile feedback, and collision-aware motion planning autonomously separates garments from an unsorted basket, transfers them to an inspection zone, and classifies them using state of the art Visual Language Models (VLMs). We benchmark nine VLM s from five model families on a dataset of 223 inspection scenarios comprising shirts, socks, trousers, underwear, foreign objects (including garments outside of the aforementioned classes), and empty scenes. The evaluation assesses per class accuracy, hallucination behavior, and computational performance under practical hardware constraints. Results show that the Qwen model family achieves the highest overall accuracy (up to 87.9 %), with strong foreign object detection performance, while lighter models such as Gemma3 offer competitive speed accuracy trade offs for edge deployment. A digital twin combined with MoveIt enables collision aware path planning and integrates segmented 3D point clouds of inspected garments into the virtual environment for improved manipulation reliability. The presented system demonstrates the feasibility of combining semantic VLM reasoning with conventional grasp detection and digital twin technology for scalable, autonomous textile sorting in realistic industrial settings.

AI.tldr: A digital twin–driven dual-arm robot sorts deformable textiles using RGBD, capacitive sensing, and VLM-based inspection; benchmarks nine VLMs on 223 scenarios; Qwen achieves up to 87.9% accuracy; system confirms feasibility of autonomous textile sorting in industrial settings.
AI.motivation: Growing demand for sustainable textile recycling calls for robust automation capable of handling deformable garments and foreign objects in cluttered environments; prior work lacks integrated perception, manipulation, and semantic reasoning.
AI.method: A dual-arm robotic cell equipped with RGBD sensing, capacitive tactile feedback, and collision-aware planning uses a digital twin (MoveIt) to perform autonomous garment separation, transfer to an inspection zone, and classification via state-of-the-art Visual Language Models (VLMs). The evaluation benchmarks nine VLMs (five families) on 223 inspection scenarios (shirts, socks, trousers, underwear, foreign objects, and empty scenes), assessing per-class accuracy, hallucinations, and computational performance. The system fuses segmented 3D garment point clouds with the virtual environment for improved manipulation.
AI.result: Qwen family attains the highest overall accuracy (up to 87.9%), with strong foreign object detection; lighter models like Gemma3 provide competitive speed–accuracy trade-offs for edge deployment. The digital twin with MoveIt enables collision-aware path planning and integrates segmented garment point clouds into the virtual environment, improving manipulation reliability. Overall, the work demonstrates feasibility of combining semantic VLM reasoning with traditional grasp detection and digital-twin tech for scalable autonomous textile sorting in realistic industrial settings.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that integrating semantic VLM reasoning with grasp detection and digital twin technology is feasible and scalable for autonomous textile sorting, offering improved manipulation reliability and practical deployment in industrial contexts; it also highlights model trade-offs between accuracy and latency and suggests avenues for broader garment classes and robust real-time performance.

title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
comment: Accepted at Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond Workshop at ICLR 2026 - April 26, 2026, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary: We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that test the reliability of LLM judges. As LLM based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, more tooling is needed to efficiently assess the reliability of these methods. Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. We evaluate four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior, and find meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, highlighting opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. No judge that we evaluated is uniformly reliable across benchmarks using our harness. For example, our preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues as measured by accuracy in judging another LLM's ability to complete a task due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping the ground truth label in LLM-produced responses. The code for this tool is available at: https://github.com/RANDCorporation/judge-reliability-harness

AI.tldr: Open-source harness to test LLM judge reliability; uncovers meaningful variation and lack of uniform reliability across benchmarks, highlighting robustness issues and the need for better evaluation tooling.
AI.motivation: LLM-based scoring is widely used in AI benchmarks, but current judges show inconsistent reliability; there is a need for tooling to systematically evaluate and improve the robustness of LLM judges.
AI.method: Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness automatically generates reliability tests assessing binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading for both free-response and agentic task formats. The evaluation covers four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks (safety, persuasion, misuse, agentic behavior) and examines perturbations (text formatting changes, paraphrasing, verbosity, ground-truth label flips) to measure robustness.
AI.result: Significant variation in judge performance across models and perturbations; no judge is uniformly reliable across benchmarks; simple text perturbations can cause consistency issues in judging task ability.
AI.conclusion: The harness reveals robustness gaps in LLM judges and provides a path toward more reliable evaluation, with open-source code available at the provided GitHub repository to facilitate broader testing and improvement.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems promise efficient spectrum utilization by jointly supporting radar sensing and wireless communication. This paper presents a deep learning-driven framework for enhancing physical-layer security in multicarrier ISAC systems under imperfect channel state information (CSI) and in the presence of unknown eavesdropper (Eve) locations. Unlike conventional ISAC-based friendly jamming (FJ) approaches that require Eve's CSI or precise angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimates, our method exploits radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without explicit Eve's information. To enhance robustness to radar sensing uncertainty, we propose a radar-aware neural network that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming by integrating a novel nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence. The jamming design satisfies the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) constraints even in the presence of noisy AoA. For efficient implementation, we introduce a quantized tensor train-based encoder that reduces the model size by more than 100 times with negligible performance loss. We also integrate a non-overlapping secure scheme into the proposed framework, in which specific sub-bands can be dedicated solely to communication. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed solution achieves significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced block error rate (BLER), and strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors, underscoring the effectiveness of the proposed deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework under practical ISAC impairments.

AI.tldr: DL-driven ISAC framework for secure multicarrier systems with imperfect CSI and unknown Eve locations; uses radar echo feedback to steer jamming without Eve info; radar-aware NN with nonparametric FIM estimator via f-divergence; CRLB-compliant jamming under noisy AoA; TT-based encoder for large model compression; adds non-overlapping secure sub-bands.
AI.motivation: Improve physical-layer security in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) under practical impairments: imperfect CSI, unknown eavesdropper locations, and angular estimation noise, where traditional friendly jamming (requiring Eve CSI/AoA) is inadequate.
AI.method: Radar echo feedback guides directional jamming; radar-aware neural network jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming; nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence enhances robustness; CRLB constraints maintain jamming efficacy under noisy AoA; quantized tensor train-based encoder reduces model size by >100x with negligible performance loss; integrate a non-overlapping secure scheme with sub-bands dedicated to communication.
AI.result: Significant gains in secrecy rate and reduced BLER; robust performance under CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors; model-size reduction (>100x) with negligible performance loss; demonstrates effectiveness of deep learning-driven friendly jamming in pragmatic ISAC impairments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed radar-guided DL secure ISAC framework offers robust, scalable physical-layer security enhancements in multicarrier ISAC with imperfect CSI and unknown Eve locations, leveraging sensing feedback, FIM-based robustness, and model compression, while preserving communication integrity via secure sub-bands.

title: MoRe: Motion-aware Feed-forward 4D Reconstruction Transformer
summary: Reconstructing dynamic 4D scenes remains challenging due to the presence of moving objects that corrupt camera pose estimation. Existing optimization methods alleviate this issue with additional supervision, but they are mostly computationally expensive and impractical in real-time applications. To address these limitations, we propose MoRe, a feedforward 4D reconstruction network that efficiently recovers dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos. Built upon a strong static reconstruction backbone, MoRe employs an attention-forcing strategy to disentangle dynamic motion from static structure. To further enhance robustness, we fine-tune the model on large-scale, diverse datasets encompassing both dynamic and static scenes. Moreover, our grouped causal attention captures temporal dependencies and adapts to varying token lengths across frames, ensuring temporally coherent geometry reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that MoRe achieves high-quality dynamic reconstructions with exceptional efficiency.

AI.tldr: MoRe is a fast, feedforward network for dynamic 4D reconstruction from monocular videos that disentangles motion from structure via attention-guided forcing and temporal grouping, achieving high-quality results with real-time-like efficiency after large-scale fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Dynamic 4D reconstruction is hindered by moving objects corrupting camera pose estimation. Optimization-based methods are computationally intensive and impractical for real-time applications, motivating a fast, feedforward alternative.
AI.method: MoRe builds on a static reconstruction backbone and introduces an attention-forcing strategy to separate dynamic motion from static structure. It uses grouped causal attention to capture temporal dependencies and handles varying token lengths across frames. The model is fine-tuned on large-scale, diverse datasets containing both dynamic and static scenes, and operates on monocular video in a feedforward manner.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that MoRe achieves high-quality dynamic reconstructions with exceptional efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MoRe provides an efficient, robust solution for dynamic 4D reconstruction from monocular videos by disentangling motion from structure with attention, leveraging temporal grouping, and benefiting from large-scale fine-tuning to deliver temporally coherent geometry in real time-like performance.

title: Planning in 8 Tokens: A Compact Discrete Tokenizer for Latent World Model
summary: World models provide a powerful framework for simulating environment dynamics conditioned on actions or instructions, enabling downstream tasks such as action planning or policy learning. Recent approaches leverage world models as learned simulators, but its application to decision-time planning remains computationally prohibitive for real-time control. A key bottleneck lies in latent representations: conventional tokenizers encode each observation into hundreds of tokens, making planning both slow and resource-intensive. To address this, we propose CompACT, a discrete tokenizer that compresses each observation into as few as 8 tokens, drastically reducing computational cost while preserving essential information for planning. An action-conditioned world model that occupies CompACT tokenizer achieves competitive planning performance with orders-of-magnitude faster planning, offering a practical step toward real-world deployment of world models.

AI.tldr: CompACT introduces a discrete tokenizer that compresses each observation to ~8 tokens for an action-conditioned world model, enabling real-time planning with orders-of-magnitude speedups while preserving planning quality.
AI.motivation: Address the computational bottleneck in world-model-based planning caused by high-token observations; reduce latency to enable real-time control and practical deployment.
AI.method: Develop a discrete tokenizer (CompACT) that outputs a small fixed number of tokens per observation (as few as 8) and integrate it with an action-conditioned world model; assess planning performance and computational cost against conventional tokenizers.
AI.result: Planning becomes orders of magnitude faster with competitive planning performance, demonstrating a practical step toward real-world deployment of world-model-based planning.
AI.conclusion: Discrete tokenization can dramatically reduce planning costs while preserving essential information for planning, making world-model-based planning more feasible for real-time control.

title: Dissociating Direct Access from Inference in AI Introspection
summary: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability, but its mechanism is not well understood. Recent work has shown that AI models can introspect. We study their mechanism of introspection, first extensively replicating Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models. We show that these models detect injected representations via two separable mechanisms: (i) probability-matching (inferring from perceived anomaly of the prompt) and (ii) direct access to internal states. The direct access mechanism is content-agnostic: models detect that an anomaly occurred but cannot reliably identify its semantic content. The two model classes we study confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete (e.g., "apple'"); for them correct concept guesses typically require significantly more tokens. This content-agnostic introspective mechanism is consistent with leading theories in philosophy and psychology.

AI.tldr: Two separable introspective mechanisms in open-source models: probability-matching (detecting anomalies in prompts) and direct access to internal states (content-agnostic). Models confabulate high-frequency concrete concepts; more tokens needed for correct guesses. Content-agnostic introspection aligns with philosophical/psychological theories.
AI.motivation: To uncover how AI systems perform introspection by extending the thought-injection-detection paradigm to open-source models, and to distinguish content-based vs. content-agnostic mechanisms.
