AI.method: Replicate Lindsey et al. 2025's thought-injection-detection paradigm in large open-source models. Analyze two detection mechanisms: (i) probability-matching via perceived prompt anomalies, and (ii) direct access to internal states. Compare model classes and assess content dependence by examining concept confabulations and token requirements.
AI.result: Identified two separable introspective mechanisms. Probability-matching leverages prompt anomaly signals; direct access to internal states is content-agnostic and can detect anomalies without reliable semantic content identification. Model classes tend to confabulate high-frequency concrete concepts (e.g., 'apple'), with correct concept guesses requiring more tokens. Findings support theories in philosophy and psychology.
AI.conclusion: Introspection in AI comprises distinct, separable mechanisms, including a content-agnostic pathway. This aligns with cognitive theories and has implications for interpretability, suggesting further work to disentangle mechanisms, test across architectures, and explore robustness to adversarial prompts.

title: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: RL agents are typically trained under a single, fixed reward function, which makes them brittle to reward misspecification and limits their ability to adapt to changing task preferences. We introduce Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL), a framework that trains a single agent to optimize a family of reward specifications while collecting experience under only one nominal objective. RCRL conditions the agent on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from a shared replay data entirely off-policy, enabling a single policy to represent reward-specific behaviors. Across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks, we show that RCRL not only improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization, but also enables efficient adaptation to new parameterizations. Our results demonstrate that RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust, steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training.

AI.tldr: RCRL trains one policy conditioned on a parameterized reward to handle multiple reward specifications off-policy from shared data, improving nominal performance and enabling rapid adaptation to new rewards.
AI.motivation: To address brittleness from reward misspecification and to enable adaptable policies by learning across a family of reward functions using a single agent and shared replay data.
AI.method: Introduce reward-conditioned reinforcement learning (RCRL): train a single policy conditioned on reward parameterizations, learn multiple objectives from a shared replay buffer entirely off-policy, enabling reward-specific behaviors within one policy.
AI.result: RCRL improves performance under the nominal reward and enables efficient adaptation to new reward parameterizations across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: RCRL provides a scalable approach to robust, steerable policies while preserving the simplicity of single-task training.

title: Orthogonal Spatial-temporal Distributional Transfer for 4D Generation
summary: In the AIGC era, generating high-quality 4D content has garnered increasing research attention. Unfortunately, current 4D synthesis research is severely constrained by the lack of large-scale 4D datasets, preventing models from adequately learning the critical spatial-temporal features necessary for high-quality 4D generation, thus hindering progress in this domain. To combat this, we propose a novel framework that transfers rich spatial priors from existing 3D diffusion models and temporal priors from video diffusion models to enhance 4D synthesis. We develop a spatial-temporal-disentangled 4D (STD-4D) Diffusion model, which synthesizes 4D-aware videos through disentangled spatial and temporal latents. To facilitate the best feature transfer, we design a novel Orthogonal Spatial-temporal Distributional Transfer (Orster) mechanism, where the spatiotemporal feature distributions are carefully modeled and injected into the STD-4D Diffusion. Furthermore, during the 4D construction, we devise a spatial-temporal-aware HexPlane (ST-HexPlane) to integrate the transferred spatiotemporal features, thereby improving 4D deformation and 4D Gaussian feature modeling. Experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving superior spatial-temporal consistency and higher-quality 4D synthesis.

AI.tldr: Proposes STD-4D Diffusion to synthesize 4D content by transferring spatial priors from 3D diffusion models and temporal priors from video diffusion models, using Orster for orthogonal spatiotemporal distribution transfer and ST-HexPlane for integration, achieving improved spatial-temporal consistency in 4D synthesis.
AI.motivation: Lack of large-scale 4D datasets hampers learning of essential spatial-temporal features for 4D generation; leverage existing 3D and video diffusion priors to boost 4D synthesis.
AI.method: Introduce a spatial-temporal-disentangled 4D (STD-4D) Diffusion model that uses disentangled spatial/temporal latents. Orster mechanism models/injects spatiotemporal distributions from priors. ST-HexPlane integrates transferred features for 4D deformation and Gaussian feature modeling.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant improvement over baselines, with better spatial-temporal consistency and higher-quality 4D synthesis.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively transfers rich spatial-temporal priors to enhance 4D content generation, achieving superior 4D synthesis; ST-HexPlane and Orster are key components enabling robust transfer and integration.

title: Distributed Partial Information Puzzles: Examining Common Ground Construction Under Epistemic Asymmetry
summary: Establishing common ground, a shared set of beliefs and mutually recognized facts, is fundamental to collaboration, yet remains a challenge for current AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings, where the collaborators bring different information to the table. We introduce the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP), a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. We present a multimodal dataset of these interactions, annotated and temporally aligned across speech, gesture, and action modalities to support reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics. We then evaluate two paradigms for modeling common ground (CG): (1) state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline grounded in Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) that incrementally performs the same task. Results on the annotated DPIP data indicate that it poses a challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state.

AI.tldr: DPIP introduces a multimodal collaborative puzzle to study common ground under epistemic asymmetry, with a dataset annotated across speech, gesture, and action; it compares LLM prompting with a Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) pipeline; LLMs struggle to track task progression and belief states.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of establishing common ground in multi-agent, multimodal collaboration and provide a benchmark dataset and evaluation framework to compare data-driven and formal epistemic reasoning approaches.
AI.method: Define the DPIP task; collect and annotate a multimodal corpus (speech, gesture, action) aligned temporally; enable reasoning about propositional content and belief dynamics; evaluate (i) LLMs prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (ii) an axiomatic DEL-based pipeline that incrementally performs the same task.
AI.result: On the annotated DPIP data, modern LLMs have difficulty consistently tracking both the progression of the task and the evolving belief state, indicating a gap in current models’ multimodal and epistemic reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: DPIP provides a challenging benchmark for common-ground reasoning in multimodal, multiparty settings; future work may explore hybrid approaches that integrate DEL-style epistemic reasoning with LLM capabilities and improved multimodal grounding to better track belief dynamics.

title: Synchronization-based clustering on the unit hypersphere
summary: Clustering on the unit hypersphere is a fundamental problem in various fields, with applications ranging from gene expression analysis to text and image classification. Traditional clustering methods are not always suitable for unit sphere data, as they do not account for the geometric structure of the sphere. We introduce a novel algorithm for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere $\mathbf{S}^{d-1}$. Our method is based on the $d$-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model. The effectiveness of the introduced method is demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets. Results are compared with some of the traditional clustering methods, showing that our method achieves similar or better results in terms of clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: A sphere-aware clustering algorithm on S^{d-1} leveraging the d-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model; shows competitive accuracy vs traditional methods on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Unit-sphere data appear in many domains (e.g., gene expression, text, images). Traditional clustering often ignores spherical geometry, potentially harming performance; a geometry-respecting approach is desirable.
AI.method: Proposes an algorithm based on the d-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model to cluster data on the unit sphere. The method uses generalized Kuramoto dynamics to drive angular synchronization/cluster formation on S^{d-1} and compares its performance to conventional clustering techniques.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows that the proposed method achieves similar or better clustering accuracy compared to traditional clustering methods.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a geometry-aware clustering framework for spherical data with competitive performance; highlights applicability to hyperspherical data and suggests avenues for further work on robustness and scalability.

title: GEM-TFL: Bridging Weak and Full Supervision for Forgery Localization through EM-Guided Decomposition and Temporal Refinement
summary: Temporal Forgery Localization (TFL) aims to precisely identify manipulated segments within videos or audio streams, providing interpretable evidence for multimedia forensics and security. While most existing TFL methods rely on dense frame-level labels in a fully supervised manner, Weakly Supervised TFL (WS-TFL) reduces labeling cost by learning only from binary video-level labels. However, current WS-TFL approaches suffer from mismatched training and inference objectives, limited supervision from binary labels, gradient blockage caused by non-differentiable top-k aggregation, and the absence of explicit modeling of inter-proposal relationships. To address these issues, we propose GEM-TFL (Graph-based EM-powered Temporal Forgery Localization), a two-phase classification-regression framework that effectively bridges the supervision gap between training and inference. Built upon this foundation, (1) we enhance weak supervision by reformulating binary labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes through an EM-based optimization process; (2) we introduce a training-free temporal consistency refinement that realigns frame-level predictions for smoother temporal dynamics; and (3) we design a graph-based proposal refinement module that models temporal-semantic relationships among proposals for globally consistent confidence estimation. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that GEM-TFL achieves more accurate and robust temporal forgery localization, substantially narrowing the gap with fully supervised methods.

AI.tldr: GEM-TFL introduces an EM-powered latent-attribute reformulation for weakly supervised temporal forgery localization, augmented by a training-free temporal consistency refinement and a graph-based proposal refinement. It bridges training/inference gaps and narrows the performance gap to fully supervised methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome mismatched training/inference objectives, limited supervision from binary labels, non-differentiable top-k aggregation, and lack of inter-proposal modeling in WS-TFL.
AI.method: A two-phase classification-regression framework. Phase 1 uses EM-based optimization to convert binary video-level labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes guiding frame-level decisions. A training-free temporal consistency refinement realigns frame-level predictions for smoother dynamics. A graph-based refinement module models temporal-semantic relationships among proposals to produce globally consistent confidence estimates.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that GEM-TFL achieves more accurate and robust temporal forgery localization and substantially narrows the gap with fully supervised methods.
AI.conclusion: GEM-TFL effectively bridges supervision gaps in WS-TFL via latent attributes, temporal consistency, and graph-based refinement, offering a robust framework that narrows the WS-FS gap and can inspire future weakly supervised localization research.

title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
summary: As AI models progress beyond simple chatbots into more complex workflows, we draw ever closer to the event horizon beyond which AI systems will be utilized in autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops. Any autonomous AI system will depend on automated, verifiable rewards and feedback; in settings where ground truth is sparse or non-deterministic, one practical source of such rewards is an LLM-as-a-Judge. Although LLM judges continue to improve, the literature has yet to introduce systems capable of enforcing standards with strong guarantees, particularly when bias vectors are unknown or adversarially discovered. To remedy this issue, we propose average bias-boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework which formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact as a result of any measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluating on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, we achieve (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80%. The code to reproduce our findings is available at https://github.com/penfever/bias-bounded-evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), a formal framework to guarantee harm/impact reductions from biases in LLM-based judges within autonomous AI loops. Demonstrates bias-bounded guarantees (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, while preserving substantial correlation (61–99%) with original rankings across various bias settings, most >80%. Code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: As AI systems increasingly operate in autonomous, self-maintaining loops, ground-truth rewards become sparse or non-deterministic. LLMs are used as judges to provide automated feedback, but biases (known or unknown, possibly adversarial) can undermine guarantees. There is a need for formal methods that bound the impact of such biases.
AI.method: Proposes Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework that provides formal guarantees that harm/impact due to any measurable bias in an LLM judge is reduced. Evaluation on the Arena-Hard-Auto benchmark with four LLM judges, reporting bias-bounded guarantees (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) and measuring correlation with original rankings under multiple bias settings. Code released.
AI.result: Achieves bias-bounded guarantees with specified parameters while maintaining substantial alignment with the original rankings (61–99% correlation; most combinations >80%) across formatting and schematic bias variations.
AI.conclusion: A-BB offers a formal mechanism to constrain the influence of bias in LLM judges within autonomous workflows, balancing bias mitigation with preservation of ranking quality. The approach is empirically validated on a benchmark and complemented by public code for reproducibility.

title: Aura: Universal Multi-dimensional Exogenous Integration for Aviation Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting has witnessed an increasing demand across diverse industrial applications, where accurate predictions are pivotal for informed decision-making. Beyond numerical time series data, reliable forecasting in practical scenarios requires integrating diverse exogenous factors. Such exogenous information is often multi-dimensional or even multimodal, introducing heterogeneous interactions that unimodal time series models struggle to capture. In this paper, we delve into an aviation maintenance scenario and identify three distinct types of exogenous factors that influence temporal dynamics through distinct interaction modes. Based on this empirical insight, we propose Aura, a universal framework that explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with the target time series. Specifically, Aura utilizes a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into well-established time series models, ensuring seamless integration of non-sequential context. Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines, covering the Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets, demonstrate that Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability. Our findings highlight Aura's potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability.

AI.tldr: Aura is a universal time series forecasting framework that encodes heterogeneous exogenous information by their interaction modes with the target series through a tripartite encoding, achieving state-of-the-art results on a 3-year aviation maintenance dataset (Boeing 777, Airbus A320) from China Southern Airlines.
AI.motivation: To address multimodal, heterogeneous exogenous factors affecting temporal dynamics, which single-modality models struggle to capture; aviation maintenance is used as a case study to reveal three distinct interaction modes.
AI.method: Propose Aura with a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism that classifies exogenous features by interaction mode and embeds them into established time series models, enabling integration of non-sequential context; the framework is generic and can be plugged into various base models.
AI.result: Consistent state-of-the-art performance across baselines and strong adaptability on a large-scale dataset across two fleets, indicating the framework's effectiveness and generality.
AI.conclusion: Aura offers a general-purpose enhancement for forecasting with heterogeneous external information, with potential benefits for aviation safety and reliability and applicability to other domains.

title: Diff-ES: Stage-wise Structural Diffusion Pruning via Evolutionary Search
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity image generation but remain computationally demanding due to their multi-step denoising process and large model sizes. Although prior work improves efficiency either by reducing sampling steps or by compressing model parameters, existing structured pruning approaches still struggle to balance real acceleration and image quality preservation. In particular, prior methods such as MosaicDiff rely on heuristic, manually tuned stage-wise sparsity schedules and stitch multiple independently pruned models during inference, which increases memory overhead. However, the importance of diffusion steps is highly non-uniform and model-dependent. As a result, schedules derived from simple heuristics or empirical observations often fail to generalize and may lead to suboptimal performance. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Diff-ES}, a stage-wise structural \textbf{Diff}usion pruning framework via \textbf{E}volutionary \textbf{S}earch, which optimizes the stage-wise sparsity schedule and executes it through memory-efficient weight routing without model duplication. Diff-ES divides the diffusion trajectory into multiple stages, automatically discovers an optimal stage-wise sparsity schedule via evolutionary search, and activates stage-conditioned weights dynamically without duplicating model parameters. Our framework naturally integrates with existing structured pruning methods for diffusion models including depth and width pruning. Extensive experiments on DiT and SDXL demonstrate that Diff-ES consistently achieves wall-clock speedups while incurring minimal degradation in generation quality, establishing state-of-the-art performance for structured diffusion model pruning.

AI.tldr: Diff-ES introduces a stage-wise structural diffusion pruning framework driven by evolutionary search to optimize sparsity schedules and memory-efficient weight routing, achieving wall-clock speedups with minimal quality loss and no model duplication, outperforming heuristic methods.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are powerful but computationally heavy; existing pruning methods rely on hand-tuned, stage-wise sparsity schedules and often require duplicating models to maintain performance, causing memory overhead. There is non-uniform importance across diffusion steps and model-dependent optimal schedules.
AI.method: Divide the diffusion trajectory into multiple stages; use evolutionary search to automatically discover an optimal stage-wise sparsity schedule; implement memory-efficient weight routing that activates stage-conditioned weights without duplicating parameters; integrate with existing structured pruning (depth/width) methods; validated on DiT and SDXL.
AI.result: Diff-ES achieves consistent wall-clock speedups with minimal degradation in generation quality, establishing state-of-the-art performance for structured diffusion model pruning.
AI.conclusion: A general, scalable pruning framework for diffusion models that learns stage-wise sparsity schedules and uses memory-efficient routing to reduce compute without sacrificing quality, compatible with existing structured pruning techniques.

title: The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink: Anatomy of Massive Activations and Attention Sinks
summary: We study two recurring phenomena in Transformer language models: massive activations, in which a small number of tokens exhibit extreme outliers in a few channels, and attention sinks, in which certain tokens attract disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Prior work observes that these phenomena frequently co-occur and often involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Through systematic experiments, we show that the co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact of modern Transformer design, and that the two phenomena serve related but distinct functions. Massive activations operate globally: they induce near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers, effectively functioning as implicit parameters of the model. Attention sinks operate locally: they modulate attention outputs across heads and bias individual heads toward short-range dependencies. We identify the pre-norm configuration as the key choice that enables the co-occurrence, and show that ablating it causes the two phenomena to decouple.

AI.tldr: Massive activations and attention sinks frequently co-occur in Transformer LMs, but this co-occurrence is largely a byproduct of architecture. They are functionally distinct: massive activations act globally as implicit parameters; attention sinks act locally by biasing heads toward short-range dependencies. The pre-norm configuration enables co-occurrence, and ablating it decouples the phenomena.
AI.motivation: Resolve the ambiguity around two observed Transformer phenomena—massive activations and attention sinks—by examining their functional roles and causal relationship, and identify architectural factors that govern their co-occurrence.
AI.method: Systematic experiments on Transformer models, analyzing activation patterns and attention distributions across layers and heads; ablation studies (notably removing or altering pre-norm configuration) to test causal links; examination of token-level vs. head-level effects to distinguish global vs. local behavior.
AI.result: Co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact rather than a causal necessity; massive activations produce near-constant hidden states across layers, acting as implicit parameters; attention sinks modulate attention outputs across heads, biasing toward short-range dependencies; pre-norm is the key enabler of co-occurrence; ablating pre-norm decouples the two phenomena.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choices shape how these phenomena manifest and interact. Recognizing the global vs. local roles clarifies their functional significance and suggests targeted interventions (e.g., pre-norm adjustments) to control or disentangle them, with implications for interpretability and robustness of Transformer models.

title: Axiomatic On-Manifold Shapley via Optimal Generative Flows
summary: Shapley-based attribution is critical for post-hoc XAI but suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baselines. While generative methods attempt to address this, they often introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift. We propose a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions driven by optimal generative flows. We prove a representation theorem establishing the gradient line integral as the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms, notably reparameterization invariance. To resolve path ambiguity, we select the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. This yields a canonical attribution family that recovers classical Shapley for additive models and admits provable stability bounds against flow approximation errors. By reframing baseline selection as a variational problem, our method experimentally outperforms baselines, achieving strict manifold adherence via vanishing Flow Consistency Error and superior semantic alignment characterized by Structure-Aware Total Variation. Our code is on https://github.com/cenweizhang/OTFlowSHAP.

AI.tldr: A theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions via optimal generative flows (Wasserstein-2 geodesics) yielding a unique gradient-line integral with efficiency, reparameterization invariance, and stability; recovers Shapley for additive models and empirically improves manifold adherence and semantic alignment over baselines.
AI.motivation: Shapley attribution suffers off-manifold artifacts from heuristic baselines; generative approaches introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift. A principled, invariant, on-manifold Shapley framework is needed, with baseline selection cast as a variational problem to reduce artifacts.
AI.method: Prove a representation theorem: the gradient line integral is the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms, including reparameterization invariance. Resolve path ambiguity by using the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. Define a canonical attribution family; show it recovers Shapley for additive models and bound its sensitivity to flow approximation errors. Reform baseline selection as a variational problem and evaluate via Flow Consistency Error and Structure-Aware Total Variation.
AI.result: Canonical attribution family with provable stability bounds against flow-approximation errors; recovers classical Shapley for additive models; experiments show strict manifold adherence (vanishing Flow Consistency Error) and improved semantic alignment as measured by Structure-Aware Total Variation; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, on-manifold Shapley attribution grounded in optimal transport and generative flows, with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical gains, and links Shapley to a variational baseline design for robust, geometry-aware explanations.

title: BLINK: Behavioral Latent Modeling of NK Cell Cytotoxicity
summary: Machine learning models of cellular interaction dynamics hold promise for understanding cell behavior. Natural killer (NK) cell cytotoxicity is a prominent example of such interaction dynamics and is commonly studied using time-resolved multi-channel fluorescence microscopy. Although tumor cell death events can be annotated at single frames, NK cytotoxic outcome emerges over time from cellular interactions and cannot be reliably inferred from frame-wise classification alone. We introduce BLINK, a trajectory-based recurrent state-space model that serves as a cell world model for NK-tumor interactions. BLINK learns latent interaction dynamics from partially observed NK-tumor interaction sequences and predicts apoptosis increments that accumulate into cytotoxic outcomes. Experiments on long-term time-lapse NK-tumor recordings show improved cytotoxic outcome detection and enable forecasting of future outcomes, together with an interpretable latent representation that organizes NK trajectories into coherent behavioral modes and temporally structured interaction phases. BLINK provides a unified framework for quantitative evaluation and structured modeling of NK cytotoxic behavior at the single-cell level.

AI.tldr: BLINK is a trajectory-based recurrent state-space model for NK–tumor interactions that learns latent, partially observed interaction dynamics to predict apoptosis increments and cytotoxic outcomes, enabling forecasting and interpretable behavioral modes at the single-cell level.
AI.motivation: To capture time-evolving NK cytotoxic outcomes that cannot be inferred from frame-wise labels alone, leveraging time-resolved single-cell data to build a generative, interpretable model of interaction dynamics.
AI.method: A trajectory-based recurrent state-space model (BLINK) that operates on partially observed NK–tumor interaction sequences, learns latent dynamics, predicts cumulative apoptosis increments, and yields an interpretable latent space organizing trajectories into behavioral modes and phases.
AI.result: Improved cytotoxic outcome detection and forecasting; interpretable latent representations that structure NK trajectories into coherent modes and temporally structured phases; a unified framework for quantitative evaluation and modeling at single-cell resolution.
AI.conclusion: BLINK provides a unified, interpretable, trajectory-aware framework for quantitatively modeling NK cytotoxic behavior, advancing single-cell level understanding of NK–tumor interactions.

title: UniPAR: A Unified Framework for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
summary: Pedestrian Attribute Recognition is a foundational computer vision task that provides essential support for downstream applications, including person retrieval in video surveillance and intelligent retail analytics. However, existing research is frequently constrained by the ``one-model-per-dataset" paradigm and struggles to handle significant discrepancies across domains in terms of modalities, attribute definitions, and environmental scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose UniPAR, a unified Transformer-based framework for PAR. By incorporating a unified data scheduling strategy and a dynamic classification head, UniPAR enables a single model to simultaneously process diverse datasets from heterogeneous modalities, including RGB images, video sequences, and event streams. We also introduce an innovative phased fusion encoder that explicitly aligns visual features with textual attribute queries through a late deep fusion strategy. Experimental results on the widely used benchmark datasets, including MSP60K, DukeMTMC, and EventPAR, demonstrate that UniPAR achieves performance comparable to specialized SOTA methods. Furthermore, multi-dataset joint training significantly enhances the model's cross-domain generalization and recognition robustness in extreme environments characterized by low light and motion blur. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenPAR

AI.tldr: UniPAR: a unified Transformer-based framework for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) that jointly learns from diverse datasets and modalities (RGB, video, event streams) via unified data scheduling, a dynamic classification head, and a phased fusion encoder, achieving competitive SOTA and improved cross-domain robustness without dataset-specific tailoring.
AI.motivation: Overcome the 'one-model-per-dataset' limitation and significant cross-domain disparities in PAR (modalities, attribute definitions, environments) by enabling a single model to learn from heterogeneous datasets and modalities.
AI.method: A unified Transformer-based PAR model with: (1) unified data scheduling to ingest heterogeneous datasets; (2) a dynamic classification head adapting to varied attribute definitions; (3) a phased fusion encoder with late deep fusion that aligns visual features with textual attribute queries; (4) multi-dataset joint training across RGB images, video sequences, and event streams.
AI.result: Empirical results on MSP60K, DukeMTMC, and EventPAR show UniPAR matches the performance of specialized SOTA methods. Joint training across datasets improves cross-domain generalization and robustness under challenging conditions (low light, motion blur). Code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: UniPAR demonstrates that a single, modality- and dataset-agnostic model can achieve competitive PAR performance and improved cross-domain robustness, highlighting the viability of unified training and phased fusion in multi-modal PAR.

title: FedBCD:Communication-Efficient Accelerated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent for Federated Learning
summary:
Although Federated Learning has been widely studied in recent years, there are still high overhead expenses in each communication round for large-scale models such as Vision Transformer. To lower the communication complexity, we propose a novel Federated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent (FedBCGD) method for communication efficiency. The proposed method splits model parameters into several blocks, including a shared block and enables uploading a specific parameter block by each client, which can significantly reduce communication overhead. Moreover, we also develop an accelerated FedBCGD algorithm (called FedBCGD+) with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first work on parameter block communication for training large-scale deep models. We also provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Our theoretical results show that the communication complexities of our algorithms are a factor $1/N$ lower than those of existing methods, where $N$ is the number of parameter blocks, and they enjoy much faster convergence than their counterparts. Empirical results indicate the superiority of the proposed algorithms compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.
  The code is available at https://github.com/junkangLiu0/FedBCGD.

AI.tldr: FedBCGD introduces parameter-block communication in federated learning to reduce communication rounds for large models by uploading only a chosen parameter block; FedBCGD+ adds drift control and variance reduction; claimed 1/N reduction in communication complexity and faster convergence, with empirical gains and public code.
AI.motivation: Mitigate high communication overhead in FL for large-scale models (e.g., Vision Transformers) by enabling block-wise parameter updates.
AI.method: Partition model parameters into blocks, including a shared block; each client uploads a specific block; propose FedBCGD and accelerated FedBCGD+ with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction; conduct convergence analysis.
AI.result: Theoretical: communication complexity reduced by factor 1/N; faster convergence than baselines; empirical: superior performance against state-of-the-art; code provided.
AI.conclusion: First to study parameter block communication for large-scale deep models; demonstrates substantial communication savings and improved convergence; opens path for block-wise FL in practice.

title: SRasP: Self-Reorientation Adversarial Style Perturbation for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CD-FSL) aims to transfer knowledge from a seen source domain to unseen target domains, serving as a key benchmark for evaluating the robustness and transferability of models. Existing style-based perturbation methods mitigate domain shift but often suffer from gradient instability and convergence to sharp minima.To address these limitations, we propose a novel crop-global style perturbation network, termed Self-Reorientation Adversarial \underline{S}tyle \underline{P}erturbation (SRasP). Specifically, SRasP leverages global semantic guidance to identify incoherent crops, followed by reorienting and aggregating the style gradients of these crops with the global style gradients within one image. Furthermore, we propose a novel multi-objective optimization function to maximize visual discrepancy while enforcing semantic consistency among global, crop, and adversarial features. Applying the stabilized perturbations during training encourages convergence toward flatter and more transferable solutions, improving generalization to unseen domains. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple CD-FSL benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces SRasP, a crop-global style perturbation method for cross-domain few-shot learning (CD-FSL) that stabilizes training and promotes flatter minima via reoriented crop-style gradients and a multi-objective loss, yielding improved generalization on CD-FSL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing style-based perturbations help reduce domain shift but suffer from gradient instability and convergence to sharp minima, limiting transferability to unseen domains.
AI.method: Propose Self-Reorientation Adversarial Style Perturbation (SRasP): use global semantic guidance to identify incoherent crops, reorient and aggregate style gradients from crops with global gradients within a single image; introduce a multi-objective optimization to maximize visual discrepancy while enforcing semantic consistency among global, crop, and adversarial features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple CD-FSL benchmarks show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods across various settings.
AI.conclusion: Stabilized perturbations during training drive convergence toward flatter, more transferable solutions, improving generalization to unseen domains.

title: Federated Causal Discovery Across Heterogeneous Datasets under Latent Confounding
summary: Causal discovery across multiple datasets is often constrained by data privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity, limiting the use of conventional methods that require a single, centralized dataset. To address these challenges, we introduce fedCI, a federated conditional independence test that rigorously handles heterogeneous datasets with non-identical sets of variables, site-specific effects, and mixed variable types, including continuous, ordinal, binary, and categorical variables. At its core, fedCI uses a federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) procedure to estimate the parameters of generalized linear models underlying likelihood-ratio tests for conditional independence. Building on this, we develop fedCI-IOD, a federated extension of the Integration of Overlapping Datasets (IOD) algorithm, that replaces its meta-analysis strategy and enables, for the fist time, federated causal discovery under latent confounding across distributed and heterogeneous datasets. By aggregating evidence federatively, fedCI-IOD not only preserves privacy but also substantially enhances statistical power, achieving performance comparable to fully pooled analyses and mitigating artifacts from low local sample sizes. Our tools are publicly available as the fedCI Python package, a privacy-preserving R implementation of IOD, and a web application for the fedCI-IOD pipeline, providing versatile, user-friendly solutions for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery.

AI.tldr: Introduces fedCI and fedCI-IOD for privacy-preserving federated causal discovery across heterogeneous datasets; uses federated IRLS for GLM-based conditional-independence tests and a federated extension of IOD; achieves near-pooled performance with improved power while preserving privacy; tools released (fedCI Python package, R IOD, and a web app).
AI.motivation: Privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity prevent centralized causal discovery. Challenges include non-identical variable sets, site-specific effects, and mixed variable types (continuous, ordinal, binary, categorical). A federated approach is needed to avoid data pooling while maintaining statistical power.
AI.method: fedCI uses a federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) procedure to estimate GLM parameters underlying likelihood-ratio tests for conditional independence. fedCI-IOD extends the Integration of Overlapping Datasets (IOD) to the federated setting by replacing meta-analysis with federated aggregation, enabling causal discovery under latent confounding across distributed, heterogeneous datasets.
AI.result: Federated methods preserve privacy and substantially boost statistical power, achieving performance comparable to fully pooled analyses and mitigating artifacts from small local sample sizes.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, privacy-preserving framework for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery across heterogeneous datasets; tooling is publicly available to empower researchers to perform federated causal inference without centralizing data.

title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
summary:
In federated learning (FL), balancing privacy protection, learning quality, and efficiency remains a challenge. Privacy protection mechanisms, such as Differential Privacy (DP), degrade learning quality, or, as in the case of Homomorphic Encryption (HE), incur substantial system overhead. To address this, we propose Alt-FL, a privacy-preserving FL framework that combines DP, HE, and synthetic data via a novel round-based interleaving strategy. Alt-FL introduces three new methods, Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE), that enable flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs while providing privacy protection.
  We systematically evaluate Alt-FL against representative reconstruction attacks, including Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, and Robbing the Fed, using a LeNet-5 model on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST. To enable fair comparison between DP- and HE-based defenses, we introduce a new attacker-centric framework that compares empirical attack success rates across the three proposed interleaving methods. Our results show that, for the studied attacker model and dataset, PI achieves the most balanced trade-offs at high privacy protection levels, while DP-based methods are preferable at intermediate privacy requirements. We also discuss how such results can be the basis for selecting privacy-preserving FL methods under varying privacy and resource constraints.

AI.tldr: Alt-FL blends DP, HE, and synthetic data via round-based interleaving to trade privacy, accuracy, and efficiency in federated learning. It introduces Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE), and an attacker-centric evaluation framework. Results: PI offers best balance under high privacy; DP-based methods better at intermediate privacy; provides guidance for method selection under privacy/resource constraints.
AI.motivation: Balancing privacy protection with learning quality and efficiency remains difficult in FL; DP reduces utility; HE provides strong privacy but high overhead; synthetic data can help; need flexible framework to accommodate varying privacy/resource constraints.
AI.method: Round-based interleaving strategy across three schemes (PI, SI/DP, SI/HE); evaluation against multiple reconstruction attacks (Deep Leakage from Gradients, Inverting Gradients, When the Curious Abandon Honesty, Robbing the Fed); LeNet-5 on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST; attacker-centric framework to compare attack success rates across interleaving methods; performance metrics across privacy levels.
AI.result: PI achieves most balanced trade-offs at high privacy; DP-based methods preferable at intermediate privacy; results dataset- and attacker-model dependent; framework helps select privacy-preserving FL methods under constraints.
AI.conclusion: Proposes Alt-FL as flexible privacy-preserving FL; enables quality-efficiency-privacy trade-offs via interleaving; provides attacker-centric evaluation; offers guidance for choosing methods by privacy level and resource constraints; potential to extend to other models/datasets and real-world deployments.

title: SSR-GS: Separating Specular Reflection in Gaussian Splatting for Glossy Surface Reconstruction
summary:
In recent years, 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) has achieved remarkable progress in novel view synthesis. However, accurately reconstructing glossy surfaces under complex illumination remains challenging, particularly in scenes with strong specular reflections and multi-surface interreflections. To address this issue, we propose SSR-GS, a specular reflection modeling framework for glossy surface reconstruction. Specifically, we introduce a prefiltered Mip-Cubemap to model direct specular reflections efficiently, and propose an IndiASG module to capture indirect specular reflections.
  Furthermore, we design Visual Geometry Priors (VGP) that couple a reflection-aware visual prior via a reflection score (RS) to downweight the photometric loss contribution of reflection-dominated regions, with geometry priors derived from VGGT, including progressively decayed depth supervision and transformed normal constraints. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that SSR-GS achieves state-of-the-art performance in glossy surface reconstruction.

AI.tldr: SSR-GS introduces a specular reflection modeling framework for glossy surfaces in 3D Gaussian splatting, using a prefiltered Mip-Cubemap for direct reflections, an IndiASG module for indirect reflections, and Visual Geometry Priors with a reflection score to robustify training; it achieves state-of-the-art results on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Glossy surfaces under complex illumination with strong specular reflections and multi-surface interreflections remain challenging for 3DGS-based novel view synthesis; there is a need for accurate direct and indirect reflection modeling and robust supervision.
AI.method: 1) Prefiltered Mip-Cubemap to model direct specular reflections; 2) IndiASG module to capture indirect specular reflections; 3) Visual Geometry Priors (VGP) coupling a reflection-aware prior via a reflection score to downweight reflection-dominated regions, with geometry priors derived from VGGT, including progressively decayed depth supervision and transformed normal constraints.
AI.result: SSR-GS achieves state-of-the-art performance in glossy surface reconstruction on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively tackles challenging specular reflections and interreflections, improving glossy surface reconstruction, and demonstrates the value of combining direct/indirect reflection modeling with priors; future work could address scalability and broader material generalization.

title: Trainable Bitwise Soft Quantization for Input Feature Compression
summary: The growing demand for machine learning applications in the context of the Internet of Things calls for new approaches to optimize the use of limited compute and memory resources. Despite significant progress that has been made w.r.t. reducing model sizes and improving efficiency, many applications still require remote servers to provide the required resources. However, such approaches rely on transmitting data from edge devices to remote servers, which may not always be feasible due to bandwidth, latency, or energy constraints. We propose a task-specific, trainable feature quantization layer that compresses the input features of a neural network. This can significantly reduce the amount of data that needs to be transferred from the device to a remote server. In particular, the layer allows each input feature to be quantized to a user-defined number of bits, enabling a simple on-device compression at the time of data collection. The layer is designed to approximate step functions with sigmoids, enabling trainable quantization thresholds. By concatenating outputs from multiple sigmoids, introduced as bitwise soft quantization, it achieves trainable quantized values when integrated with a neural network. We compare our method to full-precision inference as well as to several quantization baselines. Experiments show that our approach outperforms standard quantization methods, while maintaining accuracy levels close to those of full-precision models. In particular, depending on the dataset, compression factors of $5\times$ to $16\times$ can be achieved compared to $32$-bit input without significant performance loss.

AI.tldr: A trainable feature quantization layer using bitwise soft quantization (sigmoid-based thresholds) compresses edge inputs for IoT NN, enabling on-device data reduction with minimal accuracy loss (5–16x) compared to 32-bit input.
AI.motivation: Edge IoT devices face strict bandwidth, latency, and energy constraints; transmitting raw features to remote servers is often impractical. A trainable, per-feature quantizer aims to reduce data transfer while preserving model performance.
AI.method: Introduce a trainable quantization layer that approximates step functions with sigmoids. Concatenate outputs from multiple sigmoids to form bitwise soft quantization, allowing per-feature quantization to a user-defined number of bits and seamless integration into neural networks; evaluate against full-precision and standard quantization baselines.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms standard quantization baselines and achieves accuracy close to full-precision models. Compression factors of about 5× to 16× are reported relative to 32-bit input, with dataset-dependent variation.
AI.conclusion: Bitwise soft quantization enables effective on-device feature compression for edge–cloud ML, delivering substantial data reduction with minimal accuracy loss and broad applicability to resource-constrained IoT scenarios.

title: The Impact of Preprocessing Methods on Racial Encoding and Model Robustness in CXR Diagnosis
comment: Preprint accepted for publication at BVM 2026 (https://www.bvm-conf.org/)
summary: Deep learning models can identify racial identity with high accuracy from chest X-ray (CXR) recordings. Thus, there is widespread concern about the potential for racial shortcut learning, where a model inadvertently learns to systematically bias its diagnostic predictions as a function of racial identity. Such racial biases threaten healthcare equity and model reliability, as models may systematically misdiagnose certain demographic groups. Since racial shortcuts are diffuse - non-localized and distributed throughout the whole CXR recording - image preprocessing methods may influence racial shortcut learning, yet the potential of such methods for reducing biases remains underexplored. Here, we investigate the effects of image preprocessing methods including lung masking, lung cropping, and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE). These approaches aim to suppress spurious cues encoding racial information while preserving diagnostic accuracy. Our experiments reveal that simple bounding box-based lung cropping can be an effective strategy for reducing racial shortcut learning while maintaining diagnostic model performance, bypassing frequently postulated fairness-accuracy trade-offs.

AI.tldr: Bounding box lung cropping reduces racial shortcut learning in chest X-ray models while preserving diagnostic accuracy, outperforming more complex preprocessing like CLAHE or lung masking.
AI.motivation: Address racial bias and shortcut learning in deep learning for chest radiographs; racial cues may be diffusely embedded across the image, risking biased predictions.
AI.method: Compare preprocessing strategies (lung masking, lung cropping via bounding box, CLAHE) to assess their effect on racial shortcut learning and diagnostic performance in DL models on CXR data.
AI.result: Bounding box-based lung cropping effectively reduces racial shortcut learning while maintaining diagnostic accuracy, potentially avoiding the typical fairness-accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Simple, targeted preprocessing (lung cropping) can mitigate racial biases in CXR models without sacrificing performance, suggesting a practical bias-mitigation strategy.

title: Incentive Aware AI Regulations: A Credal Characterisation
summary: While high-stakes ML applications demand strict regulations, strategic ML providers often evade them to lower development costs. To address this challenge, we cast AI regulation as a mechanism design problem under uncertainty and introduce regulation mechanisms: a framework that maps empirical evidence from models to a license for some market share. The providers can select from a set of licenses, effectively forcing them to bet on their model's ability to fulfil regulation. We aim at regulation mechanisms that achieve perfect market outcome, i.e. (a) drive non-compliant providers to self-exclude, and (b) ensure participation from compliant providers. We prove that a mechanism has perfect market outcome if and only if the set of non-compliant distributions forms a credal set, i.e., a closed, convex set of probability measures. This result connects mechanism design and imprecise probability by establishing a duality between regulation mechanisms and the set of non-compliant distributions. We also demonstrate these mechanisms in practice via experiments on regulating use of spurious features for prediction and fairness. Our framework provides new insights at the intersection of mechanism design and imprecise probability, offering a foundation for development of enforceable AI regulations.

AI.tldr: Regulation mechanisms map model evidence to licenses to control market share; a perfect market outcome requires the non-compliant distribution set to be a credal set; establishes duality with imprecise probability; validated via experiments on spurious features and fairness.
AI.motivation: High-stakes ML applications require regulation; current practice allows evasion to cut costs; a principled mechanism-design approach under uncertainty is proposed to enforce compliance.
AI.method: Formulate AI regulation as a mechanism design problem under uncertainty; define a family of licenses that providers can purchase; demonstrate that perfect market outcomes occur iff non-compliant distributions form a closed convex credal set; establish duality between regulation mechanisms and non-compliant distributions; validate with experiments on controlling spurious features and on fairness.
AI.result: Theoretical result: equivalence between perfect market outcome and credal set of non-compliant distributions; practical demonstration via experiments; shows how regulation mechanisms can enforce compliance and self-exclusion of non-compliant providers while keeping compliant ones.
AI.conclusion: Bridges mechanism design and imprecise probability; provides a foundational framework for enforceable AI regulation and guidance for future research and practice.

title: Generic Camera Calibration using Blurry Images
summary: Camera calibration is the foundation of 3D vision. Generic camera calibration can yield more accurate results than parametric cam era calibration. However, calibrating a generic camera model using printed calibration boards requires far more images than parametric calibration, making motion blur practically unavoidable for individual users. As a f irst attempt to address this problem, we draw on geometric constraints and a local parametric illumination model to simultaneously estimate feature locations and spatially varying point spread functions, while re solving the translational ambiguity that need not be considered in con ventional image deblurring tasks. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: Joint estimation of feature locations and spatially varying PSFs for generic camera calibration using geometric constraints and a local illumination model, addressing motion blur and translational ambiguity; validated experimentally.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate generic camera calibration without a large number of images and to mitigate motion blur effects by integrating deblurring within the calibration process.
AI.method: Develops a joint optimization framework that leverages geometric constraints and a local parametric illumination model to simultaneously estimate feature locations and spatially varying point spread functions (PSFs); includes resolving translational ambiguity not addressed in standard deblurring tasks.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, showing improved calibration robustness under blur.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a viable method for robust generic camera calibration under motion blur by coupling feature localization with spatially varying PSF estimation and addressing translational ambiguity, with empirical validation supporting its potential.

title: Towards a data-scale independent regulariser for robust sparse identification of non-linear dynamics
summary: Data normalisation, a common and often necessary preprocessing step in engineering and scientific applications, can severely distort the discovery of governing equations by magnitudebased sparse regression methods. This issue is particularly acute for the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) framework, where the core assumption of sparsity is undermined by the interaction between data scaling and measurement noise. The resulting discovered models can be dense, uninterpretable, and physically incorrect. To address this critical vulnerability, we introduce the Sequential Thresholding of Coefficient of Variation (STCV), a novel, computationally efficient sparse regression algorithm that is inherently robust to data scaling. STCV replaces conventional magnitude-based thresholding with a dimensionless statistical metric, the Coefficient Presence (CP), which assesses the statistical validity and consistency of candidate terms in the model library. This shift from magnitude to statistical significance makes the discovery process invariant to arbitrary data scaling. Through comprehensive benchmarking on canonical dynamical systems and practical engineering problems, including a physical mass-spring-damper experiment, we demonstrate that STCV consistently and significantly outperforms standard Sequential Thresholding Least Squares (STLSQ) and Ensemble-SINDy (E-SINDy) on normalised, noisy datasets. The results show that STCV-based methods can successfully identify the correct, sparse physical laws even when other methods fail. By mitigating the distorting effects of normalisation, STCV makes sparse system identification a more reliable and automated tool for real-world applications, thereby enhancing model interpretability and trustworthiness.

AI.tldr: STCV is a scale-invariant sparse regression method for SINDy that uses a Coefficient Presence statistic to replace magnitude-based thresholding, yielding robust discovery of sparse governing equations under data normalization and noise, outperforming STLSQ and E-SINDy.
AI.motivation: Data normalization distorts discovery in sparse, physics-informed regression (SINDy); magnitude-based thresholding is sensitive to scaling and noise, leading to dense, incorrect models.
AI.method: Introduce STCV with Coefficient Presence (CP); sequential thresholding based on CP to identify significant terms; CP is dimensionless and assesses statistical validity/consistency of candidate terms; robust to data scaling; benchmarked on canonical dynamical systems and a mass-spring-damper experiment.
AI.result: STCV-based methods consistently and significantly outperform STLSQ and E-SINDy on normalized, noisy datasets, identifying correct sparse physical laws where others fail.
AI.conclusion: By mitigating normalization distortion, STCV enhances reliability, interpretability, and automation of sparse system identification in real-world applications.

title: Mario: Multimodal Graph Reasoning with Large Language Models
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have opened new avenues for multimodal reasoning. Yet, most existing methods still rely on pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to encode image-text pairs in isolation, ignoring the relational structure that real-world multimodal data naturally form. This motivates reasoning on multimodal graphs (MMGs), where each node has textual and visual attributes and edges provide structural cues. Enabling LLM-based reasoning on such heterogeneous multimodal signals while preserving graph topology introduces two key challenges: resolving weak cross-modal consistency and handling heterogeneous modality preference. To address this, we propose Mario, a unified framework that simultaneously resolves the two above challenges and enables effective LLM-based reasoning over MMGs. Mario consists of two innovative stages. Firstly, a graph-conditioned VLM design that jointly refines textual and visual features through fine-grained cross-modal contrastive learning guided by graph topology. Secondly, a modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning mechanism that organizes aligned multimodal features into graph-aware instruction views and employs a learnable router to surface, for each node and its neighborhood, the most informative modality configuration to the LLM. Extensive experiments across diverse MMG benchmarks demonstrate that Mario consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph models in both supervised and zero-shot scenarios for node classification and link prediction. The code will be made available at https://github.com/sunyuanfu/Mario.

AI.tldr: Mario presents a two-stage framework for reasoning over heterogeneous multimodal graphs (MMGs) with LLMs: (1) graph-conditioned VLM that refines text and image features using graph topology-guided cross-modal contrastive learning, and (2) a modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning with a learnable router to select the most informative modality configuration per node and its neighborhood for LLM reasoning. It outperforms state-of-the-art graph models on node classification and link prediction in supervised and zero-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM-based multimodal reasoning often treats image-text data in isolation and ignores relational graph structure. Real-world multimodal data form MMGs where topology and cross-modal signals must be jointly leveraged for reasoning tasks like node classification and link prediction. The work aims to integrate graph structure into both feature learning and reasoning with LLMs.
AI.method: Two-stage approach. Stage 1: graph-conditioned VLM that jointly refines textual and visual features via fine-grained cross-modal contrastive learning guided by the graph topology, aligning modalities within the graph context. Stage 2: modality-adaptive graph instruction tuning that creates graph-aware instruction views and uses a learnable router to surface, for each node and neighborhood, the most informative modality configuration to the LLM. The router presumably selects modality combinations (e.g., text-only, image-only, or multimodal) conditioned on local graph context.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on diverse MMG benchmarks shows Mario consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph models in node classification and link prediction under both supervised and zero-shot settings. Code release planned.
AI.conclusion: Integrating graph topology into both feature learning and instruction tuning enables effective LLM-based reasoning over MMGs, addressing weak cross-modal consistency and heterogeneous modality preferences, and yielding strong performance gains across tasks.

title: Stable-LoRA: Stabilizing Feature Learning of Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a widely adopted parameter-efficient method for fine-tuning Large Langauge Models. It updates the weight matrix as $W=W_0+sBA$, where $W_0$ is the original frozen weight, $s$ is a scaling factor and $A$,$B$ are trainable low-rank matrices. Despite its robust empirical effectiveness, the theoretical foundations of LoRA remain insufficiently understood, particularly with respect to feature learning stability. In this paper, we first establish that, LoRA can, in principle, naturally achieve and sustain stable feature learning (i.e., be self-stabilized) under appropriate hyper-parameters and initializations of $A$ and $B$. However, we also uncover a fundamental limitation that the necessary non-zero initialization of $A$ compromises self-stability, leading to suboptimal performances. To address this challenge, we propose Stable-LoRA, a weight-shrinkage optimization strategy that dynamically enhances stability of LoRA feature learning. By progressively shrinking $A$ during the earliest training steps, Stable-LoRA is both theoretically and empirically validated to effectively eliminate instability of LoRA feature learning while preserving the benefits of the non-zero start. Experiments show that Stable-LoRA consistently outperforms other baselines across diverse models and tasks, with no additional memory usage and only negligible computation overheads. The code is available at https://github.com/Yize-Wu/Stable-LoRA.

AI.tldr: Stable-LoRA introduces a weight-shrinkage schedule that progressively reduces the A matrix during early training to stabilize LoRA feature learning, addressing instability caused by non-zero initialization while preserving LoRA benefits with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: The theoretical foundation of LoRA, particularly feature-learning stability, is unclear. While LoRA can be self-stabilized under certain hyperparameters, non-zero initialization of A compromises stability, leading to suboptimal performance. A method to ensure stability without sacrificing the favorable initialization is needed.
AI.method: Propose Stable-LoRA, a weight-shrinkage optimization that gradually shrinks A in the initial training steps. Provide theoretical analysis for stability conditions and empirical validation across diverse models and tasks. Demonstrate negligible memory and computation overhead and no additional memory usage versus standard LoRA. Code available at the authors' repository.
AI.result: The approach yields self-stabilizing LoRA feature learning by suppressing destabilizing effects of non-zero A initialization. Theoretical results delineate stability regimes, and empirical experiments show Stable-LoRA consistently outperforms baselines across tasks and models, with minimal overhead and no additional memory requirements.
AI.conclusion: Stable-LoRA effectively eliminates instability in LoRA feature learning while preserving its benefits, offering a practical optimization strategy with theoretical grounding and open-source implementation.

title: Logi-PAR: Logic-Infused Patient Activity Recognition via Differentiable Rule
summary: Patient Activity Recognition (PAR) in clinical settings uses activity data to improve safety and quality of care. Although significant progress has been made, current models mainly identify which activity is occurring. They often spatially compose sub-sparse visual cues using global and local attention mechanisms, yet only learn logically implicit patterns due to their neural-pipeline. Advancing clinical safety requires methods that can infer why a set of visual cues implies a risk, and how these can be compositionally reasoned through explicit logic beyond mere classification. To address this, we proposed Logi-PAR, the first Logic-Infused Patient Activity Recognition Framework that integrates contextual fact fusion as a multi-view primitive extractor and injects neural-guided differentiable rules. Our method automatically learns rules from visual cues, optimizing them end-to-end while enabling the implicit emergence patterns to be explicitly labelled during training. To the best of our knowledge, Logi-PAR is the first framework to recognize patient activity by applying learnable logic rules to symbolic mappings. It produces auditable why explanations as rule traces and supports counterfactual interventions (e.g., risk would decrease by 65% if assistance were present). Extensive evaluation on clinical benchmarks (VAST and OmniFall) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming Vision-Language Models and transformer baselines. The code is available via: https://github.com/zararkhan985/Logi-PAR.git}

AI.tldr: Logi-PAR introduces a Logic-Infused Patient Activity Recognition framework that learns differentiable, human-readable logic rules from visual cues, enabling auditable explanations (rule traces) and counterfactuals, and achieves state-of-the-art results on clinical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current PAR methods largely perform classification with neural pipelines, yielding implicit patterns and opaque decisions. There is a need for explicit, auditable reasoning that explains why a visual cue combination implies risk and supports counterfactuals to guide clinical interventions.
AI.method: Logi-PAR integrates contextual fact fusion as a multi-view primitive extractor and injects neural-guided differentiable rules, learning rules from visual cues end-to-end with symbolic mappings. It produces rule traces for explanations and supports counterfactual interventions.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on VAST and OmniFall, significantly outperforming Vision-Language Models and transformer baselines; provides auditable why explanations and trains with end-to-end differentiable logic; code available.
AI.conclusion: First framework to apply learnable logic rules to symbolic mappings for PAR, enabling explicit, auditable reasoning and counterfactuals, with potential to enhance clinical safety and interpretability.

title: Early Warning of Intraoperative Adverse Events via Transformer-Driven Multi-Label Learning
summary: Early warning of intraoperative adverse events plays a vital role in reducing surgical risk and improving patient safety. While deep learning has shown promise in predicting the single adverse event, several key challenges remain: overlooking adverse event dependencies, underutilizing heterogeneous clinical data, and suffering from the class imbalance inherent in medical datasets. To address these issues, we construct the first Multi-label Adverse Events dataset (MuAE) for intraoperative adverse events prediction, covering six critical events. Next, we propose a novel Transformerbased multi-label learning framework (IAENet) that combines an improved Time-Aware Feature-wise Linear Modulation (TAFiLM) module for static covariates and dynamic variables robust fusion and complex temporal dependencies modeling. Furthermore, we introduce a Label-Constrained Reweighting Loss (LCRLoss) with co-occurrence regularization to effectively mitigate intra-event imbalance and enforce structured consistency among frequently co-occurring events. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IAENet consistently outperforms strong baselines on 5, 10, and 15-minute early warning tasks, achieving improvements of +5.05%, +2.82%, and +7.57% on average F1 score. These results highlight the potential of IAENet for supporting intelligent intraoperative decision-making in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: First MuAE multi-label intraoperative adverse events dataset; proposes IAENet with TAfiLM and LCRLoss; demonstrates F1 gains in 5/10/15-min early warning.
AI.motivation: Address adverse-event dependencies, underutilization of heterogeneous clinical data, and class imbalance in intraoperative adverse-event prediction to enable reliable multi-label early warnings.
AI.method: Transformer-based IAENet with an improved Time-Aware FiLM module (TAFiLM) for static/dynamic data fusion and temporal modeling; include Label-Constrained Reweighting Loss (LCRLoss) with co-occurrence regularization; construct MuAE dataset covering six events.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines on 5/10/15-minute tasks; average F1 gains of 5.05, 2.82, and 7.57 percentage points, respectively.
AI.conclusion: IAENet demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of multi-label, temporally aware warning for intraoperative safety, leveraging heterogeneous data and event co-occurrence patterns to improve early predictions.

title: Semantic Class Distribution Learning for Debiasing Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Medical image segmentation is critical for computer-aided diagnosis. However, dense pixel-level annotation is time-consuming and expensive, and medical datasets often exhibit severe class imbalance. Such imbalance causes minority structures to be overwhelmed by dominant classes in feature representations, hindering the learning of discriminative features and making reliable segmentation particularly challenging. To address this, we propose the Semantic Class Distribution Learning (SCDL) framework, a plug-and-play module that mitigates supervision and representation biases by learning structured class-conditional feature distributions. SCDL integrates Class Distribution Bidirectional Alignment (CDBA) to align embeddings with learnable class proxies and leverages Semantic Anchor Constraints (SAC) to guide proxies using labeled data. Experiments on the Synapse and AMOS datasets demonstrate that SCDL significantly improves segmentation performance across both overall and class-level metrics, with particularly strong gains on minority classes, achieving state-of-the-art results. Our code is released at https://github.com/Zyh55555/SCDL.

AI.tldr: SCDL is a plug-and-play framework for medical image segmentation that learns structured class-conditional feature distributions to combat label scarcity and class imbalance, achieving state-of-the-art results on Synapse and AMOS, especially for minority classes.
AI.motivation: Medical segmentation suffers from dense annotation cost and severe class imbalance; minority structures are underrepresented in feature space, hindering discriminative learning.
AI.method: Introduce Semantic Class Distribution Learning (SCDL) with Class Distribution Bidirectional Alignment (CDBA) to align embeddings with learnable class proxies, and Semantic Anchor Constraints (SAC) to guide proxies with labeled data; plug-and-play module.
AI.result: On Synapse and AMOS datasets, SCDL improves overall and per-class metrics, especially minority classes; achieves state-of-the-art results; code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: SCDL effectively mitigates supervision and representation biases in medical segmentation and demonstrates strong generalization across datasets, with significant gains on minority classes.

title: The Geometric Inductive Bias of Grokking: Bypassing Phase Transitions via Architectural Topology
comment: 19 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code available at https://github.com/AlperYildirim1/geometric-grokking
summary:
Mechanistic interpretability typically relies on post-hoc analysis of trained networks. We instead adopt an interventional approach: testing hypotheses a priori by modifying architectural topology to observe training dynamics. We study grokking - delayed generalization in Transformers trained on cyclic modular addition (Zp) - investigating if specific architectural degrees of freedom prolong the memorization phase.
  We identify two independent structural factors in standard Transformers: unbounded representational magnitude and data-dependent attention routing. First, we introduce a fully bounded spherical topology enforcing L2 normalization throughout the residual stream and an unembedding matrix with a fixed temperature scale. This removes magnitude-based degrees of freedom, reducing grokking onset time by over 20x without weight decay. Second, a Uniform Attention Ablation overrides data-dependent query-key routing with a uniform distribution, reducing the attention layer to a Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) aggregator. Despite removing adaptive routing, these models achieve 100% generalization across all seeds and bypass the grokking delay entirely.
  To evaluate whether this acceleration is a task-specific geometric alignment rather than a generic optimization stabilizer, we use non-commutative S5 permutation composition as a negative control. Enforcing spherical constraints on S5 does not accelerate generalization. This suggests eliminating the memorization phase depends strongly on aligning architectural priors with the task's intrinsic symmetries. Together, these findings provide interventional evidence that architectural degrees of freedom substantially influence grokking, suggesting a predictive structural perspective on training dynamics.

AI.tldr: Interventional architectural edits in Transformers show architectural priors can eliminate grokking: enforcing bounded representations and uniform attention speeds up generalization on cyclic modular addition (Zp); a negative control using S5 shows the effect is geometry-specific.
AI.motivation: Test whether grokking delays arise from architectural degrees of freedom, by making a priori architectural hypotheses and observing training dynamics, moving beyond post-hoc analyses.
AI.method: Interventional study: (1) enforce a fully bounded spherical topology with L2 normalization in the residual stream and a fixed-temperature unembedding matrix to remove magnitude degrees of freedom; (2) apply Uniform Attention Ablation to replace data-dependent query-key routing with a uniform distribution, reducing attention to a CBOW-like aggregator; test on transformers trained on cyclic modular addition (Zp). Use multiple seeds to assess generalization timing; include non-commutative S5 permutation composition as a negative control (spherical constraints on S5) to test geometry specificity.
AI.result: Bounded topology reduces grokking onset time by more than 20x without weight decay. Uniform Attention Ablation yields 100% generalization across all seeds and bypasses the grokking delay entirely. S5 spherical constraint does not accelerate generalization, indicating the effect is tied to task geometry rather than generic optimization stabilization.
