AI.conclusion: Architectural priors aligned with the task's intrinsic symmetries can causally influence training dynamics, providing a predictive, structural perspective on grokking and supporting interventional mechanistic interpretability.

title: SPyCer: Semi-Supervised Physics-Guided Contextual Attention for Near-Surface Air Temperature Estimation from Satellite Imagery
summary: Modern Earth observation relies on satellites to capture detailed surface properties. Yet, many phenomena that affect humans and ecosystems unfold in the atmosphere close to the surface. Near-ground sensors provide accurate measurements of certain environmental characteristics, such as near-surface air temperature (NSAT). However, they remain sparse and unevenly distributed, limiting their ability to provide continuous spatial measurements. To bridge this gap, we introduce SPyCer, a semi-supervised physics-guided network that can leverage pixel information and physical modeling to guide the learning process through meaningful physical properties. It is designed for continuous estimation of NSAT by proxy using satellite imagery. SPyCer frames NSAT prediction as a pixel-wise vision problem, where each near-ground sensor is projected onto satellite image coordinates and positioned at the center of a local image patch. The corresponding sensor pixel is supervised using both observed NSAT and physics-based constraints, while surrounding pixels contribute through physics-guided regularization derived from the surface energy balance and advection-diffusion-reaction partial differential equations. To capture the physical influence of neighboring pixels, SPyCer employs a multi-head attention guided by land cover characteristics and modulated with Gaussian distance weighting. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SPyCer produces spatially coherent and physically consistent NSAT estimates, outperforming existing baselines in terms of accuracy, generalization, and alignment with underlying physical processes.

AI.tldr: SPyCer is a semi-supervised, physics-guided network for dense estimation of near-ground air temperature (NSAT) using satellite imagery. It treats NSAT prediction as a pixel-wise vision task with sensor supervision and physics-based regularization, leveraging surface energy balance and ADEs, plus a land-cover–aware, distance-weighted multi-head attention. It yields spatially coherent, physically consistent NSAT maps that surpass baselines in accuracy, generalization, and physical realism.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between sparse near-ground sensors and the need for continuous NSAT fields. Satellite data can bridge this gap, but purely data-driven methods may lack physical plausibility. Incorporating physics-based constraints and grid-level regularization can improve both accuracy and consistency with underlying processes.
AI.method: Formulate NSAT estimation as a pixel-wise learning problem where each sensor is projected to a central patch. Train with supervised NSAT at the sensor pixel and enforce physics-based constraints (surface energy balance, advection-diffusion-reaction PDEs) on surrounding pixels. Use a multi-head attention mechanism guided by land cover and fused with Gaussian distance weighting to capture spatial influence. Semi-supervised learning leverages both labeled sensors and unlabeled pixels through physics guidance.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world data show SPyCer produces spatially coherent NSAT estimates that align with physical processes and outperform baselines in accuracy and generalization.
AI.conclusion: A semi-supervised, physics-guided approach can effectively estimate near-ground atmospheric variables from satellite imagery by combining pixel-wise supervision with physics-based regularization and a spatially aware attention mechanism.

title: SlideSparse: Fast and Flexible (2N-2):2N Structured Sparsity
summary: NVIDIA's 2:4 Sparse Tensor Cores deliver 2x throughput but demand strict 50% pruning -- a ratio that collapses LLM reasoning accuracy (Qwen3: 54% to 15%). Milder $(2N-2):2N$ patterns (e.g., 6:8, 25% pruning) preserve accuracy yet receive no hardware support, falling back to dense execution without any benefit from sparsity. We present SlideSparse, the first system to unlock Sparse Tensor Core acceleration for the $(2N-2):2N$ model family on commodity GPUs. Our Sliding Window Decomposition reconstructs any $(2N-2):2N$ weight block into $N-1$ overlapping 2:4-compliant windows without any accuracy loss; Activation Lifting fuses the corresponding activation rearrangement into per-token quantization at near-zero cost. Integrated into vLLM, SlideSparse is evaluated across various GPUs (A100, H100, B200, RTX 4090, RTX 5080, DGX-spark), precisions (FP4, INT8, FP8, BF16, FP16), and model families (Llama, Qwen, BitNet). On compute-bound workloads, the measured speedup ratio (1.33x) approaches the theoretical upper-bound $N/(N-1)=4/3$ at 6:8 weight sparsity in Qwen2.5-7B, establishing $(2N-2):2N$ as a practical path to accuracy-preserving LLM acceleration. Code available at https://github.com/bcacdwk/vllmbench.

AI.tldr: SlideSparse unlocks Sparse Tensor Core acceleration for (2N-2):2N LLM weight patterns on commodity GPUs, achieving up to 1.33x speedup at 6:8 sparsity by reconstructing blocks into overlapping 2:4 windows and fusing activations; enabling practical accuracy-preserving sparsity for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing 2:4 Sparse Tensor Cores demand strict 50% pruning, which degrades LLM reasoning accuracy. Milder (2N-2):2N patterns lack hardware support and fall back to dense execution. There is a need to enable acceleration for these patterns without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Sliding Window Decomposition: rewrite any (2N-2):2N weight block as N-1 overlapping 2:4-compliant windows. Activation Lifting: fuse activation rearrangement into per-token quantization at near-zero cost. Integration into vLLM; evaluation across GPUs, precisions, and model families.
AI.result: Measured speedup 1.33x on compute-bound workloads, approaching the theoretical upper bound N/(N-1)=4/3 at 6:8 weight sparsity in Qwen2.5-7B. Demonstrates that (2N-2):2N is a practical path to accuracy-preserving LLM acceleration.
AI.conclusion: SlideSparse is the first system to enable Sparse Tensor Core acceleration for the (2N-2):2N model family on commodity GPUs, preserving accuracy while delivering near-upper-bound speedups; supports multiple GPUs, precisions, and model families; code released.

title: Recursive Inference Machines for Neural Reasoning
summary: Neural reasoners such as Tiny Recursive Models (TRMs) solve complex problems by combining neural backbones with specialized inference schemes. Such inference schemes have been a central component of stochastic reasoning systems, where inference rules are applied to a stochastic model to derive answers to complex queries. In this work, we bridge these two paradigms by introducing Recursive Inference Machines (RIMs), a neural reasoning framework that explicitly incorporates recursive inference mechanisms inspired by classical inference engines. We show that TRMs can be expressed as an instance of RIMs, allowing us to extend them through a reweighting component, yielding better performance on challenging reasoning benchmarks, including ARC-AGI-1, ARC-AGI-2, and Sudoku Extreme. Furthermore, we show that RIMs can be used to improve reasoning on other tasks, such as the classification of tabular data, outperforming TabPFNs.

AI.tldr: Introduces Recursive Inference Machines (RIMs) that subsume Tiny Recursive Models (TRMs) and enhance neural reasoning via recursive inference, achieving stronger results on ARC-AGI and Sudoku Extreme, and improving tabular data classification over TabPFNs.
AI.motivation: Bridge neural reasoning architectures with classical inference engines; address limitations of TRMs by embedding them within a recursive inference framework and enabling reweighting for better inference dynamics.
AI.method: Define RIMs as a neural framework with recursive inference; demonstrate TRMs as a special case of RIMs; extend RIMs with a reweighting component; evaluate on reasoning benchmarks (ARC-AGI-1/2, Sudoku Extreme) and tabular data classification against TabPFNs.
AI.result: RIMs improve performance on ARC-AGI-1, ARC-AGI-2, Sudoku Extreme; reweighting yields gains; in tabular data, RIMs outperform TabPFNs.
AI.conclusion: RIMs offer a unified neural-recursive inference framework that strengthens reasoning capabilities across diverse tasks, by integrating reinterpretations of existing models and a reweighting mechanism.

title: CATNet: Collaborative Alignment and Transformation Network for Cooperative Perception
summary: Cooperative perception significantly enhances scene understanding by integrating complementary information from diverse agents. However, existing research often overlooks critical challenges inherent in real-world multi-source data integration, specifically high temporal latency and multi-source noise. To address these practical limitations, we propose Collaborative Alignment and Transformation Network (CATNet), an adaptive compensation framework that resolves temporal latency and noise interference in multi-agent systems. Our key innovations can be summarized in three aspects. First, we introduce a Spatio-Temporal Recurrent Synchronization (STSync) that aligns asynchronous feature streams via adjacent-frame differential modeling, establishing a temporal-spatially unified representation space. Second, we design a Dual-Branch Wavelet Enhanced Denoiser (WTDen) that suppresses global noise and reconstructs localized feature distortions within aligned representations. Third, we construct an Adaptive Feature Selector (AdpSel) that dynamically focuses on critical perceptual features for robust fusion. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that CATNet consistently outperforms existing methods under complex traffic conditions, proving its superior robustness and adaptability.

AI.tldr: CATNet proposes a cooperative perception framework addressing temporal latency and noise through (1) STSync for spatio-temporal synchronization, (2) WTDen denoiser for global/local noise reduction, and (3) AdpSel for adaptive feature fusion, yielding robust multi-agent perception under complex traffic.
AI.motivation: Real-world multi-source perception suffers from high temporal latency and multi-source noise, hindering accurate fusion and scene understanding; a method that aligns asynchronous streams, denoises representations, and adaptively selects features is needed.
AI.method: Spatio-Temporal Recurrent Synchronization (STSync) aligns asynchronous feature streams via adjacent-frame differential modeling to create a unified temporal-spatial representation. Dual-Branch Wavelet Enhanced Denoiser (WTDen) suppresses global noise and reconstructs local distortions within aligned features. Adaptive Feature Selector (AdpSel) dynamically emphasizes salient perceptual features to improve fusion robustness.
AI.result: CATNet consistently outperforms existing methods across multiple datasets and complex traffic scenarios, demonstrating enhanced robustness and adaptability in cooperative perception.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CATNet effectively mitigates latency and noise challenges in multi-agent perception by integrating (STSync), (WTDen), and (AdpSel), enabling robust and adaptive cooperative perception in dynamic environments.

title: A Behaviour-Aware Federated Forecasting Framework for Distributed Stand-Alone Wind Turbines
summary: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is essential for grid dispatch and market operations, yet centralising turbine data raises privacy, cost, and heterogeneity concerns. We propose a two-stage federated learning framework that first clusters turbines by long-term behavioural statistics using Double Roulette Selection (DRS) initialisation with recursive Auto-split refinement, and then trains cluster-specific LSTM models via FedAvg. Experiments on 400 stand-alone turbines in Denmark show that DRS-auto discovers behaviourally coherent groups and achieves competitive forecasting accuracy while preserving data locality. Behaviour-aware grouping consistently outperforms geographic partitioning and matches strong k-means++ baselines, suggesting a practical privacy-friendly solution for heterogeneous distributed turbine fleets.

AI.tldr: A two-stage federated learning approach for wind power forecasting clusters turbines by long-term behavior using Double Roulette Selection (DRS) with auto-split refinement, then trains cluster-specific LSTMs via FedAvg, achieving privacy-preserving, accurate short-term forecasts.
AI.motivation: Turbine data is highly distributed, heterogeneous, and sensitive. Centralized data sharing is costly and raises privacy and governance concerns, motivating a privacy-preserving approach that leverages local data while capturing inter-turbine heterogeneity.
AI.method: Stage 1: Cluster turbines by long-term behavioral statistics using Double Roulette Selection (DRS) initialisation with recursive Auto-split refinement to form behaviorally coherent groups. Stage 2: Train cluster-specific LSTM models using Federated Averaging (FedAvg) across turbines within each cluster. Evaluation on 400 stand-alone turbines in Denmark comparing behavior-aware grouping to geographic partitioning and k-means++ baselines.
AI.result: DRS-auto clustering yields behaviorally coherent groups and competitive forecasting accuracy while preserving data locality. Behavior-aware grouping consistently outperforms geographic partitioning and matches strong k-means++ baselines, demonstrating a practical privacy-friendly solution for heterogeneous distributed turbine fleets.
AI.conclusion: Behavior-aware turbine clustering via DRS initialization plus recursive refinement enables effective cluster-specific forecasting with federated learning, offering privacy-preserving gains and robust performance compared to traditional partitions and baselines.

title: Wiki-R1: Incentivizing Multimodal Reasoning for Knowledge-based VQA via Data and Sampling Curriculum
comment: Accepted by ICLR 26, code and weights are publicly available
summary: Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to answer questions about an image by integrating external knowledge, posing significant challenges due to noisy retrieval and the structured, encyclopedic nature of the knowledge base. These characteristics create a distributional gap from pretrained multimodal large language models (MLLMs), making effective reasoning and domain adaptation difficult in the post-training stage. In this work, we propose \textit{Wiki-R1}, a data-generation-based curriculum reinforcement learning framework that systematically incentivizes reasoning in MLLMs for KB-VQA. Wiki-R1 constructs a sequence of training distributions aligned with the model's evolving capability, bridging the gap from pretraining to the KB-VQA target distribution. We introduce \textit{controllable curriculum data generation}, which manipulates the retriever to produce samples at desired difficulty levels, and a \textit{curriculum sampling strategy} that selects informative samples likely to yield non-zero advantages during RL updates. Sample difficulty is estimated using observed rewards and propagated to unobserved samples to guide learning. Experiments on two KB-VQA benchmarks, Encyclopedic VQA and InfoSeek, demonstrate that Wiki-R1 achieves new state-of-the-art results, improving accuracy from 35.5\% to 37.1\% on Encyclopedic VQA and from 40.1\% to 44.1\% on InfoSeek. The project page is available at https://artanic30.github.io/project_pages/WikiR1/.

AI.tldr: Wiki-R1 is a data-generation-based curriculum RL framework for KB-VQA that aligns training distributions with model capability, using controllable curriculum data generation and curriculum sampling to improve KB-VQA performance; it achieves SOTA on Encyclopedic VQA and InfoSeek.
AI.motivation: KB-VQA struggles due to noisy retrieval and the encyclopedic nature of knowledge bases, causing a distributional gap with pretrained MLLMs and hindering post-training adaptation and reasoning.
AI.method: Builds a sequence of training distributions that evolve with the model's capability. Introduces controllable curriculum data generation by manipulating the retriever to generate samples at target difficulty. Employs a curriculum sampling strategy to select informative samples likely to yield non-zero advantages during RL updates. Estimates sample difficulty from observed rewards and propagates this to unobserved samples to guide learning.
AI.result: On Encyclopedic VQA and InfoSeek benchmarks, Wiki-R1 improves accuracy from 35.5% to 37.1% and from 40.1% to 44.1%, respectively, achieving new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: Data-generation-based curriculum reinforcement learning effectively bridges the gap between pretraining and KB-VQA target distributions, enabling improved reasoning in MLLMs for knowledge-grounded visual QA.

title: Beyond Word Error Rate: Auditing the Diversity Tax in Speech Recognition through Dataset Cartography
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems are predominantly evaluated using the Word Error Rate (WER). However, raw token-level metrics fail to capture semantic fidelity and routinely obscures the `diversity tax', the disproportionate burden on marginalized and atypical speaker due to systematic recognition failures. In this paper, we explore the limitations of relying solely on lexical counts by systematically evaluating a broader class of non-linear and semantic metrics. To enable rigorous model auditing, we introduce the sample difficulty index (SDI), a novel metric that quantifies how intrinsic demographic and acoustic factors drive model failure. By mapping SDI on data cartography, we demonstrate that metrics EmbER and SemDist expose hidden systemic biases and inter-model disagreements that WER ignores. Finally, our findings are the first steps towards a robust audit framework for prospective safety analysis, empowering developers to audit and mitigate ASR disparities prior to deployment.

AI.tldr: Beyond WER: non-linear and semantic metrics for ASR auditing; introduces sample difficulty index (SDI) to quantify demographic/acoustic drivers of failures; data cartography to reveal biases and inter-model disagreements via EmbER/SemDist; frames robust, prospective safety auditing for ASR disparities.
AI.motivation: To address the insufficiency of lexical tokens (WER) in capturing semantic fidelity and disparate impact; to enable rigorous auditing by measuring non-linear/semantic aspects and demographic/acoustic drivers of errors.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of semantic and non-linear metrics (e.g., EmbER, SemDist) beyond WER; development of Sample Difficulty Index (SDI); mapping SDI in data cartography to analyze bias and model failure modes; comparison across ASR models.
AI.result: SDI and data cartography reveal drivers of failures tied to demographic/acoustic factors; EmbER and SemDist expose hidden systemic biases and inter-model disagreements that WER misses; evidence supports a prospective safety audit framework for ASR disparities.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a foundational step toward a robust audit framework for ASR disparities prior to deployment, enabling developers to audit and mitigate inequities and safety concerns in ASR systems.

title: Layer by layer, module by module: Choose both for optimal OOD probing of ViT
summary: Recent studies have observed that intermediate layers of foundation models often yield more discriminative representations than the final layer. While initially attributed to autoregressive pretraining, this phenomenon has also been identified in models trained via supervised and discriminative self-supervised objectives. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive study to analyze the behavior of intermediate layers in pretrained vision transformers. Through extensive linear probing experiments across a diverse set of image classification benchmarks, we find that distribution shift between pretraining and downstream data is the primary cause of performance degradation in deeper layers. Furthermore, we perform a fine-grained analysis at the module level. Our findings reveal that standard probing of transformer block outputs is suboptimal; instead, probing the activation within the feedforward network yields the best performance under significant distribution shift, whereas the normalized output of the multi-head self-attention module is optimal when the shift is weak.

AI.tldr: Distribution shift drives deeper-layer degradation in pretrained vision transformers; FFN-activation probing is most effective under large shifts, while normalised MSA outputs are best under weak shifts.
AI.motivation: To understand why intermediate layers sometimes provide better representations than final layers in pretrained vision transformers across supervised and self-supervised objectives, and how probing strategies interact with distribution shift.
AI.method: Extensive linear probing across diverse image classification benchmarks, with fine-grained module-level probing comparing transformer block outputs, FFN activations, and MSA outputs under varying distribution-shift conditions.
AI.result: Deep layers degrade mainly due to distribution shift between pretraining and downstream data. Standard transformer-block probes are suboptimal. Probing FFN activations yields the best performance under significant distribution shift, while probing the normalized MHA output is optimal when the shift is weak.
AI.conclusion: Layer- and probe-choice should be aligned with the degree of distribution shift; FFN-focused probes are robust for transfer under substantial shift, whereas MSA-output probes suffice for less shifted domains. These insights inform evaluation, layer selection, and fine-tuning of vision transformers.

title: Whispering to a Blackbox: Bootstrapping Frozen OCR with Visual Prompts
summary: In the landscape of modern machine learning, frozen pre-trained models provide stability and efficiency but often underperform on specific tasks due to mismatched data distributions. This paper introduces the Whisperer, a novel visual prompting framework that learns diffusion-based preprocessors to adapt inputs in pixel space, effectively "whispering" enhancements to frozen downstream models like EasyOCR. By framing the process as behavioral cloning of stochastically discovered improvement policies, our method achieves an 8% absolute (10.6% relative) reduction in Character Error Rate (CER) on a challenging dataset of 300k degraded synthetic text images, surpassing hand-engineered baselines such as CLAHE. The key innovation is a four-stage training curriculum that uses behavioral cloning to amplify "lucky" improvements discovered through the stochastic exploration of a partially trained diffusion model. This approach is highly sample-efficient and avoids the pitfalls of traditional reinforcement learning. Crucially, we frame this not as naive reinforcement learning, but as behavioral cloning of an exploration policy: we stochastically sample intermediate diffusion outputs, select those that improve CER by chance, and then train the model to reproduce them. This bootstrapping curriculum (4 stages over 60 GPU-hours) amplifies random successes into a systematic strategy. In summary, by whispering to the frozen OCR through its inputs, we improve an imperfect classifier without touching its weights.

AI.tldr: Whisperer is a diffusion-based visual preprocessor that adapts inputs to frozen OCR models via a four-stage behavioral cloning curriculum, achieving 8 percentage-point absolute CER improvement (10.6% relative) on 300k degraded synthetic text images and surpassing CLAHE baselines.
AI.motivation: Frozen pre-trained models offer stability but underperform on task-specific distributions. The paper proposes input-space adaptation (whispering) to improve performance without weights fine-tuning, aiming for sample-efficient gains with clear gains over traditional baselines.
AI.method: Train a diffusion-based preprocessor that operates in pixel space to subtly modify inputs. Frame the search for improvements as stochastic exploration of a partially trained diffusion model, and use behavioral cloning to reproduce successful intermediate outputs. A four-stage curriculum over ~60 GPU-hours bootstraps a policy that amplifies rare lucky improvements into a systematic strategy, avoiding reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Achieves 8% absolute CER reduction (10.6% relative) on a dataset of 300k degraded synthetic text images, outperforming hand-engineered baselines like CLAHE.
AI.conclusion: Whisperer demonstrates that prompting frozen OCR via input whispering, guided by a four-stage behavioral cloning curriculum, can yield substantial performance gains without touching model weights, offering a sample-efficient pathway for adapting frozen models.

title: Fusion4CA: Boosting 3D Object Detection via Comprehensive Image Exploitation
summary: Nowadays, an increasing number of works fuse LiDAR and RGB data in the bird's-eye view (BEV) space for 3D object detection in autonomous driving systems. However, existing methods suffer from over-reliance on the LiDAR branch, with insufficient exploration of RGB information. To tackle this issue, we propose Fusion4CA, which is built upon the classic BEVFusion framework and dedicated to fully exploiting visual input with plug-and-play components. Specifically, a contrastive alignment module is designed to calibrate image features with 3D geometry, and a camera auxiliary branch is introduced to mine RGB information sufficiently during training. For further performance enhancement, we leverage an off-the-shelf cognitive adapter to make the most of pretrained image weights, and integrate a standard coordinate attention module into the fusion stage as a supplementary boost. Experiments on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate that our method achieves 69.7% mAP with only 6 training epochs and a mere 3.48% increase in inference parameters, yielding a 1.2% improvement over the baseline which is fully trained for 20 epochs. Extensive experiments in a simulated lunar environment further validate the effectiveness and generalization of our method. Our code will be released through Fusion4CA.

AI.tldr: Fusion4CA augments BEVFusion with contrastive image-geometry alignment, a camera auxiliary branch, a pretrained image-weight adapter, and coordinate attention to better exploit RGB for BEV-based 3D detection. It delivers notable gains on nuScenes with short training and modest parameter overhead, and generalizes to a simulated lunar domain.
AI.motivation: Existing BEV fusion methods over-rely on LiDAR and underutilize RGB data. There is a need for effective, plug-and-play RGB enhancement components that improve 3D detection without heavy training costs or large parameter increases.
AI.method: Builds on BEVFusion and adds (1) a contrastive alignment module to align image features with 3D geometry, (2) a camera auxiliary branch to mine RGB information during training, (3) a cognitive adapter to leverage pretrained image weights, and (4) a coordinate attention module in the fusion stage.
AI.result: On nuScenes, achieves 69.7% mAP with only 6 training epochs and a 3.48% increase in inference parameters; improves over a fully-trained baseline by 1.2%. Additional experiments in a simulated lunar environment support generalization.
AI.conclusion: Fusion4CA demonstrates that carefully integrated RGB-centric modules can substantially boost BEV-based 3D detection with limited training and modest model overhead, validating the value of RGB information in fusion-based autonomous driving pipelines.

title: Knowledge Divergence and the Value of Debate for Scalable Oversight
summary: AI safety via debate and reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF) are both proposed methods for scalable oversight of advanced AI systems, yet no formal framework relates them or characterizes when debate offers an advantage. We analyze this by parameterizing debate's value through the geometry of knowledge divergence between debating models. Using principal angles between models' representation subspaces, we prove that the debate advantage admits an exact closed form. When models share identical training corpora, debate reduces to RLAIF-like where a single-agent method recovers the same optimum. When models possess divergent knowledge, debate advantage scales with a phase transition from quadratic regime (debate offers negligible benefit) to linear regime (debate is essential). We classify three regimes of knowledge divergence (shared, one-sided, and compositional) and provide existence results showing that debate can achieve outcomes inaccessible to either model alone, alongside a negative result showing that sufficiently strong adversarial incentives cause coordination failure in the compositional regime, with a sharp threshold separating effective from ineffective debate. We offer the first formal connection between debate and RLAIF, a geometric foundation for understanding when adversarial oversight protocols are justified, and connection to the problem of eliciting latent knowledge across models with complementary information.

AI.tldr: A formal geometric analysis of AI safety debate vs RLAIF, deriving a closed-form debate value via principal angles of representation subspaces, showing when debate helps, and linking to RLAIF with regimes based on knowledge divergence.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a formal framework relating debate and RLAIF, and characterize when debate offers advantages in scalable oversight.
AI.method: Model knowledge divergence via subspace geometry; compute debate value using principal angles; classify regimes (shared, one-sided, compositional); prove existence results and a phase-transition in benefit; analyze adversarial incentives.
AI.result: Debate advantage has an exact closed form; in identical corpora it reduces to RLAIF; with divergent knowledge, benefits scale from quadratic to linear; three regimes identified; debate can unlock outcomes inaccessible to either model; adversarial incentives can cause coordination failure in compositional regime with a sharp threshold.
AI.conclusion: Provides a geometric foundation linking debate and RLAIF, clarifies when adversarial oversight is justified, and relates debate to eliciting latent knowledge across complementary models.

title: Frequency-Aware Error-Bounded Caching for Accelerating Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have emerged as the dominant architecture for high-quality image and video generation, yet their iterative denoising process incurs substantial computational cost during inference. Existing caching methods accelerate DiTs by reusing intermediate computations across timesteps, but they share a common limitation: treating the denoising process as uniform across time,depth, and feature dimensions. In this work, we identify three orthogonal axes of non-uniformity in DiT denoising: (1) temporal -- sensitivity to caching errors varies dramatically across the denoising trajectory; (2) depth -- consecutive caching decisions lead to cascading approximation errors; and (3) feature -- different components of the hidden state exhibit heterogeneous temporal dynamics. Based on these observations, we propose SpectralCache, a unified caching framework comprising Timestep-Aware Dynamic Scheduling (TADS), Cumulative Error Budgets (CEB), and Frequency-Decomposed Caching (FDC). On FLUX.1-schnell at 512x512 resolution, SpectralCache achieves 2.46x speedup with LPIPS 0.217 and SSIM 0.727, outperforming TeaCache (2.12x, LPIPS 0.215, SSIM 0.734) by 16% in speed while maintaining comparable quality (LPIPS difference < 1%). Our approach is training-free, plug-and-play, and compatible with existing DiT architectures.

AI.tldr: SpectralCache accelerates diffusion transformers by non-uniform caching across time, depth, and feature, introducing TADS, CEB, and FDC; achieves 2.46x speedup on 512x512 FLUX.1-schnell with comparable perceptual metrics, training-free, plug-and-play.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost of diffusion transformers by exploiting non-uniform denoising dynamics across temporal steps, depth, and feature channels, preventing error accumulation and preserving quality.
AI.method: SpectralCache combines three components: (1) Timestep-Aware Dynamic Scheduling (adaptive caching across timesteps), (2) Cumulative Error Budgets (control of errors from caching to bound quality loss), (3) Frequency-Decomposed Caching (separate handling of dynamics in different frequency components). It is training-free and compatible with existing DiT architectures.
AI.result: On FLUX.1-schnell at 512x512, achieves 2.46x speedup with LPIPS 0.217 and SSIM 0.727; outperforms TeaCache (2.12x, LPIPS 0.215, SSIM 0.734) by ~16% in speed while maintaining similar quality (LPIPS diff < 0.001).
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, orthogonal caching along temporal, depth, and feature axes yields substantial inference speedups for diffusion transformers without retraining, with broad plug-and-play applicability to existing DiT models.

title: WavSLM: Single-Stream Speech Language Modeling via WavLM Distillation
summary: Large language models show that simple autoregressive training can yield scalable and coherent generation, but extending this paradigm to speech remains challenging due to the entanglement of semantic and acoustic information. Most existing speech language models rely on text supervision, hierarchical token streams, or complex hybrid architectures, departing from the single-stream generative pretraining paradigm that has proven effective in text. In this work, we introduce WavSLM, a speech language model trained by quantizing and distilling self-supervised WavLM representations into a single codebook and optimizing an autoregressive next-chunk prediction objective. WavSLM jointly models semantic and acoustic information within a single token stream without text supervision or text pretraining. Despite its simplicity, it achieves competitive performance on consistency benchmarks and speech generation while using fewer parameters, less training data, and supporting streaming inference. Demo samples are available at https://lucadellalib.github.io/wavslm-web/.

AI.tldr: WavSLM proposes a single-stream autoregressive speech language model by quantizing and distilling WavLM into a codebook to predict next chunks, enabling textless semantic-acoustic joint modeling and streaming inference.
AI.motivation: To realize a simple, scalable autoregressive pretraining paradigm for speech that avoids text supervision, hierarchical tokenization, or hybrid architectures and supports streaming generation.
AI.method: Quantize and distill self-supervised WavLM representations into a single codebook and train an autoregressive next-chunk predictor, yielding a single token stream that models both semantic and acoustic information without text supervision.
AI.result: Competitive performance on consistency benchmarks and speech generation with fewer parameters and less training data, and support for streaming inference; demonstrates efficacy of single-stream autoregressive modeling in speech.
AI.conclusion: A single-token-stream autoregressive framework can jointly encode semantic and acoustic speech information without text supervision, enabling scalable, coherent speech generation with reduced resources.

title: Dark3R: Learning Structure from Motion in the Dark
summary: We introduce Dark3R, a framework for structure from motion in the dark that operates directly on raw images with signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) below $-4$ dB -- a regime where conventional feature- and learning-based methods break down. Our key insight is to adapt large-scale 3D foundation models to extreme low-light conditions through a teacher--student distillation process, enabling robust feature matching and camera pose estimation in low light. Dark3R requires no 3D supervision; it is trained solely on noisy--clean raw image pairs, which can be either captured directly or synthesized using a simple Poisson--Gaussian noise model applied to well-exposed raw images. To train and evaluate our approach, we introduce a new, exposure-bracketed dataset that includes $\sim$42,000 multi-view raw images with ground-truth 3D annotations, and we demonstrate that Dark3R achieves state-of-the-art structure from motion in the low-SNR regime. Further, we demonstrate state-of-the-art novel view synthesis in the dark using Dark3R's predicted poses and a coarse-to-fine radiance field optimization procedure.

AI.tldr: Dark3R adapts large-scale 3D foundation models for structure-from-motion in extreme low-light by teacher–student distillation on raw, ultra-low-SNR imagery, without 3D supervision; introduces an exposure-bracketed dataset (~42k multi-view raw images with ground-truth 3D); achieves state-of-the-art SfM and novel view synthesis in the dark via coarse-to-fine radiance-field optimization.
AI.motivation: Conventional feature- and learning-based SfM break down at SNRs below roughly -4 dB; there is a need for robust feature matching and pose estimation under extreme low light, with training that does not require 3D supervision.
AI.method: Distill knowledge from large 3D foundation models into a framework that operates directly on raw low-light images. Train on noisy–clean raw pairs, with Poisson–Gaussian noise to synthesize data when needed. No 3D supervision is used. Introduce an exposure-bracketed dataset with ~42k multi-view raw images and ground-truth 3D annotations. For novel view synthesis, employ predicted poses with a coarse-to-fine radiance-field optimization.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art structure-from-motion performance in the low-SNR regime. Demonstrates state-of-the-art novel view synthesis in darkness leveraging Dark3R poses and radiance-field optimization.
AI.conclusion: Dark3R enables robust 3D reconstruction and view synthesis under extreme low-light conditions without 3D supervision, supported by a large exposure-bracketed dataset, highlighting potential for broader low-light 3D vision tasks.

title: GALACTIC: Global and Local Agnostic Counterfactuals for Time-series Clustering
summary: Time-series clustering is a fundamental tool for pattern discovery, yet existing explainability methods, primarily based on feature attribution or metadata, fail to identify the transitions that move an instance across cluster boundaries. While Counterfactual Explanations (CEs) identify the minimal temporal perturbations required to alter the prediction of a model, they have been mostly confined to supervised settings. This paper introduces GALACTIC, the first unified framework to bridge local and global counterfactual explainability for unsupervised time-series clustering. At instance level (local), GALACTIC generates perturbations via a cluster-aware optimization objective that respects the target and underlying cluster assignments. At cluster level (global), to mitigate cognitive load and enhance interpretability, we formulate a representative CE selection problem. We propose a Minimum Description Length (MDL) objective to extract a non-redundant summary of global explanations that characterize the transitions between clusters. We prove that our MDL objective is supermodular, which allows the corresponding MDL reduction to be framed as a monotone submodular set function. This enables an efficient greedy selection algorithm with provable $(1-1/e)$ approximation guarantees. Extensive experimental evaluation on the UCR Archive demonstrates that GALACTIC produces significantly sparser local CEs and more concise global summaries than state-of-the-art baselines adapted for our problem, offering the first unified approach for interpreting clustered time-series through counterfactuals.

AI.tldr: GALACTIC blends local and global counterfactual explanations for unsupervised time-series clustering, introducing a cluster-aware local perturbation mechanism and a minimum description length (MDL) based global summary with a greedy, provably good selection strategy.
AI.motivation: Explainability for time-series clustering is underdeveloped; existing methods rely on feature attributions or metadata and fail to identify transitions across cluster boundaries. There is a need for a unified local-global counterfactual framework in the unsupervised setting to reveal how cluster assignments change.
AI.method: Two-level design: (1) Local: generate perturbations via a cluster-aware optimization objective that respects target and underlying cluster assignments. (2) Global: formulate a representative CE selection problem; derive an MDL objective to extract a non-redundant summary of global explanations of transitions between clusters; prove MDL objective is supermodular and use this to frame MDL reduction as a monotone submodular set function, enabling a greedy algorithm with (1-1/e) approximation guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the UCR Archive shows GALACTIC yields significantly sparser local counterfactual explanations and more concise global summaries than adapted baselines, providing the first unified approach for interpreting clustered time-series through counterfactuals.
AI.conclusion: GALACTIC provides a unified, efficient framework for local-global counterfactual explainability in unsupervised time-series clustering. Its MDL-based global summaries offer compact, non-redundant characterizations of cluster transitions, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

title: ORMOT: A Dataset and Framework for Omnidirectional Referring Multi-Object Tracking
summary: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) is a fundamental task in computer vision, aiming to track targets across video frames. Existing MOT methods perform well in general visual scenes, but face significant challenges and limitations when extended to visual-language settings. To bridge this gap, the task of Referring Multi-Object Tracking (RMOT) has recently been proposed, which aims to track objects that correspond to language descriptions. However, current RMOT methods are primarily developed on datasets captured by conventional cameras, which suffer from limited field of view. This constraint often causes targets to move out of the frame, leading to fragmented tracking and loss of contextual information. In this work, we propose a novel task, called Omnidirectional Referring Multi-Object Tracking (ORMOT), which extends RMOT to omnidirectional imagery, aiming to overcome the field-of-view (FoV) limitation of conventional datasets and improve the model's ability to understand long-horizon language descriptions. To advance the ORMOT task, we construct ORSet, an Omnidirectional Referring Multi-Object Tracking dataset, which contains 27 diverse omnidirectional scenes, 848 language descriptions, and 3,401 annotated objects, providing rich visual, temporal, and language information. Furthermore, we propose ORTrack, a Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM)-driven framework tailored for Omnidirectional Referring Multi-Object Tracking. Extensive experiments on the ORSet dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our ORTrack framework. The dataset and code will be open-sourced at https://github.com/chen-si-jia/ORMOT.

AI.tldr: Proposes Omnidirectional Referring Multi-Object Tracking (ORMOT) with a new dataset (ORSet) and an LVLM-driven framework (ORTrack) to address field-of-view limitations and long-horizon language understanding in RMOT; results show effectiveness and plans to release code.
AI.motivation: RMOT methods struggle with limited FoV in conventional cameras, leading to fragmentation and loss of context. Omnidirectional imagery offers full-field panoramic context, enabling robust long-horizon language grounding for multi-object tracking.
AI.method: Define the ORMOT task, construct ORSet (27 omnidirectional scenes, 848 language descriptions, 3,401 annotated objects), and develop ORTrack, a Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM)-driven framework tailored for ORMOT. Conduct extensive experiments on ORSet to validate effectiveness; plan to open-source code.
AI.result: Experiments on ORSet demonstrate the effectiveness of ORTrack for ORMOT, indicating improved capability to track described objects in omnidirectional video and maintain associations across wide FoV.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a new task, dataset, and LVLM-driven framework for omnidirectional referring MOT. ORSet and ORTrack provide benchmarks and baselines for future research, with code and data to be released to the community.

title: FairFinGAN: Fairness-aware Synthetic Financial Data Generation
comment: Accepted to Special Session: Data Science: Foundations and Applications (DSFA), PAKDD 2026
summary: Financial datasets often suffer from bias that can lead to unfair decision-making in automated systems. In this work, we propose FairFinGAN, a WGAN-based framework designed to generate synthetic financial data while mitigating bias with respect to the protected attribute. Our approach incorporates fairness constraints directly into the training process through a classifier, ensuring that the synthetic data is both fair and preserves utility for downstream predictive tasks. We evaluate our proposed model on five real-world financial datasets and compare it with existing GAN-based data generation methods. Experimental results show that our approach achieves superior fairness metrics without significant loss in data utility, demonstrating its potential as a tool for bias-aware data generation in financial applications.

AI.tldr: FairFinGAN is a Wasserstein GAN-based model that embeds a fairness constraint via a classifier to generate synthetic financial data that is fair with respect to protected attributes while preserving downstream utility; evaluated on five real-world datasets, it outperforms existing GAN baselines on fairness with minimal utility loss.
AI.motivation: Financial datasets frequently exhibit bias that can propagate to automated decision systems; synthetic data with fairness guarantees can support fairer downstream analytics and policy compliance.
AI.method: A WGAN framework augmented with a classifier-driven fairness constraint; the generator is trained to produce data that is useful for downstream tasks while reducing dependence on protected attributes, leveraging an auxiliary classifier to enforce fairness during training.
AI.result: Across five real-world financial datasets, FairFinGAN achieves superior fairness metrics compared to GAN-based baselines, with no significant degradation in data utility.
AI.conclusion: FairFinGAN demonstrates the viability of integrating fairness constraints into GAN-based synthetic data generation for finance, offering a tool for bias-aware data generation in financial applications.

title: Fusion-CAM: Integrating Gradient and Region-Based Class Activation Maps for Robust Visual Explanations
summary: Interpreting the decision-making process of deep convolutional neural networks remains a central challenge in achieving trustworthy and transparent artificial intelligence. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, particularly Class Activation Map (CAM) methods, are widely adopted to visualize the input regions influencing model predictions. Gradient-based approaches (e.g. Grad-CAM) provide highly discriminative, fine-grained details by computing gradients of class activations but often yield noisy and incomplete maps that emphasize only the most salient regions rather than the complete objects. Region-based approaches (e.g. Score-CAM) aggregate information over larger areas, capturing broader object coverage at the cost of over-smoothing and reduced sensitivity to subtle features. We introduce Fusion-CAM, a novel framework that bridges this explanatory gap by unifying both paradigms through a dedicated fusion mechanism to produce robust and highly discriminative visual explanations. Our method first denoises gradient-based maps, yielding cleaner and more focused activations. It then combines the refined gradient map with region-based maps using contribution weights to enhance class coverage. Finally, we propose an adaptive similarity-based pixel-level fusion that evaluates the agreement between both paradigms and dynamically adjusts the fusion strength. This adaptive mechanism reinforces consistent activations while softly blending conflicting regions, resulting in richer, context-aware, and input-adaptive visual explanations. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show that Fusion-CAM consistently outperforms existing CAM variants in both qualitative visualization and quantitative evaluation, providing a robust and flexible tool for interpreting deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: Fusion-CAM unifies gradient-based and region-based CAMs via denoising, fusion with weighted contribution, and adaptive similarity-based pixel-level fusion to produce robust, discriminative explanations that cover the full object and are input-adaptive.
AI.motivation: Existing CAM methods trade off between fine-grained but noisy gradient maps (Grad-CAM) and broader, smoother region maps (Score-CAM). A unified approach aims to combine their strengths while mitigating their weaknesses to provide more complete and reliable explanations for DNN decisions.
AI.method: 1) Denoise gradient-based activation maps to produce cleaner activations. 2) Fuse the refined gradient map with region-based maps using contribution weights to improve class coverage. 3) Apply an adaptive similarity-based pixel-level fusion that measures agreement between both paradigms and adjusts fusion strength dynamically, softly blending conflicting regions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks show Fusion-CAM outperforms existing CAM variants in both qualitative visualizations and quantitative metrics, indicating more robust, context-aware explanations with improved object coverage and discriminativity.
AI.conclusion: Fusion-CAM effectively bridges gradient-based and region-based CAMs, delivering an input-adaptive, richer explanation that leverages the strengths of both approaches to enhance interpretability of deep CNNs.

title: Preserving Continuous Symmetry in Discrete Spaces: Geometric-Aware Quantization for SO(3)-Equivariant GNNs
summary: Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are essential for physically consistent molecular simulations but suffer from high computational costs and memory bottlenecks, especially with high-order representations. While low-bit quantization offers a solution, applying it naively to rotation-sensitive features destroys the SO(3)-equivariant structure, leading to significant errors and violations of conservation laws. To address this issue, in this work, we propose a Geometric-Aware Quantization (GAQ) framework that compresses and accelerates equivariant models while rigorously preserving continuous symmetry in discrete spaces. Our approach introduces three key contributions: (1) a Magnitude-Direction Decoupled Quantization (MDDQ) scheme that separates invariant lengths from equivariant orientations to maintain geometric fidelity; (2) a symmetry-aware training strategy that treats scalar and vector features with distinct quantization schedules; and (3) a robust attention normalization mechanism to stabilize gradients in low-bit regimes. Experiments on the rMD17 benchmark demonstrate that our W4A8 models match the accuracy of FP32 baselines (9.31 meV vs. 23.20 meV) while reducing Local Equivariance Error (LEE) by over 30x compared to naive quantization. On consumer hardware, GAQ achieves 2.39x inference speedup and 4x memory reduction, enabling stable, energy-conserving molecular dynamics simulations for nanosecond timescales.

AI.tldr: GAQ enables low-bit quantization of equivariant GNNs by decoupling magnitude and direction, preserving continuous SO(3) symmetry while boosting speed and reducing errors.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational and memory costs of high-order equivariant GNNs without breaking symmetry constraints; naive low-bit quantization breaks equivariance and conservation laws.
AI.method: MDDQ decouples invariant lengths from equivariant orientations; symmetry-aware training treats scalar and vector features with distinct quantization schedules; robust attention normalization stabilizes gradients in low-bit regimes.
AI.result: On the rMD17 benchmark, W4A8 matches FP32 accuracy (9.31 meV vs 23.20 meV) and reduces Local Equivariance Error by >30x versus naive quantization; achieves 2.39x inference speedup and 4x memory reduction on consumer hardware, enabling nanosecond organic MD with energy conservation.
AI.conclusion: GAQ provides a practical, geometry-preserving quantization framework for equivariant GNNs, enabling fast, memory-efficient molecular simulations without sacrificing physical fidelity.

title: Video-based Locomotion Analysis for Fish Health Monitoring
summary: Monitoring the health conditions of fish is essential, as it enables the early detection of disease, safeguards animal welfare, and contributes to sustainable aquaculture practices. Physiological and pathological conditions of cultivated fish can be inferred by analyzing locomotion activities. In this paper, we present a system that estimates the locomotion activities from videos using multi object tracking. The core of our approach is a YOLOv11 detector embedded in a tracking-by-detection framework. We investigate various configurations of the YOLOv11-architecture as well as extensions that incorporate multiple frames to improve detection accuracy. Our system is evaluated on a manually annotated dataset of Sulawesi ricefish recorded in a home-aquarium-like setup, demonstrating its ability to reliably measure swimming direction and speed for fish health monitoring. The dataset will be made publicly available upon publication.

AI.tldr: A YOLOv11-based multi-object tracking system estimates fish locomotion from video in a tracking-by-detection framework, evaluated on a Sulawesi ricefish dataset; dataset to be released.
AI.motivation: Enable early disease detection and welfare monitoring in aquaculture by inferring physiological/pathological states from fish locomotion patterns.
AI.method: Tracking-by-detection pipeline using YOLOv11 as the detector; exploring multiple YOLOv11 configurations and multi-frame extensions to boost detection accuracy; evaluated on a manually annotated home-aquarium dataset of Sulawesi ricefish; dataset to be released.
AI.result: The system reliably measures swimming direction and speed, enabling health monitoring; shows that multi-frame extensions and detector variants impact accuracy; dataset will be made publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The approach is effective for aquaculture health surveillance via non-invasive video analysis; potential for scalable welfare monitoring, with a public dataset to support reproducibility and further research.

title: InfoFlow KV: Information-Flow-Aware KV Recomputation for Long Context
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for long-context question answering is bottlenecked by inference-time prefilling over large retrieved contexts. A common strategy is to precompute key-value (KV) caches for individual documents and selectively recompute a small subset of tokens to restore global causal dependencies, but existing methods rely on heuristics or representation discrepancies without modeling whether selected tokens can effectively influence generation. We cast selective KV recomputation as an information flow problem and show that a simple attention-norm signal from the query reliably identifies tokens that are both semantically relevant and structurally positioned to propagate information, when computed under an inference-consistent RoPE geometry. We therefore reconstruct global positional assignments for retrieved chunks and introduce an information-flow-guided chunk reordering strategy. Experiments on LLM and VLM benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains over prior methods under comparable efficiency budgets.

AI.tldr: Proposes an information-flow-guided KV recomputation and chunk reordering for retrieval-augmented generation in long-context QA, improving efficiency and accuracy by using an attention-norm signal under consistent RoPE geometry to select tokens for recomputation.
AI.motivation: Long-context RAG suffers from inference-time prefilling over large retrieved contexts; existing cache strategies rely on heuristics and lack modeling of token influence on generation.
AI.method: Model selective KV recomputation as an information-flow problem. Use a query-attention norm under inference-consistent RoPE geometry to identify tokens that are semantically relevant and structurally propagative. Reconstruct global positional assignments for retrieved chunks and apply an information-flow-guided chunk reordering strategy.
AI.result: Empirical evidence on LLM and VLM benchmarks shows consistent gains over prior methods within the same efficiency budget.
AI.conclusion: An information-flow-guided approach to selective KV recomputation and chunk reordering improves long-context RAG performance and efficiency.

title: MobileFetalCLIP: Selective Repulsive Knowledge Distillation for Mobile Fetal Ultrasound Analysis
summary: Fetal ultrasound AI could transform prenatal care in low-resource settings, yet current foundation models exceed 300M visual parameters, precluding deployment on point-of-care devices. Standard knowledge distillation fails under such extreme capacity gaps (~26x), as compact students waste capacity mimicking architectural artifacts of oversized teachers. We introduce Selective Repulsive Knowledge Distillation, which decomposes contrastive KD into diagonal and off-diagonal components: matched pair alignment is preserved while the off-diagonal weight decays into negative values, repelling the student from the teacher's inter-class confusions and forcing discovery of architecturally native features. Our 11.4M parameter student surpasses the 304M-parameter FetalCLIP teacher on zero-shot HC18 biometry validity (88.6% vs. 83.5%) and brain sub-plane F1 (0.784 vs. 0.702), while running at 1.6 ms on iPhone 16 Pro, enabling real-time assistive AI on handheld ultrasound devices. Our code, models, and app are publicly available at https://github.com/numanai/MobileFetalCLIP.

AI.tldr: An 11.4M parameter student model with Selective Repulsive Knowledge Distillation (SRKD) matches or surpasses a 304M FetalCLIP teacher on zero-shot fetal ultrasound tasks, enabling real-time deployment on handheld devices with 1.6 ms inference on iPhone 16 Pro.
AI.motivation: Enable effective fetal ultrasound AI in low-resource settings by enabling extreme-capacity-gap distillation where standard KD fails and compact models must avoid simply mimicking oversized teachers' architectural artefacts.
AI.method: Introduce Selective Repulsive Knowledge Distillation that decomposes contrastive KD into diagonal (matched pair alignment preserved) and off-diagonal components; shrink off-diagonal weights to negative values to repel the student from teacher confusions and promote learning architecturally native features.
AI.result: A 11.4M-parameter student surpasses the 304M-parameter teacher on zero-shot HC18 biometry validity (88.6% vs. 83.5%) and brain sub-plane F1 (0.784 vs. 0.702), while running at 1.6 ms on iPhone 16 Pro.
AI.conclusion: SRKD enables extreme-compact models to achieve competitive fetal ultrasound performance on mobile devices, facilitating real-time assistive AI in point-of-care settings; code and app are publicly available.

title: Learning Causal Structure of Time Series using Best Order Score Search
summary: Causal structure learning from observational data is central to many scientific and policy domains, but the time series setting common to many disciplines poses several challenges due to temporal dependence. In this paper we focus on score-based causal discovery for multivariate time series and introduce TS-BOSS, a time series extension of the recently proposed Best Order Score Search (BOSS) (Andrews et al. 2023). TS-BOSS performs a permutation-based search over dynamic Bayesian network structures while leveraging grow-shrink trees to cache intermediate score computations, preserving the scalability and strong empirical performance of BOSS in the static setting. We provide theoretical guarantees establishing the soundness of TS-BOSS under suitable assumptions, and we present an intermediate result that extends classical subgraph minimality results for permutation-based methods to the dynamic (time series) setting. Our experiments on synthetic data show that TS-BOSS is especially effective in high auto-correlation regimes, where it consistently achieves higher adjacency recall at comparable precision than standard constraint-based methods. Overall, TS-BOSS offers a high-performing, scalable approach for time series causal discovery and our results provide a principled bridge for extending sparsity-based, permutation-driven causal learning theory to dynamic settings.

AI.tldr: TS-BOSS extends BOSS to multivariate time series for causal discovery by performing a permutation-based search over dynamic Bayesian networks and employing grow-shrink caches to retain scalability. It provides theoretical soundness guarantees and an intermediate subgraph-minimality result for dynamic settings, with empirical gains in high auto-correlation regimes.
AI.motivation: Bridge sparsity-based, permutation-driven causal learning theory from static data to dynamic, time-series data; improve scalable and accurate causal discovery under temporal dependence.
AI.method: Time-series extension of Best Order Score Search (BOSS) using a permutation-based search over dynamic Bayesian networks. Introduces grow-shrink trees to cache intermediate score computations, enabling scalable search. Proves soundness under suitable assumptions and derives a dynamic-subgraph minimality result. Evaluates on synthetic data, focusing on high auto-correlation regimes.
AI.result: TS-BOSS achieves higher adjacency recall at comparable precision than standard constraint-based methods in high auto-correlation regimes, while maintaining scalability due to caching. Provides a principled theoretical framework extending permutation-based causal learning to dynamic settings.
AI.conclusion: TS-BOSS is a high-performing, scalable approach for time-series causal discovery that extends BOSS theory to dynamic data, offering both practical accuracy and theoretical guarantees.

title: RelaxFlow: Text-Driven Amodal 3D Generation
summary: Image-to-3D generation faces inherent semantic ambiguity under occlusion, where partial observation alone is often insufficient to determine object category. In this work, we formalize text-driven amodal 3D generation, where text prompts steer the completion of unseen regions while strictly preserving input observation. Crucially, we identify that these objectives demand distinct control granularities: rigid control for the observation versus relaxed structural control for the prompt. To this end, we propose RelaxFlow, a training-free dual-branch framework that decouples control granularity via a Multi-Prior Consensus Module and a Relaxation Mechanism. Theoretically, we prove that our relaxation is equivalent to applying a low-pass filter on the generative vector field, which suppresses high-frequency instance details to isolate geometric structure that accommodates the observation. To facilitate evaluation, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks, ExtremeOcc-3D and AmbiSem-3D. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RelaxFlow successfully steers the generation of unseen regions to match the prompt intent without compromising visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: Proposes RelaxFlow, a training-free dual-branch framework for text-driven amodal 3D generation under occlusion that decouples control granularity between observed regions and prompt-driven unseen regions. The relaxation mechanism acts as a low-pass filter on the generative vector field to isolate geometric structure compatible with the input. Introduces ExtremeOcc-3D and AmbiSem-3D benchmarks and demonstrates faithful, prompt-guided completion without sacrificing fidelity.
AI.motivation: Image-to-3D generation under occlusion suffers semantic ambiguity: partial observation does not determine object category. There is a need to complete unseen regions guided by text while strictly preserving the observed input, and to support distinct control granularities for observed versus generated regions.
AI.method: RelaxFlow is a training-free dual-branch framework with a Multi-Prior Consensus Module to enforce rigid control over the observation and a Relaxation Mechanism to enable relaxed, prompt-driven structural control. The relaxation is theoretically equivalent to applying a low-pass filter on the generative vector field to suppress high-frequency details and reveal geometry that accommodates the observation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows RelaxFlow can steer unseen regions to align with the text prompt while maintaining high visual fidelity. The authors introduce two diagnostic benchmarks, ExtremeOcc-3D and AmbiSem-3D, to assess amodal 3D generation under extreme occlusion and ambigious semantics.
AI.conclusion: RelaxFlow provides a principled, training-free solution for text-guided amodal 3D generation under occlusion by decoupling control granularity and applying a relaxation that prioritizes geometric compatibility with the observed input. The introduced benchmarks enable robust evaluation of amodal capabilities.

title: Embedded Inter-Subject Variability in Adversarial Learning for Inertial Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition
comment: Accepted in the IEEE 35th International Workshop on Machine Learning for Signal Processing (MLSP). This is the author's version of the work
summary: This paper addresses the problem of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using data from wearable inertial sensors. An important challenge in HAR is the model's generalization capabilities to new unseen individuals due to inter-subject variability, i.e., the same activity is performed differently by different individuals. To address this problem, we propose a novel deep adversarial framework that integrates the concept of inter-subject variability in the adversarial task, thereby encouraging subject-invariant feature representations and enhancing the classification performance in the HAR problem. Our approach outperforms previous methods in three well-established HAR datasets using a leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation. Further results indicate that our proposed adversarial task effectively reduces inter-subject variability among different users in the feature space, and it outperforms adversarial tasks from previous works when integrated into our framework. Code: https://github.com/FranciscoCalatrava/EmbeddedSubjectVariability.git

AI.tldr: A deep adversarial framework for human activity recognition that enforces subject-invariant features by incorporating inter-subject variability into the adversarial task, improving cross-subject generalization under LOSO evaluation across three HAR datasets; code provided.
AI.motivation: Inter-subject variability causes degraded generalization in HAR models as the same activity is performed differently by individuals; need representations that are invariant to subject identity.
AI.method: A deep adversarial architecture that integrates inter-subject variability into the adversarial objective, likely via a subject-discriminator to promote subject-invariant features, trained and evaluated with leave-one-subject-out cross-validation on three HAR datasets.
AI.result: The approach yields superior performance compared to prior methods under LOSO; the adversarial task effectively reduces inter-subject variability in the feature space and outperforms previous adversarial strategies within the framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed subject-variability–aware adversarial framework improves cross-subject HAR generalization by learning subject-invariant representations; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: SAIL: Similarity-Aware Guidance and Inter-Caption Augmentation-based Learning for Weakly-Supervised Dense Video Captioning
summary: Weakly-Supervised Dense Video Captioning aims to localize and describe events in videos trained only on caption annotations, without temporal boundaries. Prior work introduced an implicit supervision paradigm based on Gaussian masking and complementary captioning. However, existing method focuses merely on generating non-overlapping masks without considering their semantic relationship to corresponding events, resulting in simplistic, uniformly distributed masks that fail to capture semantically meaningful regions. Moreover, relying solely on ground-truth captions leads to sub-optimal performance due to the inherent sparsity of existing datasets. In this work, we propose SAIL, which constructs semantically-aware masks through cross-modal alignment. Our similarity aware training objective guides masks to emphasize video regions with high similarity to their corresponding event captions. Furthermore, to guide more accurate mask generation under sparse annotation settings, we introduce an LLM-based augmentation strategy that generates synthetic captions to provide additional alignment signals. These synthetic captions are incorporated through an inter-mask mechanism, providing auxiliary guidance for precise temporal localization without degrading the main objective. Experiments on ActivityNet Captions and YouCook2 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both captioning and localization metrics.

AI.tldr: SAIL introduces semantically-aware masks for weakly-supervised dense video captioning using cross-modal alignment and LLM-based data augmentation to improve localization and captioning under sparse annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing weakly supervised methods produce non-semantic, uniformly distributed masks and underutilize ground-truth captions; data sparsity hampers performance.
AI.method: Cross-modal similarity-aware training to emphasize regions aligned with captions; LLM-generated synthetic captions with an inter-mask mechanism to augment supervision; auxiliary guidance during training improves temporal localization under sparse annotations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ActivityNet Captions and YouCook2 for both captioning and localization metrics.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-aware masking with cross-modal alignment and synthetic caption augmentation yields more accurate temporal localization and higher-quality captions under weak supervision.

title: Robust Node Affinities via Jaccard-Biased Random Walks and Rank Aggregation
summary: Estimating node similarity is a fundamental task in network analysis and graph-based machine learning, with applications in clustering, community detection, classification, and recommendation. We propose TopKGraphs, a method based on start-node-anchored random walks that bias transitions toward nodes with structurally similar neighborhoods, measured via Jaccard similarity. Rather than computing stationary distributions, walks are treated as stochastic neighborhood samplers, producing partial node rankings that are aggregated using robust rank aggregation to construct interpretable node-to-node affinity matrices. TopKGraphs provides a non-parametric, interpretable, and general-purpose representation of node similarity that can be applied in both network analysis and machine learning workflows. We evaluate the method on synthetic graphs (stochastic block models, Lancichinetti-Fortunato-Radicchi benchmark graphs), k-nearest-neighbor graphs from tabular datasets, and a curated high-confidence protein-protein interaction network. Across all scenarios, TopKGraphs achieves competitive or superior performance compared to standard similarity measures (Jaccard, Dice), a diffusion-based method (personalized PageRank), and an embedding-based approach (Node2Vec), demonstrating robustness in sparse, noisy, or heterogeneous networks. These results suggest that TopKGraphs is a versatile and interpretable tool for bridging simple local similarity measures with more complex embedding-based approaches, facilitating both data mining and network analysis applications.

AI.tldr: TopKGraphs builds node similarity by running start-node-anchored, biased random walks toward nodes with similar neighborhoods (via Jaccard similarity). Walks act as stochastic neighborhood samplers, producing partial node rankings that are merged with robust rank aggregation to form an interpretable, non-parametric node-affinity matrix. It offers a general-purpose alternative to embeddings and diffusion methods, performing well on synthetic, tabular, and biological networks.
AI.motivation: Provide an interpretable, non-parametric node-similarity representation that can bridge simple local similarity measures with embedding-based approaches, and remain robust in sparse, noisy, or heterogeneous networks where diffusion or embedding methods may fail or be less interpretable.
AI.method: For each start node, perform anchored random walks biased toward transitions to nodes with similar neighborhoods as measured by Jaccard similarity. Treat walks as stochastic neighborhood samplers rather than stationary distributions, yielding partial rankings of neighboring nodes. Aggregate these partial rankings across all start nodes using robust rank aggregation to produce a node-to-node affinity matrix. The approach is non-parametric and does not rely on learning a parametric embedding.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on SBM and LFR synthetic graphs, k-NN graphs from tabular data, and a high-confidence protein-protein interaction network shows TopKGraphs achieving competitive or superior performance relative to Jaccard, Dice, personalized PageRank, and Node2Vec, with robustness to sparsity, noise, and heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: TopKGraphs provides a versatile, interpretable node-similarity representation that can be readily integrated into data mining and network analysis workflows, offering a bridge between simple local similarity and more complex embedding-based methods.

title: On the Necessity of Learnable Sheaf Laplacians
summary: Sheaf Neural Networks (SNNs) were introduced as an extension of Graph Convolutional Networks to address oversmoothing on heterophilous graphs by attaching a sheaf to the input graph and replacing the adjacency-based operator with a sheaf Laplacian defined by (learnable) restriction maps. Prior work motivates this design through theoretical properties of sheaf diffusion and the kernel of the sheaf Laplacian, suggesting that suitable non-identity restriction maps can avoid representations converging to constants across connected components. Since oversmoothing can also be mitigated through residual connections and normalization, we revisit a trivial sheaf construction to ask whether the additional complexity of learning restriction maps is necessary. We introduce an Identity Sheaf Network baseline, where all restriction maps are fixed to the identity, and use it to ablate the empirical improvements reported by sheaf-learning architectures. Across five popular heterophilic benchmarks, the identity baseline achieves comparable performance to a range of SNN variants. Finally, we introduce the Rayleigh quotient as a normalized measure for comparing oversmoothing across models and show that, in trained networks, the behavior predicted by the diffusion-based analysis of SNNs is not reflected empirically. In particular, Identity Sheaf Networks do not appear to suffer more significant oversmoothing than their SNN counterparts.

AI.tldr: Identity-only (Identity Sheaf Network) baselines match SNN performance on heterophilic graphs, suggesting learned restriction maps may be unnecessary for mitigating oversmoothing; empirical oversmoothing in trained models does not align with diffusion-based predictions; complexity of learning maps may be unwarranted.
AI.motivation: Question the necessity of learning restriction maps in SNNs for addressing oversmoothing and achieving gains on heterophilic graphs; test whether a trivial identity construction suffices.
AI.method: Introduce Identity Sheaf Network baseline with all restriction maps fixed to identity. Evaluate on five heterophilic benchmarks and compare to SNN variants. Use Rayleigh quotient to quantify oversmoothing and diffusion behavior in trained networks.
AI.result: Identity baseline achieves comparable performance to SNN variants across five benchmarks. Rayleigh quotient analysis shows diffusion-based oversmoothing predictions do not hold empirically in trained networks; Identity SNNs do not suffer more oversmoothing than SNNs.
AI.conclusion: Learning restriction maps may be unnecessary; the purported benefits of learnable sheaf maps do not translate empirically in these settings. The diffusion-theoretic explanations of oversmoothing do not align with observed behavior in trained models; revisit SNN design focusing on normalization/residuals rather than map learnability.

title: NaiLIA: Multimodal Nail Design Retrieval Based on Dense Intent Descriptions and Palette Queries
summary: We focus on the task of retrieving nail design images based on dense intent descriptions, which represent multi-layered user intent for nail designs. This is challenging because such descriptions specify unconstrained painted elements and pre-manufactured embellishments as well as visual characteristics, themes, and overall impressions. In addition to these descriptions, we assume that users provide palette queries by specifying zero or more colors via a color picker, enabling the expression of subtle and continuous color nuances. Existing vision-language foundation models often struggle to incorporate such descriptions and palettes. To address this, we propose NaiLIA, a multimodal retrieval method for nail design images, which comprehensively aligns with dense intent descriptions and palette queries during retrieval. Our approach introduces a relaxed loss based on confidence scores for unlabeled images that can align with the descriptions. To evaluate NaiLIA, we constructed a benchmark consisting of 10,625 images collected from people with diverse cultural backgrounds. The images were annotated with long and dense intent descriptions given by over 200 annotators. Experimental results demonstrate that NaiLIA outperforms standard methods.

AI.tldr: NaiLIA is a multimodal nail-design retrieval method that aligns dense intent descriptions with optional palette queries, using a relaxed loss on unlabeled data and a large, diverse annotated benchmark; it outperforms standard retrieval baselines.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models struggle to incorporate dense, multi-layered user intent and palette-based color queries for nail designs, which include unconstrained painted elements, embellishments, themes, and nuanced colors.
AI.method: NaiLIA introduces alignment between dense textual intents and color palettes during retrieval and employs a relaxed loss based on confidence scores for unlabeled images to improve learning. The authors collected a benchmark of 10,625 nail-design images from diverse cultures, annotated by 200+ annotators with long, dense intent descriptions.
